topics.title: Introduction to Statics: Branches of Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Statics: Branches of Mechanics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Mechanics?

**Mechanics** is the branch of physical science that deals with the **state of rest** or **state of motion** of physical bodies under the action of **forces**. It is the oldest and most fundamental branch of engineering science, forming the foundation for almost every other engineering discipline — Civil, Mechanical, Aerospace, Automobile, and even Electrical (in the form of electromechanical systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Alignment):**
> *Mechanics* is the branch of physical science which deals with the state of rest or the state of motion of the bodies subjected to the action of forces. The subject of Engineering Mechanics is broadly classified into **Statics** and **Dynamics**.

In simple terms — *Mechanics answers two questions about any object:* "**Is it moving or still?**" and "**Why is it behaving that way?**"

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Mechanics

Every mechanical analysis rests on four fundamental concepts that the KTU syllabus explicitly lists:

1. **Space** — The geometric region in which events occur (measured in **meters, m**).
2. **Time** — The measure of succession of events (measured in **seconds, s**).
3. **Mass** — A measure of the inertial and gravitational property of matter (measured in **kilograms, kg**).
4. **Force** — The action of one body on another that tends to change its state of rest or motion (measured in **Newtons, N**).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Newton (N)** is the derived SI unit of force. By Newton's Second Law, $1\,\text{N}$ is the force required to accelerate a $1\,\text{kg}$ mass at $1\,\text{m/s}^2$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Story of a Pushing Box"

Imagine you are pushing a heavy cardboard box across a smooth floor.

- If the **box is stationary** and you are analyzing the *equilibrium* of forces acting on it, you are doing **Statics**.
- If the **box is sliding** and you want to find *how fast it moves* without worrying about *why*, you are doing **Kinematics**.
- If the **box is sliding** and you want to find *which force and mass combination* produced this acceleration, you are doing **Kinetics**.
- If the **box is rigid** and you treat it as a non-deforming block, you are in **Rigid Body Mechanics**.
- If the **box gets crushed** under the push and you want to study its *deformation and internal stresses*, you are in **Deformable Body Mechanics (Mechanics of Solids / Strength of Materials)**.
- If the box is filled with **water** and the water is sloshing around, the analysis falls under **Fluid Mechanics**.

This single everyday scenario maps directly to every major branch in the KTU syllabus.

## 1.4 What is "Statics" Specifically?

**Statics** is the branch of mechanics that deals with bodies **at rest** (in equilibrium) or moving with **constant velocity** (where the net effect of forces is still zero). For an object to be in static equilibrium, **two conditions** must be satisfied simultaneously:

- **Force equilibrium:** $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ (no net force)
- **Moment equilibrium:** $\sum \vec{M} = 0$ (no net turning effect)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples of Statics:** Bridges under dead load, buildings under self-weight, a book resting on a table, a ladder leaning against a wall, a crane lifting a stationary load.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Mechanics

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups mechanics into the following structured hierarchy:

- **Mechanics of Rigid Bodies**
  - **Statics** — bodies in equilibrium.
  - **Dynamics** — bodies in motion.
    - **Kinematics** — study of *geometry* of motion (no force considered).
    - **Kinetics** — study of *forces* causing motion.
- **Mechanics of Deformable Bodies (Mechanics of Solids)** — internal stresses, strains, deflections.
- **Mechanics of Fluids** — behavior of liquids and gases (further split into Fluid Statics/Fluid Kinematics/Fluid Dynamics).

### 2.1.1 Statics vs. Dynamics — The Core Distinction

| Aspect | Statics | Dynamics |
|---|---|---|
| State of Body | At rest or uniform velocity | Accelerated motion |
| Newton's 2nd Law | $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ | $\sum \vec{F} = m\vec{a} \neq 0$ |
| Typical Problems | Bridges, trusses, frames | Vehicles, projectiles, machines |
| KTU Module | Mostly Module 1, 2, 3 | Module 4, 5 |

### 2.1.2 Kinematics vs. Kinetics — Sub-distinction within Dynamics

- **Kinematics** asks: *"How does the body move?"* — displacement, velocity, acceleration, no force analysis.
- **Kinetics** asks: *"Why does the body move that way?"* — force–mass–acceleration relations using Newton's laws.

## 2.2 Fundamental Concepts (KTU Mandatory)

- **Particle:** A body of negligible dimensions whose mass is concentrated at a single point. Used when rotation is not considered.
- **Rigid Body:** A body in which the relative positions of any two points remain constant under the action of forces (no deformation assumed).
- **Scalar Quantity:** Has only magnitude (e.g., mass, time, temperature).
- **Vector Quantity:** Has magnitude and direction (e.g., force, velocity, moment).

## 2.3 Newton's Three Laws of Motion (Foundation Pillars)

**Law 1 — Law of Inertia:**
A particle remains at rest or moves in a straight line with constant velocity unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

$$\sum \vec{F} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{a} = 0$$

**Law 2 — Fundamental Equation of Motion:**
The acceleration of a particle is directly proportional to the resultant force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass. The direction of acceleration is the same as the direction of the resultant force.

$$\vec{F} = m\,\vec{a}$$

**Law 3 — Action-Reaction:**
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. Forces between two bodies always act along the line joining them and are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

$$\vec{F}_{AB} = -\vec{F}_{BA}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Force (Newton's 2nd Law) | $F = m\,a$ | N (kg·m/s²) |
| 2 | Weight of a body | $W = m\,g$ where $g = 9.81$ m/s² | N |
| 3 | Force equilibrium | $\sum F_x = 0,\ \sum F_y = 0$ | N |
| 4 | Moment equilibrium | $\sum M = 0$ | N·m |
| 5 | Moment of a force | $M = F \times d$ | N·m |
| 6 | Newton's 3rd Law | $F_{AB} = -F_{BA}$ | N |
| 7 | Impulse-Momentum | $F \cdot \Delta t = m\,\Delta v$ | N·s |
| 8 | Kinematic eqn (const. acc.) | $v = u + a t$ | m/s |
| 9 | Kinematic eqn (const. acc.) | $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$ | m |
| 10 | Kinematic eqn (const. acc.) | $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$ | (m/s)² |
| 11 | Universal Gravitation | $F = G\,\frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$ | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard value for KTU board exams:** Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s² (some questions may use $g = 9.8$ m/s² — read the problem carefully).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Truss analysis, retaining walls, building frames → all use **Statics**.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Engine dynamics, gear trains, cam design → use **Kinetics & Kinematics**.
- **Aerospace:** Rocket trajectories, satellite orbits → use **Celestial Mechanics** (a sub-branch of Kinetics).
- **Biomechanics:** Human gait analysis, prosthetic design → uses rigid body kinematics.
- **Robotics:** Path planning (kinematics) and torque/force calculations (kinetics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resultant of Two Coplanar Concurrent Forces (Parallelogram Law)

**Statement:** If two forces acting at a point are represented in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, their resultant is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from the same point.

**Given:** Two forces $P$ and $Q$ acting at point $O$ with an angle $\theta$ between them.

**To Prove:** The resultant $R$ magnitude is given by

$$R = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta}$$

and its direction $\alpha$ (with respect to $P$) is

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{Q \sin\theta}{P + Q \cos\theta}$$

**Proof (using Triangle Law of Forces as a consequence of Parallelogram Law):**

**Step 1 — Construct the parallelogram.**
Draw $OA = P$ and $OB = Q$ with $\angle AOB = \theta$. Complete the parallelogram $OACB$ such that $OA \parallel BC$ and $OB \parallel AC$, with $OC = R$ as the diagonal representing the resultant.

**Step 2 — Apply the Triangle Law.**
In triangle $OAC$, draw $CD \perp OA$ extended.

**Step 3 — Resolve $Q$ into components.**
From the parallelogram geometry, $CD = Q \sin\theta$ and $AD = Q \cos\theta$.

**Step 4 — Express $OC$ using Pythagoras.**
In right triangle $OCD$:

$$OC^2 = OD^2 + CD^2$$

Substitute $OD = OA + AD = P + Q \cos\theta$ and $CD = Q \sin\theta$:

$$R^2 = (P + Q \cos\theta)^2 + (Q \sin\theta)^2$$

**Step 5 — Expand and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= P^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta + Q^2 \cos^2\theta + Q^2 \sin^2\theta \\
    &= P^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta + Q^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) \\
    &= P^2 + Q^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root.**

$$R = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta} \quad \text{(Lami's cousin / Parallelogram Law)}$$

**Step 7 — Find the direction.**
In $\triangle OCD$:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{CD}{OD} = \frac{Q \sin\theta}{P + Q \cos\theta}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{Q \sin\theta}{P + Q \cos\theta}\right)}$$

**Special Cases (KTU favourite):**
- If $\theta = 0°$ (forces in same direction): $R = P + Q$ (maximum).
- If $\theta = 90°$: $R = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$.
- If $\theta = 180°$ (opposite): $R = \vert P - Q \vert$ (minimum).

## 3.2 Derivation: Moment of a Force about a Point

**Definition:** The moment of a force $\vec{F}$ about a point $O$ is the product of the magnitude of the force and the perpendicular distance from $O$ to the line of action of $\vec{F}$.

$$M_O = F \times d_{\perp}$$

**Derivation (using the cross-product form, KTU Module 1.3):**

If the position vector of any point on the line of action of $\vec{F}$ (say point $A$) from $O$ is $\vec{r}$, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{M}_O &= \vec{r} \times \vec{F} \\
          &= (x\,\hat{i} + y\,\hat{j} + z\,\hat{k}) \times (F_x\,\hat{i} + F_y\,\hat{j} + F_z\,\hat{k}) \\
          &= (y F_z - z F_y)\,\hat{i} + (z F_x - x F_z)\,\hat{j} + (x F_y - y F_x)\,\hat{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **magnitude** of this moment vector is

$$\vert \vec{M}_O \vert = \vert \vec{r} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{F} \vert \cdot \sin\phi = F\,d_{\perp}$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$ and $d_{\perp} = \vert \vec{r} \vert \sin\phi$ is the perpendicular distance.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Table — Branch-by-Branch

| Branch | Treats body as | Considers forces? | Considers motion? | Sample Problem |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Statics | Rigid | Yes | No (at rest) | Truss analysis |
| Kinematics | Particle/Rigid | No | Yes | Projectile path |
| Kinetics | Particle/Rigid | Yes | Yes | Car braking distance |
| Mech. of Solids | Deformable | Yes | No (often) | Shaft torsion |
| Fluid Mechanics | Fluid | Yes | Both | Pipe flow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Tree of Mechanics

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Mechanics of Physical Bodies]
    rootA --> branchA1[Mechanics of Rigid Bodies]
    rootA --> branchA2[Mechanics of Deformable Bodies]
    rootA --> branchA3[Mechanics of Fluids]

    branchA1 --> staticsA[Statics]
    branchA1 --> dynamicsA[Dynamics]
    dynamicsA --> kinematicsA[Kinematics]
    dynamicsA --> kineticsA[Kinetics]

    branchA2 --> solidA[Mechanics of Solids Strength of Materials]
    branchA3 --> fluidA[Fluid Statics and Fluid Dynamics]

    staticsA --> s1[Trusses Beams Frames]
    kinematicsA --> k1[Projectiles Cams Gears]
    kineticsA --> kt1[Newtons Laws Work Energy Impulse]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Force Resolution via Parallelogram Law

```mermaid
graph LR
    originA[Point O]
    forceA[Force P along OA]
    forceB[Force Q along OB]
    resultA[Resultant R along OC]

    originA -- P --> ptA[Point A]
    originA -- Q --> ptB[Point B]
    originA -- R --> ptC[Point C]
    ptA -- parallel side BC --> ptB
    ptB -- parallel side AC --> ptC

    classDef vector fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px
    class forceA,forceB,resultA vector
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Equilibrium Conditions (Statics Core)

```mermaid
graph TD
    eqA[Static Equilibrium of a Body]
    eqA --> eqA1[Condition 1 Force Equilibrium]
    eqA --> eqA2[Condition 2 Moment Equilibrium]
    eqA1 --> eqA1x[SUM Fx equals 0]
    eqA1 --> eqA1y[SUM Fy equals 0]
    eqA1 --> eqA1z[SUM Fz equals 0 for 3D]
    eqA2 --> eqA2x[SUM Mx equals 0]
    eqA2 --> eqA2y[SUM My equals 0]
    eqA2 --> eqA2z[SUM Mz equals 0 for 3D]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters at KTU:** Every single Statics problem (truss, frame, friction) in your university exam will end with you writing down these two conditions and solving the resulting equations. Mastering this diagram is mastering the *spine* of the subject.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Mechanics Branches

```mermaid
graph TD
    inpA[Forces and Geometry as Input]
    inpA --> procA{Body at Rest?}
    procA -- Yes --> outA[Use Statics SUM F equals 0 SUM M equals 0]
    procA -- No --> procB{Forces Known?}
    procB -- No --> outB[Use Kinematics Geometry of Motion]
    procB -- Yes --> outC[Use Kinetics F equals m times a]

    outA --> solA[Reactions and Internal Forces]
    outB --> solB[Velocity Acceleration Displacement]
    outC --> solC[Motion Under Known Forces]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **Define Mechanics. How is it broadly classified?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Mechanics* is the branch of physical science that deals with the state of rest or motion of bodies when subjected to the action of forces. The KTU syllabus broadly classifies engineering mechanics into:
> 1. **Mechanics of Rigid Bodies** — further split into **Statics** and **Dynamics** (which contains **Kinematics** and **Kinetics**).
> 2. **Mechanics of Deformable Bodies** (also called Mechanics of Solids / Strength of Materials).
> 3. **Mechanics of Fluids**.
> *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the classification tree — board marking scheme.)*

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
> **Differentiate between Statics and Dynamics. Give one engineering example for each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Statics | Dynamics |
> |---|---|
> | Body is at rest or in equilibrium ($\sum F = 0$, $\sum M = 0$) | Body is in motion (accelerated, $\sum F \neq 0$) |
> | No acceleration is considered | Acceleration is the key variable |
> | Example: Analysis of a truss bridge under dead load | Example: Motion of a vehicle on a curved road |
>
> *(1 mark each for any two correct distinguishing points + 1 mark for examples.)*

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Define the term *Particle* and *Rigid Body* as used in Engineering Mechanics. A force of **$100$ N** acts along the positive x-axis and another force of **$150$ N** acts at an angle of **$60°$** with the x-axis. Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant using the Parallelogram Law of Forces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain Newton's three laws of motion. Show that Newton's Second Law is consistent with the principle of conservation of momentum for a system of particles. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- A **Particle** is a body of negligible dimensions whose entire mass is assumed to be concentrated at a single point. It is used as a model when rotation is not significant.
- A **Rigid Body** is a body in which the relative positions of any two points remain constant (i.e., it does not deform) under the action of external forces.

**Force resolution (5 marks):**
Given $P = 100$ N, $Q = 150$ N, $\theta = 60°$.

**Step 1 — Apply the parallelogram law formula.**

$$R = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$R = \sqrt{100^2 + 150^2 + 2 \times 100 \times 150 \times \cos 60°}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{10000 + 22500 + 30000 \times 0.5} \\
  &= \sqrt{10000 + 22500 + 15000} \\
  &= \sqrt{47500} \\
  &= 217.94\ \text{N (approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Find the direction angle $\alpha$ with respect to $P$ (100 N force).**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{Q \sin\theta}{P + Q \cos\theta} = \frac{150 \sin 60°}{100 + 150 \cos 60°}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\alpha &= \frac{150 \times 0.866}{100 + 150 \times 0.5} = \frac{129.9}{175} = 0.7423 \\
\alpha &= \tan^{-1}(0.7423) = 36.58° \approx 36.6°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R = 217.94$ N at an angle of **$36.6°$** with the 100 N force.

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) — 2 marks:**
A particle remains at rest, or continues to move in a straight line with constant velocity, unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force. *Example:* A book on a table stays at rest until pushed.

**Newton's Second Law (Law of Acceleration) — 2 marks:**
The acceleration of a particle is directly proportional to the resultant force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass. The direction of acceleration coincides with the direction of the force.

$$\vec{F} = m\,\vec{a} = m\,\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$$

**Newton's Third Law (Action-Reaction) — 1 mark:**
For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. $\vec{F}_{AB} = -\vec{F}_{BA}$.

**Consistency with Conservation of Momentum — 2 marks:**
For a system of particles, total momentum $\vec{P} = \sum m_i \vec{v}_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\vec{P}}{dt} &= \sum m_i \vec{a}_i = \sum \vec{F}_i^{\text{ext}} \quad (\text{internal forces cancel in pairs by Law 3}) \\
\text{If}\ \sum \vec{F}_i^{\text{ext}} &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\vec{P}}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{P} = \text{constant}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, in the absence of external forces, total momentum is conserved — directly consistent with Newton's Second and Third Laws.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Set B]

**(a)** Two forces of magnitude **$200$ N** and **$300$ N** act at a point. If the angle between them is **$30°$**, determine the magnitude of the resultant and its direction with respect to the 200 N force. Also state the two conditions of equilibrium for a rigid body in two dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between **Statics**, **Kinematics**, and **Kinetics**. A body of mass **$5$ kg** is acted upon by a net force of **$20$ N**. Find the acceleration produced. How long will it take to attain a velocity of **$12$ m/s** starting from rest? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Solution:**

Given $P = 200$ N, $Q = 300$ N, $\theta = 30°$.

**Magnitude of Resultant:**

$$R = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{200^2 + 300^2 + 2 \times 200 \times 300 \times \cos 30°} \\
  &= \sqrt{40000 + 90000 + 120000 \times 0.8660} \\
  &= \sqrt{40000 + 90000 + 103920} \\
  &= \sqrt{233920} \\
  &= 483.65\ \text{N (approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Direction with respect to 200 N force:**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{Q \sin\theta}{P + Q \cos\theta} = \frac{300 \sin 30°}{200 + 300 \cos 30°}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\alpha &= \frac{300 \times 0.5}{200 + 300 \times 0.8660} = \frac{150}{200 + 259.81} = \frac{150}{459.81} = 0.3262 \\
\alpha &= \tan^{-1}(0.3262) = 18.07° \approx 18.1°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two Conditions of Equilibrium (for a 2D rigid body) — 2 marks:**
1. **Force equilibrium:** $\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$.
2. **Moment equilibrium:** $\sum M = 0$ (about any arbitrary point in the plane).

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Difference between Statics, Kinematics, Kinetics (sketch descriptions) — 3 marks:**
- **Statics** — Body at rest; analyze forces only (e.g., a beam at rest with a point load).
- **Kinematics** — Body in motion; analyze displacement, velocity, acceleration only; *forces are not* the focus (e.g., tracing the path of a thrown ball).
- **Kinetics** — Body in motion; analyze the *relationship between forces and motion* (e.g., calculating the thrust required for a rocket).

**Numerical solution — 4 marks:**

**Step 1 — Find acceleration from Newton's Second Law.**

$$F = m\,a \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{20}{5} = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Find time using first kinematic equation $v = u + a t$.**

Given $u = 0$ (starts from rest), $v = 12$ m/s, $a = 4$ m/s².

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= u + a t \\
12 &= 0 + 4 t \\
t &= \frac{12}{4} = 3\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Acceleration $= 4$ m/s²; Time taken $= 3$ s.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** In equilibrium equations ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$), students often forget to assign signs correctly to forces — *losing 2–3 marks instantly*. **Tip:** Always draw a clear free-body diagram and assume all unknown components as positive.
> 2. **Units Trap:** Writing mass in grams or force in kgf instead of N loses easy marks. Always convert to **SI units (kg, m, s, N)** before substituting.
> 3. **$\theta$ vs. $180° - \theta$ Trap:** In the parallelogram law, students confuse the angle between forces with the supplementary angle. **Tip:** Always measure the angle as the one *physically* drawn between the two force vectors.
> 4. **Direction Missing:** Writing only the magnitude of the resultant (and not the angle) is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in the KTU scheme. Always quote both magnitude *and* direction.
> 5. **Forgetting to State the Two Equilibrium Conditions explicitly** in word form (not just equations) is a common 1-mark cut.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mechanics** studies bodies at rest or in motion under the action of forces. *(Module 1 — KTU 2024)*
- The four pillars of mechanics are **Space, Time, Mass, Force**.
- **Statics** = study of bodies **at rest** or moving with uniform velocity. **Dynamics** = study of bodies in **accelerated motion**.
- **Dynamics** has two sub-branches: **Kinematics** (geometry of motion, no force) and **Kinetics** (force–motion relationship).
- **Rigid body mechanics** assumes no deformation; **deformable body mechanics** studies stresses and strains; **fluid mechanics** studies liquids and gases.
- A **particle** has negligible dimensions; a **rigid body** has fixed geometry.
- **Newton's Three Laws** form the foundation of the entire subject:
  - Law 1: Inertia ($\sum F = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0$).
  - Law 2: $F = m a$.
  - Law 3: $F_{AB} = -F_{BA}$.
- The **Parallelogram Law of Forces** gives $R = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2 + 2 P Q \cos\theta}$.
- The **direction** of the resultant is $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Q \sin\theta}{P + Q \cos\theta}\right)$.
- **Two equilibrium conditions** for a 2D rigid body: $\sum F_x = 0,\ \sum F_y = 0,\ \sum M = 0$.
- **Always quote the standard value** $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless the problem states otherwise.
- **Always state units** — N, m, kg, s, m/s², N·m — to fetch the *unit mark* in KTU board exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Rigid Body, Scalars, and Vectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Rigid Body, Scalars, and Vectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rigid Body — The Unchanging Object

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Rigid Body** is a body in which the distance between any two particles remains constant during its motion; i.e., the body does not undergo any deformation (no change in shape or size) when subjected to external forces.

In the real engineering world, *no body is perfectly rigid*. However, for analytical simplicity in Engineering Mechanics (Statics & Dynamics), we assume bodies such as steel beams, connecting rods, and shafts to be rigid because the deformation is negligibly small compared to their overall dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel Ruler" Intuition:**
> Imagine a thick steel ruler lying on a table. When you push it with your hand, the ruler slides as a whole — every point on the ruler moves the same distance in the same direction, and the ruler does not bend, stretch, or compress. This idealized, non-deforming object is what we call a **rigid body**. Now compare it with a rubber eraser — when you push it, it bends or changes shape; that is a *deformable body*.

## 1.2 Particle — The Dimensionless Point

A **Particle** is a body of negligible dimensions whose mass is assumed to be concentrated at a single point. When the rotational motion and size of a body are irrelevant to the problem, we treat the body as a particle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Earth Around the Sun":**
> When studying the Earth's revolution around the Sun, the Earth's own size and rotation are not important; only its mass and orbital path matter. We therefore treat the Earth as a *particle* revolving around the Sun.

## 1.3 Scalar Quantities — Magnitude Alone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **Scalar** is a physical quantity that is completely described by its **magnitude (size)** alone. It has **no direction** associated with it and obeys the ordinary laws of algebra.

**Common Examples:** Mass (kg), Length (m), Time (s), Temperature (°C), Volume (m³), Speed (m/s), Work (J), Energy (J), Power (W), Density (kg/m³).

## 1.4 Vector Quantities — Magnitude AND Direction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **Vector** is a physical quantity that is fully described only when **both its magnitude and direction** are specified. It obeys the **Parallelogram Law of Addition** (or equivalently, the Triangle Law).

**Common Examples:** Force (N), Displacement (m), Velocity (m/s), Acceleration (m/s²), Momentum (kg·m/s), Moment of Force (N·m), Weight (N).

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Vector Representation

A vector can be represented graphically and mathematically in three ways:

1. **Geometric (Arrow) Representation:** A directed line segment — length = magnitude, arrowhead = direction.
2. **Mathematical (Component) Representation:** $\vec{V} = V_x\,\hat{i} + V_y\,\hat{j} + V_z\,\hat{k}$
3. **Print/Boldface Notation:** $\vec{V}$, $\mathbf{V}$, or simply $\overline{V}$.

## 1.6 Classification of Vectors

| Category | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Free Vector** | Vector whose action point is unrestricted in space | $\vec{F}$ anywhere in space |
| **Sliding Vector** | Vector whose line of action is fixed but point of application can slide along it | Force along a rod |
| **Fixed (Bound) Vector** | Vector whose point of application is fixed in space | Velocity of a fixed point on a gear |
| **Unit Vector** | Vector of magnitude **1** indicating pure direction | $\hat{i},\hat{j},\hat{k}$ |
| **Position Vector** | Vector from origin O to point P | $\vec{r}_P$ |
| **Zero (Null) Vector** | Vector of zero magnitude with undefined direction | $\vec{0}$ |
| **Co-planar Vectors** | Vectors lying in the same plane | 2D problem forces |
| **Equal Vectors** | Same magnitude AND same direction | $\vec{A} = \vec{B}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Intuition — "Direction matters, not position":**
> If you drag a 30 N force arrow from the left side of a paper to the right side without changing its length or tilt, it is still the *same* vector. Only magnitude and direction define a vector — its position on paper is irrelevant (this is the "free vector" property).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parallelogram Law of Vector Addition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** If two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ are represented both in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram drawn from the same point, then their resultant $\vec{R}$ is represented, both in magnitude and direction, by the diagonal of the parallelogram passing through that common point.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1 — Origin:** Both vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ must start from the same tail point (the "point of concurrency").
- **Step 2 — Construction:** Complete the parallelogram by drawing a line parallel to $\vec{A}$ from the tip of $\vec{B}$, and a line parallel to $\vec{B}$ from the tip of $\vec{A}$.
- **Step 3 — Diagonal:** Draw the diagonal from the common origin to the opposite corner. This diagonal IS the resultant $\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}$.
- **Why it works:** Vectors follow the "tip-to-tail" chaining rule; the parallelogram simply packages two chained vectors into a closed geometric figure.

## 2.2 The Triangle Law of Vector Addition

An equivalent and often more convenient statement: If $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ are placed tip-to-tail, the vector from the tail of $\vec{A}$ to the tip of $\vec{B}$ is the resultant.

## 2.3 Resolution of a Vector (Reverse of Addition)

Any vector $\vec{V}$ in a plane can be split into two perpendicular **components** along the chosen $x$ and $y$ axes:

$$V_x = V\cos\theta$$
$$V_y = V\sin\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle measured from the $x$-axis to the vector $\vec{V}$.

## 2.4 The Two Key Vector Multiplications

### (a) Dot Product (Scalar Product)
$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert \cos\phi = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$$

- **Result:** A **scalar**.
- **Geometry:** Product of magnitudes times the cosine of the angle between them.
- **Physical use:** Work done $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$.

### (b) Cross Product (Vector Product)
$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert \sin\phi \, \hat{n}$$

- **Result:** A **vector** perpendicular to the plane of $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$.
- **Direction:** Given by the **Right-Hand Rule** (curl fingers from $\vec{A}$ to $\vec{B}$; thumb gives $\hat{n}$).
- **Physical use:** Moment of force $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$.

## 2.5 Lami's Theorem (Three Concurrent Coplanar Forces)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** When three coplanar, concurrent, and non-collinear forces keep a body in equilibrium, each force is proportional to the sine of the angle between the other two.

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between $F_2$ and $F_3$, and so on cyclically.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Law | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnitude of Resultant (Parallelogram Law) | $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$ | $R$ in vector units (N, m, etc.) |
| 2 | Direction of Resultant | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$ | $\alpha$ = angle of $\vec{R}$ with $\vec{A}$ |
| 3 | Position Vector of Point P | $\vec{r}_P = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$ | m |
| 4 | Unit Vector along $\vec{V}$ | $\hat{V} = \vec{V} / \vert \vec{V} \vert$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Vector Magnitude (3D) | $\vert \vec{V} \vert = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2 + V_z^2}$ | Vector units |
| 6 | Dot Product | $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vert A \vert \vert B \vert \cos\phi$ | Scalar |
| 7 | Cross Product Magnitude | $\vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert = \vert A \vert \vert B \vert \sin\phi$ | Vector units |
| 8 | Lami's Theorem | $\dfrac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$ | Equilibrium only |
| 9 | Direction Cosines | $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$ | Sum is always unity |
| 10 | Moment of Force | $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | N·m |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Resolving truss member forces into horizontal/vertical components for analysis.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Calculating torque (moment) on a rotating shaft using $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$.
- **Aerospace:** Decomposition of lift, drag, and thrust vectors in flight mechanics.
- **Robotics:** Position vectors and dot products are used to compute joint angles and end-effector positions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Resultant Magnitude Using the Parallelogram Law

**Problem Setup:** Two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ act at a common origin, making an angle $\theta$ between them. We wish to find the magnitude of their resultant $\vec{R}$.

Let us use the parallelogram $OACB$ where $OA = \vec{A}$, $OB = \vec{B}$, and $OC = \vec{R}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Triangle for Law of Cosines:**

Drop a perpendicular from $C$ to the extended line $OA$, meeting it at $D$. The triangle $ODC$ is a right-angled triangle with hypotenuse $R$ and the right angle at $D$.

**Step 2 — Express the Base Length $OD$:**

In triangle $OBC$, the side $OB = B$ and the angle at $O$ (i.e., $\angle BOD$) is supplementary to the angle $\theta$ between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$, since $OD$ is the extension of $OA$. Therefore:

$$\angle BOD = 180° - \theta$$

Using the cosine rule on the side $OD$ of triangle $OBD$:

$$OD = OA + AD = A + B\cos(180° - \theta)$$

Since $\cos(180° - \theta) = -\cos\theta$, we get:

$$OD = A - B\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Express the Perpendicular Height $DC$:**

From the right triangle $BDC$ (with hypotenuse $B$ and base $BD = B\cos\theta$ wait — corrected) — the perpendicular distance $DC$ is the side opposite the angle $\theta$ in the small right triangle with hypotenuse $B$:

$$DC = B\sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Apply Pythagoras Theorem to Triangle $ODC$:**

$$R^2 = (OD)^2 + (DC)^2$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:

$$R^2 = (A - B\cos\theta)^2 + (B\sin\theta)^2$$

**Step 5 — Expand and Simplify:**

$$R^2 = A^2 - 2AB\cos\theta + B^2\cos^2\theta + B^2\sin^2\theta$$

Using the identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$R^2 = A^2 - 2AB\cos\theta + B^2$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case Verification:**
> - If $\theta = 0°$ (parallel): $R = A + B$ ✔ (correct, both add up)
> - If $\theta = 90°$ (perpendicular): $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ ✔
> - If $\theta = 180°$ (anti-parallel): $R = A - B$ ✔ (correct, they subtract)

## 3.2 Derivation of the Direction Angle $\alpha$ of the Resultant

From the right triangle $ODC$ in the previous figure:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{DC}{OD} = \frac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}\right)}$$

This is the angle the resultant $\vec{R}$ makes with vector $\vec{A}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Cross Product from Determinant Form

For 3D vectors $\vec{A} = (A_x, A_y, A_z)$ and $\vec{B} = (B_x, B_y, B_z)$:

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \hat{i}(A_yB_z - A_zB_y) - \hat{j}(A_xB_z - A_zB_x) + \hat{k}(A_xB_y - A_yB_x)$$

$$\boxed{\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = (A_yB_z - A_zB_y)\hat{i} - (A_xB_z - A_zB_x)\hat{j} + (A_xB_y - A_yB_x)\hat{k}}$$

The magnitude of this cross product equals $\vert A \vert \vert B \vert \sin\phi$, and the direction follows the **right-hand rule**.

## 3.4 Derivation of Lami's Theorem

**Setup:** Three forces $F_1$, $F_2$, $F_3$ act at a point $O$ in equilibrium, making angles $\alpha$ (between $F_2$ and $F_3$), $\beta$ (between $F_3$ and $F_1$), and $\gamma$ (between $F_1$ and $F_2$).

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition:**

$$\vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} + \vec{F_3} = \vec{0}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Sine Rule to the Force Triangle:**

When three vectors in equilibrium are placed tip-to-tail, they form a closed triangle. The interior angles of this triangle are precisely the angles between the forces' *extensions*, and they equal $180° - \alpha$, $180° - \beta$, $180° - \gamma$ respectively.

By the sine rule in this triangle:

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin(180° - \alpha)} = \frac{F_2}{\sin(180° - \beta)} = \frac{F_3}{\sin(180° - \gamma)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\sin(180° - x) = \sin x$:**

$$\boxed{\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Cyclic Rule:** In Lami's Theorem, the denominator's angle is the one **opposite** to the force in the numerator. That is, the angle $\alpha$ in the denominator of $F_1$ is the angle between the *other two* forces $F_2$ and $F_3$.

## 3.5 Analytical Problem Matrix — Standard KTU 2024 Patterns

| Problem Type | Key Formula to Invoke | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Find magnitude of two-force resultant | $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$ | Using $\theta$ as the angle from $x$-axis (it's between the two vectors!) |
| Find direction of resultant | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$ | Forgetting to add $A$ in the denominator |
| Three concurrent forces in equilibrium | Lami's Theorem | Misidentifying which angle is opposite which force |
| Moment of a force about a point | $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | Forgetting the sign convention (clockwise vs anticlockwise) |
| Resolve a vector along axes | $V_x = V\cos\theta$, $V_y = V\sin\theta$ | Confusing $\theta$ with the inclination angle |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate clearly between a scalar quantity and a vector quantity. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Scalar | Vector |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Quantity with magnitude only | Quantity with magnitude and direction |
| Specification | Single number with unit | Number + unit + direction |
| Algebra | Follows ordinary algebra | Follows vector algebra (parallelogram law) |
| Examples | Mass, Time, Temperature, Work, Energy | Force, Displacement, Velocity, Momentum, Moment |

*(1 mark for each crisp definition + 1 mark for valid examples — total 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Parallelogram Law of Vector Addition. Using it, derive the expression for the magnitude of the resultant of two vectors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** If two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ acting at a point are represented by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, then their resultant $\vec{R}$ is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram passing through the common point.

> **Derivation (2 marks):** Using the construction with the perpendicular dropped from the diagonal's tip to the extended base, applying the Pythagoras theorem and the identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, we obtain:

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Two forces of magnitude **8 N** and **6 N** act at a point, making an angle of **60°** between them. Find the magnitude and direction of their resultant using the Parallelogram Law. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** State and prove **Lami's Theorem** for three coplanar, concurrent forces in equilibrium. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:** $A = 8$ N, $B = 6$ N, $\theta = 60°$

**Step 1 — Magnitude of Resultant:**

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

$$R = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2 + 2(8)(6)\cos 60°}$$

$$\cos 60° = 0.5$$

$$R = \sqrt{64 + 36 + 96 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{64 + 36 + 48}$$

$$R = \sqrt{148} \approx 12.166 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Direction of Resultant:**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta} = \frac{6 \times \sin 60°}{8 + 6 \times \cos 60°}$$

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{6 \times 0.866}{8 + 6 \times 0.5} = \frac{5.196}{8 + 3} = \frac{5.196}{11}$$

$$\tan\alpha = 0.4724 \implies \alpha \approx 25.28°$$

**Result:** The resultant is **$R \approx 12.17$ N**, directed at an angle of **$\alpha \approx 25.28°$** from the 8 N force.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

> **Lami's Theorem (3 marks for statement + proof):**
> *"When three coplanar, concurrent, and non-collinear forces keep a body in equilibrium, then each force is proportional to the sine of the angle between the other two forces."*

**Proof (4 marks):**

Let three forces $\vec{F_1}, \vec{F_2}, \vec{F_3}$ act at point $O$ in equilibrium:

$$\vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} + \vec{F_3} = \vec{0}$$

Placing them tip-to-tail, they form a closed triangle $PQR$ where:
- Side opposite to $F_1$ has interior angle $180° - \alpha$
- Side opposite to $F_2$ has interior angle $180° - \beta$
- Side opposite to $F_3$ has interior angle $180° - \gamma$

By the **Sine Rule** in triangle $PQR$:

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin(180° - \alpha)} = \frac{F_2}{\sin(180° - \beta)} = \frac{F_3}{\sin(180° - \gamma)}$$

Since $\sin(180° - x) = \sin x$:

$$\boxed{\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}}$$

Hence Lami's Theorem is proved.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **free vector, sliding vector, fixed vector, and unit vector** with neat sketches. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A force of magnitude **20 N** acts along the position vector $\vec{r} = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}$ m, applied at the origin. Find the moment of this force about the point $A(1, 2, 0)$ m. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i) Free Vector (2 marks):** A vector whose position in space is of no consequence — only magnitude and direction matter. *Example:* A pure translation of a rigid body can be described by a free vector.

**(ii) Sliding Vector (2 marks):** A vector whose line of action is fixed, but the point of application can slide along that line without changing its effect on the rigid body. *Example:* A force acting along a rigid rod.

**(iii) Fixed Vector (1.5 marks):** A vector whose point of application is fixed in space and cannot be moved. *Example:* Velocity of a specific point on a rotating wheel.

**(iv) Unit Vector (1.5 marks):** A vector of unit magnitude ($\vert \hat{n} \vert = 1$) used to specify pure direction. *Examples:* $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ along Cartesian axes.

*Neat labelled sketches should accompany each definition for full marks.*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $\vec{F}$ acts along the position vector $\vec{r}_P = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}$ m, with magnitude 20 N. Point $A = (1, 2, 0)$ m.

**Step 1 — Find the Unit Vector Along $\vec{F}$:**

$$\hat{u}_r = \frac{3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2}} = \frac{3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}}{5} = 0.6\hat{i} + 0.8\hat{j}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\vec{F}$ in Vector Form:**

$$\vec{F} = 20 \times \hat{u}_r = 20(0.6\hat{i} + 0.8\hat{j}) = 12\hat{i} + 16\hat{j} \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Find Position Vector from A to the Point of Application (Origin O):**

The force is applied at the origin, so the position vector from $A$ to $O$ is:

$$\vec{r}_{AO} = \vec{r}_O - \vec{r}_A = (0 - 1)\hat{i} + (0 - 2)\hat{j} + (0 - 0)\hat{k} = -\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Moment $\vec{M}_A = \vec{r}_{AO} \times \vec{F}$:**

$$\vec{M}_A = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & -2 & 0 \\ 12 & 16 & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row (z-components are zero):

$$\vec{M}_A = \hat{i}\big[(-2)(0) - (0)(16)\big] - \hat{j}\big[(-1)(0) - (0)(12)\big] + \hat{k}\big[(-1)(16) - (-2)(12)\big]$$

$$\vec{M}_A = \hat{i}(0) - \hat{j}(0) + \hat{k}(-16 + 24)$$

$$\boxed{\vec{M}_A = 8\hat{k} \text{ N·m}}$$

The positive $\hat{k}$ direction indicates the moment is **counter-clockwise** (out of the page) with magnitude **8 N·m**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Topic:**
> 1. **Angle Misidentification (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently confuse the angle $\theta$ in $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$. Remember — $\theta$ is the angle **between the two vectors**, NOT the angle of either vector with the $x$-axis.
> 2. **Lami's Theorem Angle Mapping (–2 marks):** The angle in the denominator of $F_1/\sin\alpha$ is the angle **between $F_2$ and $F_3$** (i.e., the angle *opposite* to $F_1$). Marking $\alpha$ as the angle between $F_1$ and $F_2$ is a classic error.
> 3. **Dot vs Cross Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ gives a **scalar** (used for work). $\vec{A} \times \vec{B}$ gives a **vector** (used for moment). Mixing them up leads to dimensionally wrong answers.
> 4. **Missing Units in Final Answer (–1 mark):** KTU examiners strictly deduct marks for writing just "12.17" without the unit "N". Always append the unit.
> 5. **Sign Convention in Moments:** A negative $\hat{k}$ moment (clockwise) is just as valid as positive $\hat{k}$; the **direction** must be explicitly stated, not just the magnitude.
> 6. **Skipping the Sketch:** For 14-mark questions, KTU examiners *expect* a neat labelled diagram of the parallelogram or force triangle. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Rigid Body, Scalars, Vectors**

- ✅ A **Rigid Body** does not deform; distance between any two particles remains constant.
- ✅ A **Particle** is a body of negligible size — used when rotation is irrelevant.
- ✅ **Scalars** have magnitude only; **Vectors** have magnitude AND direction.
- ✅ Vectors obey the **Parallelogram Law** and the **Triangle Law** of addition.
- ✅ Resultant of two vectors: $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$.
- ✅ Direction: $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$ (measured from $\vec{A}$).
- ✅ A **Unit Vector** has magnitude 1 and indicates direction only: $\hat{V} = \vec{V}/\vert \vec{V} \vert$.
- ✅ **Position vector** of point $P(x,y,z)$: $\vec{r}_P = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$.
- ✅ **Dot product** is a scalar: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vert A \vert \vert B \vert \cos\phi$. Use it for **work** and **projection**.
- ✅ **Cross product** is a vector: $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \vert A \vert \vert B \vert \sin\phi \, \hat{n}$. Use it for **moment** and **area**.
- ✅ **Lami's Theorem** (equilibrium of 3 forces): $\dfrac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$.
- ✅ **Direction cosines** always satisfy: $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$.
- ✅ Always include **units** in the final answer (N, N·m, m, etc.).
- ✅ Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** in long-answer questions.
- ✅ Distinguish carefully between the **angle between two vectors** ($\theta$) and the **inclination angle** of a vector with the $x$-axis ($\alpha$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vector Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vector?

A **vector** is a mathematical quantity that is completely described by two independent characteristics:
1. **Magnitude** (a non-negative scalar value representing "how much")
2. **Direction** (the orientation in space along which the quantity acts)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A vector is a directed line segment whose length represents the magnitude and whose orientation in space (angle with a reference axis) represents the direction. It obeys the parallelogram law of addition.

In contrast, a **scalar** is a quantity that has magnitude only — no direction. Examples of scalars include mass, time, temperature, work, and energy.

### Common Engineering Examples of Vector Quantities

| Vector Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Force | $\vec{F}$ | **Newton (N)** |
| Velocity | $\vec{v}$ | **m/s** |
| Acceleration | $\vec{a}$ | **m/s²** |
| Displacement | $\vec{s}$ | **m (meter)** |
| Momentum | $\vec{p}$ | **kg·m/s** |
| Electric Field | $\vec{E}$ | **N/C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Engineering Mechanics**, the force vector is the most frequently used vector. Every force acting on a body must be fully specified with its magnitude, direction, line of action, and point of application.

---

## 1.2 Vector Representation

A vector $\vec{A}$ in 3D Cartesian space is represented as:

$$\vec{A} = A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j} + A_z \hat{k}$$

Where:
- $A_x, A_y, A_z$ are the **scalar components** along the x, y, z axes
- $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are the **unit vectors** along x, y, z axes (mutually perpendicular, each of length 1)
- Magnitude: $\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Delivery Boy" 🍕

Imagine a **pizza delivery boy** is told to deliver a pizza:
- If someone says, *"Deliver 5 pizzas"* → That is a **scalar** (only magnitude, no direction needed).
- If someone says, *"Go 5 km North-East to Sector 22"* → That is a **vector** (magnitude = 5 km, direction = North-East).

The *direction* is what makes the difference! When we study forces in mechanics, the direction is just as crucial as the magnitude — pushing a wall vs. pulling a wall produces completely different effects even if the force magnitude is the same.

---

## 1.4 Types of Vectors (KTU Syllabus Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> The following classifications are **frequently asked** in KTU Part A questions (2-mark/3-mark categories).

- **Free Vector**: A vector whose effect depends only on magnitude and direction, **not on the point of application** (e.g., velocity, displacement).
- **Fixed (or Bound) Vector**: A vector whose effect depends on its point of application (e.g., force on a rigid body).
- **Sliding Vector**: Can be slid along its line of action without changing its effect (e.g., force in a rigid body).
- **Unit Vector**: A vector of **unit magnitude** ($\vert \hat{a} \vert = 1$) used only to indicate direction.
- **Zero (Null) Vector**: A vector with **zero magnitude** and indeterminate direction.
- **Position Vector**: A vector from the **origin O** of a coordinate system to a point P in space. Denoted as $\vec{r}_{OP}$ or simply $\vec{r}$.
- **Co-planar Vectors**: Vectors lying in the **same plane**.
- **Concurrent Vectors**: Vectors whose **lines of action pass through a common point**.
- **Equal Vectors**: Two vectors having the **same magnitude AND the same direction**.
- **Negative Vector**: A vector with the **same magnitude but opposite direction** to a given vector. If $\vec{B} = -\vec{A}$, then $\vec{A} + \vec{B} = \vec{0}$.

---

## 1.5 Why Vector Operations Matter in Engineering Mechanics

Every mechanical system — from a simple pulley to a complex robotic arm — is governed by forces, displacements, and velocities. **Vector operations** are the mathematical machinery that lets engineers:

- Combine multiple forces acting at a point (Resultant).
- Resolve a single force into perpendicular components.
- Check equilibrium of a body.
- Compute work, torque, and moment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always treat force, velocity, and acceleration as vectors from Day 1 of the course. Mixing up scalar and vector components is the #1 reason students lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vector Addition

Two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ can be added to produce a **resultant vector** $\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}$ using the following laws:

### (a) Triangle Law of Vector Addition

> **Statement:** If two vectors acting at a point are represented both in magnitude and direction by the two sides of a triangle taken in the **same order**, then their resultant is represented by the **third side** taken in the **opposite order**.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Draw $\vec{A}$ from origin O to point P.
2. From the head of $\vec{A}$ (point P), draw $\vec{B}$ to point Q.
3. Join O to Q. The vector $\vec{OQ} = \vec{R}$ is the resultant.
4. Magnitude: $\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}$ (geometrically, the closing side).

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$.

### (b) Parallelogram Law of Vector Addition

> **Statement:** If two vectors acting at a point are represented both in magnitude and direction by the **two adjacent sides of a parallelogram**, then their resultant is represented by the **diagonal** of the parallelogram drawn from the **common tail point**.

The formula is identical to the triangle law:

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

The direction of $\vec{R}$ with respect to $\vec{A}$ is given by:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{B \sin\theta}{A + B \cos\theta}$$

### (c) Polygon Law of Vector Addition

Used for adding **more than two vectors**:
> If a number of vectors acting at a point are represented both in magnitude and direction by the **sides of an open polygon taken in the same order**, then the resultant is represented by the **closing side** of the polygon taken in the **opposite order**.

---

## 2.2 Vector Subtraction

Subtraction is defined as **addition of the negative vector**:

$$\vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B})$$

Geometrically, this means we reverse the direction of $\vec{B}$ and then add it head-to-tail with $\vec{A}$.

$$R = \vert \vec{A} - \vec{B} \vert = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 - 2AB\cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The formula uses **−2AB cos θ** instead of +2AB cos θ. This is the same θ (angle between original $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$).

---

## 2.3 Scalar (Dot) Product

> **Definition:** The dot product of two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is a **scalar** equal to the product of their magnitudes and the cosine of the angle between them.

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vert \vec{A} \vert \vert \vec{B} \vert \cos\theta = AB \cos\theta$$

### Key Properties (Highly Important for KTU)

$$\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i} = \hat{j} \cdot \hat{j} = \hat{k} \cdot \hat{k} = 1$$

$$\hat{i} \cdot \hat{j} = \hat{j} \cdot \hat{k} = \hat{k} \cdot \hat{i} = 0$$

### Component Form

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_x B_x + A_y B_y + A_z B_z$$

### Applications in Engineering

- **Work done** by a force: $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}$
- **Power**: $P = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}$
- Finding the **angle** between two vectors: $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \vert \vec{B} \vert}$
- Checking **perpendicularity**: $\vec{A} \perp \vec{B} \iff \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 0$

---

## 2.4 Vector (Cross) Product

> **Definition:** The cross product of two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is a **vector** whose magnitude equals the product of the magnitudes and the sine of the angle between them, and whose direction is **perpendicular to the plane** of the two vectors (given by the right-hand rule).

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \vert \vec{A} \vert \vert \vec{B} \vert \sin\theta \, \hat{n} = AB\sin\theta \, \hat{n}$$

### Key Properties

$$\hat{i} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k} \times \hat{k} = \vec{0}$$

$$\hat{i} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k}, \quad \hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}, \quad \hat{k} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j}$$

$$\hat{j} \times \hat{i} = -\hat{k}, \quad \hat{k} \times \hat{j} = -\hat{i}, \quad \hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j}$$

### Determinant Form (Critical for KTU)

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}$$

### Applications in Engineering

- **Moment (Torque) of a force**: $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$
- **Angular momentum**: $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}$
- **Area of parallelogram**: $\text{Area} = \vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert$
- **Area of triangle**: $\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert$

> [!WARNING]
> **Order Matters!** $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = -(\vec{B} \times \vec{A})$. Cross product is **anti-commutative**. KTU examiners frequently test this!

---

## 2.5 Lami's Theorem

For **three concurrent, coplanar forces** in equilibrium:

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the angles **opposite** to the respective forces.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **cheat sheet** for the KTU End Semester Exam. Memorize every entry.

| # | Operation | Formula | Result Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnitude of $\vec{A}$ | $\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}$ | Scalar |
| 2 | Unit vector along $\vec{A}$ | $\hat{a} = \dfrac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$ | Unit Vector |
| 3 | Vector Addition (Two) | $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$ | Vector |
| 4 | Vector Subtraction | $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 - 2AB\cos\theta}$ | Vector |
| 5 | Direction of Resultant | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$ | Angle |
| 6 | Dot Product (Geometric) | $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = AB\cos\theta$ | Scalar |
| 7 | Dot Product (Components) | $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$ | Scalar |
| 8 | Cross Product (Magnitude) | $\vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert = AB\sin\theta$ | Scalar |
| 9 | Cross Product (Components) | Determinant formula above | Vector |
| 10 | Lami's Theorem | $\dfrac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$ | Equilibrium |
| 11 | Angle between vectors | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{AB}$ | Scalar |
| 12 | Parallelogram Area | $\text{Area} = \vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert$ | Scalar |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Civil Engineering**: Resolving forces in trusses, bridges, and dams into components.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Computing torque in rotating shafts, gear forces, and engine dynamics.
- **Aerospace Engineering**: Vector analysis of thrust, lift, drag, and gravitational forces on aircraft.
- **Robotics**: Inverse kinematics using dot and cross products for joint torque computation.
- **Structural Analysis**: Computing resultant of multiple loads (dead load, live load, wind load) on a structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition — Why two types of products?**
> - **Dot product** answers: *"How much of A points in the direction of B?"* (Projection)
> - **Cross product** answers: *"How perpendicular can I get from A and B?"* (Area/Orientation)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Resultant Magnitude (Parallelogram Law)

We have two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ acting at a point O with an angle $\theta$ between them.

**Step 1**: Construct the parallelogram OACB where:
- $\vec{OA} = \vec{A}$
- $\vec{OB} = \vec{B}$
- The diagonal $\vec{OC} = \vec{R}$ is the resultant.

**Step 2**: Drop a perpendicular CD from C to the extension of OA.

**Step 3**: In triangle OAC, by the **Law of Cosines**:

$$OC^2 = OA^2 + AC^2 - 2(OA)(AC)\cos(180^\circ - \theta)$$

**Step 4**: Note that $AC = OB = B$ and $OA = A$, and $\cos(180^\circ - \theta) = -\cos\theta$:

$$R^2 = A^2 + B^2 - 2AB(-\cos\theta)$$

**Step 5**: Simplify:

$$\boxed{R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

**Step 6**: Take square root:

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

**Step 7**: For the direction, let $\alpha$ be the angle between $\vec{R}$ and $\vec{A}$. In triangle ACD:

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{CD}{OC} = \frac{B\sin\theta}{R}$$

And from the projection:

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{OD}{OC} = \frac{A + B\cos\theta}{R}$$

Dividing:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cross Product in Component Form

Given $\vec{A} = A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j} + A_z \hat{k}$ and $\vec{B} = B_x \hat{i} + B_y \hat{j} + B_z \hat{k}$.

We need to compute $\vec{A} \times \vec{B}$ by expanding the product using distribution:

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = (A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j} + A_z \hat{k}) \times (B_x \hat{i} + B_y \hat{j} + B_z \hat{k})$$

**Step 1**: Expand term by term (9 terms total):

$$= A_x B_x (\hat{i} \times \hat{i}) + A_x B_y (\hat{i} \times \hat{j}) + A_x B_z (\hat{i} \times \hat{k})$$
$$+ A_y B_x (\hat{j} \times \hat{i}) + A_y B_y (\hat{j} \times \hat{j}) + A_y B_z (\hat{j} \times \hat{k})$$
$$+ A_z B_x (\hat{k} \times \hat{i}) + A_z B_y (\hat{k} \times \hat{j}) + A_z B_z (\hat{k} \times \hat{k})$$

**Step 2**: Apply the cross product properties of unit vectors:
- $\hat{i} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k} \times \hat{k} = \vec{0}$
- $\hat{i} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k}$, $\hat{j} \times \hat{i} = -\hat{k}$
- $\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$, $\hat{k} \times \hat{j} = -\hat{i}$
- $\hat{k} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j}$, $\hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j}$

**Step 3**: Substitute and eliminate zero terms:

$$= 0 + A_x B_y \hat{k} - A_x B_z \hat{j} - A_y B_x \hat{k} + 0 + A_y B_z \hat{i} + A_z B_x \hat{j} - A_z B_y \hat{i} + 0$$

**Step 4**: Group by $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$:

$$= (A_y B_z - A_z B_y) \hat{i} + (A_z B_x - A_x B_z) \hat{j} + (A_x B_y - A_y B_x) \hat{k}$$

**Step 5**: Write as a determinant:

$$\boxed{\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Lami's Theorem

Consider three concurrent, coplanar forces $\vec{F_1}, \vec{F_2}, \vec{F_3}$ in equilibrium. Let the angles between them be $\alpha$ (between $F_2$ and $F_3$), $\beta$ (between $F_1$ and $F_3$), $\gamma$ (between $F_1$ and $F_2$).

**Step 1**: In equilibrium, $\vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} + \vec{F_3} = \vec{0}$, so $\vec{F_1} = -(\vec{F_2} + \vec{F_3})$.

**Step 2**: Using the triangle law, we can form a closed triangle with sides $F_1, F_2, F_3$ where the interior angles are $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ (the supplements of the angles between the forces).

**Step 3**: Apply the **Law of Sines** to this triangle:

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Lami's Theorem: } \frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Example Problems

### **Example 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2022]

Two forces of magnitude **8 N** and **6 N** act at a point. If the angle between them is **60°**, find the magnitude and direction of the resultant.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 8$ N, $B = 6$ N, $\theta = 60°$.

**Step 1**: Apply the parallelogram law:

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

$$R = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2 + 2(8)(6)\cos 60°}$$

$$R = \sqrt{64 + 36 + 96 \times 0.5}$$

$$R = \sqrt{64 + 36 + 48} = \sqrt{148}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 12.166 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2**: Find the direction $\alpha$ with respect to the 8 N force:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta} = \frac{6 \sin 60°}{8 + 6\cos 60°}$$

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{6 \times 0.8660}{8 + 6 \times 0.5} = \frac{5.196}{11}$$

$$\tan\alpha = 0.4724$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 25.28°}$$

---

### **Example 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

If $\vec{A} = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} - 5\hat{k}$ and $\vec{B} = 2\hat{i} - 3\hat{j} + \hat{k}$, find $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ and $\vec{A} \times \vec{B}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1**: Dot product (component form):

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (3)(2) + (4)(-3) + (-5)(1)$$

$$= 6 - 12 - 5$$

$$\boxed{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = -11}$$

**Step 2**: Cross product (determinant form):

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 4 & -5 \\ 2 & -3 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$$

**Expanding along Row 1:**

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \hat{i}[(4)(1) - (-5)(-3)] - \hat{j}[(3)(1) - (-5)(2)] + \hat{k}[(3)(-3) - (4)(2)]$$

**Step 2a**: $\hat{i}$ component: $(4)(1) - (-5)(-3) = 4 - 15 = -11$

**Step 2b**: $\hat{j}$ component: $(3)(1) - (-5)(2) = 3 + 10 = 13$, with minus sign: $-13$

**Step 2c**: $\hat{k}$ component: $(3)(-3) - (4)(2) = -9 - 8 = -17$

$$\boxed{\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = -11\hat{i} - 13\hat{j} - 17\hat{k}}$$

---

### **Example 3** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

A particle is in equilibrium under three coplanar forces. Two of the forces are 100 N and 150 N with an angle of 60° between them. Find the third force using Lami's Theorem.

**Solution:**

**Step 1**: Given $F_1 = 100$ N, $F_2 = 150$ N, angle between them = 60°.

**Step 2**: Since all three angles around a point sum to 360° and the system is in equilibrium, if the angle between $F_1$ and $F_2$ is 60°, the other two angles are determined by the geometry. For this problem, assume:
- $\alpha$ = angle opposite to $F_3$ = 60°
- $\beta$ = angle opposite to $F_2$
- $\gamma$ = angle opposite to $F_1$

**Step 3**: Apply Lami's Theorem:

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$$

**Step 4**: Using the supplementary angle relationships, with the 60° angle between $F_1$ and $F_2$, we typically get:

$$F_3 = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos 60°}$$

$$F_3 = \sqrt{100^2 + 150^2 + 2(100)(150)(0.5)}$$

$$F_3 = \sqrt{10000 + 22500 + 15000} = \sqrt{47500}$$

$$\boxed{F_3 \approx 217.94 \text{ N}}$$

The direction of $F_3$ is opposite to the resultant of $F_1$ and $F_2$ (since the system is in equilibrium).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Guide

| **If the problem asks for...** | **Use this operation** | **Formula** |
|---|---|---|
| Single force from multiple forces | Vector Addition | $R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta$ |
| Angle between two forces | Dot Product | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{AB}$ |
| Perpendicular (normal) component | Cross Product | $\vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert = AB\sin\theta$ |
| Moment of a force | Cross Product | $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ |
| Three-force equilibrium | Lami's Theorem | $\dfrac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$ |
| Work / Energy calculation | Dot Product | $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}$ |
| Area of triangle/parallelogram | Cross Product | $\text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vector Addition — Triangle vs Parallelogram Law

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Two vectors A and B acting at a point] --> B{Choose Law}
    B --> C[Triangle Law]
    B --> D[Parallelogram Law]
    C --> C1[Draw A from O to P]
    C1 --> C2[Draw B from P to Q]
    C2 --> C3[Resultant R = O to Q]
    C3 --> C4[Vector form: R = A + B]
    D --> D1[Draw A and B from common origin O]
    D1 --> D2[Complete parallelogram OACB]
    D2 --> D3[Diagonal OC is the Resultant R]
    D3 --> D4[Vector form: R = A + B]
    C4 --> E[Same Resultant Vector]
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Magnitude: sqrt A squared + B squared + 2AB cos theta]
    E --> G[Direction: tan alpha = B sin theta over A + B cos theta]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Vector Operations Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    OP[Vector Operations Module] --> ADD[Vector Addition]
    OP --> SUB[Vector Subtraction]
    OP --> MUL[Scalar Multiplication]
    OP --> DOT[Dot Product]
    OP --> CROSS[Cross Product]
    ADD --> A1[Triangle Law]
    ADD --> A2[Parallelogram Law]
    ADD --> A3[Polygon Law]
    A1 --> R1[Resultant Vector R]
    A2 --> R1
    A3 --> R1
    SUB --> S1[Reverse direction of B]
    S1 --> S2[Add to A: R = A plus minus B]
    S2 --> R1
    MUL --> M1[Multiply magnitude by scalar k]
    M1 --> M2[Direction preserved if k positive]
    M1 --> M3[Direction reversed if k negative]
    DOT --> D1[Output is a Scalar]
    D1 --> D2[Application: Work equals F dot s]
    D1 --> D3[Application: Angle between vectors]
    CROSS --> CR1[Output is a Vector]
    CR1 --> CR2[Application: Moment equals r cross F]
    CR1 --> CR3[Application: Area of parallelogram]
    R1 --> APPS[Engineering Applications]
    D2 --> APPS
    CR2 --> APPS
    APPS --> AP1[Truss Analysis]
    APPS --> AP2[Machinery Design]
    APPS --> AP3[Robot Kinematics]
    APPS --> AP4[Structural Analysis]
```

---

## 4.3 Vector Component Resolution Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Given Vector A at angle theta] --> P1[Resolve into Rectangular Components]
    P1 --> AX[Ax equals A cos theta along x axis]
    P1 --> AY[Ay equals A sin theta along y axis]
    AX --> SUM[Sum of Components: Rx and Ry]
    AY --> SUM
    SUM --> MAG[Compute Magnitude: R equals sqrt Rx squared plus Ry squared]
    MAG --> ANG[Compute Direction: phi equals tan inverse Ry over Rx]
    ANG --> RESULT[Final Resultant Vector R]
    RESULT --> OUT[Output: Magnitude and Direction]
```

---

## 4.4 Dot vs Cross Product — Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Need to multiply two vectors?] --> Q1{Is the result a scalar or vector?}
    Q1 -->|Scalar| DOT[Use Dot Product]
    Q1 -->|Vector| Q2{Is the result perpendicular to both?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| CROSS[Use Cross Product]
    Q2 -->|No| PROJ[Use Vector Projection]
    DOT --> D1[Formula: A dot B equals AB cos theta]
    DOT --> D2[Output: Projection magnitude]
    DOT --> D3[Examples: Work, Power, Angle]
    CROSS --> C1[Formula: A cross B equals AB sin theta n-hat]
    CROSS --> C2[Output: Vector perpendicular to plane]
    CROSS --> C3[Examples: Moment, Torque, Area]
    PROJ --> P1[Formula: proj B on A equals A dot B over mag A squared times A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Dot product tells you *"how aligned"* the vectors are. Cross product tells you *"how much they twist around each other"*. This is the physical essence of why both products exist in mechanics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define:** (i) Unit vector, (ii) Position vector, (iii) Free vector.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Unit Vector:** A vector of magnitude equal to one (unity), used to specify direction only. It is denoted by $\hat{a}$ and is obtained by dividing the vector by its magnitude: $\hat{a} = \dfrac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$. For example, $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are unit vectors along x, y, z axes respectively.

(ii) **Position Vector:** A vector drawn from the **origin** of a coordinate system to a point representing the position of that point in space. If point P has coordinates $(x, y, z)$, then the position vector is $\vec{OP} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$.

(iii) **Free Vector:** A vector whose effect on a body is independent of its point of application. It is characterized solely by its magnitude and direction. **Examples:** velocity, displacement, moment of a couple.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State and explain the parallelogram law of vector addition with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** If two vectors acting simultaneously at a point are represented both in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram drawn from a common point, then their resultant is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram passing through that common point.

**Explanation:** Consider two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ acting at a point O with an angle $\theta$ between them. Construct a parallelogram OACB such that $\vec{OA} = \vec{A}$ and $\vec{OB} = \vec{B}$. The diagonal $\vec{OC}$ drawn from O represents the resultant $\vec{R}$.

**Magnitude:**

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$$

**Direction** (angle $\alpha$ made with $\vec{A}$):

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$$

---

## 📝 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Three forces of magnitudes **20 N, 30 N, and 40 N** act on a particle. The angle between the 20 N and 30 N forces is **60°**, and the angle between the 30 N and 40 N forces is **90°**. Determine the **magnitude and direction of the resultant force** using the method of resolution into rectangular components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **dot product** and **cross product** of two vectors. Given $\vec{A} = 4\hat{i} - 3\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}$ and $\vec{B} = 2\hat{i} + 5\hat{j} - \hat{k}$, find the **(i) dot product**, **(ii) cross product**, and **(iii) angle between the vectors**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Setup the coordinate system.**

Let the 30 N force be along the positive x-axis. The 20 N force makes 60° with the 30 N force (above x-axis), and the 40 N force is at 90° to the 30 N force.

**Step 2: Resolve each force into x and y components.**

For the **30 N force** (along +x):
$$F_{1x} = 30, \quad F_{1y} = 0$$

For the **20 N force** (at 60° above +x):
$$F_{2x} = 20 \cos 60° = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{2y} = 20 \sin 60° = 20 \times 0.866 = 17.32 \text{ N}$$

For the **40 N force** (at 90° to 30 N, i.e., along +y):
$$F_{3x} = 40 \cos 90° = 0$$
$$F_{3y} = 40 \sin 90° = 40 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Sum all x-components and y-components.**

$$\Sigma F_x = 30 + 10 + 0 = 40 \text{ N}$$
$$\Sigma F_y = 0 + 17.32 + 40 = 57.32 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude of the resultant.**

$$R = \sqrt{(\Sigma F_x)^2 + (\Sigma F_y)^2}$$
$$R = \sqrt{40^2 + 57.32^2}$$
$$R = \sqrt{1600 + 3285.58}$$
$$R = \sqrt{4885.58}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 69.90 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the direction.**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\Sigma F_y}{\Sigma F_x} = \frac{57.32}{40} = 1.433$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(1.433)$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 55.10° \text{ from the +x axis}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Definitions:**

- **Dot Product** (Scalar Product): $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vert \vec{A} \vert \vert \vec{B} \vert \cos\theta$. Result is a scalar.
- **Cross Product** (Vector Product): $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \vert \vec{A} \vert \vert \vec{B} \vert \sin\theta \, \hat{n}$. Result is a vector perpendicular to the plane of $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$.

**Step 1: Compute magnitudes.**

$$\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{4^2 + (-3)^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9 + 4} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.385$$

$$\vert \vec{B} \vert = \sqrt{2^2 + 5^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 25 + 1} = \sqrt{30} \approx 5.477$$

**(i) Dot Product (Component Form):**

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (4)(2) + (-3)(5) + (2)(-1)$$

$$= 8 - 15 - 2$$

$$\boxed{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = -9}$$

**(ii) Cross Product (Determinant Form):**

$$\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -3 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 & -1 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along Row 1:

**$\hat{i}$ component:** $(-3)(-1) - (2)(5) = 3 - 10 = -7$

**$\hat{j}$ component:** $-[(4)(-1) - (2)(2)] = -[-4 - 4] = 8$

**$\hat{k}$ component:** $(4)(5) - (-3)(2) = 20 + 6 = 26$

$$\boxed{\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = -7\hat{i} + 8\hat{j} + 26\hat{k}}$$

**(iii) Angle between the vectors:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \vert \vec{B} \vert} = \frac{-9}{\sqrt{29} \times \sqrt{30}} = \frac{-9}{\sqrt{870}}$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{-9}{29.50} \approx -0.3051$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 107.77°}$$

Since the dot product is negative, the angle is **obtuse** (between 90° and 180°), which is consistent with the result.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** A particle is acted upon by **three coplanar forces in equilibrium**. The angles between the forces are as follows: the angle between $F_1$ and $F_2$ is **120°**, between $F_2$ and $F_3$ is **150°**, and between $F_3$ and $F_1$ is **90°**. If $F_1 = 100$ N, find $F_2$ and $F_3$ using **Lami's Theorem**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A force $\vec{F} = 5\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} - 3\hat{k}$ N acts at a point whose position vector from the origin is $\vec{r} = 2\hat{i} - 3\hat{j} + 6\hat{k}$ m. Find the **moment of the force about the origin**. Also find the **angle between the force and the position vector**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Identify the angles opposite to each force.**

The angles between the forces are:
- Between $F_1$ and $F_2$: 120°
- Between $F_2$ and $F_3$: 150°
- Between $F_3$ and $F_1$: 90°

Sum: $120° + 150° + 90° = 360°$ ✓ (confirms full circle)

**Step 2: Apply Lami's Theorem.**

The angle **opposite** to a force in Lami's Theorem is the angle between the **other two** forces. Therefore:
- $\alpha$ (opposite to $F_1$) = angle between $F_2$ and $F_3$ = 150°
- $\beta$ (opposite to $F_2$) = angle between $F_1$ and $F_3$ = 90°
- $\gamma$ (opposite to $F_3$) = angle between $F_1$ and $F_2$ = 120°

**Step 3: Write Lami's Theorem:**

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin 150°} = \frac{F_2}{\sin 90°} = \frac{F_3}{\sin 120°}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $F_2$:**

$$\frac{100}{\sin 150°} = \frac{F_2}{\sin 90°}$$

$$F_2 = \frac{100 \times \sin 90°}{\sin 150°} = \frac{100 \times 1}{0.5}$$

$$\boxed{F_2 = 200 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $F_3$:**

$$\frac{100}{\sin 150°} = \frac{F_3}{\sin 120°}$$

$$F_3 = \frac{100 \times \sin 120°}{\sin 150°} = \frac{100 \times 0.8660}{0.5} = \frac{86.60}{0.5}$$

$$\boxed{F_3 = 173.20 \text{ N}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the moment using the cross product.**

The moment of force about the origin is:

$$\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

**Step 2: Set up the determinant.**

$$\vec{M} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -3 & 6 \\ 5 & 4 & -3 \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Expand along Row 1.**

**$\hat{i}$ component:** $(-3)(-3) - (6)(4) = 9 - 24 = -15$

**$\hat{j}$ component:** $-[(2)(-3) - (6)(5)] = -[-6 - 30] = -(-36) = 36$

**$\hat{k}$ component:** $(2)(4) - (-3)(5) = 8 + 15 = 23$

**Step 4: Write the moment vector.**

$$\boxed{\vec{M} = -15\hat{i} + 36\hat{j} + 23\hat{k} \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the magnitude of the moment.**

$$\vert \vec{M} \vert = \sqrt{(-15)^2 + 36^2 + 23^2} = \sqrt{225 + 1296 + 529} = \sqrt{2050}$$

$$\vert \vec{M} \vert \approx 45.28 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Compute the magnitudes of $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$.**

$$\vert \vec{r} \vert = \sqrt{2^2 + (-3)^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt{49} = 7 \text{ m}$$

$$\vert \vec{F} \vert = \sqrt{5^2 + 4^2 + (-3)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 16 + 9} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Compute the dot product $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{F}$.**

$$\vec{r} \cdot \vec{F} = (2)(5) + (-3)(4) + (6)(-3) = 10 - 12 - 18 = -20$$

**Step 8: Find the angle $\theta$ between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$.**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{r} \cdot \vec{F}}{\vert \vec{r} \vert \vert \vec{F} \vert} = \frac{-20}{7 \times \sqrt{50}} = \frac{-20}{49.497} \approx -0.4041$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 113.84°}$$

The angle is obtuse, which means the force has a component directed somewhat **away** from the line of the position vector.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Lami's Theorem:** Students often confuse the angle between two forces with the angle *opposite* to a force. The angle opposite to $F_1$ is the angle between $F_2$ and $F_3$, **NOT** the angle at the head of $F_1$.
>
> 2. **Determinant Expansion Mistake:** When expanding the cross product, the **$\hat{j}$** component always has a **negative sign** outside. Many students forget this and lose 1-2 marks.
>
> 3. **Cross Product Order:** Writing $\vec{B} \times \vec{A}$ instead of $\vec{A} \times \vec{B}$ gives the **negative** of the correct answer. Always verify the order.
>
> 4. **Component vs Geometric Formula:** Dot product formula $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = AB\cos\theta$ requires knowing the angle $\theta$. The component form $A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$ is preferred when the vectors are given in component form.
>
