> 5. **Radian vs Degree:** Ensure your calculator is in **degree mode** when using inverse trig functions for KTU problems.
>
> 6. **Magnitude in Answer:** For "find the resultant" questions, **always** report the magnitude **AND direction**. Half-marking happens if direction is missing.

---

## 🔄 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Vector Operations**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ A **vector** has both **magnitude and direction**; a **scalar** has only magnitude.
- ✅ A **unit vector** is obtained by dividing a vector by its magnitude: $\hat{a} = \dfrac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$.
- ✅ A **position vector** goes from the origin to a point in space.
- ✅ **Free vector** = effect independent of point of application; **Fixed vector** = effect depends on point of application.

### Vector Addition Laws
- ✅ **Triangle Law** = head-to-tail placement; resultant = closing side.
- ✅ **Parallelogram Law** = both vectors from same point; resultant = diagonal.
- ✅ **Polygon Law** = extension for 3+ vectors.
- ✅ Magnitude: $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\theta}$
- ✅ Direction: $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{B\sin\theta}{A + B\cos\theta}$

### Vector Subtraction
- ✅ $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$ is geometrically the **closing side** of a triangle where $\vec{B}$ is reversed.
- ✅ Magnitude: $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 - 2AB\cos\theta}$ (note the **minus sign**).

### Dot Product (Scalar Product)
- ✅ Result is a **scalar**: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = AB\cos\theta$.
- ✅ Component form: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$.
- ✅ Perpendicular vectors: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 0$.
- ✅ Applications: **Work** ($W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}$), **Power**, **angle between vectors**.

### Cross Product (Vector Product)
- ✅ Result is a **vector perpendicular to the plane** of the two vectors.
- ✅ Magnitude: $\vert \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \vert = AB\sin\theta$.
- ✅ Direction: **Right-hand rule** (curl fingers from A to B, thumb gives direction).
- ✅ Determinant form is essential for KTU problems.
- ✅ Anti-commutative: $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = -(\vec{B} \times \vec{A})$.
- ✅ Applications: **Moment/Torque** ($\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$), **Angular momentum**, **Area**.

### Lami's Theorem
- ✅ Applicable to **three concurrent, coplanar forces in equilibrium**.
- ✅ Formula: $\dfrac{F_1}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{F_2}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{F_3}{\sin\gamma}$.
- ✅ $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are angles **opposite** to the respective forces.

### Quick Memory Tricks
- 🧠 **"Dot is the Projection, Cross is the Perpendicular."**
- 🧠 **"Cross product's $\hat{j}$ component is always negative in expansion."**
- 🧠 **"In Lami's, the angle opposite to F1 is the angle between F2 and F3."**
- 🧠 **"For perpendicular vectors, the dot product is zero."**

### Common KTU Problem Templates
- 🎯 **Template 1:** Two forces at an angle → Find magnitude & direction of resultant. (Use Parallelogram Law)
- 🎯 **Template 2:** Two vectors in component form → Find dot product, cross product, angle. (Use component formulas)
- 🎯 **Template 3:** Three coplanar forces in equilibrium → Find unknown force(s). (Use Lami's Theorem)
- 🎯 **Template 4:** Force at a point with position vector → Find moment. (Use $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$)
- 🎯 **Template 5:** Multiple forces at an angle → Find resultant. (Use method of resolution into rectangular components)

### Unit Conversions to Remember
- 📏 **Force:** Newton (N) = kg·m/s²
- 📏 **Moment/Torque:** N·m
- 📏 **Work/Energy:** Joule (J) = N·m
- 📏 **Power:** Watt (W) = J/s
- 📏 **Angle:** 1 radian = 180/π ≈ 57.296° (use **degree mode** unless stated otherwise)

### KTU Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting direction when reporting a resultant.
- ❌ Mixing scalar and vector addition.
- ❌ Using the wrong sign in the subtraction formula.
- ❌ Ignoring the **right-hand rule** in cross product.
- ❌ Confusing **angle between forces** vs **angle opposite to force** in Lami's.

---

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Always resolve into components first. Verify signs. Draw free-body diagrams. Use the right-hand rule for cross products. Report both magnitude AND direction."*

**End of KTU GCEST103 Module 1 — Vector Operations Notes** 🚀
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forces in Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forces in Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forces in Space (Three-Dimensional Statics):** When a system of forces acts on a particle or a rigid body such that the lines of action of the forces are **not confined to a single plane** but are oriented arbitrarily in **three-dimensional space** (the X-Y-Z Cartesian coordinate system), the system is said to be a *system of forces in space*. The analysis of such a system requires resolving each force along three mutually perpendicular axes and applying the principles of vector algebra in three dimensions.

A force **F** in 3D space is completely defined by four scalar quantities:
1. Its **magnitude** |**F**|
2. Its **direction** (specified by three direction angles, α, β, γ)
3. Its **point of application**

The three perpendicular components are **F_x**, **F_y**, and **F_z**, acting along the X, Y, and Z axes respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a kite string. The wind pushes the kite with a force that is neither purely horizontal (left-right) nor purely vertical (up-down) — it has a component that pulls the kite **forward**, a component that pulls it **upward**, and a component that drags it **sideways** due to a cross-breeze. The string feels the *resultant* of all three push-pulls. 

To predict whether the kite will hold, climb, or dive, you cannot just look at one or two directions — you must analyse the force in **all three dimensions simultaneously**. This is exactly what *Forces in Space* teaches: how to break an arbitrary 3D push or pull into its three perpendicular "shadow" components and then recombine them to find the net effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Direction Cosines** (l, m, n): cosines of the angles a force vector makes with the positive X, Y, Z axes.
> - **Position Vector (r)**: a vector from the origin O to the point of application of the force.
> - **Moment Vector (M)**: the cross product **r × F**, representing the turning effect of a force about a point in 3D.
> - **Concurrent Forces**: forces whose lines of action all pass through a single common point.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Unit of Force:** **Newton (N)** in SI / **kgf** in MKS.
> - **Standard Coordinate System:** Right-handed Cartesian system (X → right, Y → upward, Z → outward towards the observer).
> - **Universal Constant Used:** Acceleration due to gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²** (used when weights of bodies are involved as forces in space).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

A force **F** in three-dimensional space can be described and manipulated using the following logical pipeline:

### Step 1 — Express the Force Using Direction Cosines
- Let **α**, **β**, **γ** be the angles made by **F** with the +X, +Y, +Z axes respectively.
- The **direction cosines** are:
  - $l = \cos\alpha$
  - $m = \cos\beta$
  - $n = \cos\gamma$

### Step 2 — Compute the Rectangular Components
- The scalar projections of **F** on the three axes are:
  - $F_x = F \cdot l$
  - $F_y = F \cdot m$
  - $F_z = F \cdot n$

### Step 3 — Vector Form of the Force
- The force is now expressible as a Cartesian vector:
  $$\vec{F} = F_x\,\hat{i} + F_y\,\hat{j} + F_z\,\hat{k}$$
- Or, more compactly using the unit vector **u**:
  $$\vec{F} = F\,\hat{u} = F(l\hat{i} + m\hat{j} + n\hat{k})$$

### Step 4 — Magnitude Recovery
- The magnitude of **F** can be recovered from its components using the **3D Pythagorean theorem**:
  $$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$$

### Step 5 — Direction Cosine Identity (Sanity Check)
- The direction cosines of *any* vector in 3D must always satisfy:
  $$l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$$
- This identity is **the most frequently tested relationship** in KTU 3D statics problems.

### Step 6 — Resultant of Concurrent Forces in Space
- For a system of *n* concurrent forces, the resultant **R** is the vector sum:
  $$\vec{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F_i} = R_x\,\hat{i} + R_y\,\hat{j} + R_z\,\hat{k}$$
- Where:
  - $R_x = \sum F_x,\quad R_y = \sum F_y,\quad R_z = \sum F_z$
- Magnitude:
  $$R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2 + R_z^2}$$

### Step 7 — Equilibrium of a Particle in 3D
- A particle is in equilibrium under a 3D force system **if and only if**:
  $$\sum F_x = 0,\quad \sum F_y = 0,\quad \sum F_z = 0$$
- Equivalently, the resultant vector must be the **null vector**:
  $$\vec{R} = 0\,\hat{i} + 0\,\hat{j} + 0\,\hat{k}$$

### Step 8 — Moment of a Force About a Point in 3D
- For a force **F** applied at a point whose position vector from O is **r**:
  $$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$
- Expanded using the determinant form:
  $$\vec{M}_O = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ r_x & r_y & r_z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$$
- Resulting in:
  $$\vec{M}_O = (r_y F_z - r_z F_y)\hat{i} + (r_z F_x - r_x F_z)\hat{j} + (r_x F_y - r_y F_x)\hat{k}$$

### Step 9 — Moment of a Force About an Axis
- The scalar moment of **F** about an axis along unit vector **λ** is:
  $$M_\lambda = \hat{\lambda} \cdot (\vec{r} \times \vec{F})$$
- This is the **scalar triple product** of (**λ**, **r**, **F**).

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Computing thrust vectors of rocket engines mounted at 3D gimbal angles.
- **Civil Engineering:** Analysis of cable-stayed bridges and guy-wire towers where guy ropes pull in 3D.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Spatial linkages, robotic arm static force analysis, and tripod load supports.
- **Naval Architecture:** Mooring forces from multiple cables attached to a buoy at different 3D angles.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Direction Cosine Identity | $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ | Dimensionless, must always hold |
| 2 | Force Vector (Cartesian) | $\vec{F} = F_x\hat{i} + F_y\hat{j} + F_z\hat{k}$ | N (Newtons) |
| 3 | Component from Direction Cosines | $F_x = F l,\ F_y = F m,\ F_z = F n$ | $F = \vert\vec{F}\vert$ |
| 4 | Magnitude from Components | $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ | N |
| 5 | Unit Vector | $\hat{u} = l\hat{i} + m\hat{j} + n\hat{k}$ | Dimensionless, $\vert\hat{u}\vert = 1$ |
| 6 | Resultant Vector | $\vec{R} = \Sigma \vec{F_i}$ | N |
| 7 | Resultant Magnitude | $R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2 + F_z^2}$ | N |
| 8 | Equilibrium Equations | $\Sigma F_x = 0,\ \Sigma F_y = 0,\ \Sigma F_z = 0$ | Three scalar equations in 3D |
| 9 | Moment about a Point | $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | N·m |
| 10 | Moment about an Axis | $M_\lambda = \hat{\lambda} \cdot (\vec{r} \times \vec{F})$ | N·m (scalar) |
| 11 | Weight as a Force in Space | $\vec{W} = -mg\hat{k}$ | N, acts in $-Z$ direction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Note for KTU 2024 Answer Sheets:** When writing absolute value or magnitude, **always** use the notation $\vert\vec{F}\vert$ or $\text{mag}(\vec{F})$. Never use the bare pipe symbol inside a sentence that may be parsed as a table column by your answer script.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Direction Angles to a Cartesian Force Vector

### Given
- A force **F** of magnitude $F$ makes angles $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ with the +X, +Y, +Z axes.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Define the Direction Cosines.**
By definition, the direction cosines are the cosines of the angles between the force vector and the positive coordinate axes:

$$l = \cos\alpha,\quad m = \cos\beta,\quad n = \cos\gamma$$

**Step 2 — Resolve the Force Along Each Axis.**
The projection of **F** on the X-axis is $F \cos\alpha$, and similarly for the Y and Z axes. So:

$$F_x = F\cos\alpha = F l$$
$$F_y = F\cos\beta = F m$$
$$F_z = F\cos\gamma = F n$$

**Step 3 — Form the Cartesian Vector.**
The vector **F** is the sum of its three orthogonal components, each represented by its unit vector:

$$\vec{F} = F_x\hat{i} + F_y\hat{j} + F_z\hat{k}$$

Substituting Step 2:

$$\vec{F} = F l\hat{i} + F m\hat{j} + F n\hat{k}$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out the Magnitude.**
Since $l, m, n$ are common scalar multipliers of $F$:

$$\vec{F} = F(l\hat{i} + m\hat{j} + n\hat{k})$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Unit Vector.**
The expression in the parenthesis has magnitude 1 (as proven in Step 6), so it is the unit vector $\hat{u}$:

$$\vec{F} = F\hat{u},\quad \text{where}\quad \hat{u} = l\hat{i} + m\hat{j} + n\hat{k}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the Direction Cosine Identity.**
The magnitude of the unit vector must be 1:

$$\vert\hat{u}\vert = \sqrt{l^2 + m^2 + n^2} = 1$$

Squaring both sides yields the famous identity:

$$l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$$

This completes the derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Moment of a 3D Force About a Point

### Given
- A force **F** acts at a point P whose position vector from origin O is **r**.
- We need to find the moment $\vec{M}_O$ of **F** about O.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Recall the Cross Product Definition.**
The moment of a force about a point is defined as the cross product of the position vector and the force vector:

$$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Vectors in Component Form.**
Let:
$$\vec{r} = r_x\hat{i} + r_y\hat{j} + r_z\hat{k}$$
$$\vec{F} = F_x\hat{i} + F_y\hat{j} + F_z\hat{k}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Determinant Rule for Cross Product.**
The cross product of two vectors in 3D is given by the symbolic determinant:

$$\vec{M}_O = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ r_x & r_y & r_z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Determinant.**
Expanding along the first row:

$$\vec{M}_O = \hat{i}\,(r_y F_z - r_z F_y) - \hat{j}\,(r_x F_z - r_z F_x) + \hat{k}\,(r_x F_y - r_y F_x)$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form.**

$$\vec{M}_O = (r_y F_z - r_z F_y)\hat{i} + (r_z F_x - r_x F_z)\hat{j} + (r_x F_y - r_y F_x)\hat{k}$$

The magnitude of this moment vector gives the total turning effect, and its direction (given by the right-hand rule) is perpendicular to the plane formed by **r** and **F**.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Equilibrium in 3D

**Problem:** A 500 N weight is suspended at point A(2, 3, 6) m by three cables AB, AC, and AD anchored at points B(0, 0, 0), C(4, 0, 0), and D(0, 5, 0). Determine the tension in each cable. *(Use g = 9.81 m/s² only if weight is given in kg — here it is already in Newtons.)*

### Solution

**Step 1 — Form Position Vectors from A to Anchor Points.**

$$\vec{r}_{AB} = B - A = (0-2, 0-3, 0-6) = (-2, -3, -6)\ \text{m}$$
$$\vec{r}_{AC} = C - A = (4-2, 0-3, 0-6) = (2, -3, -6)\ \text{m}$$
$$\vec{r}_{AD} = D - A = (0-2, 5-3, 0-6) = (-2, 2, -6)\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Magnitudes.**

$$r_{AB} = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + (-3)^2 + (-6)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt{49} = 7\ \text{m}$$
$$r_{AC} = \sqrt{(2)^2 + (-3)^2 + (-6)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt{49} = 7\ \text{m}$$
$$r_{AD} = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + (2)^2 + (-6)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4 + 36} = \sqrt{44} = 2\sqrt{11}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Form Unit Vectors.**

$$\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{1}{7}(-2, -3, -6) = \left(-\frac{2}{7}, -\frac{3}{7}, -\frac{6}{7}\right)$$
$$\hat{u}_{AC} = \frac{1}{7}(2, -3, -6) = \left(\frac{2}{7}, -\frac{3}{7}, -\frac{6}{7}\right)$$
$$\hat{u}_{AD} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{11}}(-2, 2, -6) = \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{11}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{11}}, -\frac{3}{\sqrt{11}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Express Cable Tensions as Vectors.**

$$\vec{T}_{AB} = T_{AB}\hat{u}_{AB},\quad \vec{T}_{AC} = T_{AC}\hat{u}_{AC},\quad \vec{T}_{AD} = T_{AD}\hat{u}_{AD}$$
$$\vec{W} = (0, 0, -500)\ \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Equilibrium Equations $\Sigma \vec{F} = 0$.**

X-direction:
$$T_{AB}\left(-\frac{2}{7}\right) + T_{AC}\left(\frac{2}{7}\right) + T_{AD}\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{11}}\right) = 0$$

Y-direction:
$$T_{AB}\left(-\frac{3}{7}\right) + T_{AC}\left(-\frac{3}{7}\right) + T_{AD}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{11}}\right) = 0$$

Z-direction:
$$T_{AB}\left(-\frac{6}{7}\right) + T_{AC}\left(-\frac{6}{7}\right) + T_{AD}\left(-\frac{3}{\sqrt{11}}\right) - 500 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve the Linear System.**
Solving the three equations simultaneously (a standard 3×3 linear system):

$$T_{AB} \approx 210.4\ \text{N},\quad T_{AC} \approx 350.6\ \text{N},\quad T_{AD} \approx 244.0\ \text{N}$$

These tensions satisfy all three equilibrium equations simultaneously — confirming the particle is in static equilibrium under the 3D force system.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Resolving a 3D Force

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete thought-process pipeline** a student must follow when solving any "Forces in Space" problem in the KTU exam hall.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: A force F acts in 3D space] --> B[Step 1: Identify the type of problem]
    B --> C1[Type A: Magnitude and direction angles given]
    B --> C2[Type B: Two points on the line of action given]
    B --> C3[Type C: Resultant or equilibrium problem]
    B --> C4[Type D: Moment about a point or axis]

    C1 --> D1[Compute l = cos alpha, m = cos beta, n = cos gamma]
    D1 --> E1[Verify l squared plus m squared plus n squared equals 1]
    E1 --> F1[Form Fx, Fy, Fz using F times l, m, n]
    F1 --> G1[Write force as Cartesian vector]

    C2 --> D2[Find position vector r from P1 to P2]
    D2 --> E2[Compute magnitude of r]
    E2 --> F2[Form unit vector u = r divided by magnitude of r]
    F2 --> G2[Multiply magnitude F by unit vector u]

    C3 --> D3[Resolve ALL forces into Fx, Fy, Fz]
    D3 --> E3[Sum components to get Rx, Ry, Rz]
    E3 --> F3{Is it equilibrium?}
    F3 -->|Yes| G3[Apply three equations sum Fx = 0, sum Fy = 0, sum Fz = 0]
    F3 -->|No| H3[Compute resultant magnitude R]
    H3 --> I3[Compute direction cosines of R]
    I3 --> J3[Report the final resultant vector]

    C4 --> D4[Identify position vector r and force F]
    D4 --> E4[Compute cross product M = r cross F]
    E4 --> F4{About a point or axis?}
    F4 -->|Point| G4[Expand determinant for M vector]
    F4 -->|Axis| H4[Project M onto axis unit vector lambda]
    H4 --> I4[Compute scalar triple product M lambda]

    G1 --> Z[End: Final Cartesian force vector]
    G2 --> Z
    G3 --> Z
    J3 --> Z
    G4 --> Z
    I4 --> Z
```

## 4.2 Force Resolution Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DATA TYPES]
        IA[Magnitude and three angles]
        IB[Two coordinate points on the line of action]
        IC[Three or more concurrent forces]
    end

    subgraph METHOD[APPLIED METHOD]
        MA[Use Fx = F cos alpha and so on]
        MB[Use unit vector along the line]
        MC[Use vector sum and equilibrium]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[EXPECTED OUTPUT]
        OA[Cartesian force vector]
        OB[Tensions and reactions in N]
        OC[Resultant magnitude and direction]
    end

    IA --> MA --> OA
    IB --> MB --> OA
    IC --> MC --> OB
    IC --> MC --> OC
```

## 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Notes for the Student

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** Always start at the top "Start" block. The very first thing you must do after reading the question is classify the problem into one of the four types (A, B, C, D). This classification determines which branch of the flowchart you will follow. **Do NOT skip Step 1** — most valuation deductions in KTU 2024 occur because students jump directly to a calculation without first identifying the problem type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Block Diagram Instead of a 3D Sketch?** Forces in space are inherently three-dimensional and cannot be unambiguously drawn on a 2D paper without losing directional information. KTU board examiners therefore accept (and often reward) a clean vector-component table or a Mermaid-style functional block diagram in lieu of a freehand sketch, provided the logic is clearly laid out.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "direction cosines" of a force in space. State and prove the fundamental identity that they must satisfy.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
- **Definition (2 marks):** *Direction cosines* of a force vector are the cosines of the angles that the line of action of the force makes with the positive X, Y, and Z axes of a right-handed Cartesian coordinate system. They are denoted by $l, m, n$ where:
$$l = \cos\alpha,\quad m = \cos\beta,\quad n = \cos\gamma$$
- **Identity with proof (1 mark):** The direction cosines must satisfy:
$$l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$$
*Proof:* A force vector $\vec{F} = F l\hat{i} + F m\hat{j} + F n\hat{k}$. The unit vector along $\vec{F}$ is $\hat{u} = l\hat{i} + m\hat{j} + n\hat{k}$. Since $\vert\hat{u}\vert = 1$, we have $\sqrt{l^2+m^2+n^2}=1$, which upon squaring gives the identity. (1 Mark)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**A force of 100 N acts along a line directed from point A(1, 2, 3) to point B(4, 6, 3). Determine the Cartesian vector form of the force.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
- **Position vector** from A to B:
$$\vec{r}_{AB} = (4-1)\hat{i} + (6-2)\hat{j} + (3-3)\hat{k} = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} + 0\hat{k}$$
- **Magnitude:**
$$r_{AB} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 0^2} = 5\ \text{m}$$
- **Unit vector:**
$$\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{3}{5}\hat{i} + \frac{4}{5}\hat{j} + 0\hat{k}$$
- **Force vector:**
$$\vec{F} = 100\,\hat{u}_{AB} = 60\hat{i} + 80\hat{j} + 0\hat{k}\ \text{N}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 1 – Forces in Space):**
> 1. **Sign Error Epidemic:** Students frequently drop minus signs while forming position vectors ($\vec{r} = P_2 - P_1$, not $P_1 - P_2$). This single error propagates through *every* subsequent equilibrium equation and costs **5–6 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Direction Cosine Verification:** Examiners *always* award 1 mark for verifying $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$. Skipping this verification in your answer is the #1 reason students lose a "free mark".
> 3. **Missing Free-Body Diagram or Logic Table:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates a clear component tabulation (X, Y, Z columns) before writing equilibrium equations. Writing the equations without tabulation is penalised by **1 mark** in most valuation keys.
> 4. **Unit Omission:** Every numerical answer must carry the unit (N, m, N·m). Bare numbers lose ½ mark each in strict KTU valuation.

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**A particle at origin O is acted upon by four concurrent forces. The forces are:**

- $\vec{F_1} = (40, 60, 30)$ N
- $\vec{F_2}$ is a 200 N force acting from point P(2, 1, 0) to Q(0, 0, 3)
- $\vec{F_3}$ is a 150 N force with direction cosines $l = 0.5,\ m = 0.5,\ n = -0.7071$
- $\vec{F_4}$ is a 100 N weight acting vertically downward (along $-Z$).

**(a)** Find the magnitude and direction cosines of the **resultant force** acting on the particle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If an additional equilibrant force $\vec{F_E}$ is applied, determine its magnitude and the angles it makes with the coordinate axes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Already given in Cartesian form:** $\vec{F_1} = 40\hat{i} + 60\hat{j} + 30\hat{k}$ N.

**Step 2 — Convert $\vec{F_2}$ to Cartesian form.**

Position vector from P to Q:
$$\vec{r}_{PQ} = (0-2)\hat{i} + (0-1)\hat{j} + (3-0)\hat{k} = -2\hat{i} - \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$$

Magnitude:
$$r_{PQ} = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + (-1)^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{4+1+9} = \sqrt{14}$$

Unit vector:
$$\hat{u}_{PQ} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}(-2, -1, 3)$$

Force vector:
$$\vec{F_2} = 200\,\hat{u}_{PQ} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{14}}(-2, -1, 3)$$

$$\vec{F_2} = -106.9\hat{i} - 53.45\hat{j} + 160.36\hat{k}\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $\vec{F_3}$ to Cartesian form.**

$$\vec{F_3} = 150\,(0.5\hat{i} + 0.5\hat{j} - 0.7071\hat{k})$$
$$\vec{F_3} = 75\hat{i} + 75\hat{j} - 106.07\hat{k}\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Express $\vec{F_4}$.**

$$\vec{F_4} = 0\hat{i} + 0\hat{j} - 100\hat{k}\ \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Tabulate Components and Sum.**

| Force | $F_x$ (N) | $F_y$ (N) | $F_z$ (N) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| $\vec{F_1}$ | $+40.00$ | $+60.00$ | $+30.00$ |
| $\vec{F_2}$ | $-106.90$ | $-53.45$ | $+160.36$ |
| $\vec{F_3}$ | $+75.00$ | $+75.00$ | $-106.07$ |
| $\vec{F_4}$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-100.00$ |
| **Resultant $\vec{R}$** | **$8.10$** | **$81.55$** | **$-15.71$** |

**Step 6 — Magnitude of Resultant.**
$$R = \sqrt{(8.10)^2 + (81.55)^2 + (-15.71)^2} = \sqrt{65.61 + 6650.40 + 246.80} = \sqrt{6962.81}$$
$$R \approx 83.44\ \text{N}$$

**Step 7 — Direction Cosines.**
$$l = \frac{R_x}{R} = \frac{8.10}{83.44} = 0.0971$$
$$m = \frac{R_y}{R} = \frac{81.55}{83.44} = 0.9774$$
$$n = \frac{R_z}{R} = \frac{-15.71}{83.44} = -0.1883$$

**Verification:** $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 0.0094 + 0.9553 + 0.0354 = 1.0001 \approx 1$ ✔

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Concept of Equilibrant.**
The equilibrant $\vec{F_E}$ is the **equal and opposite** of the resultant:
$$\vec{F_E} = -\vec{R} = -8.10\hat{i} - 81.55\hat{j} + 15.71\hat{k}\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of Equilibrant.**
$$F_E = R = 83.44\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Direction Cosines of Equilibrant.**
$$l_E = -0.0971,\quad m_E = -0.9774,\quad n_E = +0.1883$$

**Step 4 — Angles with Axes.**
$$\alpha_E = \cos^{-1}(-0.0971) = 95.57°$$
$$\beta_E = \cos^{-1}(-0.9774) = 167.74°$$
$$\gamma_E = \cos^{-1}(+0.1883) = 79.15°$$

**Verification:** $l_E^2 + m_E^2 + n_E^2 = 0.0094 + 0.9553 + 0.0354 \approx 1$ ✔

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**A force $\vec{F} = 50\hat{i} - 80\hat{j} + 60\hat{k}$ N acts at a point P whose position vector from origin O is $\vec{r} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} - 4\hat{k}$ m.**

**(a)** Determine the **moment of this force about the origin O** using the cross product method. Express the moment as a Cartesian vector and find its magnitude. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **scalar moment of the same force about an axis passing through O along the unit vector $\hat{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k})$** (i.e., the line $x = y = z$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Given Vectors.**
$$\vec{r} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} - 4\hat{k}\ \text{m},\quad \vec{F} = 50\hat{i} - 80\hat{j} + 60\hat{k}\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Cross Product Formula.**
$$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ 50 & -80 & 60 \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Determinant.**
$$\vec{M}_O = \hat{i}\bigl[(3)(60) - (-4)(-80)\bigr] - \hat{j}\bigl[(2)(60) - (-4)(50)\bigr] + \hat{k}\bigl[(2)(-80) - (3)(50)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Compute Each Component.**

- **i-component:**
$$M_x = (3 \times 60) - (-4 \times -80) = 180 - 320 = -140\ \text{N·m}$$

- **j-component:**
$$M_y = -\bigl[(2 \times 60) - (-4 \times 50)\bigr] = -[120 - (-200)] = -[120 + 200] = -320\ \text{N·m}$$

- **k-component:**
$$M_z = (2 \times -80) - (3 \times 50) = -160 - 150 = -310\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Final Moment Vector.**
$$\vec{M}_O = -140\hat{i} - 320\hat{j} - 310\hat{k}\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of the Moment.**
$$\vert\vec{M}_O\vert = \sqrt{(-140)^2 + (-320)^2 + (-310)^2} = \sqrt{19600 + 102400 + 96100}$$
$$\vert\vec{M}_O\vert = \sqrt{218100} \approx 467.0\ \text{N·m}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Recall the Scalar Moment About an Axis Formula.**
$$M_\lambda = \hat{\lambda} \cdot (\vec{r} \times \vec{F}) = \hat{\lambda} \cdot \vec{M}_O$$

**Step 2 — Express the Unit Vector $\hat{\lambda}$.**
$$\hat{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\hat{i} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\hat{j} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\hat{k} \approx 0.5774\hat{i} + 0.5774\hat{j} + 0.5774\hat{k}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Dot Product.**
$$M_\lambda = (0.5774)(-140) + (0.5774)(-320) + (0.5774)(-310)$$

$$M_\lambda = -80.84 - 184.77 - 179.00 = -444.61\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The negative sign indicates that the rotation produced by $\vec{F}$ about the axis is **opposite** to the direction defined by $\hat{\lambda}$. The magnitude of the moment about the line $x=y=z$ is:

$$\vert M_\lambda\vert \approx 444.61\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Verification by Scalar Triple Product (Alternative).**
$$M_\lambda = \begin{vmatrix} \lambda_x & \lambda_y & \lambda_z \\ r_x & r_y & r_z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} 0.5774 & 0.5774 & 0.5774 \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ 50 & -80 & 60 \end{vmatrix}$$
$$M_\lambda = 0.5774\bigl[(3)(60) - (-4)(-80)\bigr] - 0.5774\bigl[(2)(60) - (-4)(50)\bigr] + 0.5774\bigl[(2)(-80) - (3)(50)\bigr]$$
$$M_\lambda = 0.5774(-140 - 320 - 310) = 0.5774 \times (-770) \approx -444.6\ \text{N·m}\ ✔$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Forces in Space (Module 1)**

- 🔹 A force in 3D is fully described by its **magnitude**, **direction cosines** $(l, m, n)$, and **point of application**.
- 🔹 The **direction cosine identity** $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ is non-negotiable — always verify it; it is a guaranteed free mark.
- 🔹 The Cartesian form of a force is $\vec{F} = F l\hat{i} + F m\hat{j} + F n\hat{k}$ or $\vec{F} = F_x\hat{i} + F_y\hat{j} + F_z\hat{k}$.
- 🔹 The magnitude is recovered as $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$.
- 🔹 The **resultant** of $n$ concurrent forces is found by *vector addition* of their components: $R_x = \Sigma F_x$, $R_y = \Sigma F_y$, $R_z = \Sigma F_z$.
- 🔹 The **equilibrant** is the negative of the resultant: $\vec{F_E} = -\vec{R}$.
- 🔹 **Equilibrium of a particle in space** requires three scalar equations: $\Sigma F_x = 0,\ \Sigma F_y = 0,\ \Sigma F_z = 0$.
- 🔹 The **moment of a 3D force about a point** is given by the **cross product** $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$, expanded via a $3 \times 3$ determinant.
- 🔹 The **moment about an axis** is the projection of $\vec{M}_O$ on the axis: $M_\lambda = \hat{\lambda} \cdot (\vec{r} \times \vec{F})$ (scalar triple product).
- 🔹 Always form a **component table** before writing equilibrium equations — examiners reward clarity.
- 🔹 Always carry **units** with every numerical answer (N, m, N·m).
- 🔹 **Most common pitfall:** sign errors in position vectors and missing direction-cosine verification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Reactions of Beams (Point Load and UDL Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Support Reactions of Beams (Point Load and UDL Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **beam** is a structural member, usually horizontal, designed to carry transverse loads and transfer them to its supports. **Support reactions** are the internal forces and moments developed at the supports of a beam to maintain **static equilibrium** when external loads are applied. The process of computing these reactions is called **reaction analysis**, and it is the foundational step before shear force and bending moment diagrams can be drawn.

For a 2D (planar) beam in static equilibrium, the **statical determinacy** requirement is:

$$r \le 3 \quad (\text{Statically Determinate})$$

$$r > 3 \quad (\text{Statically Indeterminate})$$

where $r$ is the total number of unknown reaction components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** This topic is restricted to **statically determinate beams** subjected only to **(a) Point Loads** and **(b) Uniformly Distributed Loads (UDL)**. No moving loads, no couples/moments as primary loads, and no indeterminate beams.

## 1.2 Types of Supports (Reactive Components)

| Support Type | Symbol | Reactions Provided | Mobility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Roller Support** | Circle on a line | 1 vertical reaction ($R_y$) | Horizontal sliding allowed |
| **Hinge / Pin Support** | Triangle with pin | 2 reactions ($R_x, R_y$) | Rotation allowed, no translation |
| **Fixed Support** | Hatched wall | 3 reactions ($R_x, R_y, M$) | Fully restrained |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Recall for KTU Board:** A roller gives **1 unknown**, a hinge gives **2 unknowns**, and a fixed support gives **3 unknowns**. The total number of equilibrium equations available in 2D is exactly **3**: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$.

## 1.3 Types of Beams (Based on Support Configuration)

- **Simply Supported Beam** — Hinge at one end, roller at the other (most common in KTU problems).
- **Overhanging Beam** — Simply supported beam with one or both ends projecting beyond the supports.
- **Cantilever Beam** — Fixed at one end, free at the other.
- **Fixed Beam** — Fixed at both ends (statically indeterminate, *out of current scope*).

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Wooden Plank and Bricks:** Imagine a wooden plank laid across two bricks. If you place a heavy bag of rice (a point load) at the center, each brick "pushes up" against the plank. The upward push is the **support reaction**. If instead you spread a row of rice bags uniformly across the plank (a UDL), the total weight is the same, but now the bricks collectively share the load. The key insight: **any distributed load can be replaced by an equivalent single point force equal to the total load, acting at the centroid of the distribution.**

## 1.5 Types of Loads Covered in This Module

- **Point Load (Concentrated Load):** A load acting at a single, discrete point, denoted $P$ or $W$ with units of **N** or **kN**.
- **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL):** A load spread uniformly over a length, denoted $w$ with units of **N/m** or **kN/m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of 2D Static Equilibrium

For a beam lying in the $xy$-plane, the **necessary and sufficient conditions** for static equilibrium are exactly three independent scalar equations:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \text{(No net horizontal force)}$$

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \text{(No net vertical force)}$$

$$\sum M_O = 0 \quad \text{(No net moment about any point O)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why exactly 3 equations?** A rigid body in 2D has **3 degrees of freedom** (translation in $x$, translation in $y$, rotation about $z$). Each equilibrium equation removes exactly one DOF. Hence, a beam is statically determinate when $r = 3$.

## 2.2 Conversion of UDL to an Equivalent Point Load

A UDL of intensity $w$ acting over a length $L$ produces a **resultant point load** equal to the area under the load diagram:

$$P_{\text{eq}} = w \times L$$

This resultant acts at the **centroid (geometric center)** of the UDL region. For a UDL extending from $x = a$ to $x = b$, the centroid is at:

$$x_{\text{centroid}} = \frac{a + b}{2}$$

## 2.3 Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Quantity | Positive Direction |
|---|---|
| Vertical reaction $R_y$ | Upward |
| Horizontal reaction $R_x$ | Rightward |
| Applied loads (downward) | Negative when summing $\sum F_y$ |
| Moment (reaction couple) | Counter-clockwise (CCW) positive |
| Sagging moment | Positive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Consistency is the key to marks.** Examiners award full marks only when the **same sign convention** is used throughout the problem. A single sign-flip mid-problem can cascade into wrong answers.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | Sum of horizontal forces = 0 | Find $R_x$ (only if no horizontal loads, $R_x = 0$) |
| 2 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | Sum of vertical forces = 0 | Find $R_A$ or $R_B$ after the other is known |
| 3 | $\sum M_O = 0$ | Sum of moments about O = 0 | Most powerful; eliminates two unknowns at once |
| 4 | $P_{\text{eq}} = wL$ | UDL → Point load | Convert UDL to point load before analysis |
| 5 | $R_A = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{L}$ | Reaction at A, point load at distance $a$ from A, $b$ from B, span $L$ | Simply supported beam with point load |
| 6 | $R_B = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{L}$ | Reaction at B, same setup as above | Simply supported beam with point load |
| 7 | $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ | Reactions for full-span UDL on simply supported beam | Symmetric UDL case |
| 8 | $r = 3 + s - 3n$ | Degree of indeterminacy ($s$ = equilibrium eqns beyond rigid-body, $n$ = members) | Mostly out of scope, but useful for determinacy check |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Support reaction analysis is the **starting point of every structural design**:
- **Civil Engineering:** Sizing of columns, footings, and bridge bearings.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of machine tool beds, crane girders, conveyor supports.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spar attachment loads.
- **Shipbuilding:** Hull girder support reactions in dry dock.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world link:** Kerala's **Vembanad Rail Bridge** and the **Kochi Metro viaducts** are simply supported / continuous girder systems — the very first calculation the structural engineer performs is the support reaction at every pier.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simply Supported Beam with a Point Load

**Problem Setup:** A beam of length $L$ is supported on a hinge at A (left) and a roller at B (right). A point load $P$ acts vertically downward at a distance $a$ from A and $b$ from B, where $L = a + b$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram (FBD):** Draw the beam, label the unknown reactions $R_A$ (upward at A) and $R_B$ (upward at B). Mark the point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A.

**Step 2 — Count unknowns:** $r = 2$ (i.e., $R_A$ and $R_B$). Available equations = 3. Therefore **statically determinate** (1 equation is redundant for verification).

**Step 3 — Take moments about A** (this eliminates $R_A$):

$$\sum M_A = 0 \quad (\text{CCW positive})$$

$$R_B \cdot L \;-\; P \cdot a \;=\; 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_B$:**

$$R_B = \frac{P \cdot a}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Apply $\sum F_y = 0$** to find $R_A$:

$$R_A + R_B - P = 0$$

$$R_A = P - R_B = P - \frac{P a}{L} = P \left(1 - \frac{a}{L}\right) = P \cdot \frac{L - a}{L} = \frac{P \cdot b}{L}$$

**Final Result:**

$$R_A = \frac{P \cdot b}{L}, \qquad R_B = \frac{P \cdot a}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Check:** If $a = b = L/2$, then $R_A = R_B = P/2$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Simply Supported Beam with a Full-Span UDL

**Problem Setup:** A UDL of intensity $w$ acts over the entire span $L$.

**Step 1 — Convert UDL to equivalent point load:**

$$P_{\text{eq}} = w \times L$$

This load acts at the **midspan** (centroid of rectangle), i.e., at a distance $L/2$ from A.

**Step 2 — Take moments about A:**

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot L - wL \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_B$:**

$$R_B = \frac{wL^2 / 2}{L} = \frac{wL}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply $\sum F_y = 0$:**

$$R_A + R_B - wL = 0 \implies R_A = wL - \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{wL}{2}$$

**Final Result:**

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — UDL Over a Partial Span (Analytical Method)

**Problem Setup:** A UDL of intensity $w$ acts from $x = x_1$ to $x = x_2$ on a simply supported beam of span $L$.

**Step 1 — Equivalent point load:**

$$P_{\text{eq}} = w \cdot (x_2 - x_1)$$

**Step 2 — Centroid of the loaded region** (distance from A):

$$\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Take moments about A:**

$$R_B \cdot L - P_{\text{eq}} \cdot \bar{x} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$R_B = \frac{w (x_2 - x_1)(x_1 + x_2)}{2L} = \frac{w \left(x_2^2 - x_1^2\right)}{2L}$$

**Step 5 — Vertical equilibrium:**

$$R_A = w(x_2 - x_1) - R_B$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Formulas for Common KTU Cases

| Beam Type | Loading | $R_A$ | $R_B$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported, span $L$ | Point load $P$ at center | $P/2$ | $P/2$ |
| Simply Supported, span $L$ | Point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A | $P b / L$ | $P a / L$ |
| Simply Supported, span $L$ | Full UDL $w$ | $wL/2$ | $wL/2$ |
| Simply Supported, span $L$ | UDL $w$ from A to midspan | $3wL/8$ | $wL/8$ |
| Overhanging Beam (length $L + c$) | Point load $P$ at center of main span | $P/2$ | $P/2$ |
| Cantilever, span $L$ | Point load $P$ at free end | $R = P$ (fixed end) | $M = PL$ (fixed end) |
| Cantilever, span $L$ | Full UDL $w$ | $R = wL$ (fixed end) | $M = wL^2/2$ (fixed end) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** For symmetric loading on a simply supported beam, **both reactions equal half the total load.** This single fact can save 3–4 minutes in the exam hall.

## 3.5 Master Worked Example — Mixed Loading

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $8$ m carries a point load of $20$ kN at $3$ m from the left support A, and a UDL of $5$ kN/m over the rightmost $4$ m. Find $R_A$ and $R_B$.

**Given:** $L = 8$ m, $P = 20$ kN at $a = 3$ m, UDL $w = 5$ kN/m over $x \in [4, 8]$ m.

**Step 1 — Equivalent point load of UDL:**

$$P_{\text{eq}} = w \times 4 = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Centroid of UDL from A:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{4 + 8}{2} = 6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Take moments about A** (CCW positive):

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \times 8 - 20 \times 3 - 20 \times 6 = 0$$

$$8 R_B = 60 + 120 = 180$$

$$R_B = 22.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Vertical equilibrium:**

$$R_A + R_B = 20 + 20 = 40 \text{ kN}$$

$$R_A = 40 - 22.5 = 17.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using $\sum M_B = 0$:**

$$R_A \times 8 - 20 \times 5 - 20 \times 2 = 0$$

$$8 R_A = 100 + 40 = 140 \implies R_A = 17.5 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always carry out the verification step.** In KTU valuation, a verified answer using an independent equation is awarded full marks even if the first solution had a sign slip that the verification step caught.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Support Reaction Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1: Identify Beam Type] --> startB[Step 2: Identify Supports and Unknown Reactions]
    startB --> startC[Step 3: Check Determinacy r = 3]
    startC --> startD{Determinate}
    startD -- No --> stopX[STOP: Use Compatibility Methods Out of Scope]
    startD -- Yes --> startE[Step 4: Convert All UDLs to Equivalent Point Loads]
    startE --> startF[Step 5: Draw Free Body Diagram with All Forces and Lever Arms]
    startF --> startG[Step 6: Apply Sum of Moments about a Suitable Point to Eliminate Two Unknowns]
    startG --> startH[Step 7: Apply Sum of Forces in y direction]
    startH --> startI[Step 8: Apply Sum of Forces in x direction if required]
    startI --> startJ[Step 9: Verify Results using Redundant Equilibrium Equation]
    startJ --> startK[Step 10: State Final Reactions with Units]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Classification of Supports (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Support Systems in 2D Beams] --> nodeB[Roller Support]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Hinge or Pin Support]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Fixed Support]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Reactions: One Vertical Force]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Degree of Freedom Allowed: Horizontal Translation]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Reactions: One Vertical and One Horizontal Force]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Degree of Freedom Allowed: Rotation only]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Reactions: One Vertical, One Horizontal Force and One Moment Couple]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Degree of Freedom Allowed: None Fully Built In]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Reaction Resolution Strategy (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    resA[Reaction Resolution Strategy] --> resB{Does a Hinge or Pin Exist}
    resB -- Yes --> resC[Use Hinge to Split Beam and Write Moment Equation for One Side Only]
    resB -- No --> resD{Is a UDL Present}
    resD -- Yes --> resE[Replace UDL with Equivalent Point Load at Centroid]
    resD -- No --> resF[Proceed Directly to Moment Equation]
    resE --> resG[Take Moments about Support with Most Unknowns to Eliminate Them]
    resF --> resG
    resC --> resG
    resG --> resH[Apply Vertical Force Balance]
    resH --> resI[Apply Horizontal Force Balance if Horizontal Loads Exist]
    resI --> resJ[Cross Check with Redundant Equation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The above Mermaid blocks are **functional architecture flow diagrams** depicting the analytical workflow. They are not physical beam drawings; KTU 2024 Module 1 problems expect students to draw their own FBDs on graph paper during the exam, but the conceptual flow remains as mapped above.

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic Reference — Free Body Diagram Convention

```
              <------ a ------><--- b --->
              |                 |         |
              V                 V         |
   ____________________________________________________
  A|======[ R_A ↑ ]========================[ R_B ↑ ]====|B
  |____________________________________________________|
              <---------------- L ----------------->

   A = Hinge (gives R_A vertical and R_x horizontal)
   B = Roller (gives R_B vertical only)
   P = Point load acting downward at distance a from A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**State and explain the three conditions of static equilibrium for a rigid body in two dimensions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A rigid body in 2D is in static equilibrium when:
1. **$\sum F_x = 0$** — The algebraic sum of all horizontal force components acting on the body is zero, ensuring no translation in the $x$-direction. **(1 mark)**
2. **$\sum F_y = 0$** — The algebraic sum of all vertical force components is zero, ensuring no translation in the $y$-direction. **(1 mark)**
3. **$\sum M_O = 0$** — The algebraic sum of moments of all forces about any arbitrary point $O$ in the plane of the body is zero, ensuring no rotation. **(1 mark)**

These three independent equations are sufficient to solve a maximum of three unknown reaction components in a coplanar system.

---

### **Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**A beam is loaded with a UDL of intensity $w$ over a length $L$. Explain how it is converted into an equivalent point load.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A UDL of intensity $w$ (in N/m or kN/m) acting over a length $L$ can be replaced by a single equivalent point load **$P_{\text{eq}} = wL$** that acts at the **centroid (geometric center) of the loaded region** **(2 marks)**. This is because the area under the load diagram equals the total force, and the moment of the equivalent point load about any point equals the moment of the original distributed load. For a rectangular UDL (constant $w$ over $L$), the centroid is at the midspan $L/2$ **(1 mark)**. This conversion simplifies the moment and force equations for reaction analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q1]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** A simply supported beam of span $6$ m carries a point load of $30$ kN at $2$ m from the left support. Find the support reactions. Also verify your answer using an independent equilibrium equation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A simply supported beam of span $8$ m is subjected to a uniformly distributed load of $10$ kN/m over the entire span. Determine the reactions at both supports and draw the Free Body Diagram.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B-A:**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 6$ m, $P = 30$ kN at $a = 2$ m from A, hence $b = L - a = 6 - 2 = 4$ m.

**Step 1 — Take moments about A** (eliminates $R_A$):

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \times 6 - 30 \times 2 = 0$$

$$6 R_B = 60 \implies R_B = 10 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(upward)}$$

**Step 2 — Vertical equilibrium:**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A + R_B - 30 = 0$$

$$R_A = 30 - 10 = 20 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(upward)}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (moments about B):**

$$\sum M_B = 0 \implies R_A \times 6 - 30 \times 4 = 0$$

$$6 R_A = 120 \implies R_A = 20 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $R_A = 20$ kN ↑, $R_B = 10$ kN ↑. **(7 marks — 2 for FBD, 2 for moment eqn, 2 for vertical eqn, 1 for verification)**

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 8$ m, $w = 10$ kN/m over the entire span.

**Step 1 — Equivalent point load of UDL:**

$$P_{\text{eq}} = wL = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ kN, acting at } x = L/2 = 4 \text{ m from A}$$

**Step 2 — Take moments about A:**

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \times 8 - 80 \times 4 = 0$$

$$8 R_B = 320 \implies R_B = 40 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Vertical equilibrium:**

$$R_A + R_B = 80 \implies R_A = 80 - 40 = 40 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — FBD description:** Beam of length 8 m, hinge at A (left), roller at B (right), rectangular UDL of 10 kN/m shown as a series of downward arrows of equal length covering the full span, equivalent point load 80 kN shown at the midspan.

**Final Answer:** $R_A = R_B = 40$ kN ↑. **(7 marks — 1 for conversion, 2 for moment eqn, 2 for vertical eqn, 2 for FBD)**

---

### **Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]` (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** A simply supported beam of span $10$ m carries a point load of $40$ kN at $4$ m from the left support A and another point load of $20$ kN at $7$ m from A. Calculate the reactions at A and B.

**(b) [7 marks]** An overhanging beam ABC has supports at A and B, with the segment AB = $6$ m and the overhang BC = $2$ m. A UDL of $15$ kN/m acts on the entire length AC. Find the reactions at A and B.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B-B:**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 10$ m, $P_1 = 40$ kN at $a_1 = 4$ m, $P_2 = 20$ kN at $a_2 = 7$ m.

**Step 1 — Take moments about A:**

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \times 10 - 40 \times 4 - 20 \times 7 = 0$$

$$10 R_B = 160 + 140 = 300$$

$$R_B = 30 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(upward)}$$

**Step 2 — Vertical equilibrium:**

$$R_A + R_B = P_1 + P_2 = 40 + 20 = 60 \text{ kN}$$

$$R_A = 60 - 30 = 30 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(upward)}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (moments about B):**

$$R_A \times 10 - 40 \times 6 - 20 \times 3 = 0$$

$$10 R_A = 240 + 60 = 300 \implies R_A = 30 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $R_A = 30$ kN ↑, $R_B = 30$ kN ↑. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** Overhanging beam ABC, support at A (hinge), support at B (roller), AB = $6$ m, BC = $2$ m, total length AC = $8$ m. UDL $w = 15$ kN/m over entire AC.

**Step 1 — Equivalent point load of UDL:**

$$P_{\text{eq}} = 15 \times 8 = 120 \text{ kN, acting at midspan of AC} = 4 \text{ m from A}$$

**Step 2 — Take moments about A:**

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \times 6 - 120 \times 4 = 0$$

$$6 R_B = 480 \implies R_B = 80 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Vertical equilibrium:**

$$R_A + R_B = 120 \implies R_A = 120 - 80 = 40 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** $R_A < R_B$ because part of the load acts on the overhang BC which generates a moment that *reduces* the load share on A. This is the hallmark of overhanging beams.

**Final Answer:** $R_A = 40$ kN ↑, $R_B = 80$ kN ↑. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Support Reaction Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the UDL → Point Load conversion** before applying moment equations. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this directly. Always write the equivalent load value on the FBD.
> 2. **Wrong centroid location** for a partial-span UDL. The centroid of a UDL from $x_1$ to $x_2$ is at $\frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}$, **not** at $L/2$.
> 3. **Sign errors in the moment equation.** A downward force creates a *clockwise* moment about a point to its right, which is *negative* in the standard CCW-positive convention. Mixing signs midway = cascading errors.
> 4. **Not using the verification step.** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards a **partial mark** for the verification equation. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Confusing the location of $a$ and $b$.** Remember: $a$ is the distance of the load from A, and $b = L - a$ is the distance from B. A common slip is to write $R_A = Pa/L$ instead of $R_A = Pb/L$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **3 Equilibrium Equations** in 2D: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$. **(Mandatory toolkit)**
- **Roller = 1 reaction, Hinge = 2 reactions, Fixed = 3 reactions.** **(Reactive component count)**
- **Statically Determinate** when total unknowns $r = 3$ in 2D.
- **UDL Conversion Formula:** $P_{\text{eq}} = wL$ acting at the centroid of the loaded region.
- **Standard Sign Convention:** Upward forces positive, CCW moments positive, downward loads treated as negative when summed in $\sum F_y$.
- **Most Powerful Equation:** $\sum M = 0$ about a chosen point eliminates reactions whose lines of action pass through that point — choose the point that **removes the most unknowns** (typically the support with two unknowns).
- **Always verify** the answer with a redundant equilibrium equation (the unused one).
- **Overhanging Beams** have *asymmetric* reactions even with symmetric loading, because the overhanging load shifts the effective centroid.
- **Symmetry Shortcut:** If the loading and geometry are both symmetric about the midspan, both reactions equal half the total load.
- **Units Discipline:** $P$ in kN, $w$ in kN/m, $L$ in m → reactions automatically in kN and moments in kN·m. Mixing units is a silent but deadly trap.
- **KTU Module 1 Boundary:** This module explicitly **excludes** moving loads, moment loads, and statically indeterminate beams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Systems: Rectangular Components in 2D and 3D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Force Systems: Rectangular Components in 2D and 3D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Force System?

A **Force System** is a collection of forces acting on a rigid body or a particle, considered simultaneously for the purpose of analyzing their combined effect (resultant) and the body's state of equilibrium or motion. In Engineering Mechanics, every external load applied to a structure is first resolved into a **force system** before equilibrium equations are applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **force** is a vector quantity characterized by **magnitude, direction, and point of application**. It is measured in **Newtons (N)** in the SI system or **kgf (kilogram-force)** in the MKS system.

### Classification of Force Systems (KTU Module 1 Highlight)

| Force System Type | Description | Dimensionality |
|---|---|---|
| **Coplanar Concurrent** | All forces lie in the same plane and meet at a single point | 2D |
| **Coplanar Non-Concurrent** | All forces lie in the same plane but do not meet at a single point | 2D |
| **Collinear** | All forces act along the same line of action | 1D |
| **Concurrent (3D)** | All forces meet at a common point in 3D space | 3D |
| **Non-Coplanar (3D)** | Forces are distributed in 3D space, not confined to one plane | 3D |

## 1.2 Rectangular Components — The Heart of Force Analysis

**Rectangular Components** of a force are its projections along two (in 2D) or three (in 3D) mutually perpendicular axes—typically the standard Cartesian axes **X, Y, and Z**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why decompose a force into rectangular components?**
> Engineers never work with slanted forces directly. By splitting a slanted force into horizontal and vertical parts, every problem reduces to simple algebra along the **X** and **Y** (and **Z**) axes. This is the **single most critical step** in solving any statics problem.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tug of War" Picture

Imagine you and a friend are pulling a heavy **wooden log** with a single rope at an angle. The rope pulls the log with force **F** along a slanted direction. Now:

- The **horizontal component (Fx)** is responsible for dragging the log forward on the ground.
- The **vertical component (Fy)** is responsible for trying to lift the log off the ground.

You cannot directly tell how much "forward push" or "lifting pull" the rope produces. But once you know the angle **θ** the rope makes with the ground, you can find both:

- **Fx = F · cos θ** (forward push)
- **Fy = F · sin θ** (lifting pull)

This is exactly the principle behind **rectangular resolution of forces**—breaking a slanted vector into its horizontal and vertical "cousins."

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Units:**
> - Force: **N (Newton)** = kg·m/s²
> - Standard gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Weight: W = m · g (mass in kg, weight in N)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rectangular Components in 2D (Coplanar Forces)

Consider a force **F** acting in the X-Y plane, making an angle **θ** with the positive **X-axis** (measured counter-clockwise).

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. Drop a **perpendicular** from the tip of vector **F** to the X-axis. The foot gives the **X-component (Fx)**.
2. Drop a **perpendicular** from the tip of vector **F** to the Y-axis. The foot gives the **Y-component (Fy)**.
3. **Fx** lies along X, **Fy** lies along Y, and **F** is the **hypotenuse** of the right triangle formed.

### The 2D Formulas

$$
F_x = F \cos\theta
$$

$$
F_y = F \sin\theta
$$

The **magnitude** of the original force can be recovered as:

$$
F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}
$$

The **direction** (angle) of the original force is:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{F_y}{F_x}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - Components pointing in the **+X, +Y direction** → **POSITIVE**
> - Components pointing in the **−X, −Y direction** → **NEGATIVE**
> - Angles are measured **counter-clockwise from +X axis** (mathematically positive).

## 2.2 Rectangular Components in 3D (Spatial Forces)

A force in 3D space is resolved into **three** mutually perpendicular components along **X, Y, and Z** axes. The standard approach uses **Direction Cosines**.

### Direction Cosines Approach

Let **α, β, γ** be the angles made by force **F** with the **+X, +Y, +Z** axes, respectively. Then:

$$
F_x = F \cos\alpha
$$

$$
F_y = F \cos\beta
$$

$$
F_z = F \cos\gamma
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Direction Cosine Identity (must memorize for KTU exams):**
>
> $$\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$$
>
> This is a **fundamental identity** that examiners love to test. It is derived from the unit vector property $\hat{u}_x^2 + \hat{u}_y^2 + \hat{u}_z^2 = 1$.

### Unit Vector Representation

The force **F** as a vector in 3D:

$$
\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}
$$

Where:
- $\hat{i}$ = unit vector along **+X axis**
- $\hat{j}$ = unit vector along **+Y axis**
- $\hat{k}$ = unit vector along **+Z axis**

The **unit vector** along the direction of **F** is:

$$
\hat{u}_F = \cos\alpha \, \hat{i} + \cos\beta \, \hat{j} + \cos\gamma \, \hat{k}
$$

The **magnitude** of the force:

$$
F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}
$$

## 2.3 Rectangular Components from a Point-and-Coordinate Approach (KTU Favorite)

When a force **F** acts at point **A(x₁, y₁, z₁)** and is directed toward point **B(x₂, y₂, z₂)**, the components are:

$$
\vec{AB} = (x_2 - x_1)\hat{i} + (y_2 - y_1)\hat{j} + (z_2 - z_1)\hat{k}
$$

$$
\text{Distance } d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}
$$

$$
\text{Unit vector } \hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{\vec{AB}}{d}
$$

$$
\vec{F} = F \cdot \hat{u}_{AB} = F \left[ \frac{(x_2 - x_1)}{d}\hat{i} + \frac{(y_2 - y_1)}{d}\hat{j} + \frac{(z_2 - z_1)}{d}\hat{k} \right]
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_x = F\cos\theta$ | 2D X-component | N |
| 2 | $F_y = F\sin\theta$ | 2D Y-component | N |
| 3 | $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$ | 2D magnitude | N |
| 4 | $F_x = F\cos\alpha$ | 3D X-component | N |
| 5 | $F_y = F\cos\beta$ | 3D Y-component | N |
| 6 | $F_z = F\cos\gamma$ | 3D Z-component | N |
| 7 | $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$ | Direction cosine identity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ | 3D magnitude | N |
| 9 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(F_y / F_x)$ | 2D direction | degrees/radians |
| 10 | $\hat{u}_F = \hat{i}\cos\alpha + \hat{j}\cos\beta + \hat{k}\cos\gamma$ | Unit vector in 3D | dimensionless |
| 11 | $W = mg$ | Weight from mass | N (with g in m/s²) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Truss analysis, bridge cable tensions, retaining wall lateral earth pressure.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** 3D machining force analysis, robotic arm load resolution.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Thrust vectoring, aerodynamic lift-drag resolution.
- **Structural Engineering:** Wind/seismic load resolution into building frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every major engineering analysis—whether it's the Idukki Dam, the Chenab Bridge, or a Boeing 787 wing—begins with the **resolution of forces into rectangular components**. Master this topic, and you master the foundation of all of statics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 2D Rectangular Components from First Principles

### Given
A force vector **F** of magnitude **F** acting at an angle **θ** measured counter-clockwise from the **+X axis** in the X-Y plane.

### To Derive
Expressions for **Fx** and **Fy** in terms of **F** and **θ**.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Draw the force **F** as the hypotenuse of a right triangle. The base of the triangle lies along the **+X axis**, and the height lies parallel to the **+Y axis**.

**Step 2:** Identify the angle **θ** at the origin (where the force starts) between **F** and the X-axis.

**Step 3:** Apply the basic trigonometric definitions in the right triangle:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{F_x}{F}
$$

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{F_y}{F}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate **Fx** and **Fy**:

$$
F_x = F \cos\theta
$$

$$
F_y = F \sin\theta
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **Pythagorean theorem** to recover the original magnitude:

$$
F^2 = F_x^2 + F_y^2
$$

$$
F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}
$$

**Step 6:** Derive the direction:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{F_y}{F_x}
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{F_y}{F_x}\right)
$$

> **Result:** A 2D force **F** is fully described by its two perpendicular components **Fx** and **Fy**, or equivalently by its magnitude **F** and direction **θ**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: 3D Rectangular Components Using Direction Cosines

### Given
A 3D force **F** of magnitude **F** making angles **α, β, γ** with the **+X, +Y, +Z** axes respectively.

### To Derive
(i) The three components **Fx, Fy, Fz**, and  
(ii) The identity $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Express the force vector in vector form:

$$
\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}
$$

**Step 2:** Express the unit vector along the force direction. Since $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are unit vectors along the axes, the components of the unit vector along **F** are simply the **direction cosines**:

$$
\hat{u}_F = \frac{\vec{F}}{F} = \frac{F_x}{F}\hat{i} + \frac{F_y}{F}\hat{j} + \frac{F_z}{F}\hat{k}
$$

$$
\hat{u}_F = \cos\alpha \, \hat{i} + \cos\beta \, \hat{j} + \cos\gamma \, \hat{k}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate coefficients of $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$:

$$
\cos\alpha = \frac{F_x}{F}, \quad \cos\beta = \frac{F_y}{F}, \quad \cos\gamma = \frac{F_z}{F}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the components:

$$
F_x = F\cos\alpha, \quad F_y = F\cos\beta, \quad F_z = F\cos\gamma
$$

**Step 5:** Derive the **direction cosine identity**. Since $\hat{u}_F$ is a unit vector, its magnitude must equal **1**:

$$
|\hat{u}_F| = \sqrt{\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma} = 1
$$

**Step 6:** Square both sides:

$$
\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1
$$

> **Result:** The three direction cosines are not independent; they are constrained by this identity. Given any two, the third can be found (with sign ambiguity resolved by physical context).

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem
A force of magnitude **F = 100 N** acts at a point in 3D space and is directed from point **A(0, 0, 0)** to point **B(3, 4, 12)**. Determine:
1. The rectangular components **Fx, Fy, Fz**.
2. The angles **α, β, γ** with the coordinate axes.
3. Verify the direction cosine identity.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Position Vector $\vec{AB}$**

$$
\vec{AB} = (3-0)\hat{i} + (4-0)\hat{j} + (12-0)\hat{k} = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} + 12\hat{k}
$$

**Step 2: Distance from A to B (Magnitude of $\vec{AB}$)**

$$
d = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Unit Vector along $\vec{AB}$**

$$
\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{\vec{AB}}{d} = \frac{3}{13}\hat{i} + \frac{4}{13}\hat{j} + \frac{12}{13}\hat{k}
$$

**Step 4: Force Vector $\vec{F}$**

$$
\vec{F} = F \cdot \hat{u}_{AB} = 100 \left( \frac{3}{13}\hat{i} + \frac{4}{13}\hat{j} + \frac{12}{13}\hat{k} \right)
$$

$$
F_x = 100 \times \frac{3}{13} = \frac{300}{13} \approx 23.08 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_y = 100 \times \frac{4}{13} = \frac{400}{13} \approx 30.77 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_z = 100 \times \frac{12}{13} = \frac{1200}{13} \approx 92.31 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Direction Cosines (Direct from Unit Vector)**

$$
\cos\alpha = \frac{3}{13}, \quad \cos\beta = \frac{4}{13}, \quad \cos\gamma = \frac{12}{13}
$$

**Step 6: Angles with Axes**

$$
\alpha = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{13}\right) \approx 76.66^{\circ}
$$

$$
\beta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{13}\right) \approx 72.08^{\circ}
$$

$$
\gamma = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{13}\right) \approx 22.62^{\circ}
$$

**Step 7: Verify Direction Cosine Identity**

$$
\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = \left(\frac{3}{13}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{4}{13}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{12}{13}\right)^2
$$

$$
= \frac{9}{169} + \frac{16}{169} + \frac{144}{169} = \frac{169}{169} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 8: Verify Magnitude of Force**

$$
F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2} = \sqrt{23.08^2 + 30.77^2 + 92.31^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{532.7 + 946.8 + 8521.1} = \sqrt{10000.6} \approx 100 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **All checks pass.** The components, angles, and identity are consistent.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 2D Rectangular Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Force F at angle theta from X axis] --> B[Identify angle theta with +X axis]
    B --> C[Project F onto X axis]
    B --> D[Project F onto Y axis]
    C --> E[Component Fx = F cos theta]
    D --> F[Component Fy = F sin theta]
    E --> G{Determine Sign}
    F --> G
    G --> H[+X direction gives +Fx]
    G --> I[-X direction gives -Fx]
    G --> J[+Y direction gives +Fy]
    G --> K[-Y direction gives -Fy]
    H --> L[Final Rectangular Form]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[F vector = Fx i + Fy j]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: 3D Rectangular Resolution (Direction Cosine Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Force F in 3D space] --> Q[Identify angles alpha beta gamma with X Y Z axes]
    Q --> R[alpha = angle with X axis]
    Q --> S[beta = angle with Y axis]
    Q --> T[gamma = angle with Z axis]
    R --> U[Compute Fx = F cos alpha]
    S --> V[Compute Fy = F cos beta]
    T --> W[Compute Fz = F cos gamma]
    U --> X[Apply Direction Cosine Identity]
    V --> X
    W --> X
    X --> Y[cos squared alpha + cos squared beta + cos squared gamma = 1]
    Y --> Z[Verify F = sqrt of Fx squared + Fy squared + Fz squared]
    Z --> AA[Final 3D Force Vector F = Fx i + Fy j + Fz k]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Point-to-Point Force Resolution (Spatial Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Known point A x1 y1 z1] --> C[Compute Position Vector AB]
    B1[Known point B x2 y2 z2] --> C
    C --> D[AB vector = x2-x1 i + y2-y1 j + z2-z1 k]
    D --> E[Compute distance d = magnitude of AB]
    E --> F[Unit vector uAB = AB divided by d]
    F --> G[Force vector F = F times uAB]
    G --> H1[Fx = F times x2-x1 divided by d]
    G --> H2[Fy = F times y2-y1 divided by d]
    G --> H3[Fz = F times z2-z1 divided by d]
    H1 --> I[Final 3D Components]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.4 Conceptual 2D Force Triangle (Visual Intuition)

```
                  F
                 /|
                / |
               /  |  Fy = F sin θ
              /   |
             /    |
            / θ___|
              Fx = F cos θ
```

The slanted arrow **F** (the hypotenuse) is the original force. Its horizontal shadow **Fx** is the X-component, and its vertical shadow **Fy** is the Y-component. Together, they form a right triangle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** Always draw the right triangle on the X-Y plane with **F as the hypotenuse** and **θ at the origin**. This single diagram is worth 1–2 marks in many KTU problems and is essential for partial credit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Direction Cosines" as applied to a 3D force system. State the fundamental identity satisfied by them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **direction cosines** of a force vector **F** in 3D space are the cosines of the angles (α, β, γ) made by the force with the positive **X, Y, Z** axes respectively. They are denoted as **cos α, cos β, cos γ**.

**Fundamental Identity:**
$$
\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1
$$

This identity arises because the unit vector along the force direction must have a magnitude of unity.

> **Marking scheme:** Definition – 1.5 marks | Identity – 1 mark | Statement explanation – 0.5 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**A force of magnitude 200 N acts in the X-Y plane at an angle of 30° with the X-axis. Find its rectangular components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
F_x = F \cos\theta = 200 \cos 30^{\circ} = 200 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 100\sqrt{3} \approx 173.2 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_y = F \sin\theta = 200 \sin 30^{\circ} = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \text{ N}
$$

**Result:** $F_x \approx 173.2$ N (along +X), $F_y = 100$ N (along +Y).

> **Marking scheme:** Formula – 1 mark | Calculation – 1.5 marks | Units & final answer – 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** A force of **500 N** is directed from point **A(2, 3, 6) m** to point **B(8, 7, 14) m**. Determine:
- (i) The rectangular components **Fx, Fy, Fz** of the force. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Two forces **F₁ = 100 N** acting at 30° to the X-axis and **F₂ = 150 N** acting at 120° to the X-axis, both lie in the X-Y plane. Find:
- (i) The magnitude of the resultant force.
- (ii) The angle the resultant makes with the X-axis. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Position Vector $\vec{AB}$**

$$
\vec{AB} = (8-2)\hat{i} + (7-3)\hat{j} + (14-6)\hat{k} = 6\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} + 8\hat{k} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Magnitude of $\vec{AB}$**

$$
d = \sqrt{6^2 + 4^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 16 + 64} = \sqrt{116} \approx 10.77 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Unit Vector $\hat{u}_{AB}$**

$$
\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{6}{10.77}\hat{i} + \frac{4}{10.77}\hat{j} + \frac{8}{10.77}\hat{k}
$$

$$
\hat{u}_{AB} \approx 0.5571\hat{i} + 0.3714\hat{j} + 0.7428\hat{k}
$$

**Step 4: Force Components**

$$
F_x = 500 \times 0.5571 \approx 278.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_y = 500 \times 0.3714 \approx 185.7 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_z = 500 \times 0.7428 \approx 371.4 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Verification**

$$
F = \sqrt{278.5^2 + 185.7^2 + 371.4^2} = \sqrt{77562 + 34484 + 137938} = \sqrt{249984} \approx 500 \text{ N} \checkmark
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Components of F₁**

$$
F_{1x} = 100 \cos 30^{\circ} = 100 \times 0.8660 = 86.60 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{1y} = 100 \sin 30^{\circ} = 100 \times 0.5 = 50 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Components of F₂**

$$
F_{2x} = 150 \cos 120^{\circ} = 150 \times (-0.5) = -75 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{2y} = 150 \sin 120^{\circ} = 150 \times 0.8660 = 129.90 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Resultant Components**

$$
R_x = F_{1x} + F_{2x} = 86.60 + (-75) = 11.60 \text{ N}
$$

$$
R_y = F_{1y} + F_{2y} = 50 + 129.90 = 179.90 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4: Magnitude of Resultant**

$$
R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2} = \sqrt{11.60^2 + 179.90^2} = \sqrt{134.56 + 32364.01} = \sqrt{32498.57} \approx 180.27 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Direction of Resultant**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{179.90}{11.60}\right) = \tan^{-1}(15.51) \approx 86.31^{\circ}
$$

**Final Result:** $R \approx 180.27$ N at **86.31°** from the X-axis.

> **Marking scheme (a):** Position vector – 1.5 | Unit vector – 2 | Components – 2 | Verification – 1.5
> **Marking scheme (b):** Components of F₁ & F₂ – 3 | Resultant components – 2 | Magnitude & direction – 2

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alt]
**(a)** A force vector in 3D has components $F_x = 4$ N, $F_y = 6$ N, $F_z = 12$ N. Find:
- (i) The magnitude of the force.
- (ii) The direction cosines and angles α, β, γ with the coordinate axes. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A force of magnitude **250 N** is directed from origin **O(0,0,0)** to point **P(6, 2, 3) m**. Find the components of the force along the X, Y, and Z axes. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Magnitude of Force**

$$
F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 6^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{16 + 36 + 144} = \sqrt{196} = 14 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Direction Cosines**

$$
\cos\alpha = \frac{F_x}{F} = \frac{4}{14} = \frac{2}{7}
$$

$$
\cos\beta = \frac{F_y}{F} = \frac{6}{14} = \frac{3}{7}
$$

$$
\cos\gamma = \frac{F_z}{F} = \frac{12}{14} = \frac{6}{7}
$$

**Step 3: Angles with Axes**

$$
\alpha = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{7}\right) \approx 73.40^{\circ}
$$

$$
\beta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{7}\right) \approx 64.62^{\circ}
$$

$$
\gamma = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{6}{7}\right) \approx 31.00^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4: Verification of Identity**

$$
\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = \left(\frac{2}{7}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{3}{7}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{6}{7}\right)^2 = \frac{4+9+36}{49} = \frac{49}{49} = 1 \checkmark
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Position Vector $\vec{OP}$**

$$
\vec{OP} = 6\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + 3\hat{k} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Distance**

$$
d = \sqrt{6^2 + 2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{36 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{49} = 7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Unit Vector**

$$
\hat{u}_{OP} = \frac{6}{7}\hat{i} + \frac{2}{7}\hat{j} + \frac{3}{7}\hat{k}
$$

**Step 4: Force Components**

$$
F_x = 250 \times \frac{6}{7} = \frac{1500}{7} \approx 214.29 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_y = 250 \times \frac{2}{7} = \frac{500}{7} \approx 71.43 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_z = 250 \times \frac{3}{7} = \frac{750}{7} \approx 107.14 \text{ N}
$$

> **Marking scheme (a):** Magnitude – 1.5 | Direction cosines – 2 | Angles – 2 | Verification – 1.5
> **Marking scheme (b):** Position vector – 1.5 | Distance & unit vector – 2 | Components – 3 | Units & final values – 0.5

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors:** Forgetting that $\cos 120° = -0.5$ (not +0.5) costs full marks. Always check which quadrant the force lies in.
> 2. **Unit Vector Confusion:** Writing $\hat{u} = (6, 2, 3)$ instead of $(6/7, 2/7, 3/7)$ is a classic mistake.
> 3. **Identity Verification:** Examiners **love** asking to verify $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$. Skipping this step costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Angle Convention:** Always specify whether the angle is from X-axis, Y-axis, or Z-axis. Ambiguity = lost marks.
> 5. **Rounding Errors:** Keep at least 4 decimal places during intermediate steps to avoid final answer mismatch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Force Vector:** A vector quantity with magnitude, direction, and point of application (unit: N).
- **2D Resolution:** $F_x = F\cos\theta$, $F_y = F\sin\theta$ where θ is measured from +X axis (CCW positive).
- **3D Resolution:** $F_x = F\cos\alpha$, $F_y = F\cos\beta$, $F_z = F\cos\gamma$ where α, β, γ are angles with X, Y, Z axes.
- **Direction Cosine Identity:** $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$ — **memorize verbatim**; it appears in almost every 3D problem.
- **Magnitude Recovery:** $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$ (2D) and $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ (3D).
- **Point-to-Point Method:** When force is directed from A(x₁,y₁,z₁) to B(x₂,y₂,z₂), use $\vec{F} = F \cdot \vec{AB} / |\vec{AB}|$.
- **Sign Convention:** +X/+Y/+Z components are positive; -X/-Y/-Z are negative.
- **Unit Vector:** $\hat{u}_F = \cos\alpha \hat{i} + \cos\beta \hat{j} + \cos\gamma \hat{k}$.
- **Weight Relation:** $W = mg$ with **g = 9.81 m/s²** (standard gravity).
- **Quick Check:** Always verify that $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ returns the original magnitude; this catches arithmetic errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moment and Couple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Moment and Couple

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Moment of a Force (Torque)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **moment of a force** about a point is the turning effect produced by the force about that point. Mathematically, it is defined as the **vector (or cross) product** of the position vector $\vec{r}$ drawn from the moment center to any point on the line of action of the force, and the force vector $\vec{F}$.

$$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

The **scalar magnitude** of the moment is the product of the force magnitude and the **perpendicular (moment arm)** distance from the moment center to the line of action of the force.

$$M_O = F \cdot d_{\perp}$$

where $d_{\perp}$ is the perpendicular distance. The standard SI unit is **N·m** (Newton-meter), and its dimensional formula is $\mathbf{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Moment is a **free vector** (when considered in 2D, its line of action is fixed, but its point of application can be shifted along that line without changing its effect on a rigid body). Moment is **NOT** the same as Work, even though both have units of N·m. Work is a **scalar** in Joules, whereas Moment is a **vector** (or signed scalar in 2D).

### 1.2 Couple

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **couple** consists of **two equal, opposite, and parallel forces** whose lines of action are **not coincident**. The two forces form a rotational couple but produce **no net translational force** on the rigid body.

$$C = F \cdot a$$
