> 2. **Drawing a UDL on the FBD instead of replacing it by its equivalent point load.** Both representations are mathematically valid, but if the algebra uses the equivalent point load, the FBD should show it to avoid confusion. KTU examiners reward explicit diagrams.
> 3. **Adding an extra horizontal reaction** at a roller that is on a horizontal surface. A roller on a horizontal plane provides *only* a vertical reaction.
> 4. **Choosing a moment centre that does not eliminate any unknowns** (e.g., taking moments about the midspan of a simply supported beam). The strategic choice is *always* a point where at least one unknown force passes through, so that unknown drops out of the equation.
> 5. **Sign errors in moment calculations.** A clockwise couple is *negative* in the CCW-positive convention. Drawing the couple arrow on the FBD with the correct rotational sense is the only reliable way to avoid sign mistakes.
> 6. **Using the perpendicular distance incorrectly** when a force acts at an angle. The moment arm is the *perpendicular* distance from the moment centre to the *line of action* of the force — not the distance to the point of application measured along the beam.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Equilibrium** of a planar rigid body requires *both* $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$ and $\sum M_O = 0$ for *any* choice of $O$.
- A **Free-Body Diagram** is the *graphical statement* of the problem; the equations are the *algebraic* statement. Always draw the FBD *first*.
- **Isolate** the chosen body by mentally severing every contact and replacing each contact by the force (or couple) the *removed* body exerts on the *retained* body.
- **Supports — must memorise:**
  - *Roller* → 1 reaction (normal to surface).
  - *Pin/Hinge* → 2 reactions ($H$ and $V$).
  - *Fixed/Built-in* → 3 reactions ($H$, $V$, and a couple $M$).
  - *Cable/String* → 1 tension (along the cable, away from the body).
- **UDL of intensity $w$ over length $L$** is equivalent to a point load $W = wL$ at the **centroid** of the loaded region (midspan if uniform).
- **The moment reference point is arbitrary** — pick it to eliminate unknowns.
- **Always assume positive directions** for unknown forces; a negative algebraic answer is meaningful and indicates that the true direction is opposite to the assumed one. Do *not* force the answer to be positive.
- **Always include the weight** $W = mg$ (with **g = 9.81 m/s²**) on the FBD of any body that is not explicitly weightless.
- **Verify** every solution by checking equilibrium using a *different* moment centre. Consistency across all three equations is the only proof of correctness.
- **Statically determinate** in 2-D means: number of unknown reaction components $\le 3$ and the system has 3 independent equations. If the unknowns exceed 3, the problem is **statically indeterminate** and requires compatibility equations beyond Module 1.
- The FBD is the *single most-marked* step in every KTU Board problem on this topic. A clean, complete, correctly-labelled FBD typically earns 2 to 3 marks before a single equation is written.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Conditions in 2D and 3D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Conditions in 2D and 3D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Equilibrium (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Static Equilibrium** of a rigid body is defined as the state in which the body is either at rest (with respect to an inertial reference frame) or moving with constant velocity (uniform translation), without any angular acceleration.

Mathematically, a rigid body is in equilibrium when the resultant force and the resultant moment acting on it are both **zero simultaneously**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> A particle or rigid body is said to be in equilibrium if the **net force** acting on it is zero (translational equilibrium) **AND** the **net moment** about any arbitrary point is zero (rotational equilibrium). The body is then either completely at rest or moves with constant velocity in a straight line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **book resting on a flat table**.

- The downward pull of gravity (**weight = mg**) tries to push the book through the table.
- The table pushes back upward with an equal and opposite **normal reaction (N)**.
- Since these two forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, they **cancel out**. The book does not accelerate downward or upward.
- Also, the book is not rotating or spinning — its net moment is zero.

> **Net Force = 0** → no translation.
> **Net Moment = 0** → no rotation.
> Together, these two conditions guarantee the book stays perfectly still.

Now imagine a **ceiling fan switched OFF**, hanging still from the rod. The weight pulls it down, the rod's tension pulls it up — both are equal. No rotation occurs. That is static equilibrium.

In **3D**, think of a **helicopter hovering motionless in mid-air**. The lift equals the weight, and the side-thrusts from the tail rotor balance the torque from the main rotor. Six equations (three forces, three moments) hold simultaneously.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of Equilibrium (Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium requires BOTH conditions — NEVER just one:**
> 1. **Translational Equilibrium:** $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$
> 2. **Rotational Equilibrium:** $\sum \vec{M} = \vec{0}$
>
> If only translational equilibrium is satisfied, the body may still **spin** in place (e.g., a body with a pure couple). If only rotational equilibrium is satisfied, the body may still **drift** linearly (e.g., a body with unbalanced net force).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Units (KTU Standard)

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Standard Notation |
|----------|--------|---------|-------------------|
| Force | F or P | Newton (N) | 1 N = 1 kg·m/s² |
| Moment | M | Newton-metre (N·m) | Not Joule! |
| Mass | m | kilogram (kg) | — |
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | 9.81 m/s² | Approx. 9.81 m/s² |
| Angle | θ | radian (rad) or degree (°) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Pitfall:** KTU examiners frequently deduct marks when students write moment in "Joules" instead of "N·m". Although both have the same base units (kg·m²/s²), the convention is **N·m for moment** and **J for energy/work**.

## 1.5 Types of Equilibrium (Syllabus Highlight)

1. **Static Equilibrium** — Body is completely at rest ($v = 0$).
2. **Dynamic (Kinematic) Equilibrium** — Body moves with constant linear velocity and constant angular velocity (no acceleration). Newton's Second Law reduces to the same equations.

Both cases satisfy $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$ and $\sum \vec{M} = \vec{0}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Laws as the Foundation

The equilibrium conditions are **direct corollaries** of Newton's First and Second Laws.

**Newton's Second Law:**

$$\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$$

**Newton's First Law (Special Case):** When acceleration $\vec{a} = \vec{0}$:

$$\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$$

**Rotational Equivalent of Newton's Second Law:**

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = I_O \vec{\alpha}$$

**When angular acceleration** $\vec{\alpha} = \vec{0}$:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The moment equation $\sum \vec{M} = \vec{0}$ holds about **ANY arbitrary point** O, provided the body is in equilibrium. This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU exams.

## 2.2 Equilibrium Conditions in Two Dimensions (2D / Coplanar Force System)

In 2D problems, all forces lie in a single plane (typically the **xy-plane**). The body can have at most **3 degrees of freedom in translation** and **1 degree of freedom in rotation** about an axis perpendicular to that plane. Therefore, we get **3 scalar equilibrium equations**.

### 2.2.1 The 2D Equilibrium Equations (Cardinal Equations of 2D Statics)

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \text{(Sum of forces in x-direction = 0)}$$

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \text{(Sum of forces in y-direction = 0)}$$

$$\sum M_O = 0 \quad \text{(Sum of moments about ANY point O = 0)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why exactly 3 equations?**
> Because a 2D rigid body has **3 independent equilibrium equations** — two force components and one moment component. This means we can solve for **at most 3 unknown reactions** in a single 2D problem. If unknowns exceed 3, the structure is **statically indeterminate** (requires compatibility equations from Mechanics of Solids / Structural Analysis).

### 2.2.2 Logical Steps for Solving 2D Equilibrium Problems

1. **Identify the body** to be analyzed.
2. **Draw a Free Body Diagram (FBD)** — isolate the body and show **all** external forces and couples acting on it (including reactions, weights, applied loads, friction, etc.).
3. **Choose a convenient moment center O** — usually at the intersection of two unknown forces (this eliminates those unknowns from the moment equation).
4. **Resolve all inclined forces** into x and y components using trigonometry.
5. **Apply the three equilibrium equations** and solve for unknowns.
6. **Verify** the results by substituting back.

## 2.3 Equilibrium Conditions in Three Dimensions (3D / Spatial Force System)

In 3D, forces can act in any arbitrary direction in space. The body has **3 translational** and **3 rotational** degrees of freedom, giving **6 scalar equilibrium equations**.

### 2.3.1 The 3D Equilibrium Equations (Cardinal Equations of 3D Statics)

Force equations (translation along x, y, z axes):

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum F_z = 0$$

Moment equations (rotation about x, y, z axes):

$$\sum M_x = 0, \quad \sum M_y = 0, \sum M_z = 0$$

### 2.3.2 Vector Form of the 3D Equilibrium Equations

$$\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum F_x \hat{i} + \sum F_y \hat{j} + \sum F_z \hat{k} = \vec{0}$$

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum M_x \hat{i} + \sum M_y \hat{j} + \sum M_z \hat{k} = \vec{0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Condition (KTU Board Statement):**
> A rigid body is in equilibrium under a system of forces in 3D if and only if the **resultant force** is zero AND the **resultant moment** about any point is zero. The 6 scalar equations are necessary and sufficient for equilibrium of a single rigid body.

### 2.3.3 Logical Steps for Solving 3D Equilibrium Problems

1. **Draw a clear 3D Free Body Diagram** showing all forces with their tail/head at the correct application points.
2. **Express position vectors** $\vec{r}$ from moment center O to each force's point of application.
3. **Compute each moment** using the **vector cross product**: $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$.
4. **Apply the 6 scalar equations** in component form.
5. **Solve the resulting system** of linear equations for the unknowns.

## 2.4 Support Reactions and Their 2D vs 3D Count

| Support Type | 2D Reactions | 3D Reactions |
|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| Smooth / Frictionless Surface | 1 (Normal N) | 1 (Normal N) |
| Roller | 1 (Normal N) | 1 (Normal N) |
| Pin / Hinge | 2 ($A_x$, $A_y$) | — |
| Fixed Hinge in 3D | — | 3 ($A_x$, $A_y$, $A_z$) |
| Fixed Support (Cantilever) | 3 ($A_x$, $A_y$, $M_A$) | 6 ($A_x$, $A_y$, $A_z$, $M_x$, $M_y$, $M_z$) |
| Two-force member | 1 axial force | 1 axial force |
| Ball-and-socket joint | — | 3 ($A_x$, $A_y$, $A_z$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statical Determinacy Rule:**
> - In 2D: Number of unknown reactions $\le 3$ → **Statically Determinate**.
> - In 3D: Number of unknown reactions $\le 6$ → **Statically Determinate**.
> - If unknowns > equations → **Statically Indeterminate** (use deformation compatibility, not statics alone).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | 2D/3D force balance along x |
| 2 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | 2D/3D force balance along y |
| 3 | $\sum F_z = 0$ | 3D force balance along z |
| 4 | $\sum M_O = 0$ | 2D moment balance (about any O) |
| 5 | $\sum M_x = \sum M_y = \sum M_z = 0$ | 3D moment balance (about coordinate axes) |
| 6 | $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | Moment of force F about point O (3D) |
| 7 | $\vert \vec{M} \vert = r F \sin\theta$ | Magnitude of moment |
| 8 | $M = F \cdot d$ | Moment = Force × perpendicular distance (2D) |
| 9 | $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$ | Resolution of 3D force |
| 10 | $\hat{u}_F = \cos\alpha\,\hat{i} + \cos\beta\,\hat{j} + \cos\gamma\,\hat{k}$ | Unit vector along force (direction cosines) |
| 11 | $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$ | Direction cosine identity |
| 12 | $W = mg$ | Weight of body (m = mass, g = 9.81 m/s²) |
| 13 | Varignon's Theorem: $\vec{M}_O = \sum \vec{r}_i \times \vec{F}_i$ | Moment of a couple is independent of reference point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Varignon's Theorem** is **HIGH-FREQUENCY** in KTU papers. The theorem states that the moment of a force about a point equals the sum of moments of its components about the same point. It is the algebraic backbone of moment computation in statics.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Use of Equilibrium Conditions |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Structural Engineering** | Computing reactions at supports of beams, trusses, frames |
| **Machine Design** | Analyzing forces on linkages, levers, gears, cams |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Flight vehicle trim analysis — net force & moment on aircraft must vanish for steady flight |
| **Civil — Bridges** | Cable-stayed and suspension bridge cable force analysis |
| **Robotics** | Static torque computation in robotic arm joints |
| **Geotechnical** | Retaining wall and earth pressure stability |
| **Mechanical — Cranes** | Lifting capacity, tipping analysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Equilibrium Equations from Newton's Laws

### 3.1.1 Translational Equilibrium (Force Equation)

Consider a rigid body of mass $m$ acted upon by a system of external forces $\vec{F}_1, \vec{F}_2, \dots, \vec{F}_n$.

**Step 1:** By **Newton's Second Law**, the net external force equals the rate of change of linear momentum. For a rigid body (constant mass), this becomes:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_i = m \vec{a}_G$$

where $\vec{a}_G$ is the acceleration of the **center of mass** $G$.

**Step 2:** For the body to be in equilibrium (static or moving with constant velocity), the acceleration of every particle, including the center of mass, must be zero:

$$\vec{a}_G = \vec{0}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\vec{a}_G = \vec{0}$ into Newton's Second Law:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_i = m \cdot \vec{0} = \vec{0}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the **translational equilibrium condition** is:

$$\boxed{\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}}$$

**Step 5:** In scalar component form (3D):

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum F_z = 0$$

In 2D, only the first two apply.

### 3.1.2 Rotational Equilibrium (Moment Equation)

**Step 1:** By the **moment form of Newton's Second Law** (also called the **Euler equation** in rigid body dynamics):

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = I_O \vec{\alpha}$$

where $I_O$ is the moment of inertia about the reference point O and $\vec{\alpha}$ is the angular acceleration of the body.

**Step 2:** For equilibrium (no rotation or constant angular velocity), the angular acceleration must vanish:

$$\vec{\alpha} = \vec{0}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\vec{\alpha} = \vec{0}$:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = I_O \cdot \vec{0} = \vec{0}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the **rotational equilibrium condition** is:

$$\boxed{\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}}$$

**Step 5:** In scalar component form (3D), about a generic point O:

$$\sum M_x = 0, \quad \sum M_y = 0, \quad \sum M_z = 0$$

In 2D, the body can only rotate about an axis perpendicular to the plane (the z-axis), so we have just one moment equation:

$$\sum M_z = 0$$

## 3.2 Why the Moment Equation Holds for **Any** Point O

This is a very common KTU question. Let us prove this rigorously.

**Given:** A rigid body is in equilibrium, so $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$.

**Step 1:** Consider the moment of the resultant force system about two different points O and O':

$$\vec{M}_{O'} = \vec{M}_O + \vec{r}_{O'O} \times \vec{R}$$

where $\vec{R} = \sum \vec{F}_i$ is the resultant force, and $\vec{r}_{O'O}$ is the position vector from O' to O.

**Step 2:** Substitute $\vec{R} = \vec{0}$ (from translational equilibrium):

$$\vec{M}_{O'} = \vec{M}_O + \vec{r}_{O'O} \times \vec{0}$$

**Step 3:** The cross product of any vector with the zero vector is zero:

$$\vec{M}_{O'} = \vec{M}_O + \vec{0}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\vec{M}_{O'} = \vec{M}_O$$

**Conclusion:** The moment is **invariant** with respect to the choice of reference point when the force system is equivalent to zero (or a couple). Hence, in equilibrium, if $\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$ for one point O, it is **automatically zero for every other point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This invariance property is what allows us to write the moment equation about **any convenient point** to eliminate unwanted unknowns. Most KTU problems are designed with a strategic moment center where maximum unknowns vanish from the moment equation.

## 3.3 Vector Cross-Product Expansion for 3D Moment

Given a force $\vec{F} = F_x \hat{i} + F_y \hat{j} + F_z \hat{k}$ applied at a point with position vector $\vec{r} = x \hat{i} + y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}$ relative to O, the moment is:

$$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ x & y & z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$\vec{M}_O = (y F_z - z F_y)\hat{i} - (x F_z - z F_x)\hat{j} + (x F_y - y F_x)\hat{k}$$

Therefore, the three moment components are:

$$M_x = y F_z - z F_y$$

$$M_y = z F_x - x F_z$$

$$M_z = x F_y - y F_x$$

## 3.4 Solved Analytical Example — 2D Equilibrium (Worked Model)

**Problem:** A horizontal beam AB of length 6 m is simply supported at A (pin) and B (roller). A vertical point load of **20 kN** acts at the midspan (3 m from A). Determine all support reactions. Take g = 9.81 m/s². Neglect the beam's self-weight.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Draw the FBD.**

The beam is subjected to:
- Reaction at A (pin): $A_x$ (horizontal), $A_y$ (vertical)
- Reaction at B (roller): $B_y$ (vertical only)
- Applied load: $P = 20$ kN downward at midspan

**Step 2: Apply $\sum F_x = 0$.**

$$\sum F_x = A_x = 0$$

There are no horizontal applied forces, so $A_x = 0$.

**Step 3: Apply $\sum M_A = 0$ (taking moments about A to eliminate $A_x$ and $A_y$).**

The moment of a force about A = Force × perpendicular distance, with sign convention (CCW positive).

$$\sum M_A = B_y \cdot 6 - 20 \cdot 3 = 0$$

$$6 B_y = 60$$

$$B_y = 10 \text{ kN} \quad (\uparrow)$$

**Step 4: Apply $\sum F_y = 0$.**

$$\sum F_y = A_y + B_y - 20 = 0$$

$$A_y + 10 - 20 = 0$$

$$A_y = 10 \text{ kN} \quad (\uparrow)$$

**Final Reactions:**
- $A_x = 0$ kN
- $A_y = 10$ kN $\uparrow$
- $B_y = 10$ kN $\uparrow$

**Verification:** Total upward force $= 10 + 10 = 20$ kN = Total downward force. ✓

## 3.5 Solved Analytical Example — 3D Equilibrium (Worked Model)

**Problem:** A horizontal rigid bar AB is supported at A by a **ball-and-socket joint** and at B by **two cables BC and BD**. The bar has length 3 m along the x-axis. Coordinates:
- A = (0, 0, 0) m
- B = (3, 0, 0) m
- C = (3, 4, 0) m
- D = (3, 0, 4) m

A vertical load **P = 500 N** acts at point E = (1.5, 0, 0) m downward (-z direction). Determine the tensions in the two cables and the reactions at A.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: FBD and force components.**

- Reactions at A: $A_x$, $A_y$, $A_z$ (3 unknowns)
- Cable BC tension: $T_{BC}$ along the vector from B to C
- Cable BD tension: $T_{BD}$ along the vector from B to D
- Applied load: $P = -500\,\hat{k}$ N at E

**Step 2: Unit vector from B to C.**

$$\vec{r}_{BC} = C - B = (0, 4, 0) \text{ m}, \quad \vert \vec{r}_{BC} \vert = 4 \text{ m}$$

$$\hat{u}_{BC} = (0, 1, 0)$$

**Step 3: Unit vector from B to D.**

$$\vec{r}_{BD} = D - B = (0, 0, 4) \text{ m}, \quad \vert \vec{r}_{BD} \vert = 4 \text{ m}$$

$$\hat{u}_{BD} = (0, 0, 1)$$

**Step 4: Express cable forces in Cartesian form.**

$$\vec{T}_{BC} = T_{BC} \cdot (0, 1, 0) = (0, T_{BC}, 0) \text{ N}$$

$$\vec{T}_{BD} = T_{BD} \cdot (0, 0, 1) = (0, 0, T_{BD}) \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Apply $\sum F_x = 0$.**

$$A_x = 0$$

**Step 6: Apply $\sum F_z = 0$.**

$$A_z + T_{BD} - 500 = 0$$

$$A_z = 500 - T_{BD} \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 7: Apply $\sum F_y = 0$.**

$$A_y + T_{BC} = 0$$

$$A_y = -T_{BC} \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 8: Apply $\sum M_x = 0$ (moments about x-axis through A).**

Moments of forces about the x-axis come from y and z components of position vectors.

For load P at E = (1.5, 0, 0): only the z-component of force matters; $y_E = 0$, so $M_x$ from P = 0.

For $\vec{T}_{BC}$ at B = (3, 0, 0): force is $(0, T_{BC}, 0)$, position from A is (3, 0, 0).

$$M_x = y_B \cdot F_{z,BC} - z_B \cdot F_{y,BC} = 0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot T_{BC} = 0$$

No contribution.

For $\vec{T}_{BD}$ at B = (3, 0, 0): force is $(0, 0, T_{BD})$, position from A is (3, 0, 0).

$$M_x = y_B \cdot F_{z,BD} - z_B \cdot F_{y,BD} = 0 \cdot T_{BD} - 0 \cdot 0 = 0$$

So $\sum M_x = 0$ gives **no new information** (which is consistent with the chosen geometry).

**Step 9: Apply $\sum M_y = 0$ (moments about y-axis through A).**

The moment about the y-axis uses $M_y = z F_x - x F_z$.

For load P at E = (1.5, 0, 0): $F_z = -500$ N, $F_x = 0$.

$$M_y = z_E F_x - x_E F_z = 0 \cdot 0 - 1.5 \cdot (-500) = 750 \text{ N·m}$$

For $\vec{T}_{BC}$ at B = (3, 0, 0): $F_x = 0, F_z = 0$, so $M_y = 0$.

For $\vec{T}_{BD}$ at B = (3, 0, 0): $F_x = 0, F_z = T_{BD}$.

$$M_y = z_B F_x - x_B F_z = 0 - 3 T_{BD} = -3 T_{BD}$$

Setting $\sum M_y = 0$:

$$750 - 3 T_{BD} = 0$$

$$T_{BD} = 250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 10: Apply $\sum M_z = 0$ (moments about z-axis through A).**

The moment about the z-axis uses $M_z = x F_y - y F_x$.

For load P at E = (1.5, 0, 0): $F_x = 0, F_y = 0$, so $M_z = 0$.

For $\vec{T}_{BC}$ at B = (3, 0, 0): $F_x = 0, F_y = T_{BC}$.

$$M_z = x_B F_y - y_B F_x = 3 T_{BC} - 0 = 3 T_{BC}$$

For $\vec{T}_{BD}$ at B = (3, 0, 0): $F_x = 0, F_y = 0$, so $M_z = 0$.

Setting $\sum M_z = 0$:

$$3 T_{BC} = 0$$

$$T_{BC} = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 11: Solve for A reactions from (1) and (2).**

$$A_z = 500 - 250 = 250 \text{ N} \quad (\uparrow)$$

$$A_y = -0 = 0 \text{ N}$$

$$A_x = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answer:**
- $T_{BC} = 0$ N (cable BC carries no load)
- $T_{BD} = 250$ N
- $A_x = 0, A_y = 0, A_z = 250$ N

**Physical Interpretation:** Since the load P acts vertically downward, only the cable BD (which has a vertical component) takes the load. Cable BC remains slack (tension zero).

## 3.6 Summary of the 6 Equations in 3D Equilibrium

| Equation | Symbol | What it controls |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | Translation along x |
| 2 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | Translation along y |
| 3 | $\sum F_z = 0$ | Translation along z |
| 4 | $\sum M_x = 0$ | Rotation about x-axis |
| 5 | $\sum M_y = 0$ | Rotation about y-axis |
| 6 | $\sum M_z = 0$ | Rotation about z-axis |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Strategy for Solving Equilibrium Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Read problem statement"] --> B["Identify the body in equilibrium"]
    B --> C["Is the problem 2D or 3D?"]
    C --> D1["2D Path"]
    C --> D2["3D Path"]
    D1 --> E1["Draw Free Body Diagram FBD"]
    D2 --> E2["Draw 3D FBD with all forces"]
    E1 --> F1["Resolve inclined forces into x and y components"]
    E2 --> F2["Express each force in Cartesian form Fx, Fy, Fz"]
    F1 --> G1["Count unknowns"]
    F2 --> G2["Count unknowns"]
    G1 --> H1{"Unknowns equal to 3?"}
    G2 --> H2{"Unknowns equal to 6?"}
    H1 --> I1["Yes - Statically Determinate"]
    H1 --> I1No["No - Indeterminate or under-constrained"]
    H2 --> I2["Yes - Statically Determinate"]
    H2 --> I2No["No - Indeterminate or under-constrained"]
    I1 --> J1["Choose strategic moment center to eliminate unknowns"]
    I2 --> J2["Choose strategic axis or point for moment equations"]
    J1 --> K1["Apply sum Fx=0, sum Fy=0, sum M=0"]
    J2 --> K2["Apply 6 equations: sum Fx=Fy=Fz=0 and sum Mx=My=Mz=0"]
    K1 --> L1["Solve the 3 linear equations for unknowns"]
    K2 --> L2["Solve the 6 linear equations for unknowns"]
    L1 --> M1["Verify by checking force balance and a redundant moment"]
    L2 --> M2["Verify by checking force balance and a redundant moment"]
    M1 --> N1["END: Final reactions in N or kN with direction"]
    M2 --> N2["END: Final reactions in N or kN with direction"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: 2D vs 3D Equilibrium System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TWO_D["2D EQUILIBRIUM SYSTEM"]
        A1["Force along x-axis: sum Fx = 0"] --> C["Apply Equilibrium Conditions"]
        A2["Force along y-axis: sum Fy = 0"] --> C
        A3["Moment about perpendicular axis: sum Mz = 0"] --> C
    end
    subgraph THREE_D["3D EQUILIBRIUM SYSTEM"]
        B1["Force x: sum Fx = 0"] --> D["Apply 6 Equilibrium Conditions"]
        B2["Force y: sum Fy = 0"] --> D
        B3["Force z: sum Fz = 0"] --> D
        B4["Moment x: sum Mx = 0"] --> D
        B5["Moment y: sum My = 0"] --> D
        B6["Moment z: sum Mz = 0"] --> D
    end
    C --> OUTPUT1["Output: 3 Unknown Reactions in 2D"]
    D --> OUTPUT2["Output: Up to 6 Unknown Reactions in 3D"]
```

```
            APPLIED LOAD P
                 |
                 v
                 |
    A____________B____________C
    |          
    |          
    v          
   FIXED      
  SUPPORT    
  (Wall)     
```

**Reactions at fixed support A (2D):**
- $A_x$ — horizontal reaction
- $A_y$ — vertical reaction
- $M_A$ — reactive moment (couple)

These 3 reactions correspond to the 3 equilibrium equations available in 2D.

## 4.4 Free Body Diagram: 3D Ball-and-Socket System (Schematic Description)

```
                 Cable T_BC
                   \
                    \
                     B(3,0,0)------- Bar AB along x-axis
                     |  \
                     |   \ Cable T_BD
                     |    \
                     |     D
                     A(0,0,0)  Ball-and-socket joint
                     ^
                     |
                  Support
```

**Reactions at A (3D):** $A_x, A_y, A_z$ (3 components, no moment)
**Cable forces:** $T_{BC}, T_{BD}$ (2 axial tensions)
**Applied load:** P = 500 N at E (vertical, downward)

**Total unknowns = 5**, which is less than 6, so the system is **statically determinate**.

## 4.5 Moment Computation Visual Aid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph VECTOR_OPS["Vector Operations for Moment"]
        V1["Position vector r from O to point of application"]
        V2["Force vector F in Cartesian form"]
        V1 --> V3["Cross product: M = r cross F"]
        V2 --> V3
        V3 --> V4["Result: Moment vector with components Mx, My, Mz"]
        V4 --> V5["Apply sum Mx = 0, sum My = 0, sum Mz = 0"]
    end
```

## 4.6 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Force system given?"] --> Q2["All forces in one plane?"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Yes - Use 2D Equations 3 total"]
    Q2 --> Q4["No - Use 3D Equations 6 total"]
    Q3 --> R1["Set up FBD in xy-plane"]
    R1 --> R2["Choose moment center at intersection of 2 unknowns"]
    R2 --> R3["Apply sum Fx=0, sum Fy=0, sum M=0"]
    Q4 --> R4["Express all forces in Cartesian form"]
    R4 --> R5["Choose moment center O usually at a support"]
    R5 --> R6["Apply 6 scalar equations in components"]
    R3 --> FINAL["Solve and verify"]
    R6 --> FINAL
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the necessary and sufficient conditions for equilibrium of a rigid body in three dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**

A rigid body is in equilibrium under a system of forces in 3D space if and only if the following six scalar conditions are satisfied simultaneously:

**Translational Equilibrium (3 conditions):**
- $\sum F_x = 0$
- $\sum F_y = 0$
- $\sum F_z = 0$

**Rotational Equilibrium (3 conditions):**
- $\sum M_x = 0$
- $\sum M_y = 0$
- $\sum M_z = 0$

Equivalently, in vector form:
- $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$
- $\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$ (about any arbitrary point O)

These six conditions are **necessary and sufficient** because a 3D rigid body has exactly 3 translational and 3 rotational degrees of freedom, and each condition eliminates one degree of freedom.

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain why the moment equilibrium equation $\sum M_O = 0$ can be written about any arbitrary point O in a 2D equilibrium problem.**

**Model Answer:**

This is a direct consequence of the **invariance of the moment** property of an equivalent force system. Mathematically, the moment of a force system about two different points O and O' is related by:

$$\vec{M}_{O'} = \vec{M}_O + \vec{r}_{O'O} \times \vec{R}$$

where $\vec{R} = \sum \vec{F}_i$ is the resultant force. If the body is in translational equilibrium, $\vec{R} = \vec{0}$, and therefore:

$$\vec{M}_{O'} = \vec{M}_O + \vec{r}_{O'O} \times \vec{0} = \vec{M}_O + \vec{0} = \vec{M}_O$$

Hence, if $\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$ for one point, it is automatically zero for every other point. This property is known as the **theorem of moments**, derived from the principle of transmissibility and the definition of an equivalent force system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A horizontal beam **AB** of length **8 m** is supported by a **pin support at A** and a **roller support at B**. A uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity **w = 6 kN/m** acts over the entire span. Additionally, a concentrated load **P = 10 kN** acts at a distance of **2 m from A**. Determine the support reactions at A and B.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the Free Body Diagram and list all the equilibrium equations applicable.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Solve for the support reactions at A and B.

**Model Solution:**

**Sub-part (a): FBD and Equilibrium Equations**

The Free Body Diagram of the beam shows:
- Pin support at A: reactions $A_x$ (horizontal) and $A_y$ (vertical)
- Roller support at B: reaction $B_y$ (vertical only)
- UDL of intensity $w = 6$ kN/m over the entire 8 m length
- Concentrated load $P = 10$ kN at 2 m from A (downward)

The UDL can be replaced by an equivalent point load at the centroid of the UDL (at the midspan, 4 m from A):

$$W_{UDL} = w \times L = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ kN}$$

**Equilibrium equations to apply (2D, 3 equations):**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \cdots (i)$$

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \cdots (ii)$$

$$\sum M_A = 0 \quad \cdots (iii)$$

**Sub-part (b): Solving for Reactions**

**Step 1:** Apply $\sum F_x = 0$:

$$A_x = 0 \quad \text{(no horizontal applied load)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply $\sum M_A = 0$ (CCW positive):

The moments are computed as Force × perpendicular distance from A.

$$\sum M_A = B_y \cdot 8 - 10 \cdot 2 - 48 \cdot 4 = 0$$

$$8 B_y = 20 + 192$$

$$8 B_y = 212$$

$$B_y = 26.5 \text{ kN} \quad (\uparrow)$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\sum F_y = 0$:

$$A_y + B_y - 10 - 48 = 0$$

$$A_y + 26.5 = 58$$

$$A_y = 31.5 \text{ kN} \quad (\uparrow)$$

**Final Reactions:**
- $A_x = 0$ kN
- $A_y = 31.5$ kN $\uparrow$
- $B_y = 26.5$ kN $\uparrow$

**Verification:**
Total upward force = $31.5 + 26.5 = 58$ kN
Total downward force = $10 + 48 = 58$ kN ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A **uniform horizontal bar AB** of length **4 m** and weight **200 N** is hinged at end **A** to a vertical wall. The bar is held in equilibrium in a **horizontal position** by a **light inextensible cable** that connects end **B** to a point **C** on the wall **3 m above A**. The weight of the bar acts at its midpoint **G** (2 m from A). Determine the **tension in the cable** and the **reaction at the hinge A**.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the FBD, identify all forces, and resolve the cable tension into horizontal and vertical components.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Apply equilibrium equations and solve for T and the hinge reaction.

**Model Solution:**

**Sub-part (a): FBD and Force Resolution**

The bar AB is horizontal with:
- A at (0, 0)
- B at (4, 0)
- C at (0, 3) on the wall

**Forces acting on the bar:**
- Hinge reaction at A: $A_x$ (horizontal) and $A_y$ (vertical) — 2 unknowns
- Cable tension T at B, directed along BC
- Weight W = 200 N at G = (2, 0), acting vertically downward

**Geometry of the cable BC:**

$$\text{Length of BC} = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (0-3)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$

**Direction cosines of the cable (from B to C):**

$$\vec{r}_{BC} = C - B = (-4, 3) \text{ m}, \quad \vert \vec{r}_{BC} \vert = 5 \text{ m}$$

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{-4}{5} = -0.8 \quad \text{(horizontal component, pointing left)}$$

$$\cos\beta = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \quad \text{(vertical component, pointing up)}$$

**Components of cable tension at B:**

$$T_x = T \cdot \cos\alpha = -0.8 \, T \quad \text{(acts to the left on the bar)}$$

$$T_y = T \cdot \cos\beta = 0.6 \, T \quad \text{(acts upward on the bar)}$$

**Sub-part (b): Apply Equilibrium Equations**

**Step 1:** Apply $\sum M_A = 0$ (CCW positive, eliminates $A_x$ and $A_y$):

Moments about A (using sign convention: CCW positive):

$$\sum M_A = T_y \cdot 4 - W \cdot 2 = 0$$

$$0.6 \, T \cdot 4 - 200 \cdot 2 = 0$$

$$2.4 \, T = 400$$

$$T = \frac{400}{2.4} = 166.67 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Apply $\sum F_x = 0$:

$$A_x + T_x = 0$$

$$A_x - 0.8 \cdot 166.67 = 0$$

$$A_x = 0.8 \cdot 166.67 = 133.33 \text{ N} \quad (\rightarrow)$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\sum F_y = 0$:

$$A_y + T_y - W = 0$$

$$A_y + 0.6 \cdot 166.67 - 200 = 0$$

$$A_y + 100 - 200 = 0$$

$$A_y = 100 \text{ N} \quad (\uparrow)$$

**Final Answer:**
- Cable tension: $T = 166.67$ N
- Hinge reaction: $A_x = 133.33$ N (horizontal, towards the wall in opposite sense)
- Hinge reaction: $A_y = 100$ N (vertical, upward)

**Verification (magnitude of hinge reaction):**

$$R_A = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2} = \sqrt{133.33^2 + 100^2} = \sqrt{17777.78 + 10000} = \sqrt{27777.78} \approx 166.67 \text{ N}$$

The hinge reaction magnitude equals the cable tension (as expected in this symmetric configuration).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students frequently lose 2-3 marks by not following a consistent sign convention (e.g., mixing CW and CCW in moment equations). Always state: "Taking CCW as positive" at the start.
> 2. **Forgetting Direction Components:** Cable tensions MUST be resolved into x and y components. Writing $T$ directly in a force equation without resolving is the #1 mistake.
> 3. **Wrong Direction for Reactions:** Always show the direction of reactions on the FBD using arrows. If the computed value is negative, it means the assumed direction was wrong — reverse the arrow.
> 4. **Confusing 2D with 3D Equations:** In 2D, only **3** equations exist. In 3D, exactly **6** equations exist. Using a 2D equation in a 3D problem (or vice versa) leads to wrong answers.
> 5. **Forgetting the Weight's Location:** The weight of a uniform bar acts at its **geometric center** (midpoint). Many students place it at the wrong location.
> 6. **Units and Decimal Precision:** Always write the unit (N or kN) with the final numerical value. Use 3 significant figures minimum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equilibrium Conditions in 2D and 3D**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Equilibrium** = translational rest/constant velocity + rotational rest/constant velocity.
- ☐ The two cardinal conditions are $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$ AND $\sum \vec{M}_O = \vec{0}$.
- ☐ Both conditions must hold **simultaneously** — satisfying only one is insufficient.

### 2D Equilibrium
- ☐ **3 scalar equations**: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$.
- ☐ Solve for **at most 3 unknown reactions**.
- ☐ Choose moment center at the **intersection of two unknown forces** to eliminate them.
- ☐ Always replace UDL with its equivalent **point load** at the centroid.
- ☐ Varignon's theorem: Moment of a force = sum of moments of its components.

### 3D Equilibrium
- ☐ **6 scalar equations**: 3 force equations ($\sum F_x, F_y, F_z = 0$) + 3 moment equations ($\sum M_x, M_y, M_z = 0$).
- ☐ Solve for **at most 6 unknown reactions**.
- ☐ Use the **vector cross product** $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ for moment about a point.
- ☐ Express forces using **direction cosines**: $\hat{u} = \cos\alpha\,\hat{i} + \cos\beta\,\hat{j} + \cos\gamma\,\hat{k}$.
- ☐ Direction cosine identity: $\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1$.
- ☐ Moment is **invariant** with respect to reference point when the resultant force is zero.

### Support Reactions
- ☐ 2D Pin: 2 reactions ($A_x, A_y$).
- ☐ 2D Roller: 1 reaction ($A_y$).
- ☐ 2D Fixed: 3 reactions ($A_x, A_y, M_A$).
- ☐ 3D Ball-and-socket: 3 reactions ($A_x, A_y, A_z$).
- ☐ 3D Fixed: 6 reactions ($A_x, A_y, A_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$).

### Statical Determinacy
- ☐ **2D:** Unknowns $\le 3$ → Determinate; > 3 → Indeterminate.
- ☐ **3D:** Unknowns $\le 6$ → Determinate; > 6 → Indeterminate.

### Frequently Asked Derivatives
- ☐ Derivation of equilibrium equations from Newton's Second Law.
- ☐ Proof of moment invariance about any point.
- ☐ Vector cross product expansion in determinant form.
- ☐ Replacement of distributed loads with equivalent point loads.

### Common Exam Mnemonics
- ☐ **"FXFY + MOM"** — Force in X, Force in Y, Moment about O (2D).
- ☐ **"F-Force, M-Moment, 3 in 2D, 6 in 3D"**.
- ☐ **"F = ma = 0, M = Iα = 0"** — Newton's Second Law in equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction: Laws of Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Friction: Laws of Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction** is a *tangential resistive force* that automatically develops at the interface between two contacting bodies **opposing the relative motion** (or the *tendency* of relative motion) between them. It arises due to the microscopic interlocking of surface irregularities (asperities) and molecular adhesion.

**Key Terminology (As Per KTU Module 2):**

| Term | Rigorous Definition |
|---|---|
| **Friction Force (F)** | The tangential force generated at the contact surface that opposes impending or actual relative motion. |
| **Normal Reaction (N)** | The perpendicular force exerted by the supporting surface on the body, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the component of the applied load normal to the surface. |
| **Limiting Friction (F_L)** | The **maximum value** of static friction just before the body begins to slide. |
| **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** | A dimensionless scalar that quantifies the ratio of limiting friction to the normal reaction: $\mu = F_L / N$. |
| **Angle of Friction (φ)** | The angle made by the **resultant reaction (R)** with the normal reaction (N) at the point of contact when the body is on the verge of sliding. |
| **Angle of Repose (α)** | The **steepest angle of an inclined plane** with the horizontal at which a body placed on it remains in equilibrium without sliding. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Students must be able to *define, classify, and apply* the laws of friction to determine the force required to initiate or maintain motion of a body on horizontal and inclined surfaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Real World Intuition"

Imagine you are pushing a **heavy wooden almirah** across a tiled floor.
- **You push gently → it does not move.** Why? Because microscopic "teeth" on the bottom of the almirah are **interlocked** with the microscopic bumps on the tiles. The floor pushes back with an equal and opposite static friction force.
- **You push harder and harder → at one point, it suddenly jerks and starts sliding.** That "breaking point" is the **limiting friction**. It is the maximum value static friction can offer.
- **Once it slides, it feels easier.** This is because **kinetic (dynamic) friction is always slightly less** than the limiting static friction.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Friction is the *silent hero* in many applications. Without it, you could not walk, brake a car, or hold a pen. Engineers sometimes **maximize** it (brake pads, shoe soles) and sometimes **minimize** it (engine bearings, skis). The laws of friction let us **predict** and **control** this force mathematically.

## 1.3 Why Friction Exists — Physical Origin

1. **Surface Roughness (Asperities):** Even mirror-polished surfaces have microscopic hills and valleys that interlock.
2. **Molecular Adhesion:** At very close contact, intermolecular forces (van der Waals, electrostatic) bond the surfaces.
3. **Cold Welding:** In metals, contact points can momentarily weld together due to high local pressure.

These mechanisms collectively produce a resistive force whose empirical behavior is captured by **Coulomb's Laws of Friction** (next section).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coulomb's Laws of Friction (1785)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charles-Augustin de Coulomb** experimentally established **four empirical laws** that govern dry (Coulomb) friction. These are foundational to all KTU friction problems.

### **Law 1 — Direction of Friction**
> *"The force of friction always acts in a direction **opposite** to the direction of motion of the body (or its tendency of motion) at the point of contact."*

- **Why it matters:** This sign convention allows us to write equilibrium equations: $\Sigma F_{\text{tangential}} = 0$ with friction as a reactive force.

### **Law 2 — Proportionality to Normal Reaction**
> *"The magnitude of limiting friction ($F_L$) is **directly proportional** to the normal reaction (N) between the two surfaces."*

$$F_L \;\propto\; N \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F_L \;=\; \mu_s \, N$$

where $\mu_s$ is the **coefficient of static friction** (dimensionless).

- **Why it matters:** Doubling the load doubles the friction. This is why heavy trucks need bigger brakes.

### **Law 3 — Independence from Apparent Area of Contact**
> *"The force of friction is **independent** of the apparent area of contact between the surfaces, provided the normal reaction remains constant."*

- **Subtlety:** Apparent (macroscopic) area ≠ real (microscopic) area. The real contact area depends only on the load, not the footprint.

### **Law 4 — Dependence on Surface Nature**
> *"The force of friction depends on the **roughness and material pair** of the two contacting surfaces, characterized by the coefficient of friction $\mu$."*

- **Example:** $\mu$ (rubber on dry concrete) $\approx 0.65$, whereas $\mu$ (Teflon on Teflon) $\approx 0.04$.

### **Additional Empirical Observation (Law 5 — Kinetic vs Static)**
> *"Once motion begins, the **kinetic friction** $F_k$ is **slightly less** than the limiting static friction $F_L$. The body experiences a momentary 'stick-slip' jerk."*

$$F_k \;=\; \mu_k \, N, \qquad \mu_k \;<\; \mu_s$$

## 2.2 Classification of Friction (Three Regimes)

| Regime | Symbol | Coefficient | Value | Physical State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Friction** (self-adjusting) | $F_s$ | — | $0 \le F_s \le F_L$ | Body at rest, $F_s$ increases to match the applied tangential force. |
| **Limiting (Max Static) Friction** | $F_L$ | $\mu_s$ | $F_L = \mu_s N$ | Body is **on the verge** of impending motion. |
| **Kinetic / Dynamic Friction** | $F_k$ | $\mu_k$ | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | Body is in **actual sliding motion**. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table** — it covers 90% of KTU Module 2 friction numerical problems. Memorize the symbols, not just the formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Limiting Friction | $F_L = \mu_s N$ | $F_L \le \mu_s N$ (static) | **N (Newton)** |
| 2 | Kinetic Friction | $F_k = \mu_k N$ | Motion occurring | N |
| 3 | Coefficient of Friction | $\mu = F_L / N$ | $0 < \mu < 1$ (typically) | **Dimensionless** |
| 4 | Angle of Friction | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | $0 \le \phi \le 90°$ | **Radians / Degrees** |
| 5 | Angle of Repose | $\tan \alpha = \mu$ | $\alpha = \phi$ at limiting state | Degrees |
| 6 | Resultant Reaction | $R = \sqrt{N^2 + F^2} = N\sqrt{1+\mu^2}$ | — | N |
| 7 | Horizontal Pull to Just Move | $P = \mu_s W$ (with $N=W$) | — | N |
| 8 | Inclined Pull, Angle $\theta$ to Plane | $P = \dfrac{\mu_s W}{\cos\theta + \mu_s \sin\theta}$ | $\theta$ from horizontal | N |
| 9 | Minimum Pulling Force | $P_{\min} = W \sin\phi$ (along plane) | $\theta = \phi$ above plane | N |
| 10 | Least Force (any direction) | $P_{\min} = \mu_s W / \sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}$ | $\theta = \phi$ with horizontal | N |
| 11 | Self-Weight on Incline (limiting) | $\mu = \tan\alpha = W\sin\alpha \,/\, W\cos\alpha$ | $\alpha \le \phi$ for stability | — |
| 12 | Block on Plane — Impending Slide | $F = W\sin\alpha$, $N = W\cos\alpha$ | Equilibrium at $\alpha = \phi$ | N |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Friction |
|---|---|
| **Vehicle Braking Systems** | Maximize $F_k$ on disc-pad interface; high $\mu$ desired. |
| **Belt Drives & Clutches** | Transmit torque via friction; governed by $F = \mu N$. |
| **Bearing Design** | Minimize $\mu$ to reduce wear, heat, and energy loss. |
| **Soil Mechanics (Geotech)** | Angle of repose of sand/gravel determines slope stability of embankments. |
| **Skiing / Skating** | Low $\mu$ via lubrication/wax to reduce kinetic friction. |
| **Threaded Fasteners** | Friction in threads determines required tightening torque. |

## 2.5 The Concept of "Self-Adjusting" Friction

A **critical conceptual point** that KTU examiners love to test:

> [!TIP]
> Static friction is *not* a fixed value like $\mu N$ — it is a **variable reaction** that takes **whatever value is needed** to prevent motion, **up to a maximum** of $\mu_s N$. It is "passive" — it only opposes, it never assists.

$$F_{\text{static}} = \min(\text{Applied tangential force},\; \mu_s N)$$

This is the **single most common conceptual error** KTU students make: assuming $F = \mu N$ at *all* times. It is only true at the **impending motion** state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Note:** Full marks in derivations are awarded for *every* logical step shown. Skipping the "why" between equations will cost you 1–2 marks. We write out **each step explicitly**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Relationship Between Angle of Friction ($\phi$) and Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$)

**Given:** A block of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal plane. A horizontal force $P$ is applied and increased gradually until the block is on the verge of sliding.

### **Free Body Diagram Setup**

- Weight $W$ acts vertically downward.
- Normal reaction $N$ acts vertically upward.
- Limiting friction $F_L = \mu_s N$ acts horizontally, opposing the impending motion.
- Applied force $P$ acts horizontally in the direction of impending motion.

### **Step-by-Step Equilibrium**

**Step 1: Apply vertical equilibrium.**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; N - W = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{N = W}$$

**Step 2: Apply horizontal equilibrium (limiting case).**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; P - F_L = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{P = F_L = \mu_s N}$$

**Step 3: Determine the Resultant Reaction (R).**

The normal reaction $N$ and friction $F_L$ are **perpendicular** to each other. By the **Pythagorean Theorem**:

$$R \;=\; \sqrt{N^2 + F_L^2} \;=\; \sqrt{N^2 + (\mu_s N)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{N^2(1 + \mu_s^2)} \;=\; N\sqrt{1 + \mu_s^2}$$

**Step 4: Define the Angle of Friction.**

The angle $\phi$ is the angle that the resultant $R$ makes **with the normal** $N$. In the right triangle formed by $N$, $F_L$, and $R$:

$$\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} \;=\; \frac{F_L}{N} \;=\; \frac{\mu_s N}{N} \;=\; \mu_s$$

$$\boxed{\tan \phi \;=\; \mu_s \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \phi \;=\; \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The larger the coefficient of friction, the larger the angle the resultant reaction tilts away from the true normal. The contact surface behaves *as if* it were tilted by $\phi$ — this is the foundation of the "inclined surface replacement" technique used in KTU problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Angle of Repose ($\alpha$) Equals Angle of Friction ($\phi$)

**Given:** A block of weight $W$ rests on an inclined plane making angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. The plane is just rough enough to prevent the block from sliding (limiting equilibrium).

### **Free Body Setup**

Resolve weight $W$ into two components:

- Along the plane (down the slope): $W \sin\alpha$
- Perpendicular to the plane (into the surface): $W \cos\alpha$

Forces on the body:
- Weight $W$ (vertically down)
- Normal $N$ (perpendicular to plane, outward)
- Friction $F$ (up the slope, opposing tendency to slide down)

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1: Perpendicular equilibrium.**

$$\sum F_{\perp} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; N - W\cos\alpha = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{N = W\cos\alpha}$$

**Step 2: Parallel equilibrium (limiting).**

$$\sum F_{\parallel} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; F - W\sin\alpha = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{F = W\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 3: Apply the friction law at the verge of sliding.**

At the verge of sliding (limiting equilibrium):

$$F \;=\; \mu_s N$$

Substitute the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2:

$$W\sin\alpha \;=\; \mu_s \,(W\cos\alpha)$$

**Step 4: Cancel $W$ and simplify.**

$$\mu_s \;=\; \frac{W\sin\alpha}{W\cos\alpha} \;=\; \tan\alpha$$

**Step 5: Combine with Derivation I.**

From Derivation I, we proved $\tan\phi = \mu_s$. Therefore:

$$\tan\alpha \;=\; \mu_s \;=\; \tan\phi \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\alpha \;=\; \phi \;=\; \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Angle of Repose is identical to the Angle of Friction.** This is a high-yield KTU result. It also gives an experimental method to measure $\mu_s$: simply tilt a surface until the block just slides, and measure the inclination angle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Minimum Force to Drag a Block on a Horizontal Surface

**Given:** A block of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal plane. A force $P$ is applied at angle $\theta$ **above** the horizontal. Find the minimum value of $P$ that will just move the block.

### **Setup**

- $P \cos\theta$ — horizontal component (in the direction of motion)
- $P \sin\theta$ — vertical component (upward, reducing the effective normal force)

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Step 1: Vertical equilibrium.**

$$N + P\sin\theta - W = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{N = W - P\sin\theta}$$

**Step 2: Horizontal equilibrium at impending motion.**

$$P\cos\theta - F_L = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; P\cos\theta = \mu_s N$$

**Step 3: Substitute $N$ into the friction equation.**

$$P\cos\theta \;=\; \mu_s(W - P\sin\theta)$$

**Step 4: Distribute and collect terms in $P$.**

$$P\cos\theta + \mu_s P\sin\theta \;=\; \mu_s W$$

$$P(\cos\theta + \mu_s\sin\theta) \;=\; \mu_s W$$

**Step 5: Solve for $P$.**

$$\boxed{P \;=\; \frac{\mu_s W}{\cos\theta + \mu_s\sin\theta}}$$

**Step 6: Find the condition for $P_{\min}$.**

$P$ is **minimum** when the denominator $D = \cos\theta + \mu_s\sin\theta$ is **maximum**.

Set $\dfrac{dD}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$-\sin\theta + \mu_s\cos\theta = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \tan\theta = \mu_s \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta = \phi$$

**Step 7: Evaluate $P_{\min}$ using $\theta = \phi$.**

From Derivation I: $\mu_s = \tan\phi$, with $\cos\phi = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}}$ and $\sin\phi = \dfrac{\mu_s}{\sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}}$.

Substitute into the denominator:

$$D_{\max} = \cos\phi + \mu_s\sin\phi = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}} + \frac{\mu_s^2}{\sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}} = \frac{1+\mu_s^2}{\sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}} = \sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\mu_s W}{\sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}} \;=\; W\sin\phi}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $P_{\min} = W\sin\phi$ — this means the minimum pulling force is **independent of the geometry** and depends only on the weight and the angle of friction. The **angle of application** must equal the angle of friction, $\theta = \phi$, which is a beautiful symmetry.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Force Required to Pull a Block Up an Inclined Plane

**Given:** Block of weight $W$ on an inclined plane of angle $\beta$. A force $P$ is applied parallel to the plane (up the slope). Coefficient of friction $\mu_s$.

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Step 1: Perpendicular equilibrium.**

$$N - W\cos\beta = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; N = W\cos\beta$$

**Step 2: Parallel equilibrium at impending motion (up the slope).**

Friction now acts **down the slope** (opposing the upward pull).

$$P - W\sin\beta - F_L = 0$$

**Step 3: Apply $F_L = \mu_s N$.**

$$P - W\sin\beta - \mu_s(W\cos\beta) = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for $P$.**

$$\boxed{P \;=\; W\sin\beta + \mu_s W\cos\beta \;=\; W(\sin\beta + \mu_s\cos\beta)}$$

**Step 5: Verify the no-motion case.**

If $\mu_s W\cos\beta \ge W\sin\beta$, i.e., $\tan\beta \le \mu_s$, the block **does not slide even without $P$**. This is the self-locking condition.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Type Recognition (KTU 2024 Pattern)

| Problem Type | What is Asked | Key Equations to Use | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal pull to just move** | Minimum $P$ | $P = \mu_s W$ (horizontal) | Forgetting $N = W$ (no vertical component). |
| **Pull at angle on horizontal** | $P_{\min}$, optimal $\theta$ | $P = \mu W / \sqrt{1+\mu^2}$, $\theta = \phi$ | Confusing $\theta$ with horizontal vs plane. |
| **Pull along inclined plane** | $P$ to move up | $P = W(\sin\beta + \mu\cos\beta)$ | Forgetting friction direction reverses when moving **down**. |
| **Pull down an inclined plane** | $P$ to move down | $P = W(\mu\cos\beta - \sin\beta)$ | Sign error in friction component. |
| **Angle of repose** | $\alpha$ at limiting | $\tan\alpha = \mu$ | Mixing up angle of repose vs angle of friction. |
| **Ladder / wedge problems** | Friction force on each contact | Three/four equilibrium equations | Treating all surfaces as smooth or all as rough. |
| **Block on block (stacked)** | Top force to slide top block | Two-block FBD, separate $\mu$ values | Forgetting to check whether blocks move together. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** Friction problems are inherently physical (force arrows on objects). Below, we provide two complementary visual aids: (a) a **Mermaid functional flowchart** mapping the logical relationships and decision trees, and (b) **ASCII schematic representations** of the canonical FBDs that students should draw in their answer scripts.

---

## 4.1 Functional Flowchart — Friction Concept Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Contact Between Two Bodies] --> B{Is there relative motion?}
    B -- No, body at rest --> C[Static Friction Fs]
    B -- Body just about to slide --> D[Limiting Friction FL]
    B -- Body in actual motion --> E[Kinetic Friction Fk]

    C --> C1[Self-adjusting 0 le Fs le FL]
    C1 --> C2[Fs equals applied tangential force]

    D --> D1[FL = mu_s times N]
    D1 --> D2[Angle of Friction phi]
    D2 --> D3[tan phi = mu_s]

    E --> E1[Fk = mu_k times N]
    E1 --> E2[mu_k strictly less than mu_s]

    D1 --> F[Resultant Reaction R]
    E1 --> F
    F --> F1[R = N times sqrt 1 plus mu squared]
    F1 --> F2[Direction: tilted phi from normal]

    D3 --> G[Angle of Repose alpha]
    G --> G1[tan alpha = mu_s]
    G1 --> G2[alpha equals phi at limit]

    C2 --> H[Problem Solving Strategy]
    D1 --> H
    E1 --> H
    F1 --> H
    G1 --> H
    H --> H1[Step 1: Draw FBD]
    H1 --> H2[Step 2: Apply equilibrium]
    H2 --> H3[Step 3: Apply FL = mu N at limit]
    H3 --> H4[Step 4: Solve for unknowns]
```

---

## 4.2 ASCII Schematic — Canonical Free Body Diagrams

### **Diagram A: Block on Horizontal Surface (Pull at Angle θ)**

```
                 P sin θ
                   ↑
                   |
       ┌───────────┼───────────┐
       │           |           │
       │      ●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━● │   ←──── P cos θ  (to the right)
       │     (BLOCK: W,N,F)    │
       └───────────────────────┘
                   |
                   ↓
                  W  (Weight, downward)

  Forces on Block:
  ─────────────────
  • Weight W       : Vertically DOWN
  • Normal N       : Vertically UP
  • Applied P      : At angle θ above horizontal
  • Friction F     : Horizontally LEFT (opposing motion)
  ─────────────────
  Equations:
  ΣFy = 0:  N + P sin θ = W   →   N = W − P sin θ
  ΣFx = 0:  P cos θ = F       →   P cos θ = μ N
```

### **Diagram B: Block on Inclined Plane (Verge of Sliding Down)**

```
                       /|
                      / | N (normal, ⊥ to plane)
                     /  |
                    /   |
                   /    |
                  / F   |  ←── Friction UP the plane
                 /      |
                /       |
               / β (α)  |
              /  ╱      |  W sin α  (along plane, DOWN)
             /  ╱       |
            /  ╱        |
           /──          |  W cos α  (⊥ to plane, INTO surface)
          ─────────────┘
                ↓
                W (Weight, vertical)

  At the verge of sliding DOWN:
  ─────────────────────────────
  ΣF⊥ = 0:   N = W cos α
  ΣF∥ = 0:   F = W sin α
  Limiting:  F = μ N
  ∴  μ = tan α  →  α = φ = tan⁻¹(μ)
```

### **Diagram C: Block on Inclined Plane (Force P Pulling Up, at Angle θ to Plane)**

```
              P
             ↗  θ
            ╱─────────────
           ╱   α (incline)
          ╱───────────────
         ╱ Friction F
        ╱   (down the plane, opposing motion)
       ●━━━━━━━━━━
       Block
              ↓
              W
  Resolution:
  ───────────
  Along plane:  P cos θ  =  W sin α  +  F
  ⊥ to plane:   N  =  W cos α  −  P sin θ
  Limiting:     F  =  μ N
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Problem-Solving Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Problem Statement: Given W, mu, geometry] --> S1[Step 1: Identify all contact surfaces]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Isolate the body and draw FBD]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Resolve all forces into components]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Identify the condition]
    S4 --> S4A[Static - F is unknown and self-adjusting]
    S4 --> S4B[Limiting - F equals mu times N at verge]
    S4 --> S4C[Kinetic - F equals mu_k times N in motion]
    S4A --> S5[Step 5: Write equilibrium equations]
    S4B --> S5
    S4C --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Solve for unknowns]
    S6 --> OUT[Answer: P, F, phi, or alpha]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**State the laws of dry friction (Coulomb's laws of friction).**

#### **Model Answer:**

The following are the laws of dry friction:

1. **Law of Direction:** The force of friction always acts in a direction **opposite** to the direction of motion of the body, or its tendency of motion, at the point of contact.

2. **Law of Magnitude (Proportionality):** The magnitude of the **limiting friction** $F_L$ is directly proportional to the **normal reaction** $N$ between the two surfaces, i.e., $F_L = \mu_s N$.

3. **Law of Independence from Apparent Area:** The force of friction is **independent** of the apparent area of contact between the surfaces, provided the normal reaction remains unchanged.

4. **Law of Surface Dependence:** The force of friction depends upon the **roughness and material** of the two contacting surfaces, characterized quantitatively by the coefficient of friction $\mu$.

5. **Additional Observation:** Once motion begins, the **kinetic friction** is slightly less than the limiting static friction, i.e., $\mu_k < \mu_s$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Define the following terms with neat sketches:**
**(i) Angle of friction  (ii) Angle of repose  (iii) Coefficient of friction**

#### **Model Answer:**

**(i) Angle of Friction ($\phi$):**
It is the angle made by the **resultant reaction** (R) of a surface with the **normal reaction** (N) at the point of contact, when the body is on the verge of sliding. Mathematically, $\tan\phi = \mu_s$.

**(ii) Angle of Repose ($\alpha$):**
It is the **steepest angle** of an inclined plane with the horizontal at which a body placed on the plane remains in **limiting equilibrium** (just about to slide down). Mathematically, $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s) = \phi$.

**(iii) Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$):**
It is the **dimensionless ratio** of the limiting friction force to the corresponding normal reaction between two surfaces: $\mu = F_L / N$. It is a pure number, typically between 0 and 1.

| Property | Static $\mu_s$ | Kinetic $\mu_k$ |
|---|---|---|
| Value | Higher | Slightly lower |
| Condition | Body at rest / on verge of moving | Body in actual motion |
| State | Impending slip | Actual slip |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Module 2 Part B is a **choice-based question**. Attempt **ONE** of the two full questions (Question A **OR** Question B). Each is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice exists only at the **full-question** level, not at the sub-part level.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** A block weighing **800 N** rests on a horizontal plane. The coefficient of static friction is **$\mu_s = 0.30$**.
  (i) Find the horizontal force required to **just move** the block.
  (ii) Find the **minimum force** required to move the block and the **angle** at which it must be applied.  **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A body weighing **2000 N** is placed on an inclined plane that makes an angle of **25°** with the horizontal. The coefficient of friction is **$\mu_s = 0.28$**.
  (i) Determine whether the body will slide down the plane on its own.
  (ii) If not, find the **smallest force** $P$ (acting along the plane) required to **initiate upward motion**.  **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Part A (a):**

**Given:** $W = 800$ N, $\mu_s = 0.30$, horizontal surface.

**(i) Horizontal force to just move the block:**

When the pull is horizontal, the vertical equilibrium gives $N = W = 800$ N.

At the verge of motion:
$$P = \mu_s N = 0.30 \times 800 = \boxed{240 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Minimum force and its angle:**

From Derivation III, the minimum force is:
$$P_{\min} = \frac{\mu_s W}{\sqrt{1+\mu_s^2}} = \frac{0.30 \times 800}{\sqrt{1+0.30^2}} = \frac{240}{\sqrt{1.09}}$$

$$P_{\min} = \frac{240}{1.0440} = \boxed{229.88 \text{ N} \approx 229.9 \text{ N}}$$

The angle at which $P_{\min}$ is applied is the angle of friction:
$$\theta = \phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s) = \tan^{-1}(0.30) = \boxed{16.70°}$$

**Verification using $P_{\min} = W\sin\phi$:** $P_{\min} = 800 \times \sin(16.70°) = 800 \times 0.2873 = 229.9$ N ✓

#### **Model Solution — Part A (b):**

**Given:** $W = 2000$ N, $\beta = 25°$, $\mu_s = 0.28$.

**(i) Will the body slide on its own?**

Compare the angle of repose with the incline angle:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s) = \tan^{-1}(0.28) = 15.64°$$

Since $\phi = 15.64° < \beta = 25°$, the body **will slide down** on its own (the plane is steeper than the angle of repose).

**Wait — let us verify** by computing the self-sliding force:
- Driving force: $W \sin\beta = 2000 \times \sin 25° = 2000 \times 0.4226 = 845.24$ N
- Maximum opposing friction: $\mu_s W \cos\beta = 0.28 \times 2000 \times \cos 25° = 0.28 \times 2000 \times 0.9063 = 507.53$ N

Since $845.24$ N $> 507.53$ N, the body slides down unaided. ✓

**(ii) Smallest force along the plane to initiate upward motion:**

The component $P$ must overcome both the weight component **and** the friction (which now acts **down the plane**):
$$P_{\min} = W\sin\beta + \mu_s W\cos\beta = W(\sin\beta + \mu_s\cos\beta)$$

$$P_{\min} = 2000(\sin 25° + 0.28 \cos 25°)$$

$$P_{\min} = 2000(0.4226 + 0.28 \times 0.9063)$$

$$P_{\min} = 2000(0.4226 + 0.2538) = 2000 \times 0.6764$$

$$\boxed{P_{\min} = 1352.8 \text{ N}}$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** A block of weight **$W = 500$ N** rests on a horizontal surface. The coefficient of friction is **$\mu = 0.25$**. A force of **$200$ N** is applied at an angle of **$30°$** above the horizontal. Determine whether the block will move. If not, find the actual friction force. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Two blocks, $A$ (weight **100 N**, $\mu_A = 0.30$) and $B$ (weight **300 N**, $\mu_B = 0.20$), are placed on a horizontal surface as shown. Block $A$ is on top of Block $B$. A horizontal force $P$ is applied to the **lower block** $B$. Find the maximum value of $P$ such that **both blocks move together** without $A$ sliding on $B$. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Part B (a):**

**Given:** $W = 500$ N, $\mu_s = 0.25$, $P = 200$ N at $\theta = 30°$ above horizontal.

**Step 1: Compute the limiting friction force available.**

Vertical equilibrium:
$$N = W - P\sin\theta = 500 - 200 \sin 30° = 500 - 200 \times 0.5 = 500 - 100 = 400 \text{ N}$$

Maximum (limiting) static friction available:
$$F_L = \mu_s N = 0.25 \times 400 = 100 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute the horizontal component of applied force.**

$$P_x = P\cos\theta = 200 \cos 30° = 200 \times 0.8660 = 173.21 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Compare and conclude.**

Since $P_x = 173.21 \text{ N} > F_L = 100 \text{ N}$, the block **will move**.

Actually, applying the imminent-motion condition $P\cos\theta = \mu_s(W - P\sin\theta)$:
$$P\cos\theta + \mu_s P\sin\theta = \mu_s W$$
$$P = \frac{\mu_s W}{\cos\theta + \mu_s\sin\theta} = \frac{0.25 \times 500}{\cos 30° + 0.25\sin 30°}$$
$$P = \frac{125}{0.8660 + 0.125} = \frac{125}{0.9910} = 126.13 \text{ N}$$

**Conclusion:** Since the applied force $P = 200$ N **exceeds** the minimum required $P = 126.13$ N, the block **will slide**.

*(Note: As the block moves, kinetic friction takes over with $\mu_k$, but the problem asks about static / impending condition.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part B (b):**

**Given:** $W_A = 100$ N, $\mu_A = 0.30$ (between A and B); $W_B = 300$ N, $\mu_B = 0.20$ (between B and ground).

**Step 1: Analyze the upper block (A) alone.**

If both blocks move **together** with common acceleration $a$, then on block A, the **only horizontal force** is the friction $F_{AB}$ from block B acting **forward** (in the direction of $P$).

For block A to **not slide** on B, this friction must not exceed the limiting value:

$$F_{AB} \le \mu_A N_A = \mu_A \times W_A = 0.30 \times 100 = 30 \text{ N}$$

For block A (mass $m_A = W_A/g$): $F_{AB} = m_A a \le 30$ N

$$a \le \frac{30}{m_A} = \frac{30 \times g}{100} = 0.30 \, g$$

**Step 2: Analyze the lower block (B) — find the maximum $P$.**

Forces on B (horizontally):
- Applied force $P$ (forward)
- Friction from A on B: $F_{BA} = F_{AB} = 30$ N (backward, by Newton's third law)
- Friction from ground on B: $F_{\text{ground}} = \mu_B N_B$ (backward)

Normal from ground: $N_B = W_A + W_B = 100 + 300 = 400$ N

$$F_{\text{ground}} = 0.20 \times 400 = 80 \text{ N}$$

Newton's second law for B (mass $m_B = W_B / g$):

$$P - F_{AB} - F_{\text{ground}} = m_B a$$

$$P - 30 - 80 = m_B \times a$$

**Step 3: Equate the accelerations (both blocks move together).**

From Step 1: $a = 0.30 \, g$

From Step 2: $P = 30 + 80 + m_B \times 0.30 \, g = 110 + \frac{W_B}{g} \times 0.30 \, g = 110 + 0.30 \times 300$

$$P = 110 + 90 = \boxed{200 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification:** If $P$ exceeds 200 N, the lower block accelerates faster than the upper block can be dragged by friction — they will slip relative to each other.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Friction Problems (Avoid These!):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in friction direction (–2 to –3 marks):** Friction always **opposes motion or the tendency of motion**. If the block tends to slide **down**, friction acts **up**; if pulled **up**, friction acts **down**. Re-verify the direction after every force balance.
>
> 2. **Using $F = \mu N$ when the body is NOT on the verge of motion (–1 mark):** Static friction is **self-adjusting** and is *not* always equal to $\mu_s N$. Apply the limiting condition **only** at impending slip.
>
> 3. **Confusing angle of friction with angle of application (–1 mark):** The minimum force formula $P_{\min} = W\sin\phi$ requires the force to be applied at angle $\phi$ **above the horizontal** (not above the inclined plane, unless explicitly stated).
>
> 4. **Forgetting that $\mu$ is dimensionless (–1 mark):** Never write "N/m²" or any unit. $\mu$ is a pure number.
>
> 5. **Neglecting to check self-sliding (–2 marks):** Always verify: if $\tan\beta \le \mu_s$, the body is **self-locking** and does not need any force to remain static. Explicitly state this check.
>
> 6. **Incorrect resolution of weight on inclined plane (–2 marks):** $W\cos\beta$ is perpendicular to the plane (gives $N$); $W\sin\beta$ is along the plane (drives motion). Many students invert these.
>
> 7. **Unit conversion errors in $\tan^{-1}$ (–1 mark):** Calculator must be in **degree mode** unless the problem explicitly says radians. The result of $\tan^{-1}(0.30)$ in radians is meaningless for an angle in a triangle.
>
> 8. **Not stating "at the verge of motion" or "limiting equilibrium" (–1 mark):** KTU examiners award marks for the **physical interpretation** of the limiting condition. Always write one line: *"Since the body is on the verge of sliding, $F = \mu_s N$."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet — Module 2: Friction**

### 🔑 **Core Concepts**

- [x] **Friction** is a self-adjusting tangential force opposing motion or its tendency.
- [x] **Static friction** $\le \mu_s N$ (variable, not fixed).
- [x] **Limiting friction** $F_L = \mu_s N$ (at the verge of motion).
- [x] **Kinetic friction** $F_k = \mu_k N$ (during motion, $\mu_k < \mu_s$).
- [x] **Angle of friction:** $\tan\phi = \mu_s$.
- [x] **Angle of repose:** $\alpha = \phi$ (numerically equal, both = $\tan^{-1}\mu_s$).
- [x] **Resultant reaction:** $R = N\sqrt{1+\mu^2}$, tilted by $\phi$ from the normal.

### 📐 **Must-Memorize Formulas**

| Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|
| $F = \mu N$ | Friction force (limiting/kinetic) |
| $\tan\phi = \mu$ | Relate friction angle to $\mu$ |
| $P_{\min} = W\sin\phi = \mu W / \sqrt{1+\mu^2}$ | Minimum pull, horizontal surface |
| $P = W(\sin\beta + \mu\cos\beta)$ | Pull **up** an incline (along plane) |
| $P = W(\mu\cos\beta - \sin\beta)$ | Pull **down** an incline to maintain motion |

### 🧠 **Problem-Solving Heuristics**

1. **Always draw a clean FBD** — show all four forces: $W$, $N$, $F$, $P$.
2. **Resolve** all non-axial forces into components parallel and perpendicular to the surface.
3. **Check the regime:** static, limiting, or kinetic — this determines the friction formula.
4. **State the limiting condition explicitly** in words before writing the equation.
5. **Verify the direction of friction** by imagining the motion direction.

### ⚠️ **Engineering Watch-Outs**

- Friction is **independent of apparent contact area** (Law 3) — a wide flat block and a narrow block experience the same friction under the same load.
- $\mu$ is a **material property pair** (e.g., steel-on-steel $\ne$ steel-on-rubber).
- Friction is **always dissipative** — it converts mechanical energy into heat (brake design exploits this).

### 🏆 **High-Yield KTU Past Pattern**

| Frequently Asked | Mark Weightage |
|---|---|
| State laws of friction | 3 (Part A) |
| Define angle of friction and angle of repose | 3 (Part A) |
| Pull on horizontal surface (force, angle) | 7 (Part B) |
| Pull on inclined plane (up / down) | 7 (Part B) |
| Two-block / ladder problems | 7 (Part B) |

> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024:** *If your FBD is correct, the rest is algebra. If your FBD is wrong, no amount of math will save you.* Draw the FBD first — every single time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Blocks and Ladder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Blocks and Ladder ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Analysis of Blocks (Wedge, Sliding & Tipping) **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):** A *block* in Engineering Mechanics is a rigid body resting on a surface (horizontal or inclined), subjected to a system of active forces (weight, applied push/pull, tension) and reactive forces (normal reaction and friction). *Analysis of Blocks* refers to the application of the equations of static equilibrium to determine the limiting conditions for **sliding** (translational motion along the contact surface) and **tipping** (rotational motion about an edge of the base). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the **direct application of Module 1 & 2 fundamentals** — Law of Superposition, Free Body Diagram (FBD), Lami's Theorem, and **Friction (Coulomb's Laws)**. It is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark questions in University Examinations. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pushing a heavy wooden almirah (wardrobe) across your room. You push it *horizontally* — it may not move because floor friction holds it back. You then push it *downward at an angle* (so your weight adds to the normal reaction, increasing friction further) — still doesn't move! Finally, you push it *upward at an angle* — this *lifts* a part of the weight, reduces friction, and the almirah slides. The angle at which sliding begins, and the condition when the almirah tips over its front edge instead of sliding, are precisely what "Block Analysis" solves. ### 1.2 Analysis of Ladder **Formal Definition:** A *ladder* problem treats a long, slender rigid body (the ladder) resting with one end on a **horizontal floor** and the other against a **vertical wall**, as a rigid body in static equilibrium under the action of its self-weight, an optional load (a person standing on it), and the reactions at the two contact points. The floor and wall may be **smooth** or **rough**, governed by their respective coefficients of friction ($\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$). **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the metal step-ladder your electrician leans against the wall. Why does it never slip when he stands on the *bottom* rung, but slips the moment he climbs to the top? The answer lies in the **moment of his weight about the foot of the ladder** — the higher he climbs, the greater the overturning moment, and the more friction the floor must supply. There exists a *critical angle* below which the ladder will always slip — and another *critical height* of the person at which the ladder slips, regardless of the angle. Ladder analysis quantifies both. ### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters - **Weight of body ($W$):** Acts vertically downward through the **Center of Gravity (C.G.)**. Unit: Newton (N). - **Angle of Friction ($\phi$):** $\tan \phi = \mu_s$ where $\mu_s$ is the static coefficient of friction. Dimensionless. - **Angle of Repose ($\alpha_r$):** The steepest angle of an inclined plane on which a block remains stationary without sliding. Mathematically, $\alpha_r = \phi$. - **Normal Reaction ($N$):** Perpendicular to the contact surface. Unit: N. - **Limiting Friction Force ($F_L$):** Maximum value of static friction, $F_L = \mu_s N$. Unit: N. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Convention Used Throughout KTU Solutions:** > - Weight ($W$) → vertically **downward**. > - Applied force ($P$) → at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. > - Normal ($N$) → perpendicular to surface, **outward** from surface. > - Friction ($F$) → opposes *impending* motion (i.e., acts opposite to the direction the body *tends* to move). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block on a Horizontal Surface — Force at an Angle $\theta$ Consider a block of weight $W$ resting on a rough horizontal plane ($\mu = \mu_s$). A force $P$ is applied at angle $\theta$ above the horizontal. **Equilibrium Equations (Resolving forces):** $$\sum F_x = 0: \quad P \cos\theta - F = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = P \cos\theta$$ $$\sum F_y = 0: \quad N + P \sin\theta - W = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = W - P \sin\theta$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Insight:** Notice that the *vertical component* of $P$ **reduces** the normal reaction. Lower normal reaction → lower available friction → block slides more easily. This is why pulling *upward* is more effective than pushing *downward*. **Condition for Sliding (Limiting Equilibrium):** The block is on the verge of sliding when $F = \mu_s N$: $$P \cos\theta = \mu_s (W - P \sin\theta)$$ Solving for the **minimum force required to initiate sliding** ($P_{sliding}$): $$P_{sliding} = \frac{\mu_s W}{\cos\theta + \mu_s \sin\theta}$$ **Condition for Tipping:** The block (of width $b$ and height $h$) tips about its front edge when the **overturning moment equals the restoring moment**: $$P \cos\theta \cdot \frac{h}{2} = W \cdot \frac{b}{2}$$ $$P_{tipping} = \frac{W \cdot b}{h} = \frac{W}{\tan\theta} \quad \text{(at the tipping edge)}$$ The block will **slide first** if $P_{sliding} < P_{tipping}$, and **tip first** if $P_{tipping} < P_{sliding}$. ### 2.2 Block on an Inclined Plane (Angle of Repose) A block rests on a rough inclined plane of inclination $\alpha$. Resolving $W$ parallel and perpendicular to the plane: - Component *down the plane*: $W \sin\alpha$ - Component *into the plane*: $W \cos\alpha$ Normal reaction: $N = W \cos\alpha$. Limiting friction: $F_L = \mu_s W \cos\alpha$. **Block remains stationary** if $W \sin\alpha \leq \mu_s W \cos\alpha$, i.e., $\tan\alpha \leq \mu_s$. **Angle of Repose:** $\tan \alpha_r = \mu_s = \tan\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_r = \phi$. ### 2.3 Ladder Analysis (Standard Configuration) A ladder of length $L$ and weight $W$ rests against a **smooth vertical wall** (roughness $\mu_2 = 0$, so only horizontal reaction $N_B$) and a **rough horizontal floor** (roughness $\mu_1 = \mu$). The ladder makes angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal.
