C.G. assumed at the geometric center ($L/2$ from either end). **Reactions:** - At the foot (A): $N_A$ (vertical, upward) and $F_A$ (horizontal friction). - At the wall (B): $N_B$ (horizontal, away from wall — since wall is smooth). **Equilibrium Equations:** $$\sum F_x = 0: \quad N_B - F_A = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_A = N_B$$ $$\sum F_y = 0: \quad N_A - W = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A = W$$ **Taking moments about the foot A** (to eliminate $N_A$ and $F_A$): $$N_B \cdot (L \sin\alpha) = W \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2} \cos\alpha\right)$$ $$N_B = \frac{W \cos\alpha}{2 \sin\alpha} = \frac{W}{2 \tan\alpha}$$ **Friction required at foot:** $F_A = N_B = \dfrac{W}{2 \tan\alpha}$. **Condition for Ladder to be on the Verge of Slipping:** The floor's friction is at its limit: $F_A = \mu N_A$. $$\frac{W}{2 \tan\alpha} = \mu W \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\alpha = \frac{1}{2\mu}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical (Minimum) Angle of Inclination for a Ladder:** > $$\alpha_{min} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2\mu}\right)$$ > The ladder will slip if the actual angle $\alpha < \alpha_{min}$. To make a ladder safe, we always lean it **steeper** than this critical angle. ### 2.4 Ladder with a Person at Height $h$ If a person of weight $W_p$ stands on the ladder at a horizontal distance $x$ from the foot (measured along the ladder, $x = $ distance from foot up the ladder), the moment equation about A becomes: $$N_B \cdot L \sin\alpha = W \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cos\alpha + W_p \cdot x \cos\alpha$$ $$N_B = \left(\frac{W}{2} + \frac{W_p \cdot x}{L}\right) \cot\alpha$$ ### 2.5 Ladder with Rough Wall AND Rough Floor Now $\mu_1$ at floor and $\mu_2$ at wall. Reactions become: - Foot: $N_A \uparrow$, $F_A = \mu_1 N_A \rightarrow$ (opposing slip) - Wall: $N_B \leftarrow$, $F_B = \mu_2 N_B \downarrow$ (wall friction acts downward because the wall pushes the ladder outward and the ladder tends to slip down) The moment equation about A yields the **quadratic in $\tan\alpha$** form, which is the classical 14-mark KTU problem. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Situation | Governing Formula | Use Case | |---|-----------|-------------------|----------| | 1 | Block on horizontal plane, force $P$ at angle $\theta$ | $P_{slide} = \dfrac{\mu W}{\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta}$ | Minimum push/pull force | | 2 | Block on horizontal plane, tipping | $P_{tip} = \dfrac{W \cdot b}{h}$ | Tipping threshold | | 3 | Block on inclined plane (no applied force) | $\tan\alpha_r = \mu$ | Angle of repose | | 4 | Block on inclined plane with $P$ parallel to plane | $P = W(\sin\alpha + \mu\cos\alpha)$ | Push up the incline | | 5 | Ladder — smooth wall, rough floor | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}$ | Minimum safe angle | | 6 | Ladder — moment about foot | $N_B = \dfrac{W}{2\tan\alpha}$ | Wall reaction | | 7 | Ladder with man at distance $d$ from foot | $N_B = \left(\dfrac{W}{2} + \dfrac{W_p d}{L}\right)\cot\alpha$ | Real climbing scenario | | 8 | Ladder — both surfaces rough | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{1 - \mu_1 \mu_2}{2\mu_1}$ | General case | | 9 | Two-block system (connected by string over pulley) | Use $a = \dfrac{(m_1 - m_2)g}{m_1 + m_2}$ for Atwood-type problems | Connected bodies | | 10 | Wedge — self-locking | $\alpha \leq 2\phi$ where $\phi$ is the angle of friction | Wedge holding block | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in Engineering:** Ladder problems model **derricks, scaffolding, ship masts, drilling rigs, and aircraft fuselage jigs**. Block analysis models **brake shoe dynamics, screw jacks, wedge locking in tool-holders, and rock-stability on hill slopes**. KTU tests these because they are the cleanest demonstrations of *equilibrium of a non-concurrent force system with friction*. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Force to Slide a Block on Horizontal Floor **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Form):** A block of weight $W$ rests on a rough horizontal surface ($\mu_s = \mu$). A force $P$ is applied at an angle $\theta$ above the horizontal. Determine the magnitude of $P$ required to just move the block, and the angle $\theta$ at which this required force is a **minimum**. **Step 1 — Draw the Free Body Diagram (FBD):** Four forces act on the block: 1. Weight $W$ (vertically downward, through C.G.) 2. Applied force $P$ (at angle $\theta$ above horizontal) 3. Normal reaction $N$ (vertically upward) 4. Friction force $F$ (horizontally, opposite to impending motion — say, to the left) **Step 2 — Write the Equilibrium Equations:** Horizontal: $\sum F_x = 0$ $$P \cos\theta - F = 0$$ Vertical: $\sum F_y = 0$ $$N + P \sin\theta - W = 0$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Limiting Friction Condition (impending motion):** The block is *just* about to move, so friction is at its maximum: $$F = \mu N$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the horizontal equation:** $$P \cos\theta = \mu N$$ From the vertical equation: $N = W - P \sin\theta$. Substituting: $$P \cos\theta = \mu (W - P \sin\theta)$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $P$:** $$P \cos\theta + \mu P \sin\theta = \mu W$$ $$P (\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta) = \mu W$$ $$\boxed{P = \frac{\mu W}{\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta}}$$ This is the **required force to initiate sliding**. **Step 6 — Find the angle $\theta$ at which $P$ is minimum:** To minimize $P$, we must **maximize** the denominator $D(\theta) = \cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta$. $$\frac{dD}{d\theta} = -\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta = 0$$ $$\tan\theta = \mu = \tan\phi$$ $$\theta_{optimal} = \phi \quad \text{(the angle of friction!)}$$ **Step 7 — Compute the minimum force:** Substitute $\theta = \phi$, so $\cos\theta = \cos\phi$ and $\sin\theta = \sin\phi$. Using the identity $\cos\phi = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}$ and $\sin\phi = \dfrac{\mu}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}}$: $$D_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} + \mu \cdot \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} = \frac{1 + \mu^2}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} = \sqrt{1+\mu^2}$$ $$P_{min} = \frac{\mu W}{\sqrt{1+\mu^2}} = W \sin\phi$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Result (Memorize This!):** The **minimum** force required to slide a block on a rough horizontal surface is $P_{min} = W \sin\phi$, and this occurs when the force is applied at the **angle of friction** ($\theta = \phi$) measured from the horizontal. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Angle of a Ladder Resting Against a Smooth Wall and Rough Floor **Problem Statement:** A uniform ladder of length $L$ and weight $W$ rests with its foot on a rough horizontal floor ($\mu$) and its top against a smooth vertical wall. The ladder makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. Find the minimum value of $\alpha$ for equilibrium. **Step 1 — FBD of the Ladder:** Forces acting: - $W$ at the mid-point, vertically downward. - At foot (A): $N_A$ upward, $F_A$ horizontal (friction, pointing *toward* the wall, opposing the foot's tendency to slip *away* from the wall). - At wall (B): $N_B$ horizontal, pointing *away* from the wall (smooth wall → no vertical friction). **Step 2 — Equilibrium Equations:** $$\sum F_y = 0: \quad N_A - W = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A = W$$ $$\sum F_x = 0: \quad N_B - F_A = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_A = N_B$$ **Step 3 — Take Moments about the Foot (A) — to eliminate $N_A$ and $F_A$:** The perpendicular distance from A to the line of action of $N_B$ is $L \sin\alpha$. The perpendicular distance from A to the line of action of $W$ is $(L/2)\cos\alpha$. $$\sum M_A = 0: \quad N_B \cdot (L \sin\alpha) - W \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2} \cos\alpha\right) = 0$$ $$N_B = \frac{W L \cos\alpha}{2 L \sin\alpha} = \frac{W}{2} \cot\alpha = \frac{W}{2 \tan\alpha}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Limiting Friction Condition:** The ladder is on the verge of slipping when $F_A = \mu N_A$: $$\frac{W}{2 \tan\alpha} = \mu W$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $\alpha$:** $$\tan\alpha = \frac{1}{2\mu}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha_{min} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2\mu}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification Check:** If $\mu = 0.5$, then $\alpha_{min} = \tan^{-1}(1.0) = 45°$. This matches the well-known rule of thumb: "A ladder should be placed at an angle of at least **75°** with the ground (or 15° from the wall) for safety," which corresponds to $\mu \approx 0.27$ and a steeper-than-critical angle of about 75° from the ground. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Ladder with Both Wall and Floor Rough **Problem Statement (Full 14-Mark KTU Form):** A ladder of length $L$ and weight $W$ rests on a rough floor ($\mu_1$) against a rough wall ($\mu_2$). Find the angle $\alpha$ with the floor at which the ladder is on the verge of slipping. **Step 1 — FBD:** - Foot A: $N_A$ up, $F_A = \mu_1 N_A$ horizontal (toward wall, opposing slip). - Wall B: $N_B$ away from wall, $F_B = \mu_2 N_B$ vertically *downward* (opposing the ladder's tendency to slip *down* at the top). **Step 2 — Equilibrium:** $$N_A + \mu_2 N_B = W$$ $$N_B = \mu_1 N_A$$ **Step 3 — Moment about A:** $$N_B \cdot L \sin\alpha + F_B \cdot L \cos\alpha = W \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cos\alpha$$ $$N_B \sin\alpha + \mu_2 N_B \cos\alpha = \frac{W}{2} \cos\alpha$$ $$N_B (\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha) = \frac{W}{2} \cos\alpha$$ $$N_B = \frac{W \cos\alpha}{2(\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)}$$ **Step 4 — From the horizontal equilibrium:** $N_B = \mu_1 N_A$, so $N_A = N_B / \mu_1 = \dfrac{W \cos\alpha}{2\mu_1(\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)}$. **Step 5 — Substitute $N_A$ into the vertical equilibrium:** $$\frac{W \cos\alpha}{2\mu_1(\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)} + \mu_2 \cdot \frac{W \cos\alpha}{2(\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)} = W$$ Divide both sides by $W \cos\alpha$: $$\frac{1}{2\mu_1(\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)} + \frac{\mu_2}{2(\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)} = \frac{1}{\cos\alpha}$$ $$\frac{1 + \mu_1 \mu_2}{2\mu_1(\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)} = \frac{1}{\cos\alpha}$$ $$(1 + \mu_1 \mu_2) \cos\alpha = 2\mu_1 (\sin\alpha + \mu_2 \cos\alpha)$$ $$\cos\alpha + \mu_1 \mu_2 \cos\alpha = 2\mu_1 \sin\alpha + 2\mu_1 \mu_2 \cos\alpha$$ $$\cos\alpha - \mu_1 \mu_2 \cos\alpha = 2\mu_1 \sin\alpha$$ $$\cos\alpha (1 - \mu_1 \mu_2) = 2\mu_1 \sin\alpha$$ $$\boxed{\tan\alpha = \frac{1 - \mu_1 \mu_2}{2\mu_1}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check (Limit Cases):** > - If wall is smooth ($\mu_2 = 0$): $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_1}$ ✓ (matches Section 3.2) > - If floor is also smooth ($\mu_1 = 0$): $\tan\alpha \to \infty$ → $\alpha \to 90°$. The ladder would have to be vertical, which is physically impossible. Hence **a ladder on a smooth floor will never rest in equilibrium against a wall**, which matches intuition. --- ### 3.4 Two-Block System (Connected Blocks) — Quick Derivation A block of mass $m_1$ on a smooth incline $\alpha$ is connected by a light inextensible string over a pulley at the top to a hanging block $m_2$. Find the acceleration. **Step 1 — Free Body of $m_1$ (along the incline, upward positive):** $$T - m_1 g \sin\alpha = m_1 a$$ **Step 2 — Free Body of $m_2$ (downward positive):** $$m_2 g - T = m_2 a$$ **Step 3 — Add the two equations (string is light, so $T$ cancels):** $$m_2 g - m_1 g \sin\alpha = (m_1 + m_2) a$$ $$a = \frac{(m_2 - m_1 \sin\alpha) g}{m_1 + m_2}$$ The system is in equilibrium if $a = 0 \Rightarrow m_2 = m_1 \sin\alpha$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trap:** Many students forget to include the *weight component* $m_1 g \sin\alpha$ when writing the equation for the block on the incline. Always resolve $m_1 g$ *parallel* and *perpendicular* to the incline first. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Analysis — Methodology Flowchart The following Mermaid flow captures the decision logic a student should follow when solving any "block on a surface" problem in KTU exams. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Block Problem]) --> Q1{Inclined or
Horizontal surface?} Q1 -- Horizontal --> H1[Resolve P into Px and Py] H1 --> H2[Equations: N + P sinθ = W,
F = P cosθ] H2 --> H3{F = μN ?} H3 -- Yes --> H4[Compute Pslide = μW /
cosθ + μ sinθ] H3 -- No --> H5[Equilibrium: static case] H4 --> T1[Compute Ptip = Wb / h] T1 --> Comp{Pslide vs Ptip?} Comp -- Pslide < Ptip --> ResultA[Block SLIDES first] Comp -- Ptip < Pslide --> ResultB[Block TIPS first] Q1 -- Inclined --> I1[Resolve W into
W sinα and W cosα] I1 --> I2{F = μN? Check
W sinα vs μW cosα} I2 -- W sinα < μW cosα --> I3[Block stationary
α less than repose angle] I2 -- W sinα = μW cosα --> I4[Block on verge
α = angle of repose] I2 -- W sinα > μW cosα --> I5[Block slides down] ResultA --> Final([Final answer: P, μ, or α]) ResultB --> Final I3 --> Final I4 --> Final I5 --> Final ``` ### 4.2 Ladder Analysis — Free Body Diagram Logic This block diagram shows the systematic decomposition of a ladder problem into its equilibrium components. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Setup [Problem Setup] A1[Ladder length L, weight W,
angle alpha with floor] A2{Surface Roughness
Specification} end Setup --> B1[Draw FBD with all 4 forces] B1 --> B2[Resolve forces into
X and Y components] B2 --> C1[Sum Fx = 0] B2 --> C2[Sum Fy = 0] B2 --> C3[Sum MA = 0
about foot of ladder] C1 --> D1[Equilibrium Equations] C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> E1{Verge of slipping?} E1 -- Yes --> F1[Apply F = μN at
the slipping contact] E1 -- No --> F2[Static equilibrium:
solve for unknown force] F1 --> G1[Compute tan alpha = formula
for given surface condition] F2 --> G1 G1 --> Out([Final: alpha, N_A, N_B]) ``` ### 4.3 Force Resolution Geometry (Block on Horizontal Surface) The following ASCII schematic illustrates the standard FBD of a block with an inclined applied force, showing how $P$ is resolved and how the friction force $F$ opposes the horizontal component. ``` ↑ N (Normal reaction) | | W ↓ | ┌──┐ | │ │←── F ────│ (Friction, opposing slide) │ │ │ │ ← C.G. (center of gravity) │ │ ↗ P sinθ │ │ ↗ │ │ ↗ P │ │ ↗ (angle θ above horizontal) └──┘ ↗ ←────────────── ←─ P cosθ ─→ ``` ### 4.4 Ladder Free Body Diagram (Smooth Wall, Rough Floor) ``` N_B ──────→ (Smooth wall: horizontal only) │ │ (Wall, smooth) │ ● ← B (top of ladder) ╱│ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ │ W (weight, at L/2) ╱ ↓ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ α │ ╱ ╱ │ ╱ ╱ │ ●──────────────● ← A (foot of ladder) ↑ N_A │ F_A →──┘ (Friction at foot, toward wall) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** At the foot (A), the floor exerts a vertical normal $N_A$ *upward* and a horizontal friction $F_A$ *toward the wall* (the wall pushes the ladder out via $N_B$, and the floor resists the foot sliding away). The ladder is on the verge of slipping outward at the foot and downward at the top. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Define the terms: (i) Angle of friction, (ii) Angle of repose. Establish the relationship between them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **(i) Angle of Friction ($\phi$):** It is the angle made by the **resultant** of the normal reaction $N$ and the limiting friction force $F_L = \mu_s N$ with the direction of the normal reaction. Mathematically, $\tan\phi = \dfrac{F_L}{N} = \mu_s$. **(ii) Angle of Repose ($\alpha_r$):** It is the **steepest angle** of an inclined plane (made with the horizontal) on which a block, placed on the plane, can rest in equilibrium without sliding down. **Relationship:** Consider a block on the verge of sliding down an incline of angle $\alpha$. The forces along and perpendicular to the plane balance: $$W \sin\alpha_r = \mu_s N \quad \text{and} \quad N = W \cos\alpha_r$$ Dividing: $\tan\alpha_r = \mu_s = \tan\phi$, hence $\boxed{\alpha_r = \phi}$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **A uniform ladder of weight $W$ rests with its upper end against a smooth vertical wall and lower end on a rough horizontal floor. If the angle of friction at the floor is $\phi$, derive the expression for the minimum angle the ladder makes with the floor to prevent slipping.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** From the FBD, taking moments about the foot: $N_B L \sin\alpha = W \dfrac{L}{2} \cos\alpha \Rightarrow N_B = \dfrac{W}{2\tan\alpha}$. Vertical equilibrium: $N_A = W$. Friction at foot: $F_A = N_B = \dfrac{W}{2\tan\alpha}$. For the ladder to be on the verge of slipping: $F_A = \mu N_A = (\tan\phi) \cdot W$. Equating: $\dfrac{W}{2\tan\alpha} = W \tan\phi$ $$\tan\alpha = \frac{1}{2\tan\phi} = \frac{1}{2\mu}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha_{min} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2\mu}\right)}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** A block of weight $W = 500$ N rests on a rough horizontal surface ($\mu = 0.4$). A force $P$ is applied at an angle of $20°$ above the horizontal. Determine: (i) The magnitude of $P$ required to just move the block. (ii) The angle $\theta$ at which $P$ must be applied to minimize its value, and find $P_{min}$. **(b)** A uniform ladder of length $6$ m and weight $400$ N rests against a smooth vertical wall and a rough horizontal floor ($\mu = 0.3$). A man of weight $600$ N climbs the ladder. Find how far up the ladder the man can climb safely without causing it to slip. The ladder makes an angle of $60°$ with the horizontal. --- #### **Model Solution A (14 marks):** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **(i) Force $P$ to just move the block:** Using the formula derived in Section 3.1: $$P = \frac{\mu W}{\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta}$$ Substituting $W = 500$ N, $\mu = 0.4$, $\theta = 20°$: $$P = \frac{0.4 \times 500}{\cos 20° + 0.4 \times \sin 20°} = \frac{200}{0.9397 + 0.4 \times 0.3420}$$ $$P = \frac{200}{0.9397 + 0.1368} = \frac{200}{1.0765} \approx \boxed{185.8 \text{ N}}$$ **(ii) Angle for minimum $P$ and $P_{min}$:** The minimum occurs when $\theta = \phi$, where $\tan\phi = \mu = 0.4$: $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.4) = 21.80° \quad \text{or} \quad \theta_{optimal} = 21.80°$$ $$P_{min} = W \sin\phi = 500 \times \sin(21.80°) = 500 \times 0.3714 \approx \boxed{185.7 \text{ N}}$$ The two results are consistent (minor rounding difference). The minimum force is achieved when the force is applied at the **angle of friction**. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Let the man climb a distance $d$ along the ladder from the foot. Given: $L = 6$ m, $W = 400$ N, $W_p = 600$ N, $\mu = 0.3$, $\alpha = 60°$. **FBD:** Four forces — $W$ at $L/2 = 3$ m, $W_p$ at $d$, $N_A$ at foot, $N_B$ at wall (smooth), $F_A$ at foot. **Vertical equilibrium:** $N_A = W + W_p = 400 + 600 = 1000$ N. **Moment about foot A:** $$N_B \cdot (L \sin\alpha) = W \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2}\cos\alpha\right) + W_p \cdot (d \cos\alpha)$$ $$N_B \cdot 6 \sin 60° = 400 \cdot 3 \cos 60° + 600 \cdot d \cos 60°$$ $$N_B \cdot 6 \cdot 0.8660 = 400 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.5 + 600 \cdot d \cdot 0.5$$ $$N_B \cdot 5.196 = 600 + 300 d$$ $$N_B = \frac{600 + 300 d}{5.196} = 115.47 + 57.74 d$$ **Horizontal equilibrium:** $F_A = N_B = 115.47 + 57.74 d$. **Limiting friction:** $F_A = \mu N_A$: $$115.47 + 57.74 d = 0.3 \times 1000 = 300$$ $$57.74 d = 184.53$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 3.196 \text{ m}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):** > 1. Students often forget to **multiply the man's distance $d$ by $\cos\alpha$** when taking moments. The horizontal distance from the foot is $d \cos\alpha$, not $d$. > 2. A common error is to take $N_A = W$ (forgetting the man's weight). Always include ALL vertical forces. > 3. Many students write $N_B = F_A$ without checking signs; verify the direction of friction (toward the wall, opposing the foot's tendency to slip outward). > 4. **Carry at least 4 significant figures** in intermediate steps to avoid propagation errors in the final answer. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** A block of weight $W = 200$ N is placed on a rough inclined plane ($\mu = 0.25$). The plane is inclined at $30°$ to the horizontal. A force $P$ parallel to the plane is applied to just prevent the block from sliding *down*. Determine: (i) The magnitude of $P$. (ii) The minimum force $P_{min}$ required to just push the block *up* the plane, and the direction in which it must act. **(b)** Two blocks A and B of weights $100$ N and $200$ N respectively are connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth pulley at the top of a double-inclined plane. The inclines are $30°$ and $45°$ to the horizontal on the sides of blocks A and B respectively. Block A is on the $30°$ incline, block B is on the $45°$ incline. Assuming smooth contact between blocks and inclines, find the acceleration of the system and the tension in the string. --- #### **Model Solution B (14 marks):** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **(i) Force $P$ to prevent sliding down:** Resolve $W$ along the plane: $W \sin 30° = 200 \times 0.5 = 100$ N (down the plane). Normal reaction: $N = W \cos 30° = 200 \times 0.866 = 173.2$ N. Maximum friction (acting *up* the plane, opposing the tendency to slide down): $F = \mu N = 0.25 \times 173.2 = 43.3$ N. For the block to be on the verge of sliding down, the applied force $P$ (parallel to plane, *up*) plus friction balances the weight component: $$P + F = W \sin\alpha$$ $$P = W \sin\alpha - \mu W \cos\alpha = 100 - 43.3 = \boxed{56.7 \text{ N (up the plane)}}$$ **(ii) Minimum force to push the block up:** When the block is pushed *up*, friction now acts *down* the plane (opposing motion). The required force: $$P_{up} = W \sin\alpha + \mu W \cos\alpha = 100 + 43.3 = \boxed{143.3 \text{ N (up the plane)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Insight:** Notice the dramatic difference: only 56.7 N is needed to *hold* the block from sliding down, but 143.3 N is required to *push it up*. This asymmetry (factor of $\sim 2.5$) is a hallmark of friction and is the principle behind **self-locking wedges** and **brake shoe design**. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Let the system accelerate such that block A moves *up* the $30°$ incline and block B moves *down* the $45°$ incline. Let $T$ be the tension in the string and $a$ the common acceleration. **Equation of motion for block A (along $30°$ incline, upward positive):** $$T - W_A \sin 30° = \frac{W_A}{g} a$$ $$T - 100 \times 0.5 = \frac{100}{9.81} a \quad \Rightarrow \quad T - 50 = 10.19 a \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Equation of motion for block B (along $45°$ incline, downward positive):** $$W_B \sin 45° - T = \frac{W_B}{g} a$$ $$200 \times 0.7071 - T = \frac{200}{9.81} a \quad \Rightarrow \quad 141.42 - T = 20.39 a \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Add equations (1) and (2):** $$-50 + 141.42 = (10.19 + 20.39) a$$ $$91.42 = 30.58 a$$ $$a = \frac{91.42}{30.58} \approx \boxed{2.989 \text{ m/s}^2}$$ **Substitute back into (1) to find $T$:** $$T = 50 + 10.19 \times 2.989 = 50 + 30.46 \approx \boxed{80.46 \text{ N}}$$ **Verification (Substitute in (2)):** $$141.42 - 80.46 = 60.96 \quad \text{vs} \quad 20.39 \times 2.989 = 60.95 \text{ ✓}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) (Two-Block System):** > 1. **Sign Convention is Critical:** Define the *positive direction* consistently (e.g., direction of motion of block A). Inconsistent signs are the #1 reason for losing 3-4 marks. > 2. Students commonly write $m_1 g \sin\alpha$ on **both** sides of the equation, double-counting. Ensure the weight component of A is *resisted* by tension, while the weight component of B *drives* the motion. > 3. The string is **light and inextensible**, so $T$ is the same on both sides, and $a$ is the same for both blocks. State this assumption explicitly at the start. > 4. Always **substitute your answer back** to verify, as shown above. It earns you method marks even if the final answer has a small arithmetic error. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 10 Golden Rules for Block and Ladder Problems:** > > 1. **Always draw a clean FBD** — show ALL forces (weight, normal, friction, applied) with their *correct* directions and *points of application*. > > 2. **Identify the contact point that is on the verge of slipping** — apply $F = \mu N$ *only* at that point. At other contact points, friction may be sub-limiting. > > 3. **Smooth surface** → friction is **zero** at that contact. Only a normal reaction exists. > > 4. **Rough surface at verge of slipping** → $F = \mu_s N$. Direction of $F$ is **opposite to the direction the body tends to move**. > > 5. **Two unknowns?** Use two equilibrium equations — *sum of forces* and *sum of moments* about a strategically chosen point (to eliminate unknown reactions). > > 6. **Tipping of a block:** Compare $P_{sliding}$ and $P_{tipping}$. The smaller value wins (the block does *that* action first). > > 7. **Ladder critical angle (smooth wall, rough floor):** $\tan\alpha_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}$. Ladder slips if actual $\alpha < \alpha_{min}$. > > 8. **Ladder critical angle (both surfaces rough):** $\tan\alpha_{min} = \dfrac{1 - \mu_1 \mu_2}{2\mu_1}$. Always use this general form for full-mark KTU questions. > > 9. **Ladder with man:** Always take moments about the **foot** to eliminate the two reactions at the foot. Include the man's weight and his **horizontal distance** from the foot. > > 10. **Sanity check your answer:** Does the direction of friction match the physical tendency of motion? Does $\alpha_{min}$ fall in the plausible range $0° < \alpha < 90°$? If not, re-examine your equations. **Common Pitfalls (Lose 1-2 Marks Each If Committed):** - ❌ Writing $F = \mu N$ when the body is **not** on the verge of slipping (it is in static equilibrium with $F < \mu N$). - ❌ Taking moments about a point that has *two unknowns* (e.g., the foot, when both $N_A$ and $F_A$ are unknown). *Workaround:* Take moments about a point where the unknowns' lines of action intersect. - ❌ Forgetting to include the **weight of the ladder itself** in ladder-with-man problems. - ❌ Confusing $\tan\phi = \mu$ with $\phi = \mu$ (degrees vs. radians is not the issue — it's the trigonometric function). - ❌ Using $\mu$ (dynamic) instead of $\mu_s$ (static) in "verge of slipping" problems. They are different physical quantities. > [!NOTE] > **Final Note:** The single most valuable skill in KTU Block & Ladder problems is **identifying the unknowns** and choosing the **simplest combination of equilibrium equations** to solve for them. Practice at least 5-7 problems of varying types (block on incline, block with two forces, ladder smooth/rough, ladder with man) and you will master this topic.

topics.title: Centroid of Composite Areas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Centroid of Composite Areas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **centroid** is the geometric centre of a plane figure, defined as the point where the entire area of the figure can be assumed to be concentrated for the purpose of computing the **first moment of area** about any axis. For a composite area (also called a *compound area* or *built-up section*), the centroid is located using the **Principle of Moments**, which states that the moment of the total area about any reference axis is equal to the algebraic sum of the moments of its constituent parts about the same axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"The centroid of a plane figure is the point at which the entire area of the figure may be considered to be concentrated. For a composite figure made up of a number of simple geometric shapes, the centroid is obtained by the principle of moments."*

The Cartesian coordinates of the centroid $( \bar{x}, \bar{y} )$ for a composite area are mathematically defined as:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i} $$

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i} $$

where:
- $A_i$ = Area of the $i^{th}$ constituent shape
- $x_i, y_i$ = Coordinates of the centroid of the $i^{th}$ shape from the chosen reference axes
- $n$ = Total number of constituent shapes
- $\sum A_i$ = Total area of the composite figure (algebraic sum, i.e., subtractions considered negative)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Pizza Slice Balance" Analogy 🍕**
>
> Imagine cutting an irregularly shaped flat pizza into a few simple standard slices: a rectangular slice, a triangular slice, and a half-moon slice. If you place all three slices on a flat table, each slice has its own balance point. Now, if you were to balance the *combined* pizza on a single needle (a pivot point), that needle must sit exactly at the "average" balance location, weighted by the size of each slice. The bigger the slice, the more it "pulls" the balance point towards itself.
>
> This is exactly what the centroid formula does. It finds the **weighted average** of all sub-shapes' centres, where the **weight is the area**. If a piece is removed (a "hole" cut out), we treat that piece as having **negative weight**, which physically makes sense because removing material shifts the centroid away from the hole.

## 1.3 Physical Significance & Engineering Relevance

- **Units**: Length (typically **mm** or **m** in SI units).
- The centroid of an area is also referred to as the **centre of gravity** of a thin homogeneous plate of the same shape, because the mass is directly proportional to the area when thickness and density are uniform.
- The centroid is a purely **geometric property** and is used as a precursor to computing the **Moment of Inertia (Second Moment of Area)** in later modules — this makes it a high-yield foundational topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> **Centroid** → Geometric property (depends only on shape).
> **Centre of Gravity** → Physical property (depends on shape AND density distribution).
> For a *uniform density, uniform thickness* body, the two points **coincide**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Framework

The entire theory of composite centroids rests on **three pillars**:

### Pillar 1: The First Moment of Area
The *first moment of area* of a plane figure about an axis is defined as the product of its area and the perpendicular distance of its centroid from that axis. The **total first moment** of a composite figure about an axis equals the **algebraic sum** of the first moments of its constituent parts.

### Pillar 2: The Principle of Moments (Varignon's Theorem for Areas)
This principle, adapted for areas, is the foundation of the entire computation. It enables us to break a complex figure into simple, well-known geometric components, compute the centroid of each, and then combine them algebraically.

### Pillar 3: The Negative Area Concept
When a portion of a solid figure is removed (a hole, slot, or cut-out), its area is treated as **negative** in the summation. This is because physically, we are subtracting its moment-contribution from the whole. This elegant convention allows us to handle even complex cut-out regions without any change in formula structure.

## 2.2 Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

The standard KTU-approved procedure for solving composite-area centroid problems is:

1. **Draw a clear Free-Body Diagram (FBD)** of the composite area with a chosen reference axis (usually the bottom edge as the X-axis, and the left edge as the Y-axis).
2. **Decompose** the given shape into a minimum number of *standard simple shapes* (rectangle, triangle, circle, semicircle, quarter circle, etc.) for which the centroid location is known.
3. **Label each part** as Part 1, Part 2, … Part $n$. Identify any **holes or cut-outs** — these will use **negative area**.
4. **Tabulate** the following for each part: (a) Area $A_i$, (b) Centroid coordinates $x_i$ and $y_i$, (c) Product $A_i x_i$ and $A_i y_i$.
5. **Compute** $\sum A_i$, $\sum A_i x_i$, $\sum A_i y_i$ (all with proper signs).
6. **Apply the centroid formula** to obtain $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this method works:**
> The integral definition of the centroid is:
> $$ \bar{x} = \frac{\int x \, dA}{\int dA} $$
> Since the composite area is a union (or set difference) of simpler regions, the integral of $x\,dA$ over the whole equals the sum of integrals over the parts. This is a direct consequence of the **additivity property of integrals** (linearity).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the centroids of ALL standard shapes that you MUST memorize for KTU exams.** Mastery of this table alone solves 90% of Module 2 problems.

| **Shape** | **Sketch Description** | **Area ($A$)** | **$\bar{x}$ (from left edge)** | **$\bar{y}$ (from bottom edge)** |
|:----------|:-----------------------|:--------------|:------------------------------|:--------------------------------|
| Rectangle (width $b$, height $h$) | ▭ | $b \cdot h$ | $b/2$ | $h/2$ |
| Right Triangle (base $b$, height $h$, right angle at origin) | ◣ | $\frac{1}{2} b h$ | $b/3$ | $h/3$ |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $h$, base on X-axis) | △ | $\frac{1}{2} b h$ | $b/2$ | $h/3$ |
| Trapezoid (parallel sides $a$, $b$; height $h$) | ⏢ | $\frac{(a+b)}{2} h$ | $\frac{a + 2b}{a+b} \cdot \frac{h}{3}$ (approx) | $\bar{y}$ varies |
| Circle (radius $r$) | ● | $\pi r^{2}$ | $r$ | $r$ |
| Semicircle (radius $r$, diameter on X-axis) | ◐ | $\frac{\pi r^{2}}{2}$ | $r$ | $\frac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,r$ |
| Quarter Circle (radius $r$, in first quadrant) | ◗ | $\frac{\pi r^{2}}{4}$ | $\frac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,r$ | $\frac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,r$ |
| Circular Sector (radius $r$, angle $2\alpha$ in radians) | ⌒ | $\alpha r^{2}$ | $\frac{2r \sin\alpha}{3\alpha}$ | $0$ (on X-axis) |

**Core Composite Centroid Formulas:**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|:-------------|:------------|:----------|
| X-Coordinate of Centroid | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i}$ | Reference axis: left edge (Y-axis) |
| Y-Coordinate of Centroid | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$ | Reference axis: bottom edge (X-axis) |
| Total Area | $A = \sum A_i$ | Add for solid parts, subtract for holes |
| Convention for Hole | $A_{\text{hole}} \to -A_{\text{hole}}$ | Always treat holes as negative area |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Engineering**: Locating centroid of I-sections, T-sections, L-sections, and channel sections — the first step before calculating Moment of Inertia, which governs bending stress.
- **Mechanical Design**: Centroid of machine components (gears, flywheels, brackets) determines their balance and dynamic behaviour.
- **Aerospace**: Centroid of wing cross-sections and fuselage sections is critical for stability analysis.
- **Civil Engineering**: Centroid of irregular dam cross-sections, retaining wall profiles, and foundation footings.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners reward *clean tabular presentation*. Always tabulate your decomposition — it earns **easy 2–3 marks** in the 14-mark problems just for systematic working.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of the Centroid Formula

We start from the **fundamental integral definition** of the centroid of a plane lamina (a region with uniform density).

### Derivation of $\bar{x}$:

Consider an infinitesimal element of area $dA$ at position $(x, y)$. The first moment of this element about the **Y-axis** is $x \cdot dA$. The total first moment of the entire composite area about the Y-axis is obtained by integrating over the full region $R$:

$$ M_y = \iint_R x \, dA $$

By the **additivity of the double integral over disjoint regions**, if the composite area $R$ is the union of $n$ sub-regions $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$, then:

$$ M_y = \iint_{R_1} x \, dA + \iint_{R_2} x \, dA + \cdots + \iint_{R_n} x \, dA $$

For each sub-region $R_i$ with total area $A_i$ and centroid at $(x_i, y_i)$, by definition of the centroid:

$$ \iint_{R_i} x \, dA = A_i \cdot x_i $$

Therefore, the total moment becomes:

$$ M_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i $$

Since the centroid $\bar{x}$ of the total area is defined as the point where the *total moment* equals *total area times centroid distance*:

$$ M_y = A \cdot \bar{x} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \right) \bar{x} $$

Equating the two expressions for $M_y$ and solving for $\bar{x}$:

$$ \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \right) \bar{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i $$

$$ \boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, x_i}{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}} $$

### Derivation of $\bar{y}$:

The derivation follows an **identical logical chain** for the moment about the X-axis. Defining $M_x = \iint_R y \, dA$, and noting that $\iint_{R_i} y \, dA = A_i \cdot y_i$:

$$ M_x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, y_i = A \cdot \bar{y} $$

Solving for $\bar{y}$:

$$ \boxed{\bar{y} = \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, y_i}{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **The mathematical heart of this result is the linearity of integration.** Without this property, the composite formula would not exist, and we would be forced to evaluate complex double integrals for every irregular shape.

## 3.2 Worked Example: L-Shaped Composite Section (KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement
An L-shaped section has a vertical leg of **$200 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$** and a horizontal leg of **$100 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$**, joined at the bottom-left corner. A **circular hole of radius $15 \text{ mm}$** is drilled at the centroid of the vertical leg. Find the centroid of the resulting composite area, taking the bottom-left corner as the origin.

### Step 1: Decomposition and Reference Axes
We split the L-section into **two rectangles** (Rect 1: vertical, Rect 2: horizontal) and one **circular hole** (Part 3, with negative area).

- **Rect 1 (Vertical leg):** $b_1 = 20$ mm, $h_1 = 200$ mm
- **Rect 2 (Horizontal leg):** $b_2 = 100 - 20 = 80$ mm (the part *not* overlapping Rect 1), $h_2 = 20$ mm
- **Part 3 (Hole):** Radius $r = 15$ mm, located at the centroid of Rect 1, i.e., at $(x_3, y_3) = (10, 100)$ mm

### Step 2: Compute Individual Areas

$$ A_1 = 20 \times 200 = 4000 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ A_2 = 80 \times 20 = 1600 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ A_3 = -\pi r^2 = -\pi (15)^2 = -225\pi \approx -706.86 \text{ mm}^2 $$

### Step 3: Compute Individual Centroids

**Part 1 (Vertical Rectangle):** Centroid at its geometric centre.
$$ x_1 = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ mm} $$
$$ y_1 = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ mm} $$

**Part 2 (Horizontal Rectangle):** Located in the right portion of the L.
$$ x_2 = 20 + \frac{80}{2} = 20 + 40 = 60 \text{ mm} $$
$$ y_2 = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ mm} $$

**Part 3 (Hole):** At the centroid of the vertical leg.
$$ x_3 = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad y_3 = 100 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 4: Tabulate the Decomposition

| **Part** | **Description** | **Area $A_i$ (mm²)** | **$x_i$ (mm)** | **$y_i$ (mm)** | **$A_i x_i$ (mm³)** | **$A_i y_i$ (mm³)** |
|:--------:|:----------------|:---------------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------:|
| 1 | Vertical Rectangle | $+4000$ | $10$ | $100$ | $40{,}000$ | $400{,}000$ |
| 2 | Horizontal Rectangle | $+1600$ | $60$ | $10$ | $96{,}000$ | $16{,}000$ |
| 3 | Circular Hole | $-706.86$ | $10$ | $100$ | $-7{,}068.6$ | $-70{,}686$ |
| **Total** | **Composite** | **$\sum A_i = 4893.14$** | — | — | **$\sum A_i x_i = 128{,}931.4$** | **$\sum A_i y_i = 345{,}314$** |

### Step 5: Apply the Centroid Formula

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{128{,}931.4}{4893.14} \approx 26.35 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{345{,}314}{4893.14} \approx 70.57 \text{ mm} $$

### Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\bar{x} \approx 26.35 \text{ mm}, \quad \bar{y} \approx 70.57 \text{ mm}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Sign Convention:** Notice that the hole has a *negative area*. If the hole were on the *opposite* side of the centroid from the reference axis, its moment contribution would be positive but its area would still be negative — this would mathematically pull the centroid *toward* the hole, which is correct. Always remember: **area sign is determined by whether the part is added or removed; moment sign is just area $\times$ distance (with appropriate direction).**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Decomposition Patterns

| **Composite Shape Type** | **Standard Decomposition** | **Critical Pitfall** |
|:-------------------------|:--------------------------|:---------------------|
| I-Section (Symmetric) | Top flange + Web + Bottom flange | Forgetting to subtract flange thickness from total width |
| T-Section | Flange + Web | Centroid Y of flange is at $t_f/2$, not at flange mid-height |
| Channel Section | Two flanges + Web (one large rectangle minus two) | Use the subtraction method: full rectangle $-$ two side cut-outs |
| Hollow Rectangle | Outer rectangle $-$ Inner rectangle | Inner rectangle has $b_i$ and $h_i$ reduced by $2t$ on each side |
| Sector of Circle | Treat as a single sector | $\bar{x}$ formula is $\frac{2r \sin\alpha}{3\alpha}$, not $\frac{r}{2}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Centroid Computation Process

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision logic** a student should follow when solving any composite centroid problem in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Composite Area Given] --> B[Choose Reference X and Y Axes]
    B --> C{Is the shape a single primitive or union?}
    C -- Single primitive --> D[Look up centroid from Standard Table]
    C -- Union of shapes --> E[Decompose into n Simple Parts]
    C -- Subtraction case --> F[Decompose as Full Shape minus Holes]
    E --> G[Label Parts 1 to n]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Area A sub i for each part]
    H --> I[Find Centroid x sub i and y sub i for each part]
    I --> J{Is the part a hole?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Assign Negative Sign to A sub i]
    J -- No --> L[Assign Positive Sign to A sub i]
    K --> M[Tabulate A sub i, x sub i, y sub i, A sub i x sub i, A sub i y sub i]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Sum each column of the table]
    N --> O[Compute x bar = Sum of A sub i x sub i over Sum of A sub i]
    O --> P[Compute y bar = Sum of A sub i y sub i over Sum of A sub i]
    P --> Q[Report Final Centroid Coordinates]
    Q --> R[End]
```

## 4.2 Decomposition Logic Tree — Standard KTU Shape Categories

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Composite Area] --> Cat1[Symmetric Profiles]
    Root --> Cat2[Asymmetric Profiles]
    Root --> Cat3[Profiles with Holes]
    Cat1 --> Isec[I-Section]
    Cat1 --> Rec[Hollow Rectangle]
    Cat1 --> Chn[Channel]
    Cat2 --> Lsec[L-Section]
    Cat2 --> Tsec[T-Section]
    Cat2 --> Zsec[Z-Section]
    Cat3 --> Pl1[Use Negative Area Method]
    Pl1 --> Sub1[Decompose as Big Rectangle minus Small Holes]
    Pl1 --> Sub2[Decompose as Sum of Solids minus Cut-outs]
    Cat1 --> MethodA[Direct Addition Method]
    Cat2 --> MethodA
    MethodA --> Final1[Apply Centroid Formula]
    Cat3 --> MethodB[Addition minus Subtraction Method]
    MethodB --> Final1
    Final1 --> Final2[Compute x bar and y bar]
```

## 4.3 Sign Convention Reference Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the **direction of moment arms** and the **sign convention** used in the standard KTU table format.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Y Axis at Left Edge] -->|Distance x bar| B[Centroid C of Composite Area]
    A -->|Distance x sub i| C[Centroid of Part i]
    D[Reference X Axis at Bottom Edge] -->|Distance y sub i| C
    D -->|Distance y bar| B
    E[All distances measured as positive from origin] --> F[Use Negative Area for Holes]
    F --> G[Moment A sub i times x sub i becomes negative for holes far right]
    G --> H[Centroid shifts LEFT and DOWN if hole is at top right]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **Y-axis** is conventionally placed on the **left edge** of the figure.
> - The **X-axis** is conventionally placed on the **bottom edge** of the figure.
> - All distances are positive when measured *in the direction of increasing x or y*.
> - Holes receive **negative area**, but their centroid coordinates are still positive numbers. The sign is applied only to $A_i$ and consequently to $A_i x_i$ and $A_i y_i$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "centroid" of a plane figure. How is it different from the centre of gravity?**

**Model Answer:**
The *centroid* of a plane figure is the point at which the entire area of the figure can be considered to be concentrated. It is a purely **geometric property** depending only on the shape and dimensions of the figure.

The *centre of gravity*, on the other hand, is the point at which the **entire weight** of the body is assumed to act. It is a **physical property** that depends on the geometry *and* the density distribution of the material.

For a body of **uniform density and uniform thickness**, the centroid and the centre of gravity **coincide**. For non-uniform bodies, they may differ.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **State the principle of moments as applied to composite areas. Write the formula used to locate the centroid of a composite area.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Principle of Moments** states that the moment of the *total area* of a composite figure about any reference axis is equal to the **algebraic sum of the moments** of the individual constituent areas about the same axis.

Mathematically, for a composite area made up of $n$ parts:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i}, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} $$

where $A_i$ is the area of the $i^{th}$ part, and $(x_i, y_i)$ are the coordinates of its centroid from the reference axes. **Holes are accounted for by assigning negative area** to the cut-out portion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

> **A composite area consists of a rectangle of size $300 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$ with a semicircular cut-out of radius $50 \text{ mm}$ removed from the top edge. Find the coordinates of the centroid of the remaining area, taking the bottom-left corner as the origin. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Decomposition, Area Calculation, and Centroid Tabulation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Choose Reference Axes:**
Take X-axis along the bottom edge of the rectangle, and Y-axis along its left edge. The origin is at the bottom-left corner.

**Step 2 — Decompose the Shape:**
- **Part 1 (Rectangle):** $b_1 = 300$ mm, $h_1 = 200$ mm
- **Part 2 (Semicircular Hole):** Radius $r = 50$ mm, with diameter on the top edge of the rectangle. The centre of the semicircle lies on the top edge.

**Step 3 — Compute Individual Areas:**

$$ A_1 = 300 \times 200 = 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ A_2 = -\frac{\pi r^2}{2} = -\frac{\pi (50)^2}{2} = -1250\pi \approx -3926.99 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Locate Centroids:**

For Part 1 (Rectangle), the centroid is at its geometric centre:
$$ x_1 = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad y_1 = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ mm} $$

For Part 2 (Semicircular Hole), the centroid of a semicircle from its flat (diameter) edge is at a distance $\frac{4r}{3\pi}$ from the diameter. Since the diameter is on the top edge, the centroid is *below* the top edge, so:
$$ x_2 = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ mm} $$
$$ y_2 = 200 - \frac{4r}{3\pi} = 200 - \frac{4(50)}{3\pi} = 200 - \frac{200}{3\pi} \approx 200 - 21.22 = 178.78 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 5 — Tabulation:**

| **Part** | **Description** | **$A_i$ (mm²)** | **$x_i$ (mm)** | **$y_i$ (mm)** | **$A_i x_i$ (mm³)** | **$A_i y_i$ (mm³)** |
|:--------:|:----------------|:---------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | Rectangle | $+60{,}000$ | $150$ | $100$ | $9{,}000{,}000$ | $6{,}000{,}000$ |
| 2 | Semicircle (hole) | $-3926.99$ | $150$ | $178.78$ | $-589{,}048.5$ | $-702{,}108$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$56{,}073.01$** | — | — | **$8{,}410{,}951.5$** | **$5{,}297{,}892$** |

#### Part (b): Final Centroid Computation (7 Marks)

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{8{,}410{,}951.5}{56{,}073.01} \approx 150.0 \text{ mm} $$

Since the shape is **symmetric about the vertical centre line**, $\bar{x} = 150$ mm (the half-width) is expected and serves as a **consistency check**.

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{5{,}297{,}892}{56{,}073.01} \approx 94.49 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \boxed{\bar{x} = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad \bar{y} \approx 94.49 \text{ mm}} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often forget the **minus sign** for the hole's area, leading to wrong total area and shifted centroid.
> 2. The centroid of a semicircle is at $\frac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,r$ from the *diameter* (straight edge), **not** from the curved edge. Misremembering this costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. Always verify that $\bar{x}$ matches the symmetry line (if any) — this is a **free consistency check**.

---

### **Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **A T-section has a flange of $150 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ and a web of $30 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm}$. Locate the centroid of the section, taking the bottom of the web as the X-axis and the centre line of the web as the Y-axis. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Setup, Decomposition, and Centroid Locations (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Reference Axes:**
- **X-axis**: along the bottom of the web
- **Y-axis**: along the vertical centre line of the web (passing through its mid-width)

**Step 2 — Decomposition:**

- **Part 1 (Flange):** A horizontal rectangle of width $150$ mm and thickness $20$ mm, placed on top of the web.
- **Part 2 (Web):** A vertical rectangle of width $30$ mm and height $100$ mm.

**Step 3 — Areas:**

$$ A_1 = 150 \times 20 = 3000 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ A_2 = 30 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Centroid Coordinates:**

For Part 1 (Flange), the centroid is at the centre of the flange:
$$ x_1 = 0 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{since Y-axis passes through the centre of the web and flange}) $$
$$ y_1 = 100 + \frac{20}{2} = 100 + 10 = 110 \text{ mm} $$

For Part 2 (Web), the centroid is at its geometric centre:
$$ x_2 = 0 \text{ mm} $$
$$ y_2 = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 5 — Tabulation:**

| **Part** | **Description** | **$A_i$ (mm²)** | **$x_i$ (mm)** | **$y_i$ (mm)** | **$A_i x_i$ (mm³)** | **$A_i y_i$ (mm³)** |
|:--------:|:----------------|:---------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | Flange | $3000$ | $0$ | $110$ | $0$ | $330{,}000$ |
| 2 | Web | $3000$ | $0$ | $50$ | $0$ | $150{,}000$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$6000$** | — | — | **$0$** | **$480{,}000$** |

#### Part (b): Final Centroid Computation (7 Marks)

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{0}{6000} = 0 \text{ mm} $$

This is expected because the T-section is **symmetric about the vertical centre line**.

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{480{,}000}{6000} = 80 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \boxed{\bar{x} = 0 \text{ mm}, \quad \bar{y} = 80 \text{ mm}} $$

The centroid is located on the vertical centre line, at a height of **80 mm above the bottom of the web**, which is **inside the web portion** (since the web is 100 mm tall and the flange starts at 100 mm). This is physically consistent with a T-section, where the centroid is closer to the flange because the flange carries significant area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for T-Sections:**
> 1. A common mistake is placing the centroid of the flange at $y_1 = 100$ mm (treating it as if it sits on the top of the web with zero thickness). The correct value is $y_1 = 100 + t_f/2$, where $t_f$ is flange thickness.
> 2. Some students incorrectly assume $\bar{y}$ must lie *inside the flange*. For a T-section with equal flange and web areas, $\bar{y}$ is actually at the **flange-web interface**, not inside the flange.
> 3. **Always re-state the answer with units** in the final box — KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Centroid of Composite Areas**

- **Definition**: The centroid is the *geometric centre* of a plane figure, computed as the *weighted average* of the centroids of its constituent parts, weighted by their areas.
- **Core Formulas** (must be memorized verbatim):
  - $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum A_i x_i}{\sum A_i}$
  - $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$
- **Units**: All distances in mm or m, areas in mm² or m², moments in mm³ or m³.
- **Standard Centroid Coordinates** (must know without referring to any table):
  - Rectangle: at $(b/2, h/2)$
  - Triangle: at $(b/2, h/3)$ from the base
  - Semicircle: at $\frac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,r$ from the diameter
  - Quarter circle: at $\frac{4r}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244\,r$ along each axis from the corner
  - Circle: at the geometric centre
- **Negative Area Rule**: Holes and cut-outs are assigned a **negative sign for area** only. Their centroid coordinates are still positive distances from the reference axes.
- **Symmetry Shortcut**: If the figure has an axis of symmetry, the centroid lies *on* that axis — use this to halve your work and as a verification.
- **Step-by-Step Method** (KTU-preferred): Choose axes → Decompose → Tabulate → Sum → Apply formula → State answer with units.
- **Foundation for Later Modules**: This topic is a **direct prerequisite** for computing the *Moment of Inertia* in Module 3, using the *Parallel Axis Theorem*. Master it now to save time later.
- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid**:
  1. Forgetting the negative sign for holes.
  2. Using the wrong centroid coordinate for triangles (use $h/3$ from base, **not** $h/2$).
  3. Forgetting to subtract flange/web overlap when decomposing complex sections.
  4. Missing units in the final answer.
  5. Not drawing a clean FBD — KTU examiners reward visual clarity with partial marks even when answers are slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moment of Inertia: Parallel Axis and Perpendicular Axis Theorems, Polar Moment of Inertia, Radius of Gyration, Mass Moment of Inertia of Ring and Disc

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Moment of Inertia (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moment of Inertia (I)** is a geometric property of a cross-section or a mass distribution that quantifies its **resistance to rotational motion** (for masses) or **resistance to bending/torsion** (for areas). It is the rotational analogue of mass in linear mechanics.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **GCEST103 - Engineering Mechanics (Module 2)**, Moment of Inertia is studied in **two distinct contexts**:

### (A) Area Moment of Inertia (Second Moment of Area)
For a planar area $A$ in the $xy$-plane, the second moment of area about the $x$-axis is mathematically defined as:

$$I_x = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

$$I_y = \int_A x^2 \, dA$$

- **Units:** $m^4$ or $mm^4$
- **Physical Meaning:** Measures how the area is distributed about a reference axis. Critical for **bending stress** ($\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$) and **deflection** calculations in structural design.

### (B) Mass Moment of Inertia
For a rigid body of mass $m$ having a differential mass element $dm$ at a perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis of rotation, the mass moment of inertia is:

$$I = \int_V r^2 \, dm$$

- **Units:** $kg \cdot m^2$
- **Physical Meaning:** Direct measure of **rotational inertia** (analogous to mass in $F = ma$). It appears in the rotational equation of motion $T = I\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is angular acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Key Distinction:** The term *"Moment of Inertia"* without qualifier in Module 2 generally refers to **Mass Moment of Inertia**, while **Area Moment of Inertia** is explicitly named. Students must be careful to use the correct formula in numerical problems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### 🍕 The Pizza & Bicycle Wheel Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **bicycle wheel** by its axle. Now imagine holding a **small spinning disc** (like a pizza cutter) at the same axle.

- The **bicycle wheel** is hard to start spinning and hard to stop, because most of its mass is concentrated at the **rim**, far from the axle.
- The **pizza disc** spins up and slows down easily, because its mass is distributed **closer to the axle**.

**This "resistance to change in rotation" is exactly what Moment of Inertia measures.**

> The further the mass is from the axis, the harder it is to rotate — and this is captured by the squared distance term ($r^2$) in the integral.

### Why is it $r^2$ and not $r$?

In **linear motion**, kinetic energy is $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$. For rotation, $v = r\omega$, so:

$$KE_{rot} = \frac{1}{2}m(r\omega)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(mr^2)\omega^2$$

The term in brackets, $mr^2$, is the moment of inertia. The **square** appears naturally because of how linear velocity scales with distance from the axis.

### Key Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Area Moment of Inertia units:** $m^4$, $cm^4$, $mm^4$
- **Mass Moment of Inertia units:** $kg \cdot m^2$
- **Standard acceleration of gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ (used when relating $W = mg$ to mass)
- **Radius of Gyration units:** $m$ (or $mm$ for area-based problems)

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Design (Shafts, Gears)** | Torque transmission: $T = I\alpha$ |
| **Civil Engineering (Beams)** | Bending stress and deflection: $\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$ |
| **Aerospace (Turbine Blades)** | Rotational dynamics and angular momentum |
| **Automotive (Flywheels, Braking)** | Energy storage: $E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ |
| **Robotics (Linkages)** | Motor torque sizing and acceleration profiles |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parallel Axis Theorem (PAT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** The moment of inertia of a body about any axis is equal to the **sum** of its moment of inertia about a **parallel axis passing through the centroid** and the **product of its mass (or area)** with the **square of the perpendicular distance** between the two axes.

This is the most heavily tested theorem in KTU Module 2 — used in nearly every numerical problem.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic (Mass Moment of Inertia)

1. Consider a rigid body of mass $M$ with centroid at $G$.
2. Let $G$ have a centroidal axis $G$ parallel to the reference axis $O$ at perpendicular distance $d$.
3. Set up coordinates: body-fixed frame $(x_G, y_G)$ at centroid, and global frame $(x, y)$ at $O$.
4. The relationship between coordinates: $x = x_G + d$ and $y = y_G$ (for the 2D projection case).
5. The definition gives: $I_O = \int r^2 \, dm$ where $r$ is distance from $O$.
6. Since $r^2 = r_G^2 + d^2 + 2 r_G d \cos\theta$ (by cosine rule on the moment-arm triangle).
7. The cross term $\int 2 r_G d \cos\theta \, dm = 0$ because the centroid is, by definition, the "balance point" of the mass distribution.
8. Result: $I_O = I_G + Md^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Area Moment of Inertia**, replace $M$ with $A$ (area) and $dm$ with $dA$:
> $I_x = I_{G,x} + A \cdot d^2$

### 2.1.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Parallel Axis Theorem is the **workhorse of KTU numerical problems**. Whenever a body rotates about a point that is NOT its centroid (e.g., a wheel rotating about an off-center hub, a pendulum about its pivot, a disc rolling on a surface), you **must** apply the Parallel Axis Theorem to compute the effective moment of inertia.

---

## 2.2 Perpendicular Axis Theorem (PAT-P)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** For a **planar lamina** (a flat 2D body of negligible thickness), the moment of inertia about an axis **perpendicular to the plane** of the lamina is equal to the **sum** of the moments of inertia about **two mutually perpendicular axes** lying in the plane of the lamina and intersecting at the point where the perpendicular axis passes.

$$I_z = I_x + I_y$$

### Critical KTU Restriction:
> [!WARNING]
> The Perpendicular Axis Theorem is **ONLY valid for planar (2D) laminae**. It **CANNOT** be applied to 3D solids like cubes, spheres, or cylinders. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU exams.

### Real-World Utility
Used to determine the **polar moment of inertia** ($I_z$) for circular sections where one of the in-plane moments of inertia is identical to the other due to symmetry (e.g., $I_x = I_y$ for a circle/square/diamond about centroid).

---

## 2.3 Polar Moment of Inertia (J)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polar Moment of Inertia** is the moment of inertia of a planar area about an axis **perpendicular to the plane** of the area and passing through a specific point (typically the origin or centroid).

$$J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA = I_x + I_y$$

For a **circular area**, due to symmetry: $I_x = I_y = I_d$ (moment about diameter).

$$J_O = 2 I_d$$

**Engineering Use:** Polar moment of inertia directly governs the **torsional rigidity** of a circular shaft:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L} = \frac{\tau}{r}$$

---

## 2.4 Radius of Gyration (k)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radius of Gyration (k)** is the **imaginary distance** from the axis at which the **entire mass (or area) could be assumed to be concentrated** such that the moment of inertia remains unchanged.

$$I = M k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{M}}$$

For Area: $I = A k^2 \Rightarrow k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$

**Physical Meaning:** It represents an "effective" distance. A higher $k$ means the mass is spread farther from the axis.

**Units:** $m$ (for mass-based) or $mm$ (for area-based)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** summary for KTU ESE Module 2. Every formula here has appeared in past KTU papers.

### Table 1: Area Moment of Inertia (Standard Shapes)

| Shape | Centroidal $I_x$ (or $I_y$) | Polar $J_O$ | Radius of Gyration $k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular Area** ($b \times h$) | $\dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{bh(b^2+h^2)}{12}$ | $\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{12}}$ |
| **Solid Circle (Disc)** (radius $r$) | $\dfrac{\pi r^4}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi r^4}{2}$ | $\dfrac{r}{2}$ |
| **Hollow Circle (Ring)** ($r_o, r_i$) | $\dfrac{\pi (r_o^4 - r_i^4)}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (r_o^4 - r_i^4)}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{r_o^2 + r_i^2}}{2}$ |
| **Triangular Area** (base $b$, height $h$) | $\dfrac{bh^3}{36}$ | — | $\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{18}}$ |
| **Thin Ring** ($r_i \to r_o = r$) | $r^2 \cdot A = \pi r^4$ | $2\pi r^4$ | $r$ |

### Table 2: Mass Moment of Inertia (Standard Bodies)

| Body | Axis of Rotation | Mass Moment of Inertia $I$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin Ring** (mass $M$, radius $r$) | About central axis (perpendicular) | $Mr^2$ |
| **Thin Ring** (mass $M$, radius $r$) | About diameter | $\dfrac{Mr^2}{2}$ |
| **Thin Disc** (mass $M$, radius $r$) | About central axis (perpendicular) | $\dfrac{Mr^2}{2}$ |
| **Thin Disc** (mass $M$, radius $r$) | About diameter | $\dfrac{Mr^4}{4}$ (area) or $\dfrac{Mr^2}{4}$ (mass) |
| **Solid Sphere** (mass $M$, radius $r$) | About any diameter | $\dfrac{2Mr^2}{5}$ |
| **Solid Cylinder** (mass $M$, $r$) | About longitudinal axis | $\dfrac{Mr^2}{2}$ |
| **Thin Rod** (mass $M$, length $L$) | About center, perpendicular | $\dfrac{ML^2}{12}$ |
| **Thin Rod** (mass $M$, length $L$) | About end, perpendicular | $\dfrac{ML^2}{3}$ |

### Table 3: Theorem Summary (Reference Card)

| Theorem | Formula | Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Axis Theorem** | $I_O = I_G + Md^2$ | Any rigid body (2D or 3D) |
| **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** | $I_z = I_x + I_y$ | **ONLY** planar laminae |
| **Radius of Gyration** | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{M}}$ | Any body |
| **Polar Moment Relation** | $J = I_x + I_y$ | Any planar figure |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **complete, exhaustive derivations**. Every algebraic step is shown — no skips, no placeholders. All students preparing for KTU ESE must understand these derivations line-by-line.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Axis Theorem (Mass Form)

**Given:** A rigid body of total mass $M$ with centroid $G$. Two parallel axes: one through the centroid (axis $G$) and one through an arbitrary point $O$ at perpendicular distance $d$.

**Goal:** Prove that $I_O = I_G + Md^2$.

### Step 1: Set Up the Coordinate System
Let the centroid $G$ be the origin of a body-fixed coordinate system $(X, Y)$. Let $O$ be the origin of a global coordinate system $(x, y)$, displaced by distance $d$ from $G$ along the $x$-direction.

For any differential mass element $dm$ at point $P$:
- Coordinates in the $G$-frame: $(X, Y)$
- Coordinates in the $O$-frame: $(x, y)$

The displacement vector from $G$ to $O$ is $(d, 0)$.

**Coordinate Transformation:**

$$x = X + d$$

$$y = Y$$

### Step 2: Write the Moment of Inertia About Axis O
The moment of inertia about an axis is the integral of the squared perpendicular distance from the axis. For an axis perpendicular to the plane through $O$:

$$I_O = \int_M (x^2 + y^2) \, dm$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Transformation
Replace $x$ and $y$ with their expressions in the $G$-frame:

$$I_O = \int_M \left[(X + d)^2 + Y^2\right] dm$$

### Step 4: Expand the Squared Binomial
Expand $(X + d)^2$ using the standard algebraic identity $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$I_O = \int_M \left[X^2 + 2Xd + d^2 + Y^2\right] dm$$

### Step 5: Distribute the Integral Over the Four Terms
Since integration is a linear operator, we can split the integral into separate terms:

$$I_O = \int_M X^2 \, dm + \int_M 2Xd \, dm + \int_M d^2 \, dm + \int_M Y^2 \, dm$$

### Step 6: Evaluate Each Integral Term-by-Term

**Term 1:** $\int_M X^2 \, dm$ — This is the moment of inertia of the body about the $Y$-axis through $G$. Denote as $I_{G, y}$ but in 2D, $I_{G, y} = I_{G,x}$ (it's the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis perpendicular to the plane we are using, i.e., the polar moment at $G$).

**Term 2:** $\int_M 2Xd \, dm = 2d \int_M X \, dm$. Now, $\int_M X \, dm$ is the **first moment of mass** about the $Y$-axis through $G$. Since the centroid is located at $X = 0$ in the body-fixed frame (by definition of centroid), this integral equals **zero**:

$$\int_M X \, dm = M \cdot \bar{X} = M \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Term 3:** $\int_M d^2 \, dm$. Here, $d$ is a constant (the distance between axes), so it comes out of the integral:

$$\int_M d^2 \, dm = d^2 \int_M dm = d^2 \cdot M = Md^2$$

**Term 4:** $\int_M Y^2 \, dm$ — This is the moment of inertia about the $X$-axis through $G$. Combined with Term 1, it gives the **polar moment of inertia at the centroid**:

$$\int_M (X^2 + Y^2) \, dm = I_G$$

### Step 7: Combine All Terms

$$\boxed{I_O = I_G + Md^2}$$

**Q.E.D. — Parallel Axis Theorem is Proved.**

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical insight** in this derivation is Step 6, Term 2. The vanishing of the cross term $\int X \, dm$ is what makes the centroid the *unique* "natural axis" of the body. This is the deep reason why the centroidal axis minimizes the moment of inertia.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Perpendicular Axis Theorem

**Given:** A planar lamina lying in the $xy$-plane. The lamina has area $A$, and we are interested in three axes:
- $x$-axis (in the plane)
- $y$-axis (in the plane, perpendicular to $x$)
- $z$-axis (perpendicular to the plane, intersecting $x$ and $y$ at the origin $O$)

**Goal:** Prove that $I_z = I_x + I_y$.

### Step 1: Definition of Polar Moment of Inertia
By definition, the polar moment of inertia about the $z$-axis (perpendicular to the lamina) is:

$$I_z = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

where $r$ is the perpendicular distance from $O$ to the differential area $dA$.

### Step 2: Express r in Terms of x and y
For any point $(x, y)$ in the $xy$-plane, the distance to the origin is given by the Pythagorean theorem:

$$r^2 = x^2 + y^2$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Polar Moment

$$I_z = \int_A (x^2 + y^2) \, dA$$

### Step 4: Distribute the Integral

$$I_z = \int_A x^2 \, dA + \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

### Step 5: Identify the Two In-Plane Moments

By definition:
- $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I_x$ (moment of inertia about $x$-axis, since $y$ is the perpendicular distance)
- $\int_A x^2 \, dA = I_y$ (moment of inertia about $y$-axis, since $x$ is the perpendicular distance)

### Step 6: Write the Final Relationship

$$\boxed{I_z = I_x + I_y}$$

**Q.E.D. — Perpendicular Axis Theorem is Proved.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this fails for 3D bodies:** In a 3D body, the point $(x, y, z)$ would have $r^2 = x^2 + y^2 + z^2$. Integrating over the full volume gives $I_z = \int (x^2 + y^2 + z^2) \, dm$, which is the sum of three in-plane moments — but only the first two ($I_x$ and $I_y$) can be related to 2D area integrals. The third term $\int z^2 \, dm$ is the polar moment about an axis in the plane, not in the perpendicular direction. Hence, the theorem does NOT hold for 3D bodies.

---

## 3.3 Mass Moment of Inertia of a Thin Ring (Slender Hoop)

**Definition:** A thin ring is a circular hoop of mass $M$ and radius $R$ where the cross-sectional dimensions are negligibly small compared to $R$. All the mass is essentially concentrated at distance $R$ from the central axis.

### Case 1: About Central Axis (Perpendicular to Plane)

Every mass element $dm$ is at distance $r = R$ from the central axis.

$$I = \int_M r^2 \, dm = \int_M R^2 \, dm = R^2 \int_M dm = R^2 \cdot M$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{central}} = MR^2}$$

### Case 2: About a Diametral Axis (In-Plane)

By symmetry, the moments about any two perpendicular diametral axes are equal:
$I_x = I_y$.

Apply the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** (since the ring is a planar lamina):

$$I_z = I_x + I_y = 2I_x$$

We know $I_z = MR^2$ (from Case 1), so:

$$I_x = \frac{MR^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{diameter}} = \frac{MR^2}{2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Mass Moment of Inertia of a Solid Disc (Circular Plate)

**Definition:** A uniform solid disc of mass $M$, radius $R$, and uniform thickness $t$, with surface mass density $\sigma = \frac{M}{\pi R^2}$.

### Case 1: About Central Axis (Perpendicular to Plane)

Consider a differential ring element at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$dm = \sigma \, dA = \frac{M}{\pi R^2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2M r}{R^2} \, dr$$

The moment of inertia contribution from this ring (using the thin-ring result) is:

$$dI = r^2 \, dm = r^2 \cdot \frac{2M r}{R^2} \, dr = \frac{2M}{R^2} \cdot r^3 \, dr$$

Integrate from $r = 0$ to $r = R$:

$$I = \int_0^R \frac{2M}{R^2} r^3 \, dr = \frac{2M}{R^2} \cdot \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{2M}{R^2} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{central}} = \frac{MR^2}{2}}$$

### Case 2: About a Diametral Axis (In-Plane)

By symmetry: $I_x = I_y$. Apply the Perpendicular Axis Theorem:

$$I_z = I_x + I_y = 2I_x$$

$$I_x = \frac{I_z}{2} = \frac{MR^2}{4}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{diameter}} = \frac{MR^2}{4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of PAT for Disc:** The disc is symmetric, so $I_G = \frac{MR^2}{2}$ about the center. If we compute the moment about a point on the rim (distance $d = R$):
> $I_{\text{rim}} = I_G + Md^2 = \frac{MR^2}{2} + MR^2 = \frac{3MR^2}{2}$. This matches the standard result for a disc rotating about a point on its rim. **Test this in your KTU exam!**

---

## 3.5 Mass Moment of Inertia of a Solid Sphere (Bonus Derivation — Frequently Asked)

Although not explicitly in the topic title, KTU often links this for **comparative analysis questions**.

The disc method is extended: a sphere is treated as a stack of infinitesimal discs.

For a solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$:

$$I = \frac{2}{5}MR^2$$

**For a Hollow Sphere (thin spherical shell):**

$$I = \frac{2}{3}MR^2$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Theorem (Decision Tree)

| Scenario | Use This Theorem | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Body rotating about an off-centroid axis | **Parallel Axis Theorem** | Need to shift from centroidal to arbitrary axis |
| Planar shape, need polar moment from in-plane | **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** | Sum of two in-plane moments gives polar |
| Need to express moment in terms of a single parameter $k$ | **Radius of Gyration** | $I = Mk^2$ |
| Solid cylinder rotating about its own central axis | Direct Formula: $I = \frac{MR^2}{2}$ | No theorem needed (it's the standard case) |
| Ring rotating about a tangent line | **Parallel Axis Theorem** | $d = R$; $I = MR^2 + MR^2 = 2MR^2$ |
| Disc with off-center hole (annulus) | **Subtract** two discs | Use $I_{\text{ring}} = I_{\text{outer}} - I_{\text{inner}}$ |
| Need moment of inertia of a composite section | **Additivity of $I$** + PAT | Sum components, then shift axis if needed |

---

## 3.7 Mass Moment of Inertia of a Hollow Ring (Annular Disc) — Composite Method

**Problem:** Find the mass moment of inertia of a hollow disc of outer radius $R_o$, inner radius $R_i$, and total mass $M$ about its central axis perpendicular to the face.

**Solution Strategy:** Treat the hollow disc as **(Solid outer disc) − (Solid inner disc removed)**.

### Step 1: Mass Distribution
Mass of solid outer disc (if filled) of radius $R_o$: $M_{\text{full,outer}} = \sigma \pi R_o^2$
Mass of solid inner disc (removed) of radius $R_i$: $M_{\text{inner}} = \sigma \pi R_i^2$

Actual mass of annulus: $M = M_{\text{outer}} - M_{\text{inner}} = \sigma \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$

### Step 2: Moment of Inertia of Each Part

For the (hypothetical) full outer disc:
$$I_{\text{outer}} = \frac{M_{\text{outer}} R_o^2}{2} = \frac{\sigma \pi R_o^2 \cdot R_o^2}{2} = \frac{\sigma \pi R_o^4}{2}$$

For the removed inner disc:
$$I_{\text{inner}} = \frac{M_{\text{inner}} R_i^2}{2} = \frac{\sigma \pi R_i^2 \cdot R_i^2}{2} = \frac{\sigma \pi R_i^4}{2}$$

### Step 3: Subtract

$$I_{\text{ring}} = I_{\text{outer}} - I_{\text{inner}} = \frac{\sigma \pi}{2} (R_o^4 - R_i^4)$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Actual Mass $M$

From Step 1: $\sigma \pi = \frac{M}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$

Substitute:

$$I_{\text{ring}} = \frac{M}{R_o^2 - R_i^2} \cdot \frac{R_o^4 - R_i^4}{2}$$

Factor: $R_o^4 - R_i^4 = (R_o^2 - R_i^2)(R_o^2 + R_i^2)$

$$I_{\text{ring}} = \frac{M (R_o^2 - R_i^2)(R_o^2 + R_i^2)}{2(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} = \frac{M(R_o^2 + R_i^2)}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{annulus, central}} = \frac{M(R_o^2 + R_i^2)}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** For a thin ring where $R_i \to R_o = R$: $I = \frac{M(2R^2)}{2} = MR^2$. ✓ Matches the thin-ring result. **This is a KTU-favorite verification question!**

```mermaid
    F --> F2["Power Grids<br/>Frequency Regulation"]
    F --> F3["Mechanical Punch Press<br/>Energy Buffer"]
    F --> F4["Satellite Attitude Control<br/>Momentum Wheels"]
```

---

## 4.6 Problem-Solving Decision Tree (For KTU Numerical Problems)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["START: Find Moment of Inertia"]
    S --> Q1["Is the body standard<br/>ring disc sphere<br/>cylinder or rod?"]
    Q1 -- YES --> S1["Use standard formula<br/>directly from table"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2["Is it a composite of<br/>standard shapes?"]
    Q2 -- YES --> S2["Compute I for each part<br/>Apply Parallel Axis Theorem<br/>for off-center parts<br/>Sum all contributions"]
    Q2 -- NO --> S3["Use direct integration<br/>dI equals r squared dm<br/>over the body"]

    S1 --> Q3["Is the rotation axis<br/>NOT through centroid?"]
    S2 --> Q3
    S3 --> Q3

    Q3 -- YES --> APPLY["APPLY PARALLEL AXIS THEOREM<br/>I about O equals I about G plus M d squared"]
    Q3 -- NO --> DONE["FINAL ANSWER<br/>I about Centroid"]

    APPLY --> DONE
```
