# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled **exactly** on the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for GCEST103 Engineering Mechanics. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference. Mark distribution follows the official KTU pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks with internal choice, split 7+7)**.

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define:**
(a) Radius of Gyration
(b) Polar Moment of Inertia

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Radius of Gyration (k):** It is the imaginary distance from the reference axis at which the entire mass of the body may be assumed to be concentrated so that the moment of inertia of the body about that axis remains unchanged.

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{M}}$$

For area: $k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$. Units: $m$ (mass-based) or $mm$ (area-based).

**(b) Polar Moment of Inertia ($J_O$):** It is the moment of inertia of a planar area about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the area and passing through a given point (usually the origin or centroid).

$$J_O = \int_A r^2 \, dA = I_x + I_y$$

**Units:** $m^4$ or $mm^4$. **Engineering Use:** Torsional rigidity of circular shafts.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the Perpendicular Axis Theorem. Why is it applicable only for planar laminae?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For a planar lamina lying in the $xy$-plane, the moment of inertia about an axis perpendicular to the plane ($I_z$) is equal to the sum of the moments of inertia about two mutually perpendicular axes ($I_x$ and $I_y$) lying in the plane of the lamina and intersecting at the point where the perpendicular axis passes.

$$I_z = I_x + I_y$$

**Why only for planar laminae:** The theorem is derived using $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ (Pythagoras), which is valid only when all points of the body have $z = 0$. For 3D bodies, an additional term $\int z^2 \, dm$ appears that cannot be expressed as a simple planar moment. Hence the theorem fails for 3D solids like spheres and cylinders.

---

## PART B: Full Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **internal choice**: you must answer **either Question A OR Question B** in full. Each sub-part is worth 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**A.** (a) State and prove the Parallel Axis Theorem for mass moment of inertia. (7 marks)

(b) A solid disc of mass $12 \, kg$ and radius $0.4 \, m$ is mounted such that it rotates about a point on its rim. The axis of rotation is perpendicular to the plane of the disc. Calculate the moment of inertia of the disc about this axis. (7 marks)

#### Solution to A(a):

**Statement:** The moment of inertia of a body about any axis is equal to the sum of its moment of inertia about a parallel centroidal axis and the product of its mass with the square of the perpendicular distance between the two axes.

$$I_O = I_G + Md^2$$

**Proof:** [Refer to the complete derivation in Section 3.1, Steps 1–7. The student should reproduce the full derivation with the coordinate transformation, integral expansion, and the cancellation of the cross-term using the centroid property.]

For a 7-mark answer, the student should:
- Draw a clear diagram with the body, centroid G, and the parallel axes O and G (1 mark)
- Write the coordinate transformation $x = X + d$ (1 mark)
- Write the definition $I_O = \int (x^2 + y^2) \, dm$ (1 mark)
- Expand the integral into 4 terms (2 marks)
- Show that $\int X \, dm = 0$ using centroid definition (1 mark)
- Combine and write final result (1 mark)

#### Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- Mass of disc: $M = 12 \, kg$
- Radius: $R = 0.4 \, m$
- Axis: Perpendicular to plane, passing through a point on the rim
- Distance from centroid to rim: $d = R = 0.4 \, m$

**Step 1:** Moment of inertia of a solid disc about the centroidal axis (perpendicular to plane):

$$I_G = \frac{MR^2}{2} = \frac{12 \times (0.4)^2}{2}$$

$$I_G = \frac{12 \times 0.16}{2} = \frac{1.92}{2} = 0.96 \, kg \cdot m^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem. The rim point is at distance $d = R$ from the centroid:

$$I_O = I_G + Md^2$$

$$I_O = 0.96 + 12 \times (0.4)^2$$

$$I_O = 0.96 + 12 \times 0.16 = 0.96 + 1.92$$

$$\boxed{I_O = 2.88 \, kg \cdot m^2}$$

**Verification using direct formula:** For a disc about a point on its rim, the standard result is:

$$I_{\text{rim}} = \frac{3MR^2}{2} = \frac{3 \times 12 \times 0.16}{2} = \frac{5.76}{2} = 2.88 \, kg \cdot m^2 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**B.** (a) Derive the expression for the mass moment of inertia of a solid disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about:
    (i) An axis perpendicular to the plane and passing through the center.
    (ii) A diameter of the disc. (7 marks)

(b) A thin ring of mass $5 \, kg$ and radius $0.5 \, m$ is welded to a thin rod of mass $3 \, kg$ and length $1.2 \, m$ such that the rod passes through the center of the ring and lies in the plane of the ring. Find the moment of inertia of the combined system about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the ring and passing through one end of the rod. (7 marks)

#### Solution to B(a):

**(i) Central axis perpendicular to plane:**

Consider a differential ring of radius $r$, thickness $dr$. Its area: $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. Mass per unit area: $\sigma = \frac{M}{\pi R^2}$.

$$dm = \sigma \, dA = \frac{2Mr}{R^2} \, dr$$

$$dI = r^2 \, dm = \frac{2M r^3}{R^2} \, dr$$

$$I = \int_0^R \frac{2M r^3}{R^2} dr = \frac{2M}{R^2} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{MR^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{central}} = \frac{MR^2}{2}}$$

**(ii) About a diameter:**

By symmetry, $I_x = I_y$. Apply the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem:**

$$I_z = I_x + I_y = 2 I_x$$

$$I_x = \frac{I_z}{2} = \frac{MR^2}{4}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{diameter}} = \frac{MR^2}{4}}$$

#### Solution to B(b):

**Setup:** Let the rod lie along the $x$-axis, with the ring centered at the midpoint. Let the system rotate about the $z$-axis (perpendicular to the ring's plane) through one end of the rod.

**Identify distances from the rotation axis:**

- **Ring:** The ring's center is at the midpoint of the rod, distance $d_1 = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6 \, m$ from the rotation axis.
- **Rod:** The rod's center is at the midpoint, distance $d_2 = \frac{L}{2} = 0.6 \, m$ from the rotation axis.

**Step 1:** Moment of inertia of the ring about its own centroidal axis (perpendicular to plane):

$$I_{\text{ring, G}} = M_{\text{ring}} R^2 = 5 \times (0.5)^2 = 5 \times 0.25 = 1.25 \, kg \cdot m^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply Parallel Axis Theorem for the ring about the end of the rod:

$$I_{\text{ring, end}} = I_{\text{ring, G}} + M_{\text{ring}} d_1^2 = 1.25 + 5 \times (0.6)^2$$

$$I_{\text{ring, end}} = 1.25 + 5 \times 0.36 = 1.25 + 1.80 = 3.05 \, kg \cdot m^2$$

**Step 3:** Moment of inertia of the rod about its own center (perpendicular axis):

$$I_{\text{rod, G}} = \frac{M_{\text{rod}} L^2}{12} = \frac{3 \times (1.2)^2}{12} = \frac{3 \times 1.44}{12} = \frac{4.32}{12} = 0.36 \, kg \cdot m^2$$

**Step 4:** Apply Parallel Axis Theorem for the rod about the end:

$$I_{\text{rod, end}} = I_{\text{rod, G}} + M_{\text{rod}} d_2^2 = 0.36 + 3 \times (0.6)^2$$

$$I_{\text{rod, end}} = 0.36 + 3 \times 0.36 = 0.36 + 1.08 = 1.44 \, kg \cdot m^2$$

**Step 5:** Total moment of inertia of the combined system (by additivity):

$$I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{ring, end}} + I_{\text{rod, end}}$$

$$I_{\text{total}} = 3.05 + 1.44$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{total}} = 4.49 \, kg \cdot m^2}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in KTU ESE Module 2 — Moment of Inertia Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Area vs. Mass Moment of Inertia:** Students often write $\frac{\pi r^4}{4}$ (area formula) when the question asks for mass moment. **Always check** what is being asked: if mass is given, use $\frac{Mr^2}{2}$; if only dimensions are given, use area formulas.
>
> 2. **Misapplying the Perpendicular Axis Theorem to 3D bodies:** If a question asks for the moment of inertia of a *solid cylinder* about its axis, do **NOT** use $I_z = I_x + I_y$. The cylinder is a 3D body. Use the direct formula $I = \frac{MR^2}{2}$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the distance in PAT:** When the body rotates about a point **not** at its centroid, students frequently use $I_G$ directly as the answer, missing the $Md^2$ term. This costs **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 4. **Wrong $d$ in PAT:** The distance $d$ is the **perpendicular distance between the two parallel axes**, not the slant distance. Draw a clear perpendicular line in the diagram.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $cm$ with $m$ in the same calculation is a guaranteed **1-mark** loss. Standardize units at the very start of the problem.
>
> 6. **Not adding components in composite problems:** In a system of multiple bodies, the *total* moment of inertia is the **sum** of individual moments. Forgetting to sum is a frequent error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section. Read it 10 minutes before the exam.**

### 🎯 The 4 Must-Know Theorems
- ✅ **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $I_O = I_G + Md^2$ — applicable to **any rigid body** (2D or 3D).
- ✅ **Perpendicular Axis Theorem:** $I_z = I_x + I_y$ — applicable **ONLY to planar laminae**.
- ✅ **Polar Moment:** $J = I_x + I_y$ — used for **torsion of circular shafts**.
- ✅ **Radius of Gyration:** $k = \sqrt{I/M}$ or $\sqrt{I/A}$ — a "characteristic distance" parameter.

### 🎯 The 5 Must-Memorize Standard Bodies
- ✅ **Thin Ring (perpendicular axis):** $I = MR^2$
- ✅ **Thin Ring (diameter):** $I = \frac{MR^2}{2}$
- ✅ **Solid Disc (perpendicular axis):** $I = \frac{MR^2}{2}$
- ✅ **Solid Disc (diameter):** $I = \frac{MR^2}{4}$
- ✅ **Thin Rod (about end):** $I = \frac{ML^2}{3}$  | **(about center):** $I = \frac{ML^2}{12}$

### 🎯 The 3 High-Frequency Problem Types in KTU
- ✅ **Direct application of standard formula** — read the question, identify the body and axis, plug into table.
- ✅ **Off-axis rotation** — apply PAT ($I_O = I_G + Md^2$) to shift the reference axis.
- ✅ **Composite bodies (annulus, multi-part system)** — use additivity of $I$ and PAT for off-center parts.

### 🎯 Common Sense Checks Before Submission
- ✅ Are units consistent (SI: $kg \cdot m^2$)?
- ✅ Did you include the $Md^2$ term in PAT?
- ✅ Did you verify whether the body is **planar** (PAT-P applies) or **3D** (only PAT applies)?
- ✅ Does the answer have the right **order of magnitude**? (e.g., for $M = 10 \, kg$, $R = 0.5 \, m$, $I$ should be on the order of $1$ to $5 \, kg \cdot m^2$, not $10^6$ or $10^{-3}$).

### 🎯 One-Line Memory Hooks
- **"Ring: M-R-squared, all the way."** — $MR^2$ for central axis.
- **"Disc: Half of ring, perpendicular; Quarter of ring, diameter."** — $\frac{MR^2}{2}$ vs $\frac{MR^2}{4}$.
- **"PAT moves the axis. PAT-P does not move — it relates three."**
- **"Tangent point on rim: three-halves MR squared."** — $I = \frac{3MR^2}{2}$ (disc rotating about rim).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamics** is the branch of engineering mechanics that deals with the study of bodies in motion under the action of forces. Unlike Statics (Module 1 & 2), where bodies are in equilibrium, Dynamics explicitly accounts for the time-dependent behaviour of particles and rigid bodies.

The discipline of Dynamics is formally bifurcated into two foundational sub-branches:

1. **Kinematics** – The geometric study of motion. It describes *how* a body moves (displacement, velocity, acceleration) without reference to the forces causing the motion. The **mass** and **force** parameters are entirely absent.

2. **Kinetics** – The physical study of motion. It relates the *cause* (force) to the *effect* (motion) using **Newton's Laws of Motion**. Here, the **mass** of the body becomes a critical parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** Students are expected to master the kinematics of rectilinear and curvilinear motion, the kinetics of particles using Newton's laws and the work–energy theorem, impulse–momentum principles, and the dynamics of circular motion including D'Alembert's principle for rigid bodies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a cricket ball being bowled.

- **Kinematics** is your *commentator's description*: "The ball is travelling at 140 km/h, pitched 6 m from the batsman, and dipped sharply." You are only describing the motion — speed, direction, trajectory.

- **Kinetics** is the *bowler's biomechanics and physics*: "The bowler applied 250 N of force through a 2 m arm rotation, the ball has a mass of 0.156 kg, so it left the hand with that acceleration." You are now explaining *why* the ball moves the way it does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Dynamics:**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** (standard, near Earth's surface)
> - **Mass (m)** measured in **kilograms (kg)**
> - **Force (F)** measured in **newtons (N)**, where 1 N = 1 kg·m/s²
> - **Work / Energy** in **joules (J)**, where 1 J = 1 N·m
> - **Power** in **watts (W)**, where 1 W = 1 J/s
> - **Momentum (p)** in **kg·m/s**

### 1.3 Classification of Motion in Dynamics

Motion can be broadly classified along two axes:

| Classification Axis | Types |
|---|---|
| **Path of motion** | Rectilinear (straight line), Curvilinear (curved path), Projectile (parabolic) |
| **Type of motion** | Translation, Rotation, General plane motion |
| **Acceleration profile** | Uniform (constant velocity), Uniformly accelerated, Variable acceleration |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rectilinear Motion (Motion along a Straight Line)

A particle is said to undergo rectilinear motion when it travels along a single straight line. Let **s** be the displacement, **v** the velocity, and **a** the acceleration along the line. All are functions of time **t**.

**The fundamental relationships are:**

- **Velocity** is the time rate of change of displacement:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

- **Acceleration** is the time rate of change of velocity:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^{2}s}{dt^{2}}$$

**Case A — Uniform Velocity (a = 0):**
When acceleration is zero, velocity remains constant. The displacement varies linearly with time.

$$s = s_0 + vt$$

**Case B — Uniformly Accelerated Motion (a = constant):**
This is the most-tested case in KTU examinations. The governing equations are:

$$v = u + at$$
$$s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^{2}$$
$$v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$$

Where **u** is the initial velocity, **v** is the final velocity, **a** is the constant acceleration, **s** is the displacement, and **t** is the time elapsed.

> [!NOTE]
> These three equations are called the **Equations of Motion** (also known as **Kinematic Equations**). They are *scalar* forms (no vector arrows) when the motion is along a straight, known direction. The KTU board examiner will deduct marks if a student forgets to specify *direction* in curvilinear applications.

### 2.2 Motion under Gravity (Free Fall & Vertical Projection)

When a body moves vertically under the influence of gravity alone (air resistance neglected), the constant acceleration is **g = 9.81 m/s²** directed downward.

- For a body **dropped from rest**: $u = 0$, $a = +g$ (taking downward as positive).
- For a body **thrown upward**: $u$ is positive (upward), and $a = -g$ (deceleration).

**Key facts:**
- Time to reach maximum height = $\frac{u}{g}$
- Maximum height reached = $\frac{u^{2}}{2g}$
- Total time of flight (up and back) = $\frac{2u}{g}$

### 2.3 Projectile Motion

A projectile is a body launched into space and allowed to move under gravity alone. Its path is a **parabola**. Resolving into two independent perpendicular components:

| Component | Initial Value | Acceleration |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal (x) | $u_x = u\cos\theta$ | $a_x = 0$ |
| Vertical (y) | $u_y = u\sin\theta$ | $a_y = -g$ |

**Critical derived results:**

- **Time of flight** $T = \frac{2u\sin\theta}{g}$
- **Maximum height** $H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}$
- **Horizontal range** $R = \frac{u^{2}\sin 2\theta}{g}$
- Maximum range occurs at $\theta = 45°$, giving $R_{max} = \frac{u^{2}}{g}$

### 2.4 Newton's Laws of Motion (The Bedrock of Kinetics)

- **First Law (Law of Inertia):** A body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force. → This defines **force** as the cause of *change* in motion.

- **Second Law:** The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the net applied force and occurs in the direction of that force.

$$\vec{F} = m\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$$

where $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$ is the linear momentum.

- **Third Law:** For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. → The basis of free-body diagrams in Kinetics.

### 2.5 Work, Power & Energy Theorem

- **Work done** by a constant force over a displacement: $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s} = Fs\cos\phi$
- **Kinetic Energy** of a moving particle: $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$
- **Work–Energy Theorem:** $W_{net} = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv_{2}^{2} - \frac{1}{2}mv_{1}^{2}$
- **Power:** $P = \frac{dW}{dt} = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}$ (measured in **W**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Potential Energy (PE):** $PE = mgh$, where **h** is the height above a reference datum. Under conservative forces (like gravity), the **Law of Conservation of Mechanical Energy** holds: $KE + PE = $ constant.

### 2.6 Impulse and Momentum

- **Linear impulse** of a force over a time interval: $\vec{J} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F}\, dt$
- **Impulse–Momentum Theorem:** $\vec{J} = m\vec{v}_2 - m\vec{v}_1 = \Delta \vec{p}$

For a **constant force**, $\vec{J} = \vec{F} \cdot \Delta t$.

- **Law of Conservation of Linear Momentum:** When the net external force on a system is zero, the total linear momentum remains constant. This principle governs **collisions** and **rocket propulsion**.

### 2.7 Collision of Elastic Bodies

When two bodies collide, the collision is classified by the **coefficient of restitution, e**, defined as:

$$e = \frac{\text{relative velocity of separation}}{\text{relative velocity of approach}} = -\frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_2 - u_1}$$

- **Perfectly elastic collision:** e = 1 (kinetic energy conserved)
- **Perfectly inelastic collision:** e = 0 (bodies stick together)
- **Partially elastic:** 0 < e < 1

### 2.8 Circular Motion (Kinematics of Rotation)

For a particle moving in a circle of radius **r** with angular velocity **ω** and angular acceleration **α**:

- Linear velocity: $v = r\omega$
- Tangential acceleration: $a_t = r\alpha$
- Normal (centripetal) acceleration: $a_n = \frac{v^{2}}{r} = r\omega^{2}$ (always directed toward the centre)

### 2.9 D'Alembert's Principle

D'Alembert's principle reduces a **dynamic problem** into an equivalent **static problem** by introducing a fictitious **inertia force** (equal and opposite to the mass-acceleration product).

$$\vec{F}_{net} + (-m\vec{a}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{F}_{net} = m\vec{a}$$

The term $-m\vec{a}$ is called the **inertia force** or **reversed effective force**. By treating the inertia force as a real force, we can apply static equilibrium equations ($\Sigma F = 0$) to a body in motion. This is the gateway concept to **vibrations** and **machine dynamics** in higher semesters.

### 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity (instantaneous) | $v = ds/dt$ | m/s |
| 2 | Acceleration (instantaneous) | $a = dv/dt$ | m/s² |
| 3 | Uniform acceleration 1 | $v = u + at$ | m/s |
| 4 | Uniform acceleration 2 | $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$ | m |
| 5 | Uniform acceleration 3 | $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$ | (m/s)² |
| 6 | Projectile — Time of flight | $T = 2u\sin\theta / g$ | s |
| 7 | Projectile — Max height | $H = u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta / 2g$ | m |
| 8 | Projectile — Range | $R = u^{2}\sin 2\theta / g$ | m |
| 9 | Newton's 2nd law | $F = ma$ | N |
| 10 | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ | J |
| 11 | Work–Energy Theorem | $W_{net} = \Delta KE$ | J |
| 12 | Power | $P = Fv$ | W |
| 13 | Linear Impulse | $J = F \cdot \Delta t$ | N·s |
| 14 | Impulse–Momentum | $J = m(v_2 - v_1)$ | kg·m/s |
| 15 | Coefficient of restitution | $e = \vert v_2 - v_1 \vert / \vert u_2 - u_1 \vert$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Centripetal acceleration | $a_n = v^{2}/r$ | m/s² |
| 17 | Tangential acceleration | $a_t = r\alpha$ | m/s² |
| 18 | D'Alembert inertia force | $F_{inertia} = -ma$ | N |
| 19 | Angular velocity (linear) | $v = r\omega$ | m/s |
| 20 | Conservation of Energy | $KE_1 + PE_1 = KE_2 + PE_2$ | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Dynamics is the foundation of vehicle crashworthiness design (impulse–momentum), rocket trajectory optimization (projectile + Newton's law), turbomachinery (circular motion), sports biomechanics, robotics (D'Alembert), and structural earthquake engineering (inertia forces from $F = ma$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Three Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion

**Starting premise:** Consider a particle moving along a straight line with **constant acceleration a**. Let its initial velocity at time $t = 0$ be **u**, and let its velocity at time $t = t$ be **v**. Let the displacement in this time interval be **s**.

**Step 1 — Definition of acceleration.**
By definition, acceleration is the time rate of change of velocity.

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Rearranging: $dv = a\, dt$.

**Step 2 — Integration with constant acceleration.**
Since $a$ is constant, we can integrate both sides between the initial and final states.

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv = a \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

This yields:

$$v - u = a t$$

Hence, the **first equation of motion:**

$$v = u + a t \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 3 — Definition of velocity.**
Velocity is the time rate of change of displacement.

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

Rearranging: $ds = v\, dt$.

**Step 4 — Substitute v from Equation (I) and integrate.**

$$\int_{0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} (u + a t)\, dt$$

Carrying out the integration term by term:

$$s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$$

Hence, the **second equation of motion:**

$$s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate t to obtain the third equation.**
From Equation (I): $t = \dfrac{v - u}{a}$. Substituting into Equation (II):

$$s = u \left(\frac{v - u}{a}\right) + \tfrac{1}{2}a \left(\frac{v - u}{a}\right)^{2}$$

Expanding the squared term:

$$s = \frac{u(v - u)}{a} + \tfrac{1}{2}a \cdot \frac{(v - u)^{2}}{a^{2}} = \frac{u(v - u)}{a} + \frac{(v - u)^{2}}{2a}$$

Multiply throughout by $2a$:

$$2as = 2u(v - u) + (v - u)^{2}$$

Expand $(v - u)^{2} = v^{2} - 2uv + u^{2}$:

$$2as = 2uv - 2u^{2} + v^{2} - 2uv + u^{2}$$

The $+2uv$ and $-2uv$ cancel. The $-2u^2 + u^2 = -u^2$:

$$2as = v^{2} - u^{2}$$

Rearranging gives the **third equation of motion:**

$$v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as \quad \text{...(III)}$$

**Verification check (dimensional consistency):**
Each side of $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$ has units of $(\text{m/s})^2 = \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$, which is dimensionally consistent. The derivation is valid for *any* straight-line motion with constant acceleration (gravity, frictionless inclined planes, vehicles with constant engine force minus drag, etc.).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Range of a Projectile

Consider a projectile fired with velocity **u** at an angle **θ** with the horizontal. The acceleration is $-g$ in the vertical direction and 0 in the horizontal direction.

**Step 1 — Resolve the initial velocity into components.**

$$u_x = u \cos\theta, \qquad u_y = u \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Vertical motion (decelerated by gravity).**
Using $v = u + at$ in the y-direction, the vertical velocity at time $t$ is:

$$v_y = u \sin\theta - g t$$

The projectile reaches the highest point when $v_y = 0$:

$$0 = u \sin\theta - g t_{peak} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{peak} = \frac{u \sin\theta}{g}$$

By symmetry, the **time of flight** (up + down) is twice this:

$$T = \frac{2u \sin\theta}{g}$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal motion (constant velocity).**
The horizontal distance (range) is:

$$R = u_x \cdot T = (u \cos\theta) \cdot \left(\frac{2u \sin\theta}{g}\right) = \frac{2u^{2} \sin\theta \cos\theta}{g}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $2 \sin\theta \cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{u^{2} \sin 2\theta}{g}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum range condition.**
Since $\sin 2\theta$ has a maximum value of **1** (occurring at $2\theta = 90°$, i.e., $\theta = 45°$):

$$R_{max} = \frac{u^{2}}{g} \quad \text{(at } \theta = 45°\text{)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Work–Energy Theorem

Consider a particle of mass **m** moving along a path under the action of a net force **F**. By Newton's second law:

$$F = m a = m \frac{dv}{dt}$$

The work done by this force over a small displacement $ds$ is $dW = F\, ds$. Using the chain rule $ds = v\, dt$:

$$dW = m \frac{dv}{dt} \cdot v\, dt = m v\, dv$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$W = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} m v\, dv = m \left[\frac{v^{2}}{2}\right]_{v_1}^{v_2} = \frac{1}{2}mv_{2}^{2} - \frac{1}{2}mv_{1}^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{W_{net} = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv_{2}^{2} - \frac{1}{2}mv_{1}^{2}}$$

This theorem is exceptionally powerful in KTU problems because it bypasses the need to know *time* or the *path* — only displacements, speeds, and net work are required.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Approach in KTU Problems

| Given Information | Recommended Approach | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Force, mass, time | Newton's 2nd law (direct integration) | $F = ma$ |
| Displacement, velocities, no time | Work–Energy Theorem | $W = \Delta KE$ |
| Force, time, velocities | Impulse–Momentum Theorem | $J = \Delta p$ |
| Constant acceleration, kinematics | Equations of motion | $v = u + at$, etc. |
| Collision with e known | Coefficient of restitution + momentum conservation | $e = \Delta v_{sep}/\Delta v_{app}$ |
| Rotating body, D'Alembert | Inertia force + equilibrium | $\Sigma F - ma = 0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: The Two Pillars of Dynamics

The following block-level flow shows how the discipline of Dynamics is split into its two main branches, with their governing laws and the engineering problems they solve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DYNAMICS - Study of Bodies in Motion"]:::root --> B["KINEMATICS - How a body moves"]:::branch
    A --> C["KINETICS - Why a body moves"]:::branch

    B --> B1["Rectilinear Motion"]
    B --> B2["Curvilinear Motion"]
    B --> B3["Projectile Motion"]
    B --> B4["Rotational Kinematics"]

    B1 --> B1a["Uniform velocity: s = vt"]
    B1 --> B1b["Uniformly accel: v = u + at"]
    B2 --> B2a["Circular motion: a_n = v^2/r"]
    B3 --> B3a["Range: R = u^2 sin 2 theta / g"]
    B4 --> B4a["v = r omega, a_t = r alpha"]

    C --> C1["Newton's Laws of Motion"]
    C --> C2["Work Energy Theorem"]
    C --> C3["Impulse Momentum Theorem"]
    C --> C4["D Alembert Principle"]

    C1 --> C1a["F = m a"]
    C2 --> C2a["W net = delta KE"]
    C3 --> C3a["J = m v2 - m v1"]
    C4 --> C4a["Inertia force = m a"]

    B1a --> E["ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS"]:::app
    B2a --> E
    B3a --> E
    B4a --> E
    C1a --> E
    C2a --> E
    C3a --> E
    C4a --> E

    E --> E1["Vehicle design"]
    E --> E2["Robotics"]
    E --> E3["Rocket propulsion"]
    E --> E4["Crash safety"]
    E --> E5["Turbomachinery"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef app fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree: Solving a Dynamics Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start - Read the Problem Statement"]:::start --> Q1{"What quantities are given and what is to be found?"}:::decision

    Q1 -- "Forces and masses" --> P1["Use NEWTON'S SECOND LAW - F = m a"]:::method
    Q1 -- "Velocities and displacements, no time" --> P2["Use WORK ENERGY THEOREM"]:::method
    Q1 -- "Forces and time intervals" --> P3["Use IMPULSE MOMENTUM THEOREM"]:::method
    Q1 -- "Constant acceleration kinematics" --> P4["Use EQUATIONS OF MOTION"]:::method
    Q1 -- "Rotating body, want reaction forces" --> P5["Use D ALEMBERT with inertia forces"]:::method

    P1 --> CHECK{"Does the answer have correct units and direction?"}:::decision
    P2 --> CHECK
    P3 --> CHECK
    P4 --> CHECK
    P5 --> CHECK

    CHECK -- "Yes" --> OUT["Write the final answer with units and vector direction"]:::end
    CHECK -- "No - dimensional mismatch" --> REV["Revisit the equation - check units of each term"]:::end
    CHECK -- "No - vector direction missing" --> REV2["Add direction notation - say 30 degrees above horizontal etc"]:::end

    REV --> OUT
    REV2 --> OUT

    classDef start fill:#10b981,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef method fill:#6366f1,stroke:#4338ca,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef end fill:#ef4444,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Free-Body and Inertia-Force Mapping for a Vehicle on an Incline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATIC_PART["STATIC FORCES ACTING ON VEHICLE"]
        F1["Weight: W = m g vertically downward"]:::force
        F2["Normal reaction: N perpendicular to incline"]:::force
        F3["Engine force: F_engine along incline upward"]:::force
        F4["Friction: f opposite to motion"]:::force
    end

    subgraph DYNAMIC_PART["DYNAMIC INERTIA TERM"]
        F5["Inertia force: F_inertia = m a opposite to acceleration"]:::inertia
    end

    subgraph RESOLUTION["EQUILIBRIUM RESOLUTION"]
        EQ1["Sum of forces along incline = 0"]:::eq
        EQ2["Sum of forces perpendicular to incline = 0"]:::eq
    end

    F1 --> EQ1
    F2 --> EQ2
    F3 --> EQ1
    F4 --> EQ1
    F5 --> EQ1

    EQ1 --> RESULT["Solve for unknown acceleration a or reaction N"]:::result
    EQ2 --> RESULT

    classDef inertia fill:#ef4444,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Newton's three laws of motion. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **First Law (Law of Inertia):** Every body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled by an external force to change that state. This defines force as the cause of change in motion.

2. **Second Law:** The rate of change of linear momentum of a body is directly proportional to the net external force applied and takes place in the direction of the applied force. Mathematically, $\vec{F} = \dfrac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = m\vec{a}$ (for constant mass).

3. **Third Law:** For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Action and reaction act on different bodies, never on the same body.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Impulse. How is it related to linear momentum? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Impulse** is the product of a force and the small time interval during which it acts. For a variable force, it is the integral of force over time:

$$\vec{J} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F}\, dt$$

For a constant force: $\vec{J} = \vec{F} \cdot \Delta t$.

**Relation to linear momentum:** By Newton's second law, $\vec{F} = \dfrac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$. Integrating both sides over a time interval:

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F}\, dt = \int_{p_1}^{p_2} d\vec{p} = \vec{p_2} - \vec{p_1}$$

$$\boxed{\vec{J} = \Delta \vec{p} = m\vec{v_2} - m\vec{v_1}}$$

This is the **Impulse–Momentum Theorem**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions for Module 3 typically carry 14 marks each and have an **internal choice** (you must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B, not both).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A stone is thrown vertically upward with a velocity of **29.43 m/s** from the top of a building **30 m** high. Find:
- (i) The maximum height reached above the ground.
- (ii) The time taken to reach the maximum height.
- (iii) The velocity with which it strikes the ground.
- (iv) The total time of flight.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A body of mass **5 kg** is pulled up along a smooth inclined plane (angle **30°** to the horizontal) by a force of **40 N** applied parallel to the plane. Find:
- (i) The acceleration of the body.
- (ii) The velocity after **4 seconds** if it starts from rest.
- (iii) The work done by the applied force in 4 seconds.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

Given: $u = 29.43$ m/s (upward), height of building $h = 30$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

Taking **upward as positive** and origin at the top of the building, the stone's initial position is at $s_0 = 0$ with $u = +29.43$ m/s, $a = -9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Maximum height above ground:**
At maximum height, $v = 0$. Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with $v = 0$:

$$0 = (29.43)^2 + 2(-9.81)(s_{max})$$

$$s_{max} = \frac{(29.43)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{866.12}{19.62} = 44.14 \text{ m}$$

This $s_{max}$ is the height **above the top of the building**. Total height above ground:

$$H = 30 + 44.14 = \boxed{74.14 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Time to reach maximum height:**
Using $v = u + at$ with $v = 0$:

$$0 = 29.43 + (-9.81)t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{29.43}{9.81} = \boxed{3 \text{ s}}$$

**(iii) Velocity when it strikes the ground:**
The stone falls from the maximum height $H = 74.14$ m (using $u = 0$ at peak, $a = +g$):

$$v^2 = 0^2 + 2(9.81)(74.14) = 1454.61$$

$$v = \sqrt{1454.61} = 38.14 \text{ m/s (downward)}$$

**Alternative check (taking ground as reference, downward positive):**
Initial velocity at top of building: $u = -29.43$ m/s (it is moving upward, opposite to our positive direction).
Net displacement $s = +30$ m (it ends up 30 m below the start).

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as = (-29.43)^2 + 2(9.81)(30) = 866.12 + 588.6 = 1454.72$$

$$v = \sqrt{1454.72} = 38.14 \text{ m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

So the stone strikes the ground at $\boxed{38.14 \text{ m/s (downward)}}$.

**(iv) Total time of flight:**
Total time = time to go up + time to fall from peak to ground.

Time to fall $H = 74.14$ m from rest: $H = \frac{1}{2}gt_{fall}^2$:

$$t_{fall} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 74.14}{9.81}} = \sqrt{15.11} = 3.89 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{total} = 3 + 3.89 = \boxed{6.89 \text{ s}}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

Given: $m = 5$ kg, incline angle $\alpha = 30°$, applied force $F = 40$ N (up the incline), smooth plane ($\mu = 0$).

**Free-body forces along the incline (taking up-incline as positive):**
- Applied force: $+F = +40$ N
- Weight component along incline (down-incline): $-mg\sin\alpha = -5 \times 9.81 \times \sin 30° = -5 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = -24.525$ N

**Forces perpendicular to incline (cancelling out, used only to find N if needed):**
- Normal reaction: $+N$
- Weight component perpendicular: $-mg\cos\alpha$

**(i) Acceleration:**
By Newton's second law along the incline:

$$F_{net} = ma \quad \Rightarrow \quad 40 - 24.525 = 5a$$

$$a = \frac{15.475}{5} = \boxed{3.095 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (up the incline)}}$$

**(ii) Velocity after 4 s (starting from rest):**
Using $v = u + at$ with $u = 0$:

$$v = 0 + 3.095 \times 4 = \boxed{12.38 \text{ m/s (up the incline)}}$$

**(iii) Work done by the applied force in 4 s:**
First, compute the displacement in 4 s:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2 = 0 + \frac{1}{2}(3.095)(4)^2 = 0 + 24.76 = 24.76 \text{ m}$$

Work done by the applied force (force and displacement are in the same direction):

$$W = F \cdot s \cdot \cos 0° = 40 \times 24.76 = \boxed{990.4 \text{ J}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A ball is thrown from the top of a cliff **80 m** high with a horizontal velocity of **15 m/s**. Find:
- (i) The time taken to reach the ground.
- (ii) The horizontal distance travelled (range).
- (iii) The velocity (magnitude and direction) with which it hits the ground.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car of mass **1200 kg** moving at **20 m/s** is brought to rest over a distance of **25 m** by applying brakes. Find:
- (i) The retardation produced.
- (ii) The braking force.
- (iii) The time taken to stop.
- (iv) Using the work–energy theorem, verify the work done by the braking force equals the change in kinetic energy.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

Given: Height $h = 80$ m, horizontal velocity $u_x = 15$ m/s, initial vertical velocity $u_y = 0$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Time to reach the ground:**
Vertical motion (free fall from rest in y-direction): $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$:

$$80 = \frac{1}{2}(9.81)t^2$$

$$t^2 = \frac{160}{9.81} = 16.31 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \boxed{4.04 \text{ s}}$$

**(ii) Horizontal range:**
Since horizontal velocity is constant:

$$R = u_x \cdot t = 15 \times 4.04 = \boxed{60.6 \text{ m}}$$

**(iii) Velocity on impact:**
Horizontal component (unchanged): $v_x = 15$ m/s.

Vertical component: $v_y = u_y + gt = 0 + 9.81 \times 4.04 = 39.63$ m/s (downward).

Magnitude:

$$v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} = \sqrt{15^2 + 39.63^2} = \sqrt{225 + 1570.54} = \sqrt{1795.54}$$

$$v = \boxed{42.37 \text{ m/s}}$$

Direction (angle below horizontal):

$$\tan\phi = \frac{v_y}{v_x} = \frac{39.63}{15} = 2.642$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(2.642) = \boxed{69.3° \text{ below the horizontal}}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

Given: $m = 1200$ kg, $u = 20$ m/s, $v = 0$, $s = 25$ m.

**(i) Retardation (deceleration):**
Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$:

$$0 = (20)^2 + 2a(25) = 400 + 50a$$

$$a = -\frac{400}{50} = -8 \text{ m/s}^2$$

The magnitude of retardation is $\boxed{8 \text{ m/s}^2}$ (the negative sign indicates deceleration).

**(ii) Braking force:**
By Newton's second law:

$$F = m \cdot a = 1200 \times (-8) = \boxed{-9600 \text{ N}}$$

The braking force is **9600 N** directed opposite to the motion (hence the negative sign in our chosen sign convention).

**(iii) Time taken to stop:**
Using $v = u + at$ with $v = 0$:

$$0 = 20 + (-8)t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{20}{8} = \boxed{2.5 \text{ s}}$$

**(iv) Verification using Work–Energy Theorem:**
Displacement during braking = 25 m. Braking force is $F = -9600$ N (opposing motion). Work done by braking force:

$$W_{brake} = F \cdot s = -9600 \times 25 = -240{,}000 \text{ J}$$

Change in kinetic energy:

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 - \frac{1}{2}mu^2 = 0 - \frac{1}{2}(1200)(20)^2 = -240{,}000 \text{ J}$$

Comparing:

$$W_{brake} = -240{,}000 \text{ J} = \Delta KE = -240{,}000 \text{ J} \quad \checkmark$$

The work–energy theorem is verified. The negative sign indicates that the kinetic energy of the car has decreased (i.e., the braking force has *removed* energy from the car, dissipating it as heat in the brake pads).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** In vertical motion, decide your positive direction at the *start* and stick to it. A common error is treating $g$ as positive when it should be $-9.81$ m/s² for upward motion.
> 2. **Forgetting to add the building height:** In Q-A (a), many students compute $s_{max}$ from the launch point but forget to add the 30 m building height when asked for height "above the ground."
> 3. **Mixing up 2D projectile components:** Horizontal velocity is **constant**; vertical motion is **independently** governed by $g$. Do NOT assume the projectile's "speed" is conserved.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** KTU strictly expects SI units. A numerical answer without "m/s" or "N" loses at least 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the free-body diagram (FBD):** In kinetics problems, always show the FBD explicitly. A missing FBD typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **D'Alembert misuse:** The inertia force $-ma$ is drawn **opposite** to the actual acceleration. Drawing it in the same direction as acceleration is a guaranteed mark-deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Dynamics**

- ✅ **Kinematics** describes motion; **Kinetics** explains it via forces. Know the distinction cold.
- ✅ **Three equations of motion** (uniformly accelerated): $v = u + at$; $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$; $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$. These apply *only* when $a$ is constant.
- ✅ **$g = 9.81$ m/s²** is the standard value; $g$ acts **downward** always, regardless of the body's direction of motion.
- ✅ **Projectile motion** = two independent motions: horizontal (constant $u\cos\theta$) + vertical ($u\sin\theta$ decelerated by $g$).
- ✅ **Max range of a projectile** occurs at $\theta = 45°$, with $R_{max} = u^2/g$.
- ✅ **Newton's 2nd law** is the foundation: $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$. It is a *vector* equation; resolve into components before solving.
- ✅ **Work–Energy Theorem** bypasses time: $W_{net} = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}mv_1^2$. Use this when given displacements and speeds.
- ✅ **Impulse–Momentum Theorem** bypasses displacement: $J = F \cdot \Delta t = \Delta p$. Use this when given forces and time intervals.
- ✅ **Conservation of Linear Momentum** applies only when the **net external force on the system is zero**. Always write the conservation equation *before* substituting values.
- ✅ **Coefficient of restitution** $e$ ranges from 0 (perfectly inelastic) to 1 (perfectly elastic). It is the *ratio* of relative velocities.
- ✅ **Centripetal acceleration** $a_n = v^2/r$ always points *toward* the centre of the circular path.
- ✅ **D'Alembert's Principle:** Add a fictitious inertia force $(-m\vec{a})$ to convert a dynamic problem into a static equilibrium problem. Useful for finding reaction forces on accelerating bodies.
- ✅ **Scalar vs Vector:** When motion is along a fixed straight line, the kinematic equations can be used as scalar equations. For 2D/3D motion, **always** use vectors or components.
- ✅ **Sign discipline:** Every numerical answer in dynamics must specify direction or use a sign convention; numerical values alone are incomplete.
- ✅ **FBD first, equations second:** Always draw a clean free-body diagram and label all forces with magnitude and direction *before* writing equilibrium or motion equations.
- ✅ **Dimensional check:** Every term in your final equation must have consistent units (e.g., both sides in N, J, or m/s²). This single habit catches 80% of careless errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectilinear Translation: Equations of Motion in Kinematics and Kinetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rectilinear Translation: Equations of Motion in Kinematics and Kinetics**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rectilinear Translation** is a type of rigid body motion in which every line segment on the body remains parallel to its original orientation at all times, and **every particle of the body moves along a straight-line path** (i.e., along parallel straight lines). For a body in rectilinear translation, all points have **identical velocity and identical acceleration** at any given instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematics vs. Kinetics — The KTU Distinction**
> - **Kinematics** of motion: The geometric study of motion *without* reference to the forces causing it. It deals with displacement ($s$), velocity ($v$), acceleration ($a$), and time ($t$) only.
> - **Kinetics** of motion: The study of motion *with* explicit consideration of the forces that produce it. It links Newton's Second Law to the kinematic quantities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a rectangular wooden block sliding across a smooth, frictionless glass table in a straight line. Look at any two pencil marks on the block. No matter where they are — at the leading edge, trailing edge, or middle — both marks travel in **identical straight lines, with the same speed, and pick up the same speed** if you push harder.

> **Real-world Analogy:** A train moving along a straight, level track. Every coach, the engine, the pantry car — all move along **parallel straight lines** with the same velocity and acceleration at any instant. There is no rotation, no twisting — only pure translation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this distinction important?** In KTU board problems, the *first* decision you must make when reading a problem is: "Am I being asked about *how* the body moves (kinematics — give me $s, v, a, t$), or *why* it moves that way (kinetics — give me the forces, masses, and the equation $\Sigma F = m \cdot a$)?"

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (downward) — this is the single most important constant in KTU rectilinear translation problems involving falling bodies, weights, and inclined planes.
- **Sign convention (KTU standard):** Take the direction of motion as **positive**; forces acting in that direction are positive, those opposing motion are negative.
- **Standard unit triplet:** Forces in **Newtons (N)**, masses in **kilograms (kg)**, accelerations in **m/s²** (so $1 \text{ N} = 1 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The study of rectilinear translation is split into **two distinct, but interlocking, layers**:

### Layer A — KINEMATICS (the "what" of motion)

**Step 1 — Position & Displacement:** A particle's position $s$ on a straight line is measured from a fixed origin. The change in position is displacement, also denoted $s$ in KTU textbooks.

**Step 2 — Velocity:** Velocity is the time-rate of change of position.
$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration:** Acceleration is the time-rate of change of velocity.
$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$$

**Step 4 — Constant-Acceleration Special Case:** When $a$ is constant (the most common KTU assumption), the above derivatives integrate directly into the **four kinematic equations of motion** (the "SUVAT" equations).

### Layer B — KINETICS (the "why" of motion)

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for a Particle:**
$$\Sigma F = m \cdot a$$

**Step 2 — Extension to a System of Particles (the key KTU result):** When several particles form a translating rigid body or a system of connected translating bodies, the **resultant external force** equals the **total mass** times the **acceleration of the mass center** $G$:
$$\Sigma F_x = m \cdot a_{Gx}, \quad \Sigma F_y = m \cdot a_{Gy}$$

where $m$ is the total mass of the system and $a_G$ is the acceleration of the center of mass.

**Step 3 — Moment Equation for Translation:** Because the body does **not rotate** ($\alpha = 0$), the resultant moment about $G$ must be zero:
$$\Sigma M_G = I_G \cdot \alpha = 0$$

This is a powerful auxiliary equation: you can take moments about the mass center (or any point) and set $\Sigma M = 0$ to obtain an extra independent scalar equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Kinetic Equation:** Newton's second law applies to *each* particle. Summing it over all particles, the internal forces cancel in pairs (Newton's third law), and we are left with the *external* resultant force equaling the total mass times the acceleration of the mass center. This is why the equation holds for any translating system — single block, two-block-pulley, or a train of 50 coaches.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it is the core of every Module 3 ESE question.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $v = u + a \cdot t$ | Final velocity after time $t$ | Find velocity at a given time |
| 2 | $s = u \cdot t + \tfrac{1}{2} a \cdot t^2$ | Displacement in time $t$ | Find distance travelled |
| 3 | $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a \cdot s$ | Velocity-displacement relation | Eliminates time variable |
| 4 | $s = \tfrac{1}{2}(u + v) \cdot t$ | Average-velocity displacement | Eliminates acceleration |
| 5 | $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$ | Acceleration from calculus | Variable-acceleration problems |
| 6 | $\Sigma F_x = m \cdot a_{Gx}$ | Force equation (x-direction) | Kinetics of translation |
| 7 | $\Sigma F_y = m \cdot a_{Gy}$ | Force equation (y-direction) | Vertical equilibrium + motion |
| 8 | $\Sigma M_G = 0$ | Moment equation (translation) | Pulley, multi-body systems |
| 9 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight of a body | Converting mass to force |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In exam scripts, students often write absolute values like $\vert F \vert$ or $\vert a \vert$. In markdown tables, the vertical bar can break the table. In your answer sheet, this is fine — but **never** confuse the *magnitude* of a vector with its *signed component*. A negative acceleration (deceleration) is just as physical as a positive one.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Crash Analysis:** Automotive engineers use $\Sigma F = m \cdot a$ in reverse — known deceleration of a crash-test dummy (from kinematics) lets them compute the average force on the human body, informing seat-belt and airbag design.
- **Crane & Hoist Design:** The tension in a cable lifting a load with known acceleration is found using $T - W = m \cdot a$.
- **Elevator Cable Sizing:** A cable must withstand not only the static weight $W$ but also the *dynamic* force $m \cdot a$ during starting and stopping — directly from the kinetic equation.
- **Rocket / Projectile Launch:** Multistage rockets and projectile motion analysis rely entirely on the four kinematic equations combined with the kinetic equation involving gravity as the only external force.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Four Kinematic Equations (from First Principles)

**Starting point:** Constant acceleration $a$. This means $a$ does *not* depend on $t$, $s$, or $v$.

### Step 1: Integrate acceleration to get velocity

By definition, $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$.

Separating variables and integrating from initial time $t = 0$ (where $v = u$) to a general time $t$ (where $v$ is the instantaneous velocity):

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv = \int_{0}^{t} a \, dt$$

Since $a$ is constant, the right-hand side is simply $a \cdot t$:

$$v - u = a \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{v = u + a \cdot t} \quad \cdots \text{(Equation 1)}$$

### Step 2: Integrate velocity to get displacement

By definition, $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$. From Equation 1, $v = u + a \cdot t$.

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $s = 0$, taking initial position as origin) to general time $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} (u + a \cdot t) \, dt$$

$$s = u \cdot t + \tfrac{1}{2} a \cdot t^2$$

$$\boxed{s = u \cdot t + \tfrac{1}{2} a \cdot t^2} \quad \cdots \text{(Equation 2)}$$

### Step 3: Eliminate $t$ to get $v^2$ in terms of $s$

From Equation 1, $t = \dfrac{v - u}{a}$.

Substitute into Equation 2:

$$s = u \cdot \left(\frac{v - u}{a}\right) + \frac{1}{2} a \cdot \left(\frac{v - u}{a}\right)^2$$

Multiply through by $a$:

$$a \cdot s = u(v - u) + \frac{(v - u)^2}{2}$$

Expand:

$$a \cdot s = uv - u^2 + \frac{v^2 - 2uv + u^2}{2}$$

$$a \cdot s = uv - u^2 + \frac{v^2}{2} - uv + \frac{u^2}{2}$$

$$a \cdot s = -\frac{u^2}{2} + \frac{v^2}{2}$$

Multiply by 2 and rearrange:

$$2 a s = v^2 - u^2$$

$$\boxed{v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s} \quad \cdots \text{(Equation 3)}$$

### Step 4: Eliminate $a$ to get $s$ in terms of $u$, $v$, $t$

From Equation 1, $a = \dfrac{v - u}{t}$.

Substitute into Equation 2:

$$s = u \cdot t + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{v - u}{t} \cdot t^2 = u \cdot t + \frac{1}{2}(v - u) \cdot t$$

$$s = \frac{2u t + (v - u) t}{2} = \frac{(u + v) t}{2}$$

$$\boxed{s = \tfrac{1}{2}(u + v) \cdot t} \quad \cdots \text{(Equation 4)}$$

This is also the geometric area of a trapezoid in the $v$–$t$ graph — a popular KTU conceptual question.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Equations 1, 2, 3, 4 are the *only* kinematic tools you need for constant-acceleration translation. Memorize the *logical* derivation rather than the rote forms — the derivation *is* the answer if asked "derive the equations of motion."

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kinetic Equation $\Sigma F = m \cdot a_G$ for a System of Particles

**Starting point:** Consider a system of $n$ particles, the $i$-th particle having mass $m_i$ and position vector $\vec{r}_i$ from a fixed origin $O$.

### Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law to the $i$-th particle

$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{(ext)}} + \sum_{j \ne i} \vec{F}_{ij}^{\text{(int)}} = m_i \cdot \vec{a}_i$$

where $\vec{F}_i^{\text{(ext)}}$ is the net *external* force on particle $i$, and $\vec{F}_{ij}^{\text{(int)}}$ is the *internal* force exerted on $i$ by particle $j$.

### Step 2: Sum over all particles

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_i^{\text{(ext)}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j \ne i} \vec{F}_{ij}^{\text{(int)}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \vec{a}_i$$

### Step 3: Internal forces cancel

By **Newton's Third Law**, the force exerted by $i$ on $j$ is equal and opposite to that exerted by $j$ on $i$:

$$\vec{F}_{ij}^{\text{(int)}} = -\vec{F}_{ji}^{\text{(int)}}$$

In the double sum, every such pair cancels, so:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j \ne i} \vec{F}_{ij}^{\text{(int)}} = \vec{0}$$

### Step 4: Introduce the mass center $G$

The position of the mass center is defined by:

$$m \cdot \vec{r}_G = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \vec{r}_i, \quad \text{where } m = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

Differentiating twice with respect to time:

$$m \cdot \vec{a}_G = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \vec{a}_i$$

### Step 5: Combine the results

The equation from Step 2 reduces to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_i^{\text{(ext)}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \vec{a}_i = m \cdot \vec{a}_G$$

Denoting the net external force as $\vec{F}_R^{\text{(ext)}}$:

$$\boxed{\vec{F}_R^{\text{(ext)}} = m \cdot \vec{a}_G}$$

In component form (the form you will use in KTU problems):

$$\Sigma F_x = m \cdot a_{Gx}, \quad \Sigma F_y = m \cdot a_{Gy}$$

**Why this is the workhorse equation:** It applies to a *single block*, a *system of connected blocks*, a *rigid body in translation*, or even a *multi-particle system* — as long as the motion is rectilinear translation. The only requirement is that you correctly identify the *external* forces (gravity, normal reactions, applied forces, friction, tension) and the *total mass*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auxiliary equation for connected systems:** For a rigid body or a pulley in pure translation, $\Sigma M_G = 0$. For an *ideal pulley* (massless, frictionless) in a two-block system, the tension on both sides is equal, *and* the pulley itself has $\Sigma M = 0$ about its center. This gives you an extra equation to solve for unknowns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — From Physical Problem to Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Read KTU Problem Statement] --> A1[Identify the Body or System]
    A1 --> A2{Is the motion straight-line?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[STOP: This is not rectilinear translation]
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Is the question about how the body moves?]
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Use KINEMATIC Equations SUVAT]
    A4 -- No --> A6[Use KINETIC Equation: Sigma F equals m times a sub G]
    A5 --> A7[Pick correct SUVAT relation from formula table]
    A6 --> A8[Draw Free-Body Diagram FBD]
    A8 --> A9[Resolve all forces along direction of motion]
    A9 --> A10[Apply Sigma F equals m times a and Sigma M sub G equals 0]
    A10 --> A11[Solve simultaneous equations for unknowns]
    A7 --> A12[Substitute known values and compute]
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Verify units: m per second squared for acceleration, N for force]
    A13 --> A14[END: Present answer with sign convention and units]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Anatomy of a Rectilinear Translation Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        i1[Given quantities: mass, force, angle, coefficient of friction]
        i2[Initial conditions: u and s at t equals 0]
    end
    subgraph MODEL[Physical Model Layer]
        m1[Free-Body Diagram FBD]
        m2[Kinematic Constraint: inextensible string or rigid coupling]
        m3[Sign Convention: motion direction is positive]
    end
    subgraph SOLVER[Equation Layer]
        s1[Kinematic: SUVAT relation]
        s2[Kinetics x: Sigma F sub x equals m times a]
        s3[Kinetics y: Sigma F sub y equals m times a]
        s4[Auxiliary: Sigma M sub G equals 0]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        o1[Acceleration: a in m per second squared]
        o2[Velocity: v in m per second]
        o3[Tension or normal force: T or N in N]
    end
    INPUT --> MODEL
    MODEL --> SOLVER
    SOLVER --> OUTPUT
    s1 --> o1
    s1 --> o2
    s2 --> o3
    s3 --> o3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first flowchart is the *procedural* algorithm — what to do step-by-step when you open the question paper. The second is a *functional* architecture showing which layers of physics interact. In the KTU valuation key, the examiner checks that you have at least implicitly walked through every box of this architecture. **Missing the FBD alone can cost 2 to 3 marks out of 14.**

## 4.3 Free-Body Diagram (Schematic — text description)

For a block of mass $m$ on a horizontal surface, pulled by a horizontal force $P$:
- A horizontal arrow pointing **right** (direction of motion) labelled $P$.
- A horizontal arrow pointing **left** labelled $F_f = \mu_k N$ (kinetic friction).
- A vertical arrow pointing **up** labelled $N$ (normal reaction).
- A vertical arrow pointing **down** labelled $W = m \cdot g$ (weight).

Equations:
- Vertical: $N - m g = 0 \Rightarrow N = m g$
- Horizontal: $P - \mu_k m g = m \cdot a$

> [!TIP]
> **Always draw the FBD first**, *before* writing any equation. This single habit is what separates a 14-mark answer from a 7-mark answer in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate clearly between kinematics and kinetics of motion. Give one example of each from a system undergoing rectilinear translation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kinematics | Kinetics |
|--------|------------|----------|
| Concern | The geometry of motion — displacement, velocity, acceleration, time | The relationship between motion and the forces that cause it |
| Forces involved | **Not considered** | Explicitly considered |
| Governing equation | $v = u + a t$, $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$, etc. | $\Sigma F = m \cdot a_G$ |
| Example | Finding the velocity of a freely falling body 3 s after release: $v = g \cdot t$ | Finding the tension in the rope pulling a $10 \text{ kg}$ block with acceleration $2 \text{ m/s}^2$: $T = m(g + a)$ |

> **Mark split:** Definition of kinematics (1 mark) + Definition of kinetics (1 mark) + One example each (1 mark).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Newton's Second Law of motion. Show that for a system of particles in rectilinear translation, the resultant external force equals the total mass times the acceleration of the mass center.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Newton's Second Law:** The time-rate of change of linear momentum of a body is equal to the magnitude of the net external force applied to it, and it takes place in the direction of that force.

Mathematically: $\vec{F} = \dfrac{d(m \vec{v})}{dt}$. For constant mass, $\vec{F} = m \cdot \vec{a}$.

**For a system of particles** (concise proof):
- Apply $\vec{F}_i = m_i \vec{a}_i$ to each particle $i$ in the system.
- Sum over all $n$ particles: $\sum \vec{F}_i^{\text{(ext)}} + \sum \vec{F}_{ij}^{\text{(int)}} = \sum m_i \vec{a}_i$.
- Internal forces cancel in pairs (Newton's Third Law), leaving:
- $\sum \vec{F}_i^{\text{(ext)}} = \sum m_i \vec{a}_i = m \cdot \vec{a}_G$ — QED.

> **Mark split:** Statement of Second Law (1 mark) + Summation step (1 mark) + Cancellation of internal forces and final result (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Regulation:** In the End Semester Examination, every Module question of 14 marks offers an internal choice — answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full. You may attempt both, but only the higher-scoring one is evaluated.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Two blocks connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth, light pulley:** Block $A$ of mass $m_A = 8 \text{ kg}$ rests on a smooth inclined plane at $30°$ to the horizontal. Block $B$ of mass $m_B = 12 \text{ kg}$ hangs freely from the other end of the string over the pulley at the top of the incline. The system is released from rest. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Draw the free-body diagrams of both blocks and the pulley. Determine the acceleration of the system and the tension in the string.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**After 2 seconds of motion, the string suddenly breaks (block $A$ is still on the incline). Determine how much further block $A$ travels up the incline before momentarily coming to rest. Take the incline to be smooth throughout.**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Solution to Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: FBD of Block B (hanging vertically):**
- Weight $W_B = m_B \cdot g = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N}$ acting downward.
- Tension $T$ acting upward.

**Step 2: FBD of Block A (on the incline):**
- Weight component along incline (down the slope): $W_A \sin 30° = 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 39.24 \text{ N}$.
- Weight component perpendicular to incline: $W_A \cos 30° = 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.866 = 67.96 \text{ N}$.
- Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to the incline.
- Tension $T$ acting up the slope.

**Step 3: Apply the kinetic equation to each block.** Assume block B descends and block A goes up the incline. Take the direction of motion as positive.

For Block B (downward positive):
$$\Sigma F = m_B \cdot a$$
$$m_B \cdot g - T = m_B \cdot a \quad \cdots (1)$$

For Block A (up the incline positive):
$$\Sigma F = m_A \cdot a$$
$$T - m_A \cdot g \sin 30° = m_A \cdot a \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 4: Solve the two equations simultaneously.** Add (1) and (2) to eliminate $T$:

$$m_B \cdot g - m_A \cdot g \sin 30° = (m_A + m_B) \cdot a$$

$$a = \frac{g (m_B - m_A \sin 30°)}{m_A + m_B}$$

Substitute values:
$$a = \frac{9.81 \times (12 - 8 \times 0.5)}{8 + 12} = \frac{9.81 \times (12 - 4)}{20} = \frac{9.81 \times 8}{20}$$

$$\boxed{a = 3.924 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (up the incline for A, down for B)}}$$

**Step 5: Find tension from equation (1):**
$$T = m_B (g - a) = 12 \times (9.81 - 3.924) = 12 \times 5.886$$

$$\boxed{T = 70.632 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification via equation (2):**
$$T = m_A (a + g \sin 30°) = 8 \times (3.924 + 4.905) = 8 \times 8.829 = 70.632 \text{ N} \checkmark$$

> **Mark split (Part a):** FBD (2 marks) + Kinetic equations (2 marks) + Solving for $a$ (1.5 marks) + Solving for $T$ (1.5 marks).

---

#### Solution to Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Conditions after the string breaks.** Block A is moving up the incline with velocity $v_0$ at the instant the string breaks. Compute $v_0$ using $v = u + a t$ with $u = 0$:

$$v_0 = 0 + 3.924 \times 2 = 7.848 \text{ m/s (up the incline)}$$

**Step 2: New free-body diagram (no tension, smooth incline).** The only force along the incline is gravity component $m_A g \sin 30°$, which now **decelerates** the block. The incline is smooth, so no friction.

**Step 3: Apply the kinematic equation** $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$ with the new acceleration. The block is decelerating, so the new acceleration is:

$$a_{\text{new}} = -g \sin 30° = -4.905 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Take $u = v_0 = 7.848 \text{ m/s}$, $v = 0$ (momentarily at rest):

$$0 = (7.848)^2 + 2 \times (-4.905) \times s$$

$$0 = 61.591 - 9.81 \cdot s$$

$$s = \frac{61.591}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{s = 6.279 \text{ m (further up the incline)}}$$

> **Mark split (Part b):** Finding $v_0$ (2 marks) + New acceleration (2 marks) + Application of $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$ (2 marks) + Final answer with units (1 mark).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A block of mass $m = 25 \text{ kg}$ is placed on a horizontal floor. The coefficient of static friction $\mu_s = 0.4$ and the coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu_k = 0.3$. A horizontal force $P$ is applied to the block. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Find the magnitude of the force $P$ required to (i) just start the block moving, and (ii) to keep the block moving with a constant velocity of $2 \text{ m/s}$.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the applied force is $P = 120 \text{ N}$, determine the acceleration of the block and the distance it travels in the first 3 seconds starting from rest.**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Solution to Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the weight and normal reaction.**
$$W = m g = 25 \times 9.81 = 245.25 \text{ N}$$

For a horizontal surface with a horizontal applied force, the normal reaction equals the weight:
$$N = W = 245.25 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Find $P$ to just initiate motion (impending motion).** At impending motion, friction is at its maximum static value $F_s^{\max} = \mu_s N$:

$$P = \mu_s N = 0.4 \times 245.25$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{impending}} = 98.1 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3: Find $P$ to maintain constant velocity.** Once moving, friction drops to the kinetic value $F_k = \mu_k N$. For constant velocity, $a = 0$, so $\Sigma F = 0$:

$$P - \mu_k N = 0$$
$$P = \mu_k N = 0.3 \times 245.25$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{constant}} = 73.575 \text{ N}}$$

> **Mark split (Part a):** Free-body diagram (1 mark) + Normal reaction calculation (1 mark) + Impending motion calculation (2 marks) + Constant-velocity calculation (2 marks) + Comparison/explanation (1 mark).

---

#### Solution to Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Check whether the block moves with $P = 120 \text{ N}$.** Compare $P$ with $P_{\text{impending}} = 98.1 \text{ N}$. Since $120 > 98.1$, the block **will move**. Once moving, kinetic friction applies.

**Step 2: Compute kinetic friction force.**
$$F_k = \mu_k N = 0.3 \times 245.25 = 73.575 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Apply Newton's second law in the direction of motion.**
$$P - F_k = m \cdot a$$
$$120 - 73.575 = 25 \cdot a$$
$$46.425 = 25 a$$
$$a = 1.857 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{a = 1.857 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Step 4: Find distance in 3 seconds starting from rest.** Use $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$ with $u = 0$:

$$s = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.857 \times (3)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.857 \times 9 = 8.357 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{s = 8.357 \text{ m}}$$

> **Mark split (Part b):** Comparison to identify motion (1 mark) + Kinetic friction (1 mark) + Newton's second law (2 marks) + Acceleration (1 mark) + Distance via SUVAT (2 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Static vs. Kinetic Friction:** A shocking 1 in 3 students uses $\mu_s$ in the *moving* phase or $\mu_k$ for the *impending* phase. This single error costs **2 to 3 marks** per question.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** Taking upward as positive when the body is moving down (or vice versa) flips all signs. The KTU valuation key explicitly tests this.
> 3. **Pulley Tension Equality:** In an ideal pulley, $T_1 = T_2$. Many students wrongly assume the tensions are *different*. If the problem says "smooth, light pulley," the tensions are equal. If it says "pulley with friction" or "pulley of mass $m$," the tensions are different.
> 4. **Forgetting the FBD:** Up to **2 marks** are reserved for a clear, labelled FBD in every kinetics problem. No FBD = automatic deduction.
> 5. **Units in the Final Answer:** A bare numerical answer without units receives **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction. Always write "$\text{m/s}^2$" or "$\text{N}$" with every answer.
> 6. **Using $g = 10$ in place of $9.81$:** Unless the problem explicitly says so, KTU expects $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. A small deduction is usually applied for using the wrong value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Rectilinear Translation** = straight-line motion of every particle of the body with identical velocity and acceleration.
- **Kinematics** = "what" (SUVAT). **Kinetics** = "why" ($\Sigma F = m a_G$).
- The **four kinematic equations** for constant $a$ are: $v = u + a t$, $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$, $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$, $s = \tfrac{1}{2}(u + v) t$.
- The **kinetic equation for a system** in translation: $\Sigma F_x = m a_{Gx}$ and $\Sigma F_y = m a_{Gy}$.
- The **auxiliary moment equation** for a translating body: $\Sigma M_G = 0$ (no rotation, so no angular acceleration term).
- **Newton's Second Law** is the bridge: $\vec{F} = m \vec{a}$ for one particle, $\vec{F}_R^{\text{(ext)}} = m \vec{a}_G$ for a system.
- **Internal forces cancel** in pairs by Newton's Third Law — this is why only *external* forces appear in the kinetic equation for a system.
- **Sign convention:** Always declare the direction of motion as positive, and stick to it consistently.
- **Friction rules:** Use $\mu_s N$ at impending motion; switch to $\mu_k N$ the moment the body starts moving.
- **Ideal pulley:** Smooth and light means $T$ is the same on both sides, and the pulley has no inertia to overcome.
- **Free-Body Diagram (FBD)** is non-negotiable — draw it *before* writing any equation.
- **Units:** $F$ in N, $m$ in kg, $a$ in m/s², $s$ in m, $v$ in m/s, $t$ in s. $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless told otherwise.
- **Two-equations-two-unknowns** is the standard pattern: combine the kinetic equations of the constituent bodies (or the SUVAT + kinetic pair) and solve.
- **Time-independent problems:** Use $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$ to eliminate $t$.
- **Time-given problems:** Use $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$ or $v = u + a t$.
- **Remember:** The kinetic equation is *valid* for any system in pure translation — single block, two-block-pulley, or a rigid body — as long as you identify the **external** forces correctly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** D'Alembert's Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D'Alembert's Principle (KTU 2024 Formal Statement):**
> *D'Alembert's Principle states that the sum of the applied forces and the reversed effective forces (also called the inertia forces) acting on a body in motion is equal to zero, when the body is considered to be in a state of dynamic equilibrium. Mathematically, for a particle or rigid body, if $\vec{F}$ is the resultant of all applied external forces, and $m\vec{a}$ is the resultant of the effective (inertia) forces of the particles, then:*
>
> $$\vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently,} \quad \vec{F} + \vec{F_I} = 0$$
>
> *where $\vec{F_I} = -m\vec{a}$ is the **inertia force vector**.*

The principle was formulated by the French mathematician and physicist **Jean le Rond d'Alembert** in 1743, originally as a principle in mechanics. It provides a powerful conceptual bridge between **kinematics** (motion) and **kinetics** (forces causing motion), allowing dynamic problems to be solved using the static equilibrium equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Reverse Push" on a Bus**
> Imagine you are standing in a bus that suddenly accelerates forward. To remain stationary relative to the bus, you must push backward against the floor with your feet. The bus pushes you forward (real applied force) and you can feel as though a "ghost" is pushing you backward — that ghost force is exactly the **inertia force** ($-m\vec{a}$).
>
> In your reference frame attached to the accelerating bus, the bus appears to be in equilibrium: the real forward push from friction at your feet is balanced by the fictitious backward "inertia force." This is precisely D'Alembert's Principle in action — every dynamic problem can be visualized as a static equilibrium problem by introducing inertia forces.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Units (SI) | Nature |
|--------|----------|------------|--------|
| $m$ | Mass of the body | **kg (kilogram)** | Scalar (constant for slow speeds) |
| $\vec{a}$ | Acceleration of the body's centre of mass | **m/s²** | Vector |
| $\vec{F}$ | Sum of all applied external (real) forces | **N (Newton)** | Vector |
| $m\vec{a}$ | Effective force (kinetic reaction) | **N (Newton)** | Vector |
| $-m\vec{a}$ | Inertia force (reversed effective force) | **N (Newton)** | Vector |
| $\vec{F_I}$ | Inertia force vector | **N (Newton)** | Vector |
| $I_G$ | Mass moment of inertia about centre of mass $G$ | **kg·m²** | Scalar |

### 1.4 Why D'Alembert's Principle Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> D'Alembert's Principle is the **gateway concept** for solving problems in dynamics using equilibrium techniques. It allows engineers to:
> 1. Solve complex dynamic problems using the same free-body-diagram (FBD) approach as statics.
> 2. Determine tensions, reactions, and accelerations in systems of connected bodies (pulleys, lifts, vehicles).
> 3. Analyze the motion of rigid bodies undergoing translation or rotation.
> 4. Formulate the equations of motion for mechanical systems using a unified, equilibrium-based method.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — From Newton's Second Law to D'Alembert

The principle is fundamentally a restatement of **Newton's Second Law of Motion**. Let us build the theory step by step:

### Step 1: Newton's Second Law (The Starting Point)
For a particle of mass $m$ moving with acceleration $\vec{a}$ under the action of a resultant external force $\vec{F}$:

$$\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$$

### Step 2: Defining the Effective Force
The term $m\vec{a}$ is called the **effective force** of the particle. It is the force that would produce the observed motion if the particle were free:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{eff}} = m\vec{a}$$

### Step 3: Re-arranging into a Zero-Sum Form
Bringing all terms to one side of the equation:

$$\vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0$$

### Step 4: Defining the Inertia Force
The reversed effective force is defined as the **inertia force**:

$$\vec{F_I} = -m\vec{a} = -(m\vec{a})$$

So the equilibrium-style equation becomes:

$$\vec{F} + \vec{F_I} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Interpretation:** A particle in motion is in **dynamic equilibrium** under the action of the applied forces and the inertia force. This fictitious "inertia force" is not a real force in the Newtonian sense — it is a mathematical construct that allows us to apply static equilibrium equations ($\sum F = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$) to dynamic problems.

### 2.2 Extension to Systems of Particles

For a system of $n$ particles, the principle is applied to each particle and then summed (or considered at the centre of mass):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (-m_i \vec{a_i}) = 0$$

For the system as a whole (using the centre of mass $G$):

$$\sum \vec{F_{\text{ext}}} = m \vec{a_G}$$

where $m = \sum m_i$ is the total mass and $\vec{a_G}$ is the acceleration of the centre of mass.

### 2.3 Extension to Rigid Bodies

For a rigid body undergoing general plane motion (translation + rotation), D'Alembert's Principle provides **three equilibrium equations** (two force equations and one moment equation):

$$\sum F_x = m a_{Gx}$$

$$\sum F_y = m a_{Gy}$$

$$\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$$

where:
- $a_{Gx}, a_{Gy}$ are the components of acceleration of the centre of mass $G$.
- $I_G$ is the **mass moment of inertia** of the body about an axis through $G$ perpendicular to the plane of motion.
- $\alpha$ is the **angular acceleration** of the body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inertia Couple:** For a rotating rigid body, the inertia forces do not simply reduce to a single force at $G$. They also form a **couple** (a moment) of magnitude $I_G \alpha$. This is the inertia torque that opposes the angular acceleration.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0$ | D'Alembert's Equation (Particle) | Single particle in motion |
| 2 | $\vec{F_I} = -m\vec{a}$ | Inertia Force Definition | Always opposite to acceleration |
| 3 | $\vec{F}_{\text{eff}} = m\vec{a}$ | Effective Force | Force responsible for motion |
| 4 | $\sum F_x = m a_{Gx}$ | Force eqn. along x-axis (Rigid Body) | Translation of centre of mass |
| 5 | $\sum F_y = m a_{Gy}$ | Force eqn. along y-axis (Rigid Body) | Translation of centre of mass |
| 6 | $\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$ | Moment eqn. about G (Rigid Body) | Angular motion of body |
| 7 | $I_G = m k_G^2$ | Moment of Inertia (radius of gyration form) | Rotational inertia |
| 8 | $I_O = I_G + m d^2$ | Parallel Axis Theorem | Transfer $I$ to another axis |
| 9 | $\alpha = \dfrac{a}{r}$ | Angular acceleration (rolling) | Pure rolling motion |
| 10 | $F_I = \dfrac{W}{g} a$ | Inertia force in terms of weight | Common engineering form |
| 11 | $T = I_O \alpha$ | Torque equation (about fixed axis O) | Pulleys, rotating shafts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** D'Alembert's Principle is used extensively in:
> - **Vehicle dynamics:** Computing brake forces, traction, and acceleration loads.
> - **Lift and elevator design:** Cable tension calculations.
> - **Rotating machinery:** Bearing reactions on shafts, flywheel analysis.
> - **Robotics and biomechanics:** Computing joint forces during motion.
