# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: D'Alembert's Principle from Newton's Second Law

We will derive D'Alembert's Principle rigorously from the **second law of motion** for both a single particle and a rigid body.

---

### Derivation 1: For a Single Particle

**Step 1 — Starting Hypothesis (Newton's Second Law).**
Consider a particle of mass $m$ at position $P$ moving with instantaneous acceleration $\vec{a}$. Let the resultant of all real applied forces acting on it be $\vec{F}$ (a vector with components $F_x$ and $F_y$).

By Newton's Second Law, the force is equal to the rate of change of momentum. For constant mass, this simplifies to:

$$\vec{F} = \dfrac{d(m\vec{v})}{dt} = m \dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = m\vec{a}$$

**Step 2 — Separating the Equation into Components.**
The vector equation can be broken into scalar components along the $x$ and $y$ axes:

$$F_x = m a_x \quad \text{and} \quad F_y = m a_y$$

**Step 3 — Re-arranging into the Equilibrium Form.**
Bring all terms to the left-hand side to mimic a static equilibrium equation:

$$F_x - m a_x = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad F_y - m a_y = 0$$

**Step 4 — Defining the Inertia Force Components.**
We now **interpret** the term $-m a_x$ as the $x$-component of an **inertia force** $\vec{F_I}$ acting on the particle. Similarly, $-m a_y$ is the $y$-component of the inertia force.

Formally:

$$\vec{F_I} = -m\vec{a}$$

This is a vector with components $F_{Ix} = -m a_x$ and $F_{Iy} = -m a_y$.

**Step 5 — Final D'Alembert Equation.**
Substituting, we get the equilibrium-style equations:

$$F_x + F_{Ix} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad F_y + F_{Iy} = 0$$

In compact vector form:

$$\vec{F} + \vec{F_I} = 0$$

This is the **D'Alembert Equation for a particle**. The particle is in **dynamic equilibrium** under the action of the real forces and the inertia force.

---

### Derivation 2: For a Rigid Body in Plane Motion

**Step 1 — System of Particles Approach.**
A rigid body is treated as a system of $n$ particles. For the $i$-th particle of mass $m_i$ and acceleration $\vec{a_i}$:

$$\vec{F_i^{(\text{ext})}} + \vec{F_{Ii}} = 0$$

where $\vec{F_{Ii}} = -m_i \vec{a_i}$.

**Step 2 — Summation Over All Particles.**
Summing over all $n$ particles in the body:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F_i^{(\text{ext})}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (-m_i \vec{a_i}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Reduction to Centre of Mass.**
The internal forces cancel out in pairs (Newton's Third Law). The sum of $m_i \vec{a_i}$ over all particles equals $m \vec{a_G}$, where $m$ is the total mass and $\vec{a_G}$ is the acceleration of the centre of mass. So:

$$\sum \vec{F_{\text{ext}}} - m \vec{a_G} = 0$$

Or, written in the dynamic-equilibrium form:

$$\sum \vec{F_{\text{ext}}} + \vec{F_I} = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \vec{F_I} = -m \vec{a_G}$$

This gives the two **translational** equations:

$$\sum F_x = m a_{Gx} \quad \text{and} \quad \sum F_y = m a_{Gy}$$

**Step 4 — Rotational Equation (The Moment Form).**
Taking moments about the centre of mass $G$ of all the inertia forces $-m_i \vec{a_i}$:

$$\sum M_G (\text{real forces}) + \sum M_G(\text{inertia forces}) = 0$$

The sum of moments of inertia forces about $G$ is found (using the definition of mass moment of inertia) to equal $-I_G \alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration. Therefore:

$$\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$$

**Step 5 — The Complete Three-Equation Set.**

$$\boxed{\sum F_x = m a_{Gx} \quad ; \quad \sum F_y = m a_{Gy} \quad ; \quad \sum M_G = I_G \alpha}$$

These three equations form the **kinetic equilibrium equations** for a rigid body in plane motion, derived directly from D'Alembert's Principle.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Problem Classification & Solution Strategy

| Problem Type | Key Unknown | Equations to Apply | Strategy |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------|----------|
| Single particle moving in straight line | Acceleration, Tension, Normal reaction | $\sum F = ma$ along motion direction | Draw FBD with real forces + inertia force $-ma$ |
| Two connected bodies (pulley/rope) | Tension, Acceleration | $\sum F = ma$ for each body | Separate FBDs, assume direction of motion |
| Lift / Elevator problems | Cable tension $T$ | $T - W = \dfrac{W}{g} a$ | Sign of $a$ depends on lift going up or down |
| Vehicle on incline with acceleration | Friction, Acceleration, Force | Resolve forces along and perpendicular to incline | Apply $\sum F_x = ma$ and $\sum F_y = 0$ |
| Rigid body rotation (pulley, wheel) | Torque, Angular acceleration | $\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$ | Couple inertia force $-m a_G$ at $G$ + inertia couple $I_G \alpha$ |
| Combined translation + rotation (e.g., rolling wheel) | $a, \alpha, F$ | All three equations with rolling constraint $a = r\alpha$ | Use $I_G = m k_G^2$ for standard shapes |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Illustrative Solution (KTU Typical Pattern)

> **Problem:** A body of mass $m = 20 \text{ kg}$ is being pulled up along a smooth inclined plane ($\theta = 30°$) by a force $P = 250 \text{ N}$ parallel to the incline. Find the acceleration of the body using D'Alembert's Principle. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Solution Using D'Alembert's Principle:**

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram with Inertia Force.**
The body has the following forces:
- Applied force $P$ (up the incline) = $250 \text{ N}$
- Weight $W = mg$ (vertically downward) = $20 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \text{ N}$
- Normal reaction $N$ (perpendicular to incline)
- **Inertia force** $F_I = ma$ (down the incline, opposing motion)

**Step 2 — Resolve Weight Along and Perpendicular to the Incline.**

$$W \sin \theta = 196.2 \times \sin 30° = 196.2 \times 0.5 = 98.1 \text{ N} \quad (\text{down the incline})$$

$$W \cos \theta = 196.2 \times \cos 30° = 196.2 \times 0.866 = 169.92 \text{ N} \quad (\text{into the incline})$$

**Step 3 — Apply Dynamic Equilibrium Along the Incline (D'Alembert).**
Sum of forces along the incline = 0 (real forces + inertia force):

$$P - W \sin \theta - F_I = 0$$

Substituting $F_I = ma$:

$$P - W \sin \theta - ma = 0$$

$$250 - 98.1 - 20a = 0$$

$$151.9 = 20a$$

$$a = \dfrac{151.9}{20} = 7.595 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Check Perpendicular to Incline.**

$$N = W \cos \theta = 169.92 \text{ N} \quad (\text{no acceleration perpendicular to incline})$$

> **Answer:** The acceleration of the body up the incline is $a = 7.595 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 7.6 \text{ m/s}^2$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Dynamics to Statics via D'Alembert

The following block diagram maps the conceptual flow of how D'Alembert's Principle converts a dynamic problem into a static-equilibrium problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Dynamic Problem Given] --> B[Identify the Moving Body and its Mass m]
    B --> C[Determine the Direction of Expected Motion]
    C --> D[Compute the Inertia Force F_I = m times a]
    D --> E[Draw Free Body Diagram FBD]
    E --> F[Add Inertia Force F_I Opposite to Acceleration]
    F --> G[Apply Static Equilibrium Equations]
    G --> H1[Sum of Forces along x equals 0]
    G --> H2[Sum of Forces along y equals 0]
    G --> H3[Sum of Moments about G equals 0]
    H1 --> I[Solve the Simultaneous Equations]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Extract Unknowns: a, T, N, alpha]
    J --> K[End: Solution to Dynamic Problem Using Statics Method]
    
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    style K fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#E64A19,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style H2 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    style H3 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use D'Alembert vs Pure Newton's Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the problem a dynamic one with moving bodies?}
    Q1 -- No --> S1[Use Statics Only]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the system a single particle or a simple connected set?}
    Q2 -- Single Particle --> S2[Use F = m a directly OR D Alembert]
    Q2 -- Connected Bodies --> Q3{Do you know the acceleration direction?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> S3[Use D Alembert Principle with FBDs]
    Q3 -- No --> S4[Assume a direction, solve, check sign]
    Q2 -- Rigid Body Rotating --> S5[Use Moment Equation sum M_G equals I_G alpha]
    Q2 -- Rigid Body Translating --> S6[Use Force Equations sum F equals m a_G]
    Q2 -- General Plane Motion --> S7[Use all three D Alembert equations simultaneously]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** State D'Alembert's Principle. Explain the concept of inertia force.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **D'Alembert's Principle:** It states that the sum of the applied (real) forces and the reversed effective forces (called inertia forces) acting on a body in motion is equal to zero, i.e., the body can be considered to be in **dynamic equilibrium**.
>
> **Inertia Force Concept:** The inertia force is defined as the negative of the product of mass and acceleration of the body, $\vec{F_I} = -m\vec{a}$. It is a fictitious force that represents the resistance of the body to a change in its state of motion. It is directed opposite to the direction of acceleration and has the same magnitude as the force required to produce that acceleration.
>
> **Mathematically:** $\vec{F} + \vec{F_I} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between effective force and inertia force. Give one example where D'Alembert's Principle is applied.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | Effective Force | Inertia Force |
> |--------|----------------|----------------|
> | Definition | $m \vec{a}$ (in the direction of acceleration) | $-m \vec{a}$ (opposite to acceleration) |
> | Physical meaning | The force that actually causes the motion | The body's resistance to motion (fictitious) |
> | Direction | Same as $\vec{a}$ | Opposite to $\vec{a}$ |
> | Magnitude | Equal | Equal |
>
> **Example of Application:** A lift (elevator) accelerating upward — D'Alembert's Principle gives the cable tension as $T = W + \dfrac{W}{g} a$, where the inertia force $\dfrac{W}{g} a$ acts downward, increasing the tension above the static weight.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Based on KTU Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q3]**

> A block of mass $m_1 = 30 \text{ kg}$ is placed on a smooth horizontal surface and is connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth pulley to a hanging block of mass $m_2 = 20 \text{ kg}$. Using D'Alembert's Principle:
>
> **(a)** Draw the free body diagrams of both blocks showing all real forces and the inertia forces. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Determine the acceleration of the system and the tension in the string. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Free Body Diagrams (7 marks)**

**Block $m_1$ on the table (horizontal):**
- Weight $W_1 = m_1 g = 30 \times 9.81 = 294.3 \text{ N}$ (vertically down)
- Normal reaction $N$ (vertically up)
- Tension $T$ in string (horizontal, towards pulley)
- **Inertia force** $F_{I1} = m_1 a$ (horizontal, away from pulley, opposing motion)

**Block $m_2$ hanging vertically:**
- Weight $W_2 = m_2 g = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2 \text{ N}$ (vertically down)
- Tension $T$ in string (vertically up)
- **Inertia force** $F_{I2} = m_2 a$ (vertically up, opposing downward motion)

> Both blocks have the same magnitude of acceleration $a$ (string is inextensible). The heavier block $m_2$ drives the motion downward, pulling $m_1$ horizontally.

**Part (b) — Calculation of Acceleration and Tension (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply D'Alembert's Equation to Block $m_1$ (horizontal direction):**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \text{(real forces + inertia force)}$$

$$T - m_1 a = 0$$

$$T = m_1 a \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply D'Alembert's Equation to Block $m_2$ (vertical direction):**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \text{(real forces + inertia force)}$$

$$T + m_2 a - m_2 g = 0$$

$$T = m_2 g - m_2 a \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used:** For block $m_2$, downward is the direction of motion. The inertia force $m_2 a$ acts **upward** (opposite to acceleration), so it appears with a positive sign when we balance forces in the downward direction. The applied forces are $m_2 g$ (down, +) and $T$ (up, −). So the balance is $m_2 g - T - m_2 a = 0$, which rearranges to $T = m_2 g - m_2 a$. ✓

**Step 3 — Solve Equations (i) and (ii) Simultaneously.**

From (i) and (ii):

$$m_1 a = m_2 g - m_2 a$$

$$m_1 a + m_2 a = m_2 g$$

$$a (m_1 + m_2) = m_2 g$$

$$a = \dfrac{m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values.**

$$a = \dfrac{20 \times 9.81}{30 + 20} = \dfrac{196.2}{50} = 3.924 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Find the Tension.**

$$T = m_1 a = 30 \times 3.924 = 117.72 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Acceleration: $a = 3.924 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 3.92 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Tension: $T = 117.72 \text{ N} \approx 117.7 \text{ N}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q4]**

> A lift (elevator) of mass $M = 800 \text{ kg}$ carries a load of $m = 200 \text{ kg}$. It is being pulled up by a cable with an upward acceleration of $2 \text{ m/s}^2$. Using D'Alembert's Principle:
>
> **(a)** Show all forces acting on the lift-load system on a free body diagram and derive the equation of motion. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Calculate the tension in the cable and the reaction force between the lift floor and the load. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Free Body Diagram and Equation of Motion (7 marks)**

**Combined System (Lift + Load) FBD:**
- Total weight $W = (M + m) g$ acting **downward**.
- Cable tension $T$ acting **upward**.
- **Inertia force** $F_I = (M + m) a$ acting **downward** (opposite to upward acceleration).

**D'Alembert Equation (vertical equilibrium of dynamic system):**

$$T - W - F_I = 0$$

$$T - (M + m) g - (M + m) a = 0$$

$$T = (M + m)(g + a)$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Tension in the Cable.**

$$T = (M + m)(g + a) = (800 + 200)(9.81 + 2)$$

$$T = 1000 \times 11.81 = 11810 \text{ N}$$

$$T = 11.81 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Reaction Between Load and Lift Floor.**

For the load alone (mass $m$):
- Weight $mg$ acting **down**.
- Normal reaction $N$ from floor acting **up**.
- Inertia force $ma$ acting **down**.

D'Alembert equation for the load:

$$N - mg - ma = 0$$

$$N = m(g + a) = 200 \times (9.81 + 2) = 200 \times 11.81$$

$$N = 2362 \text{ N} \approx 2.36 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Cable Tension: $T = 11810 \text{ N} = 11.81 \text{ kN}$
- Floor Reaction on Load: $N = 2362 \text{ N} = 2.36 \text{ kN}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of Inertia Force:** Students often place the inertia force in the **same** direction as motion. The inertia force is **always opposite** to the acceleration vector. (Lose 1–2 marks per occurrence.)
> 2. **Frictionless Assumption:** If the problem says "smooth" surface or "smooth pulley", do **not** include friction in the FBD.
> 3. **Newton's vs. Weight Confusion:** Some students use $W = mg$ but then forget to multiply by $g$ again when computing the inertia force $F_I = \dfrac{W}{g} \cdot a$. Use $F_I = ma$ directly to avoid this trap.
> 4. **Connected Body Constraint:** For two blocks connected by a string, the acceleration magnitudes are equal, but the tensions on the two sides of a pulley may differ (only equal if the pulley is **massless and frictionless**).
> 5. **Units Check:** Always include units in the final answer. Marks are often deducted for unit-less numerical answers.
> 6. **Rigid Body Moment Equation:** For problems with rotating bodies, the **moment equation** $\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$ is required in addition to the force equations. Forgetting the inertia couple $I_G \alpha$ is a frequent mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — D'Alembert's Principle**

- ✅ **Principle Statement:** $\vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0$ (Dynamic equilibrium form of Newton's Second Law).
- ✅ **Inertia Force:** $\vec{F_I} = -m\vec{a}$, always **opposite** to the direction of acceleration.
- ✅ **Effective Force:** $m\vec{a}$, the force that produces the motion.
- ✅ **Inertia Force in Weight Form:** $F_I = \dfrac{W}{g} \cdot a$, useful in problems where weight $W$ is given directly.
- ✅ **Three Kinetic Equilibrium Equations for a Rigid Body in Plane Motion:**
  1. $\sum F_x = m a_{Gx}$
  2. $\sum F_y = m a_{Gy}$
  3. $\sum M_G = I_G \alpha$
- ✅ **Inertia Couple:** $I_G \alpha$ acts in the direction **opposing** the angular acceleration $\alpha$.
- ✅ **Mass Moment of Inertia for Common Shapes:**
  - Solid disc: $I_G = \dfrac{1}{2} m r^2$
  - Solid sphere: $I_G = \dfrac{2}{5} m r^2$
  - Slender rod (about centre): $I_G = \dfrac{1}{12} m L^2$
  - Thin ring/hoop: $I_G = m r^2$
- ✅ **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $I_O = I_G + m d^2$ (transfer $I$ from $G$ to any parallel axis $O$).
- ✅ **Rolling Without Slipping Constraint:** $a = r \alpha$ (linear acceleration = radius × angular acceleration).
- ✅ **Lift Problem Shortcut:** Cable tension $T = W \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right)$ when accelerating upward.
- ✅ **D'Alembert's Principle is a restatement of Newton's Second Law, not an independent physical law** — the "inertia force" is a mathematical convenience, not a real interaction force.
- ✅ **Free Body Diagram is the most critical step** — always include the inertia force vector with correct direction and label.
- ✅ **Connected Body Systems:** Assume a direction of motion, write FBD for each body, apply $\sum F = 0$ (with inertia force), solve simultaneously, and verify signs of the answers.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Focus Areas:** Inclined plane problems, lift/elevator problems, pulley systems with two blocks, and basic rigid body rotation problems (e.g., pulley with string over it).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion on Horizontal and Inclined Surfaces, Motion of Connected Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion on Horizontal and Inclined Surfaces, Motion of Connected Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kinetics of Particle Motion** is the branch of Engineering Mechanics that deals with the relationship between the forces acting on a body and the resulting motion (acceleration) it produces. When a body moves on a **horizontal surface** or an **inclined plane**, or when **multiple bodies are connected** by inextensible strings passing over frictionless pulleys, the body is said to be in a state of **dynamic equilibrium** under the action of unbalanced forces.

The governing principle is **Newton's Second Law of Motion**:

$$\vec{F}_{net} = m \vec{a}$$

For motion constrained to a straight line, this reduces to a scalar equation:

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a$$

where $F_{net}$ is the algebraic sum of all forces acting along the direction of motion (in **Newtons, N**), $m$ is the mass of the body (in **kg**), and $a$ is the resulting acceleration (in **m/s²**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The topic strictly belongs to Module 3 of GCEST103. Students must master drawing Free Body Diagrams (FBDs) and resolving forces along and perpendicular to the direction of motion. Marks are awarded for clearly identifying the *net driving force* and the *resisting forces* (friction, tension, normal reaction) before applying $F = ma$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine three real-world scenarios:

**Scenario A — The Grocery Cart (Horizontal Surface):**
You are pushing a loaded shopping cart across a supermarket floor. You apply a horizontal force $P$. The cart resists motion because of friction $\mu N$. Once $P$ exceeds the static friction limit, the cart accelerates. The harder you push, the faster it accelerates. The heavier the load, the more force you need for the same acceleration. This is $F_{net} = ma$ in everyday life.

**Scenario B — The Skateboard on a Ramp (Inclined Surface):**
A child on a skateboard is at the top of a sloped driveway. Even with no push, gravity pulls the child down the slope because the component of weight along the incline ($mg \sin\theta$) is unbalanced. If the driveway is rough, friction ($\mu mg \cos\theta$) opposes the motion. Whether the child slides or stays put depends on whether $mg\sin\theta$ is greater than the maximum friction.

**Scenario C — The Elevator with Counterweight (Connected Bodies):**
Two masses connected by a rope over a pulley is the classic *Atwood Machine*. Gravity pulls the heavier mass down, which pulls the lighter mass up. The acceleration depends on *both* masses and gravity. Engineering applications include elevators, cranes, and conveyor systems.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard value used in KTU numericals)
- **Coefficient of friction:** $\mu$ — dimensionless, with $0 \le \mu \le 1.5$ for typical engineering surfaces
- **Tension in string:** $T$ — in **Newtons (N)**
- **Normal reaction:** $N$ — in **Newtons (N)**
- **Weight:** $W = mg$ — in **Newtons (N)**
- **Mass:** $m$ — in **kilograms (kg)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Rule (KTU Standard):** Take the direction of assumed motion as **positive**. All forces aiding motion are **positive**; all forces opposing motion are **negative**. If the resulting acceleration turns out to be negative, the assumed direction is wrong, but the magnitude remains valid.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three-Step Method

Every motion problem in this module can be solved using a structured **three-step method** that examiners love to see:

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram (FBD)**
Isolate the body and draw ALL forces acting on it:
- Weight $W = mg$ acting vertically downward
- Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to the contact surface
- Applied force $P$ in its given direction
- Friction force $F_f = \mu N$ opposing the *impending or actual* motion
- Tension $T$ along the string, pulling away from the body

**Step 2 — Resolve Forces Along the Direction of Motion**
Set up a coordinate axis along the line of motion. Sum all forces algebraically. For inclined surfaces, align one axis along the slope and the other perpendicular to it. This converts the problem into a 1-D problem.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law**

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a$$

Solve for the unknown — usually acceleration $a$, tension $T$, or applied force $P$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why draw an FBD?** Forces are vectors acting in different directions. An FBD converts a 3-D force problem into a 1-D algebraic problem. Without it, students sign-mistake forces and lose 3-4 marks per question.
- **Why resolve forces?** A force acting at an angle has components. Only the component *along* the motion causes acceleration. The perpendicular component is balanced by the normal reaction.
- **Why $F = ma$?** This is the *defining equation* of dynamics. Statics ($F = 0$) is a special case where $a = 0$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Physical Configuration | Governing Equation | Notes |
|---|------------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Body pulled on smooth horizontal surface by force $P$ | $P = m \cdot a$ | No friction |
| 2 | Body pulled on rough horizontal surface by horizontal force $P$ | $P - \mu m g = m \cdot a$ | Friction opposes motion |
| 3 | Body pulled on rough horizontal surface by force $P$ at angle $\alpha$ above horizontal | $P \cos\alpha - \mu(mg - P\sin\alpha) = m a$ | $N = mg - P\sin\alpha$ |
| 4 | Body sliding DOWN a smooth inclined plane (angle $\theta$) | $g \sin\theta = a$ | Independent of mass |
| 5 | Body sliding DOWN a rough inclined plane (angle $\theta$) | $g(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta) = a$ | Motion occurs only if $\tan\theta > \mu$ |
| 6 | Body pushed UP a rough inclined plane by force $P$ parallel to plane | $P - mg(\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta) = m a$ | Friction acts downward during ascent |
| 7 | Body sliding DOWN a rough incline, force $P$ applied UP along plane | $mg\sin\theta - \mu mg\cos\theta - P = m a$ | If positive, body moves down |
| 8 | Atwood Machine (two masses $m_1 > m_2$ over frictionless pulley) | $a = \dfrac{(m_1 - m_2)g}{m_1 + m_2}$, $T = \dfrac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Both masses move with same magnitude of $a$ |
| 9 | Two bodies on rough horizontal surface connected by string, pulled by force $P$ on $m_1$ | $a = \dfrac{P - \mu_1 m_1 g - \mu_2 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Tension $T$ must be found by analyzing one body |
| 10 | Mass on smooth table connected by string over pulley to hanging mass $m_2$ | $a = \dfrac{m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$, $T = \dfrac{m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$ | $m_1$ on table, $m_2$ hanging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Formula (3) is the *most-asked* type — a force applied at an angle. The vertical component $P\sin\alpha$ *reduces* the normal reaction, which *reduces* friction. This is the principle behind using trolleys with angled handles to move heavy objects easily.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Conveyor Belts and Cranes:** Designing pulley systems for moving loads requires calculating tensions and accelerations using the formulas above.
- **Vehicle Braking Systems:** The friction formula $F_f = \mu N$ is used to compute stopping distance on horizontal and inclined roads (downhill braking is a classic KTU problem).
- **Escalators and Ski Lifts:** Inclined plane motion with applied force governs the motor sizing for these systems.
- **Elevator Design:** Counterweight systems are direct applications of the Atwood Machine principle.
- **Rocket Sleds and Maglev Trains:** Understanding motion on horizontal and inclined surfaces is fundamental to high-speed transport design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Body on a Rough Horizontal Surface Pulled by an Inclined Force

**Problem Setup:** A body of mass $m$ rests on a rough horizontal surface (coefficient of friction $\mu$). A force $P$ is applied at angle $\alpha$ above the horizontal. Derive the expression for acceleration.

**Step 1 — Identify all forces (FBD):**
- Weight: $W = mg$ (downward)
- Normal reaction: $N$ (upward)
- Applied force: $P$ at angle $\alpha$ (with components $P\cos\alpha$ horizontal, $P\sin\alpha$ vertical upward)
- Kinetic friction: $F_f = \mu N$ (opposing motion, i.e., backward)

**Step 2 — Resolve forces vertically (no vertical motion):**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies N + P\sin\alpha - mg = 0$$

Solving for $N$:

$$N = mg - P\sin\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Resolve forces horizontally (direction of motion):**

$$\sum F_x = m a$$

$$P\cos\alpha - \mu N = m a$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $N$ from Step 2:**

$$P\cos\alpha - \mu (mg - P\sin\alpha) = m a$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect terms:**

$$P\cos\alpha - \mu m g + \mu P \sin\alpha = m a$$

**Step 6 — Group $P$ terms:**

$$P(\cos\alpha + \mu \sin\alpha) - \mu m g = m a$$

**Step 7 — Final expression for acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{P(\cos\alpha + \mu \sin\alpha) - \mu m g}{m}$$

$$\boxed{a = \frac{P}{m}(\cos\alpha + \mu \sin\alpha) - \mu g}$$

**Step 8 — Special case verification:**
If $\alpha = 0$ (horizontal pull): $a = \frac{P}{m} - \mu g = \frac{P - \mu m g}{m}$ ✓ (matches Formula 2)
If $\mu = 0$ (smooth surface): $a = \frac{P \cos\alpha}{m}$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Body Sliding Down a Rough Inclined Plane

**Problem Setup:** A body of mass $m$ is on an inclined plane making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. Coefficient of friction is $\mu$. Derive acceleration.

**Step 1 — Choose coordinate system:**
Let $x$-axis be along the incline (positive down the slope) and $y$-axis be perpendicular to the incline (positive outward).

**Step 2 — Resolve weight $mg$ into components:**
- Along incline (down the slope): $mg \sin\theta$
- Perpendicular to incline (into the surface): $mg \cos\theta$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium perpendicular to incline (no motion in $y$):**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies N - mg \cos\theta = 0$$

$$N = mg \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Identify friction direction:**
Since body slides down, kinetic friction acts *up* the slope, opposing motion.

$$F_f = \mu N = \mu m g \cos\theta$$

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law along the incline:**

$$mg \sin\theta - \mu m g \cos\theta = m a$$

**Step 6 — Divide throughout by $m$:**

$$g \sin\theta - \mu g \cos\theta = a$$

**Step 7 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{a = g(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)}$$

**Step 8 — Condition for motion to begin:**
The body will *not* slide if static friction can balance the driving force, i.e., when:

$$\tan\theta \le \mu_s$$

This is the **angle of friction** concept — when $\theta$ equals the angle of friction $\lambda = \tan^{-1}\mu$, the body is on the verge of sliding.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Atwood Machine (Two Masses Over a Pulley)

**Problem Setup:** Two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ (with $m_1 > m_2$) are connected by an inextensible, massless string over a frictionless, massless pulley. Derive acceleration $a$ and tension $T$.

**Step 1 — Identify motion:**
$m_1$ moves **down** with acceleration $a$; $m_2$ moves **up** with the same magnitude $a$ (string inextensible).

**Step 2 — FBD of $m_1$ (heavier mass, moving down):**
Forces: weight $m_1 g$ (down), tension $T$ (up).

$$m_1 g - T = m_1 a \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

**Step 3 — FBD of $m_2$ (lighter mass, moving up):**
Forces: weight $m_2 g$ (down), tension $T$ (up).

$$T - m_2 g = m_2 a \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

**Step 4 — Add Equations A and B** (this eliminates $T$):

$$m_1 g - T + T - m_2 g = m_1 a + m_2 a$$

$$(m_1 - m_2) g = (m_1 + m_2) a$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $a$:**

$$\boxed{a = \frac{(m_1 - m_2) g}{m_1 + m_2}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $a$ back into Equation B to find $T$:**

$$T = m_2 g + m_2 \cdot \frac{(m_1 - m_2) g}{m_1 + m_2}$$

$$T = m_2 g \left[1 + \frac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1 + m_2}\right]$$

$$T = m_2 g \left[\frac{m_1 + m_2 + m_1 - m_2}{m_1 + m_2}\right]$$

$$T = m_2 g \left[\frac{2 m_1}{m_1 + m_2}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Final tension expression:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}}$$

**Step 8 — Verification:**
If $m_1 = m_2$, then $a = 0$ and $T = m_1 g$ — system in equilibrium ✓
If $m_2 = 0$, then $a = g$ (free fall) and $T = 0$ — limit case ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Connected Bodies on a Rough Horizontal Surface

**Problem Setup:** Mass $m_1$ is connected to $m_2$ by a string. Force $P$ is applied to $m_1$ horizontally; both bodies move together. Coefficients of friction are $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$. Derive acceleration and tension.

**Step 1 — Combine the system (for acceleration):**
Treat $m_1$ and $m_2$ as a single system. The string is internal, so tension cancels.

$$\sum F_x = (m_1 + m_2) a$$

**Step 2 — External forces on the system:**
- Driving force: $P$ (forward)
- Friction on $m_1$: $\mu_1 m_1 g$ (backward)
- Friction on $m_2$: $\mu_2 m_2 g$ (backward)

$$P - \mu_1 m_1 g - \mu_2 m_2 g = (m_1 + m_2) a$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration of the system:**

$$\boxed{a = \frac{P - \mu_1 m_1 g - \mu_2 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Find tension by analyzing $m_2$ alone (FBD of $m_2$):**
Forces on $m_2$: Tension $T$ (forward), friction $\mu_2 m_2 g$ (backward).

$$T - \mu_2 m_2 g = m_2 a$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $a$ from Step 3:**

$$T = \mu_2 m_2 g + m_2 \cdot \frac{P - \mu_1 m_1 g - \mu_2 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$$

**Step 6 — Final tension expression:**

$$T = \frac{m_2 [P - \mu_1 m_1 g - \mu_2 m_2 g]}{m_1 + m_2} + \mu_2 m_2 g$$

**Step 7 — Special case (smooth surface, $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = 0$):**

$$a = \frac{P}{m_1 + m_2}, \quad T = \frac{m_2 P}{m_1 + m_2}$$

This shows tension is *less* than applied force because $m_1$ also accelerates.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Inclined Plane with Applied Force

**Problem:** A block of mass $20 \text{ kg}$ is on a rough inclined plane (angle $30°$, $\mu = 0.25$). A force $P = 300 \text{ N}$ is applied parallel to the plane, pushing the block *up* the slope. Find: (a) acceleration, (b) tension if the block is connected to a hanging $15 \text{ kg}$ mass via a pulley at the top. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Given:** $m_1 = 20 \text{ kg}$ (on incline), $m_2 = 15 \text{ kg}$ (hanging), $\theta = 30°$, $\mu = 0.25$, $P = 300 \text{ N}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Assumed motion:** Block moves UP the incline; $m_2$ moves DOWN.

**Part (a) — Without pulley connection (block alone, force $P$ up the plane):**

Friction acts DOWN the plane (opposing upward motion).

$$\sum F = m_1 a$$

$$P - m_1 g \sin\theta - \mu m_1 g \cos\theta = m_1 a$$

$$300 - (20)(9.81)(0.5) - (0.25)(20)(9.81)(0.866) = 20 a$$

$$300 - 98.1 - 42.48 = 20 a$$

$$159.42 = 20 a$$

$$\boxed{a = 7.97 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ up the incline}}$$

**Part (b) — With hanging mass $m_2$:**

Now $P$ is removed; the system is $m_1$ on incline + $m_2$ hanging.

Friction on $m_1$: opposing motion (block moves up, so friction is *down* the plane).

**For $m_1$ (on incline, moving up):**

$$T - m_1 g \sin\theta - \mu m_1 g \cos\theta = m_1 a$$

**For $m_2$ (hanging, moving down):**

$$m_2 g - T = m_2 a$$

**Adding the two equations:**

$$m_2 g - m_1 g \sin\theta - \mu m_1 g \cos\theta = (m_1 + m_2) a$$

$$(15)(9.81) - (20)(9.81)(0.5) - (0.25)(20)(9.81)(0.866) = (35) a$$

$$147.15 - 98.1 - 42.48 = 35 a$$

$$6.57 = 35 a$$

$$\boxed{a = 0.188 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Finding Tension** (from $m_2$ equation):

$$T = m_2 (g - a) = 15 (9.81 - 0.188) = 15 \times 9.622 = 144.33 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 144.3 \text{ N}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The very small acceleration in Part (b) shows that friction nearly balances gravity's pull on $m_2$. This is a typical "limiting case" that KTU examiners use to test whether students correctly identify friction direction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Free Body Diagram (FBD) Identification Flow

The following diagram shows the systematic identification process for forces on a body — a critical KTU skill.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify the Body] --> B[Isolate the Body from Surroundings]
    B --> C[Does Body Have Weight?]
    C -->|Yes| D[Add Force W = mg Downward]
    C -->|No| E[Skip Weight]
    D --> F[Is Body in Contact with a Surface?]
    E --> F
    F -->|Yes| G[Add Normal Reaction N Perpendicular to Surface]
    F -->|No| H[Skip Normal Reaction]
    G --> I[Is Surface Rough and Body Moving or Impending Motion?]
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[Add Friction Force muN Opposing Motion Direction]
    I -->|No| K[No Friction]
    J --> L[Is String Attached to Body?]
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| M[Add Tension T Pulling Away from Body Along String]
    L -->|No| N[No Tension]
    M --> O[Is Applied External Force P Given?]
    N --> O
    O -->|Yes| P[Resolve P into Components Along Axes]
    O -->|No| Q[No Applied Force]
    P --> R[Choose Coordinate Axes Along Direction of Motion]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Sum Forces Along Each Axis]
    S --> T[Apply Newtons Second Law F = ma]
    T --> U[Solve for Unknowns a, T, P, mu]
    U --> V[END: Solution Complete]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Three Configuration Templates

This diagram classifies the three primary problem types in Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Type 1: Horizontal Surface]
        A1[Body of Mass m] -->|Friction muN| A2[Horizontal Plane]
        A3[Applied Force P] -->|Angle alpha| A1
        A1 -->|Equation Pcos alpha minus muN equals ma| A4[Newton 2nd Law]
    end

    subgraph T2[Type 2: Inclined Surface]
        B1[Body of Mass m] -->|Angle theta| B2[Inclined Plane]
        B1 -->|Weight Component mg sin theta| B3[Driving Force Down Slope]
        B1 -->|Weight Component mg cos theta| B4[Normal Reaction Source]
        B4 -->|Friction mu mg cos theta| B5[Resisting Force]
        B3 --> B6[Net Force equals ma]
        B5 --> B6
    end

    subgraph T3[Type 3: Connected Bodies]
        C1[Mass m1] -->|String Tension T| C2[Frictionless Pulley]
        C2 -->|String Tension T| C3[Mass m2]
        C1 -->|Forces m1g T| C4[FBD of m1]
        C3 -->|Forces m2g T| C5[FBD of m2]
        C4 --> C6[Solve Simultaneous Equations]
        C5 --> C6
        C6 --> C7[Find a and T]
    end

    A4 --> S[Unified Solution using F equals ma]
    B6 --> S
    C7 --> S
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Atwood Machine with Rough Incline

This shows the system schematic for a complex KTU problem combining inclined surface and connected bodies.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYS[Combined System: Mass on Incline Connected to Hanging Mass]
        M1[Block of mass m1 on rough incline angle theta]
        M2[Hanging mass m2 in air]

        M1 -->|Weight m1g vertical down| F1[Resolve into m1g sin theta along plane and m1g cos theta perpendicular to plane]
        F1 --> N1[Normal Reaction N equals m1g cos theta]
        N1 --> FF1[Friction f equals mu N equals mu m1g cos theta opposing motion]

        M1 -->|String passes over pulley at top of incline| M2
        M2 -->|Weight m2g down| F2[Tension T up]

        M1 --> EQ1[Equation: T minus m1g sin theta minus f equals m1a]
        M2 --> EQ2[Equation: m2g minus T equals m2a]

        EQ1 --> SUM[Add EQ1 and EQ2 to eliminate T]
        EQ2 --> SUM
        SUM --> SOL[Solve for a and T]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Sign Convention Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC[Start: Identify Assumed Direction of Motion] --> Q1{Is the Direction Obvious?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, given in problem| D1[Take that direction as POSITIVE]
    Q1 -->|No, must guess| D2[Guess the heavier mass side or applied force direction as POSITIVE]
    D1 --> Q2{After Solving, is Acceleration Positive?}
    D2 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes, positive| OK[Assumed direction was correct. Magnitude is absolute value of a]
    Q2 -->|No, negative| REV[Reverse the assumed direction. The body actually moves opposite to assumption. Report a as positive magnitude]
    OK --> DONE[End: Report a magnitude and direction]
    REV --> DONE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Newton's Second Law of Motion. A body of mass 10 kg is moving on a smooth horizontal surface under the action of a horizontal force of 50 N. Find the acceleration of the body.**

**Model Answer:**

**Newton's Second Law of Motion:** The rate of change of linear momentum of a body is directly proportional to the net external force applied on it, and the change in momentum takes place in the direction of the applied force.

Mathematically: $\vec{F} = \dfrac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = m\vec{a}$ (for constant mass)

**Given:** $m = 10 \text{ kg}$, $F = 50 \text{ N}$, smooth surface ($\mu = 0$)

**Applying Newton's Second Law:**

$$F = m a \implies a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Acceleration of the body = 5 m/s²** in the direction of the applied force.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the terms 'angle of friction' and 'angle of repose'. A body is placed on an inclined plane. Derive the relationship between these angles and the coefficient of friction.**

**Model Answer:**

**Angle of Friction ($\lambda$):** It is the angle between the normal reaction $N$ and the resultant of $N$ and the maximum static friction force $F_{max}$, measured from the normal. It is the angle whose tangent equals the coefficient of static friction $\mu_s$.

**Angle of Repose ($\alpha$):** It is the minimum angle of inclination of an inclined plane with the horizontal at which a body placed on it just begins to slide down due to its own weight, without any external force.

**Derivation of Relationship:**

Consider a body of weight $W = mg$ on an inclined plane at angle of repose $\alpha$. At the verge of sliding, friction $F_{max} = \mu_s N$.

**Resolving forces along the incline:**

$$W \sin\alpha = F_{max} = \mu_s N$$

**Resolving forces perpendicular to the incline:**

$$N = W \cos\alpha$$

**Dividing the two equations:**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\mu_s N}{N} = \mu_s$$

Since $\lambda$ is defined by $\tan\lambda = \mu_s$:

$$\boxed{\tan\alpha = \tan\lambda = \mu_s \implies \alpha = \lambda}$$

The angle of repose and angle of friction are numerically equal for the same contact surfaces.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A body of mass 25 kg is pulled up a rough inclined plane (angle $20°$, $\mu = 0.2$) by a force of 200 N applied parallel to the plane. Determine: (i) the acceleration of the body, (ii) the time taken to travel 30 m from rest, (iii) the velocity at the end of 30 m. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Two bodies of masses 8 kg and 12 kg are connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth pulley. Find: (i) the acceleration of the system, (ii) the tension in the string, (iii) the distance traveled in 3 seconds starting from rest. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $m = 25 \text{ kg}$, $\theta = 20°$, $\mu = 0.2$, $P = 200 \text{ N}$ (parallel, up the plane), $s = 30 \text{ m}$, $u = 0$

**Free Body Diagram of body on incline:**
- Weight component along plane (down): $mg\sin\theta = 25 \times 9.81 \times \sin 20° = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.342 = 83.88 \text{ N}$
- Normal reaction: $N = mg\cos\theta = 25 \times 9.81 \times \cos 20° = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.9397 = 230.50 \text{ N}$
- Friction (down the plane, opposing upward motion): $F_f = \mu N = 0.2 \times 230.50 = 46.10 \text{ N}$
- Applied force (up the plane): $P = 200 \text{ N}$

**Applying Newton's Second Law along the plane:**

$$P - mg\sin\theta - \mu mg\cos\theta = ma$$

$$200 - 83.88 - 46.10 = 25 a$$

$$70.02 = 25 a$$

**(i) Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{70.02}{25} = 2.80 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (up the plane)}$$

**(ii) Time to travel 30 m from rest** (using $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$):

$$30 = 0 + \frac{1}{2}(2.80) t^2$$

$$t^2 = \frac{60}{2.80} = 21.43$$

$$t = \sqrt{21.43} = 4.63 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Final velocity** (using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$):

$$v^2 = 0 + 2(2.80)(30) = 168$$

$$v = \sqrt{168} = 12.96 \text{ m/s}$$

**Summary:** $a = 2.80 \text{ m/s}^2$, $t = 4.63 \text{ s}$, $v = 12.96 \text{ m/s}$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $m_1 = 12 \text{ kg}$ (heavier, will move down), $m_2 = 8 \text{ kg}$ (lighter, will move up), $u = 0$, $t = 3 \text{ s}$

**Free Body Diagram of $m_1$ (moving down):**

$$m_1 g - T = m_1 a \implies 12(9.81) - T = 12 a \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Free Body Diagram of $m_2$ (moving up):**

$$T - m_2 g = m_2 a \implies T - 8(9.81) = 8 a \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Adding equations (1) and (2):**

$$12(9.81) - 8(9.81) = 12a + 8a$$

$$(12 - 8)(9.81) = 20a$$

$$4(9.81) = 20a$$

**(i) Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{39.24}{20} = 1.962 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(ii) Tension** (substitute in equation (2)):

$$T = 8(9.81) + 8(1.962) = 78.48 + 15.696 = 94.18 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Distance in 3 seconds** (using $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$):

$$s = 0 + \frac{1}{2}(1.962)(3)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1.962)(9) = 8.83 \text{ m}$$

**Summary:** $a = 1.962 \text{ m/s}^2$, $T = 94.18 \text{ N}$, $s = 8.83 \text{ m}$

---

#### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** A block of mass 50 kg rests on a rough horizontal floor ($\mu = 0.3$). A force of 400 N is applied at an angle of $30°$ above the horizontal. Find: (i) the normal reaction, (ii) the frictional force, (iii) the acceleration of the block. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A body of mass 15 kg is placed on a rough inclined plane ($\theta = 30°$, $\mu = 0.2$). A force of 80 N is applied parallel to the plane, pushing the body *down* the slope. Determine: (i) whether the body moves or remains stationary, (ii) if it moves, find the acceleration. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for Choice B (a):**

**Given:** $m = 50 \text{ kg}$, $\mu = 0.3$, $P = 400 \text{ N}$, $\alpha = 30°$ (above horizontal)

**Step 1 — Resolve the applied force:**
- Horizontal component: $P\cos\alpha = 400 \cos 30° = 400 \times 0.866 = 346.41 \text{ N}$
- Vertical component (upward): $P\sin\alpha = 400 \sin 30° = 400 \times 0.5 = 200 \text{ N}$

**Step 2 — Find the normal reaction** (vertical equilibrium):

$$N + P\sin\alpha = mg$$

$$N = mg - P\sin\alpha = 50(9.81) - 200 = 490.5 - 200 = 290.5 \text{ N}$$

**(i) Normal reaction: $N = 290.5 \text{ N}$**

**Step 3 — Maximum static friction available:**

$$F_{max} = \mu_s N = 0.3 \times 290.5 = 87.15 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compare driving force with maximum friction:**
Driving force = $P\cos\alpha = 346.41 \text{ N}$
Since $346.41 > 87.15$, the body will move.

**(ii) Frictional force (kinetic) = 87.15 N** (assumed $\mu_k \approx \mu_s$ as is common in KTU problems)

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law (horizontal direction):**

$$P\cos\alpha - F_f = ma$$

$$346.41 - 87.15 = 50 a$$

$$259.26 = 50 a$$

**(iii) Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{259.26}{50} = 5.185 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

**Model Solution for Choice B (b):**

**Given:** $m = 15 \text{ kg}$, $\theta = 30°$, $\mu = 0.2$, $P = 80 \text{ N}$ (down the slope)

**Step 1 — Find the normal reaction** (perpendicular to incline, no motion):

$$N = mg\cos\theta = 15 \times 9.81 \times \cos 30° = 15 \times 9.81 \times 0.866 = 127.42 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum friction force:**

$$F_{max} = \mu N = 0.2 \times 127.42 = 25.48 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Find driving force down the plane:**
The driving force consists of both gravity component AND the applied force $P$ (both act down the plane):

$$F_{driving} = mg\sin\theta + P = 15(9.81)(0.5) + 80 = 73.58 + 80 = 153.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compare driving force with maximum friction:**

$$F_{driving} = 153.58 \text{ N} \gg F_{max} = 25.48 \text{ N}$$

**(i) Since driving force greatly exceeds maximum friction, the body will slide DOWN the plane.**

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law along the incline (friction acts UP, opposing downward motion):**

$$mg\sin\theta + P - \mu mg\cos\theta = ma$$

$$153.58 - 25.48 = 15 a$$

$$128.10 = 15 a$$

**(ii) Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{128.10}{15} = 8.54 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ down the slope}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Friction Direction Error (–2 marks):** Students often draw friction in the *wrong* direction. Friction *always* opposes the direction of *impending or actual motion*, not the direction of the applied force. If the body moves down, friction acts UP the plane.
> 2. **Sign Convention Mix-up (–2 marks):** When the assumed direction of motion is wrong, students sometimes report a negative acceleration. KTU expects you to clearly state the *magnitude* and *correct direction* of motion.
> 3. **Forgetting the Vertical Component (–3 marks):** When a force is applied at an angle, students often use $P$ directly without resolving it into $P\cos\alpha$ and $P\sin\alpha$. This is a fatal error in angled-force problems.
> 4. **Treating Atwood Machine as Symmetric (–2 marks):** Students sometimes write $T = (m_1 + m_2)g/2$ assuming equal tension. The correct tension $T = \frac{2m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$ is *between* the two weights: $m_2 g < T < m_1 g$.
> 5. **Units Inconsistency (–1 mark):** Use SI units throughout. Mass in kg, force in N, distance in m, time in s, velocity in m/s, acceleration in m/s².
> 6. **No FBD shown (–2 marks):** Always draw a labeled Free Body Diagram before writing equations. KTU examiners explicitly award 1-2 marks for a clean FBD.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton's Second Law** ($F = ma$) is the single most important equation for this entire module.
- **Free Body Diagrams** are *mandatory* — draw them before writing any equation.
- **Sign convention:** Assume a direction of motion; stick to it. Reverse if $a$ comes out negative.
- **Friction force** $F_f = \mu N$ always opposes motion or impending motion; its direction must be determined *carefully*.
- **On inclined planes**, always resolve weight into $mg\sin\theta$ (along plane) and $mg\cos\theta$ (perpendicular to plane). The latter equals the normal reaction $N$ if no vertical force is applied.
- **For an angled applied force** on a horizontal surface: $N = mg - P\sin\alpha$ (vertical component *reduces* $N$ and hence friction).
- **Atwood Machine:** $a = \frac{(m_1 - m_2)g}{m_1 + m_2}$ and $T = \frac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1 + m_2}$.
- **Connected bodies on horizontal surface:** solve the *whole system* for $a$ first, then isolate one body to find tension.
- **Angle of friction $\lambda$ = angle of repose $\alpha$**, both related by $\tan\lambda = \mu$.
- **Condition for motion on incline:** $\tan\theta > \mu$ (otherwise body remains stationary).
- **Kinematic equations** (use after finding $a$): $v = u + at$, $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$, $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$.
- **Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** unless specifically instructed otherwise; $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ is sometimes used for simplification.
- **Always check units** at the end and box your final answers clearly.
- **For connected bodies**, string tension is the same throughout only if the string is massless and the pulley is frictionless — assume this unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curvilinear Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Curvilinear Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curvilinear Translation** is a specific category of rigid body motion in which **every particle of the body travels along a curved path**, and these curved trajectories of all particles are **identical in shape and parallel** to each other at every instant. Since the body does not rotate, any straight line drawn on the body remains **parallel to itself** throughout the motion, but its orientation changes only because every point on the line follows the same curved arc.

The general classification of plane rigid body motion in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 places Curvilinear Translation between **Rectilinear Translation** (straight-line paths) and **General Plane Motion** (combined translation + rotation). It is the kinematic study of bodies whose every point traces congruent curved trajectories.

**Key Kinematic Quantities (SI Units):**

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Vector Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position | $\vec{r}$ | **m (meter)** | Vector |
| Velocity | $\vec{v}$ | **m/s (meter per second)** | Vector |
| Acceleration | $\vec{a}$ | **m/s² (meter per second squared)** | Vector |
| Time | $t$ | **s (second)** | Scalar |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s (radian per second)** | Vector |
| Radius of curvature | $\rho$ | **m (meter)** | Scalar |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Window View"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine you are sitting in a passenger train moving along a curved railway track that bends gently to the right. Hold a rigid ruler flat on your palm and look at the markings on the floor below. Every dust particle on the floor, the tip of your shoe, and the corner of the ruler all trace **the exact same curved path** as the train. None of them spin in place; they all just **slide along** parallel arcs. That is curvilinear translation — sliding along curved rails, with the body behaving as if it were "frozen" in orientation but gliding on a bend.

A simpler analogy: a sled sliding down a **curved toboggan run**. Every point on the sled (front, back, sides) follows a path that has the **same curvature** as the run itself. The sled never tips or spins; it just rides the curve.

### 1.3 Distinguishing Curvilinear Translation from Other Motions

> [!NOTE]
> - **Rectilinear Translation** → All particles move along **straight** parallel lines.
> - **Curvilinear Translation** → All particles move along **curved but parallel** identical paths. (No rotation.)
> - **General Plane Motion** → Particles move along **different (non-parallel)** curved paths because the body simultaneously rotates.

**Crucial Engineering Insight:** In curvilinear translation, *all points of the body have identical velocity and identical acceleration at any given instant*. This is the single most testable fact in KTU board exams on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Velocity and Acceleration in Curvilinear Translation

Since every particle of the body has the **same velocity and acceleration** at any given instant, the analysis reduces to the study of a **single representative point** (usually taken as the body's center of mass, or any convenient corner).

The position of a typical particle $P$ is described by the position vector:

$$\vec{r} = x\,\hat{i} + y\,\hat{j}$$

The velocity is the time derivative of position:

$$\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = \dot{x}\,\hat{i} + \dot{y}\,\hat{j}$$

The acceleration is the time derivative of velocity:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \ddot{x}\,\hat{i} + \ddot{y}\,\hat{j}$$

### 2.2 Normal and Tangential Components (Path Coordinates)

When a particle moves along a known curved path, the velocity is **always tangent** to the path, and the acceleration decomposes naturally into:

- **Tangential component** ($a_t$): along the tangent to the path → causes **change in speed**.
- **Normal component** ($a_n$): along the inward normal (toward the center of curvature) → causes **change in direction**.

$$\boxed{a_t = \frac{dv}{dt} = v\,\frac{dv}{ds}}$$

$$\boxed{a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho}}$$

where:
- $v$ = speed (magnitude of velocity)
- $s$ = arc length traversed along the path
- $\rho$ = local radius of curvature of the path (m)
- $a_t$ = tangential acceleration (m/s²)
- $a_n$ = normal/centripetal acceleration (m/s²)

The total acceleration magnitude:

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^{\,2} + a_n^{\,2}}$$

### 2.3 Radial and Transverse Components (Polar Coordinates)

When the path is naturally described by a polar radius $r$ and angle $\theta$, the velocity and acceleration resolve into:

**Velocity components:**

$$v_r = \dot{r}, \qquad v_\theta = r\,\dot{\theta}$$

**Acceleration components:**

$$a_r = \ddot{r} - r\,\dot{\theta}^2$$

$$a_\theta = r\,\ddot{\theta} + 2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}$$

The $2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}$ term is the famous **Coriolis acceleration**, which appears whenever $r$ and $\theta$ both change with time.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Curvilinear Translation / Motion**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | Instantaneous speed | Path-coordinate kinematics |
| 2 | $a_t = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = v\,\dfrac{dv}{ds}$ | Tangential acceleration | Change in speed along curve |
| 3 | $a_n = \dfrac{v^2}{\rho}$ | Normal (centripetal) acceleration | Change in direction along curve |
| 4 | $a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2}$ | Magnitude of total acceleration | Resultant acceleration on curve |
| 5 | $v_r = \dot{r}$ | Radial velocity | Polar coordinate kinematics |
| 6 | $v_\theta = r\,\dot{\theta}$ | Transverse velocity | Polar coordinate kinematics |
| 7 | $a_r = \ddot{r} - r\,\dot{\theta}^2$ | Radial acceleration | Polar coordinate kinematics |
| 8 | $a_\theta = r\,\ddot{\theta} + 2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}$ | Transverse acceleration | Polar coordinate kinematics |
| 9 | $R = \rho + h$ | Radius of curved road (banked/flat) | Vehicle on curved track |
| 10 | $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2\,a_t\,(s - s_0)$ | Kinematic relation along path | Constant tangential acceleration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to remember (KTU valuation tip):** Failure to write units is one of the top reasons for losing 1 mark per sub-part in KTU board papers. Always state **m, m/s, m/s², rad/s, rad/s²**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle dynamics on curved highways** → Engineers compute $a_n = v^2/\rho$ to design banking angles and friction factors so vehicles don't skid off the road.
- **Roller-coaster and toboggan track design** → Combines $a_t$ (gravity-driven speed-up) and $a_n$ (centripetal safety) to ensure rider comfort and structural safety.
- **Robotic arm path planning** → Industrial manipulators use $v_r, v_\theta$ in polar coordinates to plan smooth curved trajectories.
- **Projectile and ballistics analysis** → Both Cartesian ($\ddot{x}=0, \ddot{y}=-g$) and path ($a_t, a_n$) descriptions are used.
- **Cam and follower design in machines** → The follower tip traces a curvilinear translation profile; $a_n$ determines contact stresses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Normal (Centripetal) Acceleration $a_n = v^2/\rho$

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting premise:** A particle moves along a smooth curve of local radius of curvature $\rho$ with instantaneous speed $v$. We want to find the component of acceleration perpendicular to the velocity vector.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the small arc.** Consider the particle moving from point $A$ to point $B$ along a small arc of length $ds$ in time $dt$. The chord $AB$ subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $O$. From the arc-length formula:

$$ds = \rho\,d\theta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d\theta = \frac{ds}{\rho}$$

This means: **the change in direction of the velocity vector equals the change in path angle**.

**Step 2 — Velocity vector change in magnitude.** The speed may change from $v$ to $v + dv$ over the same interval. The change in the **magnitude** of velocity corresponds to tangential acceleration. The tangential acceleration is:

$$a_t = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

This is along the tangent (direction of $\vec{v}$).

**Step 3 — Velocity vector change in direction.** Now focus on the change in **direction** alone. The velocity vector has constant magnitude $v$ but rotates through angle $d\theta$. The magnitude of the *change* in velocity due to rotation alone is:

$$|\Delta \vec{v}_{\text{rot}}| = v\,d\theta$$

Substituting $d\theta = ds/\rho = (v\,dt)/\rho$:

$$|\Delta \vec{v}_{\text{rot}}| = v \cdot \frac{v\,dt}{\rho} = \frac{v^2}{\rho}\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Direction of the change vector.** The change vector $\Delta \vec{v}_{\text{rot}}$ is directed from the *tip* of the new velocity vector back to the *tip* of the old velocity vector. In the limit, this points **toward the center of curvature** (i.e., along the inward normal).

**Step 5 — Define the normal acceleration.** The component of acceleration in the inward normal direction is:

$$a_n = \frac{|\Delta \vec{v}_{\text{rot}}|}{dt} = \frac{v^2}{\rho}$$

This is the **centripetal** (or **normal**) acceleration. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the direction is "inward":** If you are moving along a circle, your velocity is tangent. A tiny instant later, the velocity is tangent at the *new* point. The new tangent has rotated inward by $d\theta$. The difference vector therefore points from the outer arc toward the center. This is why $a_n$ is *centripetal* (center-seeking).

### 3.2 Derivation of Radial and Transverse Acceleration in Polar Coordinates

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A particle $P$ has polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ relative to origin $O$. The unit vectors are $\hat{e}_r$ (radial, outward) and $\hat{e}_\theta$ (transverse, perpendicular to $\hat{e}_r$ in the direction of increasing $\theta$).

**Step 1 — Position vector in polar form:**

$$\vec{r} = r\,\hat{e}_r$$

**Step 2 — Time derivative of the unit vectors.** As $\theta$ changes, the unit vectors rotate:

$$\frac{d\hat{e}_r}{dt} = \dot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta$$

$$\frac{d\hat{e}_\theta}{dt} = -\dot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_r$$

**Step 3 — Velocity:**

$$\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(r\,\hat{e}_r) = \dot{r}\,\hat{e}_r + r\,\dot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta$$

So $v_r = \dot{r}$ and $v_\theta = r\,\dot{\theta}$.

**Step 4 — Acceleration:**

$$\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\dot{r}\,\hat{e}_r + r\,\dot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta\right)$$

Differentiate term by term:

$$\vec{a} = \ddot{r}\,\hat{e}_r + \dot{r}\,\frac{d\hat{e}_r}{dt} + \dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta + r\,\ddot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta + r\,\dot{\theta}\,\frac{d\hat{e}_\theta}{dt}$$

Substitute the unit-vector derivatives:

$$\vec{a} = \ddot{r}\,\hat{e}_r + \dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta + \dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta + r\,\ddot{\theta}\,\hat{e}_\theta - r\,\dot{\theta}^2\,\hat{e}_r$$

**Step 5 — Collect radial and transverse components:**

$$\boxed{\vec{a} = \left(\ddot{r} - r\,\dot{\theta}^2\right)\hat{e}_r + \left(r\,\ddot{\theta} + 2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}\right)\hat{e}_\theta}$$

Hence:
- $a_r = \ddot{r} - r\,\dot{\theta}^2$
- $a_\theta = r\,\ddot{\theta} + 2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}$ (the second term is the **Coriolis** term). $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A car travels along a curved road that follows a circular arc of radius $\rho = 200$ m. At an instant, the car's speed is $v = 20$ m/s, and it is increasing at the rate $a_t = 2$ m/s². Compute (i) the normal acceleration, (ii) the total acceleration magnitude, and (iii) the direction of the total acceleration with respect to the tangent.

**Step 1 — Compute normal acceleration:**

$$a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho} = \frac{(20)^2}{200} = \frac{400}{200} = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute total acceleration magnitude:**

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^{\,2} + a_n^{\,2}} = \sqrt{(2)^2 + (2)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8}$$

$$a = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Direction (angle from tangent toward inward normal):**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{a_n}{a_t} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = 45^\circ$$

So the total acceleration vector points **45° inward from the tangent direction**, with magnitude $2\sqrt{2}$ m/s². $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Curvilinear Translation Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a Mermaid flow-chart showing the **decision workflow** an engineer (or KTU student) follows when analyzing a curvilinear translation problem. This is preferred over attempting a hand-drawn free body figure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Problem Statement]) --> B{Is path shape given explicitly?}
    B -- Yes, circular arc --> C[Use path coords: a_t and a_n]
    B -- Yes, general curve --> D[Use path coords with local radius rho]
    B -- No, polar data given --> E[Use polar coords: a_r and a_theta]
    C --> F[Compute v from v = ds/dt]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Compute r dot and theta dot]
    G --> H[Apply a_r = r_ddot - r theta_dot^2]
    H --> I[Apply a_theta = r theta_ddot + 2 r_dot theta_dot]
    F --> J[Apply a_n = v^2 / rho]
    F --> K[Apply a_t = dv/dt]
    J --> L[Resultant: a = sqrt a_t^2 + a_n^2]
    K --> L
    I --> M[Resultant: a = sqrt a_r^2 + a_theta^2]
    L --> N([End: Acceleration Vector])
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Velocity and Acceleration Components on a Curved Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CURVE["CURVED PATH - CIRCULAR ARC"]
        O[("Center O")] --- R["Radius rho = 200 m"]
        P1[/"Particle at Point A"/] --- P2[/"Particle at Point B (later)"/]
        R --- P1
    end
    V1["Velocity v at A
    Tangent to path"] -.-> P1
    V2["Velocity v+dv at B
    Tangent to path"] -.-> P2
    AN["Normal a_n = v^2/rho
    Points INWARD to O"] -.-> P1
    AT["Tangential a_t = dv/dt
    Points ALONG tangent"] -.-> P1
    A["Total a
    At angle alpha from tangent"] -.-> P1
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Three Motions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph RT["RECTILINEAR TRANSLATION"]
        RT1["Path: Straight line"] --> RT2["v: along line"]
        RT2 --> RT3["a: along line (a_n = 0)"]
    end
    subgraph CT["CURVILINEAR TRANSLATION"]
        CT1["Path: Curved, parallel for all points"] --> CT2["v: tangent to curve"]
        CT2 --> CT3["a has BOTH a_t and a_n"]
    end
    subgraph GP["GENERAL PLANE MOTION"]
        GP1["Path: Different curves per particle"] --> GP2["v: differs per particle"]
        GP2 --> GP3["Rotation + Translation combined"]
    end
    RT --> CT
    CT --> GP
```

### 4.4 Polar Coordinate Component Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[("Origin O")] -->|r| P["Particle P"]
    O -->|theta| P
    ER["e_r: radial unit vector
    outward from O"] --- P
    ET["e_theta: transverse unit vector
    perpendicular, +theta direction"] --- P
    VR["v_r = r_dot along e_r"] -.-> P
    VT["v_theta = r theta_dot along e_theta"] -.-> P
    AR["a_r = r_ddot - r theta_dot^2
    along e_r"] -.-> P
    AT["a_theta = r theta_ddot + 2 r_dot theta_dot
    Coriolis term along e_theta"] -.-> P
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In the KTU 2024 answer script, always draw the **curved path as a smooth arc**, mark the **center of curvature O**, label the **radius $\rho$**, and show the velocity $\vec{v}$ as a tangent arrow. Then draw $a_t$ along the tangent and $a_n$ toward $O$. This single diagram can fetch 2–3 marks of full credit in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **curvilinear translation** of a rigid body. State **two** distinguishing features that separate it from general plane motion.

**Model Answer:**
Curvilinear translation is the motion of a rigid body in which every particle of the body moves along a curved path, and all these paths are **identical in shape and parallel** to one another at every instant.

Two distinguishing features:
1. The body does **not rotate** — any straight line on the body remains parallel to its original orientation throughout the motion.
2. **All points of the body have the same velocity and the same acceleration** at any given instant (since the parallel paths are congruent and instantaneously coincident in their kinematic descriptors).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
For a particle moving along a curved path, derive (any method) the expression for the **normal component of acceleration**. State its physical significance.

**Model Answer:**
Consider a particle moving on a smooth curve of local radius of curvature $\rho$ with speed $v$. In a small time $dt$, the velocity vector rotates through a small angle $d\theta$ where $d\theta = ds/\rho = (v\,dt)/\rho$. The change in velocity due to this rotation has magnitude $v\,d\theta$. Dividing by $dt$:

$$a_n = \frac{v\,d\theta}{dt} = \frac{v^2}{\rho} \quad \text{(m/s}^2\text{)}$$

Physical significance: $a_n$ is the **centripetal** (center-seeking) acceleration. It represents the rate of change of the *direction* of the velocity vector. It is responsible for keeping the particle on the curved path; remove it and the particle would fly off along the tangent.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** A cyclist travels along a horizontal curved road that is a circular arc of radius $\rho = 75$ m. At an instant, the cyclist's speed is $v = 15$ m/s and the speed is increasing uniformly at $a_t = 0.5$ m/s². Determine:
   (i) The normal component of acceleration.
   (ii) The magnitude of the total acceleration.
   (iii) The angle the total acceleration makes with the tangent to the path. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A particle moves in a plane such that its polar coordinates vary with time as $r(t) = 2t^2$ m and $\theta(t) = t^2/2$ rad, where $t$ is in seconds. Find the radial and transverse components of velocity and acceleration of the particle at $t = 2$ s. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Normal acceleration:**

$$a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho} = \frac{(15)^2}{75} = \frac{225}{75} = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Tangential acceleration (given):** $a_t = 0.5$ m/s²

**Step 3 — Total acceleration magnitude:**

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (3)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 9} = \sqrt{9.25}$$

$$a \approx 3.041 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Angle from tangent:**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{a_n}{a_t} = \frac{3}{0.5} = 6 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = \tan^{-1}(6) \approx 80.54^\circ$$

The total acceleration vector points **inward (toward the center of curvature) at about 80.5° from the tangent**. The normal component dominates, as expected at high speed on a tight curve.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Given:**

$$r(t) = 2t^2, \qquad \theta(t) = \frac{t^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute first derivatives:**

$$\dot{r} = 4t, \qquad \dot{\theta} = t$$

**Step 3 — Compute second derivatives:**

$$\ddot{r} = 4, \qquad \ddot{\theta} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = 2$ s:**

$$r = 2(2)^2 = 8 \text{ m}$$
$$\dot{r} = 4(2) = 8 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\ddot{r} = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$\dot{\theta} = 2 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\ddot{\theta} = 1 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Velocity components:**

$$v_r = \dot{r} = 8 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_\theta = r\,\dot{\theta} = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Acceleration components:**

$$a_r = \ddot{r} - r\,\dot{\theta}^2 = 4 - 8 \times (2)^2 = 4 - 32 = -28 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_\theta = r\,\ddot{\theta} + 2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta} = 8 \times 1 + 2 \times 8 \times 2 = 8 + 32 = 40 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final answers:** $v_r = 8$ m/s, $v_\theta = 16$ m/s, $a_r = -28$ m/s² (radially inward, hence negative), $a_\theta = 40$ m/s² (transverse, in direction of increasing $\theta$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A car moves along a curved highway. The car enters a circular curve of radius $\rho = 120$ m at speed $v_0 = 25$ m/s and accelerates uniformly such that the tangential acceleration is $a_t = 3$ m/s². Find:
   (i) The speed of the car after it has traveled an arc length $s = 200$ m along the curve.
   (ii) The normal acceleration at that new position.
   (iii) The total acceleration magnitude at that position. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A particle moves along a curve whose radius of curvature at a certain instant is $\rho = 50$ m. If the particle's speed at that instant is $v = 10$ m/s and the tangential acceleration is $a_t = 4$ m/s², find the **rate of change of the speed** and the **rate of change of direction** of the velocity vector. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Find final speed using kinematic relation along path:**

$$v^2 = v_0^2 + 2\,a_t\,(s - s_0)$$
$$v^2 = (25)^2 + 2 \times 3 \times 200 = 625 + 1200 = 1825$$
$$v = \sqrt{1825} \approx 42.72 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Normal acceleration at new position:**

$$a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho} = \frac{1825}{120} \approx 15.21 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total acceleration magnitude:**

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} = \sqrt{(3)^2 + (15.21)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 231.34} = \sqrt{240.34} \approx 15.50 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Rate of change of speed:** This is the tangential acceleration by definition.

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = a_t = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Rate of change of direction of velocity vector:** This is the magnitude of the normal (centripetal) acceleration.

$$a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho} = \frac{(10)^2}{50} = \frac{100}{50} = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final answer:** Rate of change of speed = **4 m/s²**, rate of change of direction = **2 m/s²** (directed toward the center of curvature).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Curvilinear Translation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** → A correct numerical answer without "m/s²" loses 0.5 to 1 mark per sub-part.
> 2. **Confusing $a_t$ with $a_n$** → Many students write $a_t = v^2/\rho$, which is the **normal** component, not tangential. Memorize: $a_t$ changes **speed**, $a_n$ changes **direction**.
> 3. **Sign error in $a_r$** → In polar coordinates, the term $-r\,\dot{\theta}^2$ is *always negative* (it represents centripetal pull). Forgetting the minus sign is the single most common error.
> 4. **Missing the Coriolis term** → In $a_\theta$, the term $2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}$ is easy to drop. It is non-zero whenever **both** $r$ and $\theta$ vary with time.
> 5. **Direction of $a_n$** → It points **toward the center of curvature** (inward normal), not outward. Outward is centrifugal (a fictitious force in rotating frames, NOT a real acceleration in inertial frames).
> 6. **Curvilinear translation vs. rotation** → A common KTU trick question: "In curvilinear translation, do all points have the same angular velocity?" **Answer: NO, angular velocity is undefined (or zero) for translation** — the body does not rotate.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Curvilinear Translation**

- ✅ **Definition:** All particles trace **parallel curved** paths of identical shape; body has **no rotation**.
- ✅ **Uniformity of motion:** All points have the **same $\vec{v}$** and the **same $\vec{a}$** at any instant.
- ✅ **Path coordinates** ($a_t, a_n$):
   - $a_t = dv/dt = v\,dv/ds$ → along tangent → changes **speed**.
   - $a_n = v^2/\rho$ → toward center of curvature → changes **direction**.
   - $a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2}$.
- ✅ **Polar coordinates** ($a_r, a_\theta$):
   - $v_r = \dot{r}, \quad v_\theta = r\,\dot{\theta}$.
   - $a_r = \ddot{r} - r\,\dot{\theta}^2$ (the second term is centripetal).
   - $a_\theta = r\,\ddot{\theta} + 2\,\dot{r}\,\dot{\theta}$ (second term is Coriolis).
- ✅ **Always remember to draw** the curved path, the center of curvature $O$, the radius $\rho$, and arrows for $\vec{v}, a_t, a_n$ — KTU examiners reward clear diagrams.
- ✅ **Units are mandatory:** m, m/s, m/s², rad/s, rad/s², m for $\rho$.
- ✅ **For uniform speed along a curve:** $a_t = 0$, only $a_n$ exists; $a = v^2/\rho$ is the entire acceleration.
- ✅ **For straight-line motion** (a special degenerate case): $\rho \to \infty$, so $a_n = 0$; only $a_t$ remains.
- ✅ **Coriolis term** appears **only** when the body has *both* radial and angular motion simultaneously (e.g., a sliding contact on a rotating arm).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme tip:** Module 4 questions on this topic usually combine curvilinear translation with Newton's second law (F = ma) — be ready to compute **net force components** from $a_t$ and $a_n$ in subsequent modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of Kinematics and Projectile Motion (Solution Starting from Differential Equations)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equations of Kinematics and Projectile Motion (Solution Starting from Differential Equations)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Engineering Mechanics, the **Equations of Kinematics** describe the motion of a particle in terms of its displacement, velocity, and acceleration **without reference to the forces causing the motion**. When the motion is rectilinear (one-dimensional), these equations are derived as the **integral solutions of the second-order differential equation of motion**:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = a(t)$$

where $\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$ represents the instantaneous acceleration of the particle along a straight-line path, and $a(t)$ is a known function of time. The integration of this differential equation, subject to appropriate **initial conditions** (initial displacement $x_0$ and initial velocity $u$), yields the three fundamental kinematic equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Kinematics):**
> The board examiner specifically expects students to **start from the differential equation of motion** and integrate step-by-step to obtain the kinematic equations. Memorizing the final formulas without showing the integration steps will cost **3 to 4 marks** in Part B (14-mark) questions.

**Projectile Motion**, in turn, is the **two-dimensional curvilinear motion** of a particle that is launched near the Earth's surface and subsequently moves under the influence of **gravity alone** (neglecting air resistance). The motion is decomposed into two mutually perpendicular components — horizontal and vertical — each of which is governed by its own differential equation derived from Newton's second law.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car and you press the accelerator pedal. The car's velocity is no longer constant — it changes every second. Now, suppose you want to know exactly **how fast** you will be going after 10 seconds, and **how far** you would have travelled. This is a kinematics problem.

Think of it like this:
- **Velocity** is the *rate of change* of your position.
- **Acceleration** is the *rate of change* of your velocity.
- If you know how acceleration changes with time, you can **integrate** (add up tiny pieces) to find velocity, and integrate again to find position.

> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine water flowing into a tank at a varying rate. If you know the *rate of water inflow* (analogous to acceleration), you can find the *amount of water* in the tank (velocity) by summing up all the inflow, and you can find the *water level* (position) by summing up the volume over time. This is exactly what integration does!

For **projectile motion**, picture a cricket ball hit by a batsman. The ball follows a curved (parabolic) path. But here's the trick: instead of analysing the complex curved path, we **split** it into:
1. A **horizontal motion** where nothing pushes or pulls the ball sideways (so it just moves at constant speed).
2. A **vertical motion** where gravity continuously pulls the ball downward (like a freely falling body, but launched upward).

The final curved path is the **combination (superposition)** of these two simple motions.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ (or $9.8 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ for KTU board exam calculations, sometimes $10 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ for quick estimates).
> - **Direction convention:** Upward is taken as **positive**, downward as **negative** for vertical motion. This sign convention must be **strictly followed** in KTU board papers.
> - **Neglected factors in KTU problems:** Air resistance and wind effects are assumed to be **zero** unless explicitly stated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Concept

#### **Part A: Rectilinear Kinematics from Differential Equations**

**Step 1: Set up the governing differential equation.**
