For a particle moving along a straight line (say, the x-axis), if acceleration $a$ is a known function of time $t$:

$$a = \frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = f(t)$$

**Step 2: First integration — Obtain velocity.**
Velocity $v$ is the first derivative of displacement. So, we integrate acceleration with respect to time:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \int a \, dt + C_{1}$$

Apply the **initial condition**: at $t = 0$, $v = u$ (initial velocity). This gives $C_{1} = u$, hence:

$$v = u + \int_{0}^{t} a \, dt$$

**Step 3: Second integration — Obtain displacement.**
Displacement $x$ is the integral of velocity with respect to time:

$$x = \int v \, dt + C_{2} = \int \left( u + \int a \, dt \right) dt + C_{2}$$

Apply the **initial condition**: at $t = 0$, $x = x_0$ (initial position). This determines $C_{2}$.

#### **Special Case: Constant Acceleration**

If $a = \text{constant} = a$, then:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = a \implies v = u + at \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = v = u + at \implies x = x_{0} + ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

From (1): $t = \frac{v-u}{a}$. Substituting into (2):

$$v^{2} = u^{2} + 2a(x - x_{0}) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

These three equations (1), (2), (3) are the **kinematic equations of motion under constant acceleration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** Often, the third equation $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$ is given as a "given" formula. But the KTU 2024 scheme explicitly asks students to **derive it from the differential equation**. The examiner wants to see: *"Integrate $\frac{dv}{dt} = a$ to get $v \, dv = a \, dx$, then integrate."*

#### **Part B: Curvilinear Motion — Projectile Motion Setup**

The position of a projectile at any time $t$ is described by two coordinates $(x, y)$. The governing differential equations are:

$$\text{Horizontal: } \frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{(no horizontal force)}$$

$$\text{Vertical: } \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} = -g \quad \text{(gravity acts downward)}$$

**First integration:**

$$v_{x} = \frac{dx}{dt} = u \cos\theta = \text{constant}$$

$$v_{y} = \frac{dy}{dt} = u \sin\theta - gt$$

**Second integration:**

$$x = (u \cos\theta) \, t$$

$$y = (u \sin\theta) \, t - \frac{1}{2}gt^{2}$$

These are the **parametric equations of projectile motion**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Acceleration definition | $a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$ | $a$ in m/s² | Fundamental definition |
| 2 | Velocity–Time relation | $v = u + at$ | $u, v$ in m/s; $t$ in s | Find final velocity |
| 3 | Displacement–Time relation | $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$ | $s$ in m | Find displacement |
| 4 | Velocity–Displacement relation | $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$ | All in SI units | Eliminating time |
| 5 | Projectile – Horizontal velocity | $v_{x} = u\cos\theta$ | $\theta$ in radians | Horizontal component |
| 6 | Projectile – Vertical velocity | $v_{y} = u\sin\theta - gt$ | $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Vertical component |
| 7 | Projectile – x-coordinate | $x = (u\cos\theta)t$ | $x$ in m | Horizontal position |
| 8 | Projectile – y-coordinate | $y = (u\sin\theta)t - \frac{1}{2}gt^{2}$ | $y$ in m | Vertical position |
| 9 | Time of flight | $T = \frac{2u\sin\theta}{g}$ | $T$ in s | Total time in air |
| 10 | Maximum height | $H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}$ | $H$ in m | Peak altitude |
| 11 | Horizontal range | $R = \frac{u^{2}\sin 2\theta}{g}$ | $R$ in m | Total horizontal distance |
| 12 | Trajectory equation | $y = x\tan\theta - \frac{gx^{2}}{2u^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}$ | $y$ as function of $x$ | Path of projectile |
| 13 | Resultant velocity | $v = \sqrt{v_{x}^{2} + v_{y}^{2}}$ | $v$ in m/s | Speed at any instant |
| 14 | Angle of velocity vector | $\tan\alpha = \frac{v_{y}}{v_{x}}$ | $\alpha$ in radians | Direction of motion |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **Ballistics and Defence:** Calculating artillery shell trajectories.
> - **Sports Engineering:** Designing golf ball flight paths, javelin throws.
> - **Aerospace:** Initial orbital insertion velocity calculations.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Trajectory of water jets from fountains, trajectory of debris in demolitions.
> - **Fire-fighting:** Determining the reach of water cannons.
> - **Agriculture:** Design of seed-spraying drones and irrigation sprinklers.

The differential-equation-based approach is fundamental because real-world accelerations are often **not constant** (e.g., rocket thrust varying with fuel burn, drag forces). Starting from $\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = a(t)$ allows engineers to handle such variable acceleration cases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: First Kinematic Equation ($v = u + at$) from Differential Equation

**Given:** A particle starts with initial velocity $u$ at time $t = 0$ and moves with constant acceleration $a$ along a straight line.

**Step 1:** Write the definition of acceleration as a differential equation.

$$\text{Acceleration} = \frac{\text{change in velocity}}{\text{change in time}} \implies a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Separate the variables to prepare for integration.

$$dv = a \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides. Since acceleration $a$ is constant, it can be taken out of the integral sign.

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv = \int_{0}^{t} a \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate both integrals.

$$\left[ v \right]_{u}^{v} = a \left[ t \right]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the limits.

$$v - u = a(t - 0)$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the first kinematic equation.

$$\boxed{v = u + at}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Second Kinematic Equation ($s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$)

**Step 1:** Velocity is the time derivative of displacement.

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expression for $v$ from the first equation (this is the key step where we use $v = u + at$).

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = u + at$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables.

$$ds = (u + at) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides, with $s = 0$ at $t = 0$ (assuming initial position is the origin).

$$\int_{0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} (u + at) \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the right-hand side integral. Use the power rule $\int t^{n} dt = \frac{t^{n+1}}{n+1}$.

$$\left[ s \right]_{0}^{s} = \left[ ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2} \right]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the limits.

$$s - 0 = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2} - 0$$

$$\boxed{s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Third Kinematic Equation ($v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$)

This derivation is the **most important** for KTU exams because it uses a clever trick — **eliminating $t$** by using the chain rule.

**Step 1:** Use the chain rule to express acceleration in terms of $v$ and $s$.

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{ds} \cdot \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{dv}{ds} \cdot v = v \, \frac{dv}{ds}$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables.

$$v \, dv = a \, ds$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides, with $v = u$ at $s = 0$.

$$\int_{u}^{v} v \, dv = \int_{0}^{s} a \, ds$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals.

$$\left[ \frac{v^{2}}{2} \right]_{u}^{v} = a \left[ s \right]_{0}^{s}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the limits.

$$\frac{v^{2}}{2} - \frac{u^{2}}{2} = a(s - 0)$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by 2 to obtain the final form.

$$\boxed{v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Projectile Motion Equations from Differential Equations

**Setup:** A projectile is launched with initial velocity $u$ at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal from the origin. The only force is gravity acting downward.

**Step 1:** Resolve the initial velocity into components.

$$u_{x} = u\cos\theta, \quad u_{y} = u\sin\theta$$

**Step 2:** Write the differential equations of motion. Gravity acts only in the y-direction, so:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{(no force in x-direction)}$$

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} = -g \quad \text{(gravity acts downward, hence negative)}$$

**Step 3: First integration — to get velocity components.**

For x-motion:

$$\frac{dv_{x}}{dt} = 0 \implies v_{x} = C_{1}$$

Apply initial condition: at $t = 0$, $v_{x} = u\cos\theta$, so $C_{1} = u\cos\theta$.

$$\boxed{v_{x} = \frac{dx}{dt} = u\cos\theta \quad \text{...(i)}}$$

For y-motion:

$$\frac{dv_{y}}{dt} = -g \implies dv_{y} = -g \, dt$$

Integrate:

$$\int_{u\sin\theta}^{v_{y}} dv_{y} = -g \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$v_{y} - u\sin\theta = -gt$$

$$\boxed{v_{y} = \frac{dy}{dt} = u\sin\theta - gt \quad \text{...(ii)}}$$

**Step 4: Second integration — to get displacement components.**

For x-displacement:

$$x = \int v_{x} \, dt = \int u\cos\theta \, dt = u\cos\theta \cdot t + C_{2}$$

Apply initial condition: at $t = 0$, $x = 0$, so $C_{2} = 0$.

$$\boxed{x = u\cos\theta \cdot t \quad \text{...(iii)}}$$

For y-displacement:

$$y = \int v_{y} \, dt = \int (u\sin\theta - gt) \, dt$$

$$y = u\sin\theta \cdot t - \frac{1}{2}gt^{2} + C_{3}$$

Apply initial condition: at $t = 0$, $y = 0$, so $C_{3} = 0$.

$$\boxed{y = u\sin\theta \cdot t - \frac{1}{2}gt^{2} \quad \text{...(iv)}}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Trajectory Equation, Time of Flight, Maximum Height, and Range

**Step 1: Eliminate $t$ to get the trajectory equation.**

From (iii): $t = \frac{x}{u\cos\theta}$. Substitute into (iv):

$$y = u\sin\theta \cdot \frac{x}{u\cos\theta} - \frac{1}{2}g\left( \frac{x}{u\cos\theta} \right)^{2}$$

$$y = x\tan\theta - \frac{gx^{2}}{2u^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}$$

$$\boxed{y = x\tan\theta - \frac{g \, x^{2}}{2u^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}}$$

This is the equation of a **parabola**, confirming projectile motion traces a parabolic path.

**Step 2: Time of flight ($T$).**

At the moment the projectile returns to the ground, $y = 0$. From equation (iv):

$$0 = u\sin\theta \cdot T - \frac{1}{2}gT^{2}$$

$$0 = T\left( u\sin\theta - \frac{1}{2}gT \right)$$

This gives two solutions: $T = 0$ (launch moment) and:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2u\sin\theta}{g}}$$

**Step 3: Maximum height ($H$).**

At maximum height, the vertical component of velocity is zero: $v_{y} = 0$.

From (ii): $0 = u\sin\theta - g t_{\text{up}}$, so the time to reach the peak is $t_{\text{up}} = \frac{u\sin\theta}{g}$.

Substitute into (iv):

$$H = u\sin\theta \cdot \frac{u\sin\theta}{g} - \frac{1}{2}g\left( \frac{u\sin\theta}{g} \right)^{2}$$

$$H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{g} - \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g} = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}}$$

**Step 4: Horizontal range ($R$).**

The range is the horizontal distance travelled during the total time of flight $T$:

$$R = v_{x} \cdot T = u\cos\theta \cdot \frac{2u\sin\theta}{g} = \frac{2u^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta}{g}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{u^{2}\sin 2\theta}{g}}$$

**Step 5: Condition for maximum range.**

Range is maximum when $\sin 2\theta = 1$, i.e., $2\theta = 90°$ or $\theta = 45°$.

$$R_{\max} = \frac{u^{2}}{g}$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of 30 m/s from the top of a 45 m tall building. Using the differential equation of motion, determine (a) the maximum height reached above the ground, and (b) the time taken to hit the ground. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Set up the differential equation.**

Taking upward as positive, the acceleration is $a = -g = -9.81$ m/s².

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = -9.81$$

**Step 2: Integrate to find velocity as a function of time.**

$$v = -9.81t + C_{1}$$

At $t = 0$, $v = u = 30$ m/s, so $C_{1} = 30$.

$$v(t) = 30 - 9.81t$$

**Step 3: Integrate to find displacement as a function of time.**

$$s(t) = 30t - \frac{1}{2}(9.81)t^{2} = 30t - 4.905t^{2}$$

Taking the launch point as origin ($s = 0$ at $t = 0$), the position above the launch point at time $t$ is $s(t)$.

**Step 4 (a): Find maximum height above the launch point.**

At maximum height, $v = 0$:

$$0 = 30 - 9.81t \implies t = \frac{30}{9.81} = 3.058 \text{ s}$$

Substitute back into $s(t)$:

$$s_{\text{max above launch}} = 30(3.058) - 4.905(3.058)^{2}$$

$$s_{\text{max}} = 91.74 - 45.87 = 45.87 \text{ m}$$

Maximum height above the ground = $45 + 45.87 = 90.87$ m.

**Step 5 (b): Find the time to hit the ground.**

The ground is 45 m below the launch point, so $s(t) = -45$ m:

$$-45 = 30t - 4.905t^{2}$$

$$4.905t^{2} - 30t - 45 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$t = \frac{30 \pm \sqrt{(30)^{2} + 4(4.905)(45)}}{2(4.905)}$$

$$t = \frac{30 \pm \sqrt{900 + 882.9}}{9.81} = \frac{30 \pm \sqrt{1782.9}}{9.81}$$

$$t = \frac{30 \pm 42.22}{9.81}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$t = \frac{30 + 42.22}{9.81} = \frac{72.22}{9.81} = 7.36 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (a) Maximum height above ground = **90.87 m**
- (b) Time to hit ground = **7.36 s**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: From Differential Equations to Kinematic Equations

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the logical progression from the fundamental differential equation of motion to the final kinematic equations used in problem-solving.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Newton Second Law Input a equals dv by dt equals d2x by dt2"] --> B["First Integration: dv equals a dt"]
    B --> C["Integrate Limits: v from u to v equals a times t from 0 to t"]
    C --> D["First Kinematic Equation: v equals u plus at"]
    D --> E["Second Integration: ds equals v dt equals u plus at times dt"]
    E --> F["Integrate Limits: s from 0 to s equals ut plus half at squared"]
    F --> G["Second Kinematic Equation: s equals ut plus half at squared"]
    D --> H["Alternative: v dv equals a ds using chain rule"]
    H --> I["Integrate Limits: half v squared from u to v equals a times s"]
    I --> J["Third Kinematic Equation: v squared equals u squared plus 2as"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Projectile Motion Decomposition and Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Projectile Launched with velocity u at angle theta"] --> P2["Resolve into two components: ux equals u cos theta and uy equals u sin theta"]
    P2 --> P3["Horizontal: d2x by dt2 equals 0 no force"]
    P2 --> P4["Vertical: d2y by dt2 equals minus g gravity only"]
    P3 --> P5["Integrate: vx equals u cos theta constant"]
    P4 --> P6["Integrate: vy equals u sin theta minus gt"]
    P5 --> P7["Integrate: x equals u cos theta times t"]
    P6 --> P8["Integrate: y equals u sin theta times t minus half g t squared"]
    P7 --> P9["Eliminate t: Trajectory y equals x tan theta minus g x squared by 2 u squared cos squared theta"]
    P8 --> P10["Set y equals 0: Time of Flight T equals 2u sin theta by g"]
    P8 --> P11["Set vy equals 0: Max Height H equals u squared sin squared theta by 2g"]
    P10 --> P12["Range R equals vx times T equals u squared sin 2 theta by g"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Physical Interpretation of Projectile Motion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Initial Velocity Vector u"] --> B["Horizontal Component ux equals u cos theta: CONSTANT throughout flight"]
    A --> C["Vertical Component uy equals u sin theta: DECREASES due to gravity"]
    B --> D["Uniform Motion: Equal horizontal distance in equal time intervals"]
    C --> E["Decelerated Ascent until vy equals 0 at peak"]
    E --> F["Accelerated Descent due to gravity"]
    D --> G["Combine: Parabolic Trajectory"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Lands at distance R from origin"]
```

### 4.4 Trajectory Visualization (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Origin 0,0, Launch angle theta, Initial speed u"] --> S2["At time t: Position is x, y on parabola"]
    S2 --> S3["At time T by 2: Peak, vy equals 0, Max Height H"]
    S3 --> S4["At time T: Lands at x equals R, y equals 0"]
    S2 -.Velocity Vector.-> S5["vx equals u cos theta: Horizontal arrow, always same length"]
    S2 -.Velocity Vector.-> S6["vy equals u sin theta minus gt: Vertical arrow, changes with time"]
    S5 --> S7["Resultant velocity at angle alpha: tan alpha equals vy by vx"]
    S6 --> S7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The arrow diagram in the trajectory block shows that at any point on the parabolic path, the **horizontal component** of velocity remains unchanged (same arrow length), while the **vertical component** shrinks as the projectile rises, becomes zero at the peak, and then grows in the opposite direction as it descends. This is why the **speed is minimum at the peak** but the **horizontal velocity is unchanged** throughout.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the following terms in kinematics: (i) Displacement, (ii) Velocity, (iii) Acceleration. State the SI units of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **(i) Displacement:** Displacement of a particle is the change in its position vector. For motion along a straight line, it is the shortest distance between the initial and final positions, along with direction. It is a **vector quantity**.
**SI Unit:** metre (m)

- **(ii) Velocity:** Velocity is the time rate of change of displacement. Mathematically, $v = \frac{ds}{dt}$.
**SI Unit:** metre per second (m/s)

- **(iii) Acceleration:** Acceleration is the time rate of change of velocity. Mathematically, $a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^{2}s}{dt^{2}}$.
**SI Unit:** metre per second squared (m/s²)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*State the three equations of motion for a particle moving with uniform acceleration along a straight line. Mention the meaning of each symbol used.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three equations of motion for a particle moving with uniform acceleration $a$ along a straight line are:

1. $v = u + at$ — relates final velocity to initial velocity, acceleration, and time.
2. $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$ — relates displacement to initial velocity, time, and acceleration.
3. $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$ — relates final velocity to initial velocity, acceleration, and displacement.

Where:
- $u$ = initial velocity of the particle (m/s)
- $v$ = final velocity of the particle (m/s)
- $a$ = uniform acceleration (m/s²)
- $t$ = time elapsed (s)
- $s$ = displacement of the particle in time $t$ (m)

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the three equations of motion for a particle moving with constant acceleration, starting from the differential equation $\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = a$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The fundamental differential equation for motion with constant acceleration is:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = a \quad \text{(where } a = \text{constant)}$$

This can be written as:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = a \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2: Derivation of the first equation $v = u + at$.**

Separating variables: $dv = a \, dt$

Integrating between limits $v = u$ at $t = 0$ and $v = v$ at $t = t$:

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv = a\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$[v]_{u}^{v} = a[t]_{0}^{t}$$

$$v - u = at$$

$$\therefore v = u + at \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3: Derivation of the second equation $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$.**

Since $v = \frac{ds}{dt}$, from equation (i):

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = u + at$$

Separating variables: $ds = (u + at) \, dt$

Integrating with $s = 0$ at $t = 0$:

$$\int_{0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} (u + at) \, dt$$

$$[s]_{0}^{s} = \left[ ut + \frac{at^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{t}$$

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4: Derivation of the third equation $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$.**

Using the chain rule: $a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{ds} \cdot \frac{ds}{dt} = v \cdot \frac{dv}{ds}$

$$\therefore v \, dv = a \, ds$$

Integrating with $v = u$ at $s = 0$:

$$\int_{u}^{v} v \, dv = a\int_{0}^{s} ds$$

$$\left[ \frac{v^{2}}{2} \right]_{u}^{v} = a[s]_{0}^{s}$$

$$\frac{v^{2}}{2} - \frac{u^{2}}{2} = as$$

$$\therefore v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

These are the three kinematic equations of motion under constant acceleration.

---

**(b) A stone is dropped from the top of a tower 80 m high. At the same time, another stone is thrown upward from the base of the tower with a velocity of 30 m/s. Find when and where the two stones will meet. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Set up coordinate system.**
Take the base of the tower as origin. Upward is positive. The top of the tower is at $y = 80$ m.

**Step 2: Equation of motion for Stone 1 (dropped from top).**
Initial position: $y_1(0) = 80$ m. Initial velocity: $u_1 = 0$ (dropped). Acceleration: $a = -9.81$ m/s².

Using $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$ with $s$ measured from the top of the tower (downward positive), the displacement of Stone 1 at time $t$ below the top is:

$$s_{1} = \frac{1}{2}(9.81)t^{2}$$

Position of Stone 1 (with base as origin, upward positive):

$$y_{1} = 80 - s_{1} = 80 - \frac{1}{2}(9.81)t^{2} = 80 - 4.905t^{2}$$

**Step 3: Equation of motion for Stone 2 (thrown upward from base).**
Initial position: $y_2(0) = 0$. Initial velocity: $u_2 = +30$ m/s. Acceleration: $a = -9.81$ m/s².

$$y_{2} = u_{2}t + \frac{1}{2}at^{2} = 30t - 4.905t^{2}$$

**Step 4: Apply the meeting condition $y_1 = y_2$.**

$$80 - 4.905t^{2} = 30t - 4.905t^{2}$$

$$80 = 30t$$

$$\boxed{t = \frac{80}{30} = 2.667 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 5: Find the meeting position.**

$$y_{2} = 30(2.667) - 4.905(2.667)^{2} = 80.0 - 34.87 = 45.13 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** The two stones meet at **$t = 2.667$ s** after the start, at a height of **45.13 m** above the base of the tower.

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) Starting from the differential equations of projectile motion, derive the expressions for: (i) Time of flight, (ii) Maximum height, and (iii) Horizontal range. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Set up the differential equations.**

For a projectile launched with initial velocity $u$ at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, the components of initial velocity are $u_x = u\cos\theta$ and $u_y = u\sin\theta$.

The governing differential equations (gravity acts only in the y-direction) are:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} = -g \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 2: First integration to get velocity components.**

Integrating (I) with initial condition $v_x(0) = u\cos\theta$:

$$v_{x} = \frac{dx}{dt} = u\cos\theta \quad \text{...(III)}$$

Integrating (II) with initial condition $v_y(0) = u\sin\theta$:

$$v_{y} = \frac{dy}{dt} = u\sin\theta - gt \quad \text{...(IV)}$$

**Step 3: Second integration to get position coordinates.**

Integrating (III) with $x(0) = 0$:

$$x = u\cos\theta \cdot t \quad \text{...(V)}$$

Integrating (IV) with $y(0) = 0$:

$$y = u\sin\theta \cdot t - \frac{1}{2}gt^{2} \quad \text{...(VI)}$$

**Step 4: Derive Time of Flight.**

At the moment the projectile returns to the ground (same horizontal level as launch), $y = 0$:

$$0 = u\sin\theta \cdot T - \frac{1}{2}gT^{2}$$

$$0 = T\left( u\sin\theta - \frac{1}{2}gT \right)$$

Discarding $T = 0$ (launch moment):

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2u\sin\theta}{g}}$$

**Step 5: Derive Maximum Height.**

At maximum height, the vertical velocity $v_y = 0$. From (IV):

$$0 = u\sin\theta - g t_{\text{peak}} \implies t_{\text{peak}} = \frac{u\sin\theta}{g}$$

Substituting $t_{\text{peak}}$ into (VI):

$$H = u\sin\theta \cdot \frac{u\sin\theta}{g} - \frac{1}{2}g \cdot \left(\frac{u\sin\theta}{g}\right)^{2}$$

$$H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{g} - \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g} = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}}$$

**Step 6: Derive Horizontal Range.**

The range is the horizontal distance covered during the time of flight:

$$R = v_{x} \cdot T = (u\cos\theta) \cdot \left(\frac{2u\sin\theta}{g}\right) = \frac{2u^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta}{g}$$

Using $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{u^{2}\sin 2\theta}{g}}$$

---

**(b) A football is kicked from the ground with an initial velocity of 25 m/s at an angle of 40° to the horizontal. Calculate: (i) the time of flight, (ii) the maximum height reached, (iii) the horizontal range, and (iv) the velocity of the football at the highest point. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $u = 25$ m/s, $\theta = 40°$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Preliminary values:**

$$u\sin\theta = 25 \sin 40° = 25 \times 0.6428 = 16.07 \text{ m/s}$$

$$u\cos\theta = 25 \cos 40° = 25 \times 0.7660 = 19.15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 1: Time of flight.**

$$T = \frac{2u\sin\theta}{g} = \frac{2(16.07)}{9.81} = \frac{32.14}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{T = 3.276 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 2: Maximum height.**

$$H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g} = \frac{(16.07)^{2}}{2(9.81)} = \frac{258.24}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{H = 13.16 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Horizontal range.**

$$R = \frac{u^{2}\sin 2\theta}{g} = \frac{(25)^{2}\sin 80°}{9.81} = \frac{625 \times 0.9848}{9.81} = \frac{615.5}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{R = 62.74 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4: Velocity at the highest point.**

At the highest point, the vertical component of velocity is zero. Only the horizontal component remains:

$$v_{x} = u\cos\theta = 19.15 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\text{peak}} = 19.15 \text{ m/s (horizontal direction)}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Skipping the differential equation step (3 marks lost):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly asks for the derivation *starting from* $\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = a$. Writing the final formulas without showing $\frac{dv}{dt} = a$ and $\frac{ds}{dt} = v$ will lead to **direct 3-mark deduction**.
> 
> 2. **Sign convention error in projectile motion (2 marks lost):** Taking upward as positive, gravity $g$ must be **negative** ($a_y = -g$). Many students mistakenly write $a_y = +g$ in the vertical equation, which inverts the trajectory.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting limits of integration (2 marks lost):** Always show the limits $[u, v]$ and $[0, t]$ explicitly in your integration steps. Simply writing $\int dv = \int a \, dt$ without limits is considered incomplete.
> 
> 4. **Using $g = 10$ m/s² in calculation but stating $g = 9.81$:** Be consistent. The KTU paper typically specifies which value to use. Mixing them up costs partial marks.
> 
> 5. **In projectile problems, using $2\theta$ where $\theta$ is needed and vice versa:** Time of flight uses $\sin\theta$, but range uses $\sin 2\theta$. A common error is to write $T = \frac{2u\sin 2\theta}{g}$ — this is **wrong**.
> 
> 6. **Confusing maximum height with range formula:** $H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}$ and $R = \frac{u^{2}\sin 2\theta}{g}$. They look similar but the trigonometric function differs.
> 
> 7. **Units inconsistency:** Always include SI units in the final answer. Writing "15" instead of "15 m" loses half a mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Kinematics from Differential Equations:**

- **Differential equation of motion:** $\frac{dv}{dt} = a(t)$ is the starting point; integrate twice to get velocity and position.

- **Three kinematic equations (constant $a$):**
  - $v = u + at$
  - $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$
  - $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2as$

- **Chain rule trick:** $a = v\frac{dv}{ds}$ is the key to deriving the third equation without time.

- **Projectile motion = 2 independent 1-D motions:** Horizontal (uniform) + Vertical (gravity-affected). Always decompose initial velocity into $u\cos\theta$ and $u\sin\theta$.

- **Sign convention is critical:** Upward positive, gravity negative ($a_y = -g$).

- **Time of flight:** $T = \frac{2u\sin\theta}{g}$ — uses $\sin\theta$, NOT $\sin 2\theta$.

- **Maximum height:** $H = \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}$ — occurs at the midpoint of the flight (at $t = T/2$).

- **Horizontal range:** $R = \frac{u^{2}\sin 2\theta}{g}$ — maximum at $\theta = 45°$, giving $R_{\max} = \frac{u^{2}}{g}$.

- **Trajectory equation:** $y = x\tan\theta - \frac{gx^{2}}{2u^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}$ — a parabola, indicating path is parabolic.

- **Velocity at peak:** Only horizontal component remains: $v_{\text{peak}} = u\cos\theta$.

- **Final velocity on landing:** Has the same magnitude as the launch velocity in the y-component but opposite direction (assuming same level launch and landing). Speed at landing equals launch speed.

- **Key assumption:** Air resistance is neglected; gravity is constant and acts vertically downward.

- **KTU exam tip:** Always start with the differential equation, show the integration with limits, and derive each formula — never quote the kinematic equations as starting points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rotation in Rigid Body Mechanics?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> **Rotation** of a rigid body is the type of planar motion in which all particles of the body move in **circular paths** about a common axis (called the **axis of rotation**), and every line in the body, perpendicular to this axis, sweeps out the **same angle** in the same time interval.

In other words, when a rigid body undergoes pure rotation:
- Every particle traces a **circular trajectory** centered on the axis of rotation.
- All particles have the **same angular displacement** ($\theta$), **same angular velocity** ($\omega$), and **same angular acceleration** ($\alpha$).
- However, their **linear (tangential) velocities and accelerations differ** based on their perpendicular distance from the axis.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Merry-Go-Round 🎠**
> Imagine you and your friends are sitting on a giant merry-go-round. Even if your friend is sitting at the very edge and you are sitting near the center pole, **both of you complete one full revolution in the exact same time** (say, 10 seconds). However, your friend at the edge travels a much **larger distance per second** than you do because the circle he traces is bigger. This is precisely how rotation works: the *angular* quantity is universal, but the *linear* quantity depends on the radial distance.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities in Rotation

The fundamental kinematic and kinetic quantities associated with rotational motion are:

1. **Angular Position ($\theta$)** – The angle (in **radians**) swept by a line from the axis to a particle, measured from a reference. SI Unit: **rad**.
2. **Angular Displacement ($\Delta\theta$)** – The change in angular position. SI Unit: **rad**.
3. **Angular Velocity ($\omega$)** – Rate of change of angular position. SI Unit: **rad/s**.
4. **Angular Acceleration ($\alpha$)** – Rate of change of angular velocity. SI Unit: **rad/s²**.
5. **Moment of Inertia ($I$)** – A measure of the resistance of a body to change in its rotational motion. SI Unit: **kg·m²**.
6. **Torque ($\tau$)** – The rotational equivalent of force; it is the cause of angular acceleration. SI Unit: **N·m**.
7. **Angular Momentum ($H$ or $L$)** – Rotational analogue of linear momentum. SI Unit: **kg·m²/s**.

### 1.4 Fixed Axis vs. General Rotation

> [!NOTE]
> **Fixed-Axis Rotation:** The axis of rotation remains stationary in space (e.g., a ceiling fan, a wheel on a fixed axle). This is the case typically covered in Module 4.
> **General Rotation:** The axis itself can change direction (e.g., a spinning top, a gyroscope). This falls under **3D Rigid Body Kinetics** and is generally addressed in higher semesters.

For KTU Module 4, we restrict ourselves to **fixed-axis rotation**, which is essentially a 1-dimensional angular problem in a 2D plane.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kinematic Foundation of Rotation

The kinematics of rotation is built on a one-to-one analogy with 1-D linear motion. By replacing linear quantities with their angular counterparts, every linear kinematic equation has a direct rotational twin.

### 2.2 Linear vs. Angular: The Critical Relationship

For a particle at perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis of rotation:

**Tangential (Linear) Velocity:**

$$v = r\omega$$

**Tangential (Linear) Acceleration:**

$$a_t = r\alpha$$

**Normal (Centripetal) Acceleration:**

$$a_n = \frac{v^2}{r} = r\omega^2$$

**Total Linear Acceleration Magnitude:**

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} = r\sqrt{\alpha^2 + \omega^4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Examiners love to ask: *"A wheel of radius $R$ rotates with $\omega$ rad/s. Find the velocity of a point on its rim."* The answer is $v = R\omega$, **not** $v = \omega$. Many students lose 1–2 marks for unit errors here.

### 2.3 The Five Kinematic Equations of Rotation (Constant $\alpha$)

When $\alpha$ is constant, the following equations govern fixed-axis rotation, *exactly mirroring* the linear SUVAT equations:

| # | Linear Equation (SUVAT) | Rotational Equivalent | Variables |
|---|------------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| 1 | $v = u + at$ | $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t$ | $\omega_1, \omega_2$ in rad/s |
| 2 | $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ | $\theta = \omega_1 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$ | $\theta$ in rad |
| 3 | $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ | $\omega_2^2 = \omega_1^2 + 2\alpha\theta$ | — |
| 4 | $s = \tfrac{1}{2}(u+v)t$ | $\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(\omega_1 + \omega_2)t$ | — |
| 5 | $s = vt - \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ | $\theta = \omega_2 t - \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Trick (SUVAT → ROTATION):**
> $s \to \theta$, $u \to \omega_1$, $v \to \omega_2$, $a \to \alpha$, $t \to t$ — simply swap the symbols and the entire linear framework is recycled!

## 2.4 The Kinetics of Rotation: Newton's Second Law for Rotation

The **angular momentum** of a particle of mass $m$ moving with tangential velocity $v$ at radius $r$ is:

$$H = mvr = mr^2 \cdot \frac{v}{r} = mr^2\omega$$

Differentiating to find the relationship between torque and angular acceleration:

$$\tau = \frac{dH}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(mr^2\omega) = mr^2\alpha$$

For a rigid body composed of many particles, we define the **Moment of Inertia (MOI)** about the axis as:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2 \quad \text{or in continuous form} \quad I = \int r^2 \, dm$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single Most Important Equation in Module 4:**
> $$\boxed{\tau = I\alpha}$$
> This is the rotational form of $F = ma$. It is the **master equation** the KTU examiner expects you to wield fluently.

## 2.5 Mass Moment of Inertia — The "Rotational Mass"

Unlike mass, the moment of inertia depends on **how mass is distributed** *about a specific axis*. A hollow cylinder has a higher $I$ than a solid cylinder of the same mass and radius, because its mass is concentrated farther from the axis.

### Radius of Gyration ($k$)

To simplify MOI calculations, define the **radius of gyration** $k$:

$$I = Mk^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{M}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $k$:** Imagine all the mass of the body is concentrated at a single point located at distance $k$ from the axis. If this point-mass $M$ has the same $I$ as the original distributed body, then $k$ is the radius of gyration.

### The Two Fundamental MOI Theorems

| Theorem | Statement | Equation |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **Parallel Axis Theorem** | The MOI about any axis parallel to a centroidal axis equals the centroidal MOI plus $Md^2$ | $I = I_G + Md^2$ |
| **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** *(only for laminar/planar bodies)* | For a thin lamina, the MOI about an axis perpendicular to the plane equals the sum of MOIs about two perpendicular in-plane axes | $I_z = I_x + I_y$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** The perpendicular axis theorem is **only valid for planar (laminar) bodies** — i.e., bodies whose mass lies in a single plane. Applying it to 3D solids like cylinders or spheres is a guaranteed loss of marks.

## 2.6 Master Formula Sheet (Print This!)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Tangential velocity | $v = r\omega$ | m/s |
| 2 | Tangential acceleration | $a_t = r\alpha$ | m/s² |
| 3 | Centripetal acceleration | $a_n = r\omega^2$ | m/s² |
| 4 | Arc length traveled | $s = r\theta$ | m |
| 5 | MOI of a particle | $I = mr^2$ | kg·m² |
| 6 | MOI of a rigid body | $I = \int r^2 \, dm$ | kg·m² |
| 7 | Radius of gyration | $k = \sqrt{I/M}$ | m |
| 8 | Parallel axis theorem | $I = I_G + Md^2$ | kg·m² |
| 9 | Perpendicular axis theorem | $I_z = I_x + I_y$ | kg·m² |
| 10 | Newton's law (rotation) | $\tau = I\alpha$ | N·m |
| 11 | Angular momentum | $H = I\omega$ | kg·m²/s |
| 12 | Rotational KE | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | J |
| 13 | Power delivered | $P = \tau\omega$ | W |
| 14 | Work done by torque | $W = \tau\theta$ | J |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flywheels in Engines:** Designed with high MOI to store rotational kinetic energy and ensure smooth power delivery.
- **Turbine Blades:** MOI must be precisely calculated to prevent resonance failures.
- **Disc Brakes:** Use the relationship $\tau = I\alpha$ to predict stopping time of rotors.
- **Gyroscopes & Drones:** Utilize conservation of angular momentum for attitude control.
- **Wheels of Vehicles:** Lower MOI at the wheel improves acceleration; higher MOI improves stability.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tangential Velocity from Arc Length

**Step 1 — Starting Definition (Linear Speed):**
Linear (tangential) speed is the rate at which the particle traces the arc.

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric Relationship:**
A particle at distance $r$ from the axis sweeps an arc length $s$ that is related to angular displacement $\theta$ by:

$$s = r\theta$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to time:**
Since $r$ is constant for a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = r \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the rates of change:**

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = v \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$\boxed{v = r\omega}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Newton's Second Law for Rotation

**Step 1 — Start with Linear Newton's Second Law (for a single particle):**
Tangential component of force on particle $i$ produces tangential acceleration $a_{t,i}$.

$$F_{t,i} = m_i a_{t,i}$$

**Step 2 — Express tangential force as torque:**
Torque on particle $i$ about the axis:

$$\tau_i = F_{t,i} \cdot r_i$$

**Step 3 — Express tangential acceleration in terms of $\alpha$:**

$$a_{t,i} = r_i \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$\tau_i = m_i (r_i \alpha) r_i = m_i r_i^2 \alpha$$

**Step 5 — Sum over all particles of the rigid body:**
Because $\alpha$ is the same for all particles:

$$\tau = \sum_i \tau_i = \left(\sum_i m_i r_i^2\right) \alpha$$

**Step 6 — Identify the bracketed sum as the Moment of Inertia:**

$$\boxed{\tau = I\alpha}$$

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — MOI of a Uniform Rod

> **Problem:** A uniform thin rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ rotates about an axis perpendicular to its length, passing through one end. Find its MOI.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Element:**
Consider a differential element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the pivot end.

**Step 2 — Express dm:**
Linear mass density $\lambda = M/L$, so:

$$dm = \lambda \, dx = \frac{M}{L} dx$$

**Step 3 — Write the MOI integral:**

$$I = \int_0^L x^2 \, dm = \int_0^L x^2 \frac{M}{L} dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$I = \frac{M}{L} \left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^L = \frac{M}{L} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{I_{\text{end}} = \frac{ML^2}{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check using Parallel Axis Theorem:**
For a uniform rod about its centroid: $I_G = \dfrac{ML^2}{12}$ (standard result).
Distance from centroid to end: $d = L/2$.

$$I_{\text{end}} = I_G + Md^2 = \frac{ML^2}{12} + M\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{ML^2}{12} + \frac{ML^2}{4} = \frac{ML^2}{12} + \frac{3ML^2}{12} = \frac{4ML^2}{12} = \frac{ML^2}{3} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Standard MOIs You MUST Memorize

| Body | Axis | MOI ($I$) |
|------|------|----------|
| Thin rod | Through center, $\perp$ to length | $\dfrac{ML^2}{12}$ |
| Thin rod | Through one end, $\perp$ to length | $\dfrac{ML^2}{3}$ |
| Solid cylinder / disc | Central longitudinal axis | $\dfrac{MR^2}{2}$ |
| Hollow cylinder / ring | Central longitudinal axis | $MR^2$ |
| Solid sphere | Any diameter | $\dfrac{2MR^2}{5}$ |
| Hollow sphere (thin shell) | Any diameter | $\dfrac{2MR^2}{3}$ |
| Rectangular plate | Through center, $\perp$ to plate | $\dfrac{M(a^2 + b^2)}{12}$ |
| Solid cone | Central axis (apex to base) | $\dfrac{3MR^2}{10}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** These 8 standard MOIs form ~70% of the numerical part. Memorize them with their *derivation sketches* in mind — examiners sometimes give a non-standard body and ask you to integrate.

## 3.5 Power, Work, and Energy in Rotation — Full Derivation Chain

**Step 1 — Work done by a constant torque over angular displacement $\theta$:**

$$W = \tau \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time to get power:**

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt} = \tau \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \tau \omega$$

**Step 3 — Work–Energy Theorem for Rotation:**
The work done on a rigid body equals the change in its rotational kinetic energy.

$$W = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2 - \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Recap:** $W = Fs$ and $P = Fv$ in linear motion $\to$ $W = \tau\theta$ and $P = \tau\omega$ in rotation. Notice how the pattern holds perfectly: *force $\to$ torque*, *displacement $\to$ angular displacement*, *velocity $\to$ angular velocity*.

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Which MOI Theorem to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Given MOI Problem] --> Q1{Is the body a thin lamina or planar?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the required axis in the plane of the lamina?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Perpendicular Axis Theorem I z = I x + I y]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use Parallel Axis Theorem I = I G + M d squared]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Is the required axis parallel to a known centroidal axis?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Parallel Axis Theorem]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Set up integration I = integral r squared dm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Many KTU Part B problems hand you the MOI about *one* axis and ask for another. Identifying the right theorem in under 10 seconds is a time-saver during exams.
