# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Q:** Define *radius of gyration* of a rigid body. How is it related to the moment of inertia?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **radius of gyration** $k$ of a rigid body about a given axis is defined as the distance from the axis at which the entire mass of the body can be assumed to be concentrated so that its moment of inertia about that axis remains the same as that of the original body.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$I = M k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{M}}$$

where $M$ is the total mass of the body. SI unit of $k$ is **metre (m)**.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** State the **Parallel Axis Theorem** and the **Perpendicular Axis Theorem** for moments of inertia.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Parallel Axis Theorem:** The moment of inertia of a rigid body about any axis is equal to the sum of its moment of inertia about a parallel axis passing through the centre of mass and the product of the mass of the body and the square of the perpendicular distance between the two axes.

$$I = I_G + M d^2$$

**Perpendicular Axis Theorem:** It is applicable only to plane laminae. The moment of inertia of a lamina about an axis perpendicular to its plane is equal to the sum of its moments of inertia about two mutually perpendicular axes lying in the plane of the lamina and intersecting at the point where the perpendicular axis passes.

$$I_z = I_x + I_y$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`**
**(a)** A grinding wheel starts from rest and reaches an angular velocity of **$30\,\text{rad/s}$** in **$10\,\text{s}$** with constant angular acceleration. Calculate: **(i)** the angular acceleration, **(ii)** the total angular displacement during this time, and **(iii)** the linear velocity and acceleration of a point on the rim, given the wheel radius is **$0.25\,\text{m}$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the moment of inertia of a thin uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its length, using integration. **(7 marks)**

---

### Full Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Given Data:**
$\omega_1 = 0$, $\omega_2 = 30\,\text{rad/s}$, $t = 10\,\text{s}$, $R = 0.25\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Find angular acceleration $\alpha$:**
Using $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t$:

$$30 = 0 + \alpha \cdot 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\alpha = 3\,\text{rad/s}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Find angular displacement $\theta$:**
Using $\theta = \omega_1 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$:

$$\theta = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(3)(10)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(3)(100) = 150\,\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 150\,\text{rad} \approx 23.87\,\text{revolutions}}$$

**Step 3 — Find linear (tangential) velocity at the rim:**

$$v = R\omega_2 = 0.25 \times 30 = \boxed{7.5\,\text{m/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Find tangential acceleration at the rim:**

$$a_t = R\alpha = 0.25 \times 3 = 0.75\,\text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Find centripetal (normal) acceleration:**

$$a_n = R\omega_2^2 = 0.25 \times (30)^2 = 0.25 \times 900 = 225\,\text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Total linear acceleration magnitude:**

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} = \sqrt{0.75^2 + 225^2} \approx \boxed{225.001\,\text{m/s}^2}$$

> **Observation:** Since $a_n \gg a_t$, the total acceleration is dominated by the centripetal component.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution: Derivation of MOI of a Uniform Rod about its Centre

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Consider a uniform thin rod of mass $M$ and length $L$, pivoted about an axis through its centre $O$ perpendicular to its length.

**Step 2 — Define mass per unit length:**

$$\lambda = \frac{M}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Consider a small element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the centre:**

$$dm = \lambda \, dx = \frac{M}{L} dx$$

**Step 4 — MOI of the element about the axis:**

$$dI = x^2 \, dm = \frac{M}{L} x^2 \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $-L/2$ to $+L/2$ (rod symmetric about centre):**

$$I = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{M}{L} x^2 \, dx = \frac{M}{L} \left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-L/2}^{L/2}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate:**

$$I = \frac{M}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{L}{2}\right)^3\right] = \frac{M}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2L^3}{8}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify:**

$$I = \frac{M}{L} \cdot \frac{L^3}{12} = \frac{ML^2}{12}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{centre}} = \frac{ML^2}{12}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`**
**(a)** A solid cylinder of mass **$20\,\text{kg}$** and radius **$0.4\,\text{m}$** is acted upon by a tangential force of **$50\,\text{N}$** at its rim. Find: **(i)** the torque, **(ii)** the angular acceleration, and **(iii)** the angular velocity after **$5\,\text{s}$** starting from rest. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flywheel of mass **$200\,\text{kg}$** has a radius of gyration of **$0.5\,\text{m}$** about its axis of rotation. It is brought to rest from an angular speed of **$300\,\text{rpm}$** in **$20\,\text{s}$** under a constant frictional torque. Find: **(i)** the moment of inertia, **(ii)** the frictional torque, and **(iii)** the work done by the friction in stopping the flywheel. **(7 marks)**

---

### Full Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Given:** $M = 20\,\text{kg}$, $R = 0.4\,\text{m}$, $F = 50\,\text{N}$, $\omega_1 = 0$, $t = 5\,\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the torque:**

$$\tau = F \cdot R = 50 \times 0.4 = \boxed{20\,\text{N·m}}$$

**Step 2 — MOI of a solid cylinder about its central axis:**

$$I = \frac{MR^2}{2} = \frac{20 \times (0.4)^2}{2} = \frac{20 \times 0.16}{2} = 1.6\,\text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\tau = I\alpha$ to find $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\tau}{I} = \frac{20}{1.6} = \boxed{12.5\,\text{rad/s}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Find $\omega_2$ after $5\,\text{s}$:**

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t = 0 + 12.5 \times 5 = \boxed{62.5\,\text{rad/s}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Given:** $M = 200\,\text{kg}$, $k = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $\omega_1 = 300\,\text{rpm}$, $t = 20\,\text{s}$, $\omega_2 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$\omega_1 = 300 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute MOI:**

$$I = M k^2 = 200 \times (0.5)^2 = 200 \times 0.25 = \boxed{50\,\text{kg·m}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Find angular acceleration $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t} = \frac{0 - 31.416}{20} = -1.5708\,\text{rad/s}^2$$

(Negative sign indicates deceleration.)

**Step 4 — Frictional torque magnitude:**

$$\tau_f = I|\alpha| = 50 \times 1.5708 = \boxed{78.54\,\text{N·m}}$$

**Step 5 — Angular displacement during stopping:**

$$\theta = \omega_1 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2 = 31.416 \times 20 + \tfrac{1}{2}(-1.5708)(20)^2$$
$$\theta = 628.32 - 314.16 = 314.16\,\text{rad}$$

(Equivalent to 50 full revolutions — consistent: $\theta = \omega_1 t / 2$ for constant deceleration.)

**Step 6 — Work done by friction (Work–Energy Theorem):**

$$W = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}I\omega_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}I\omega_1^2 = 0 - \frac{1}{2}(50)(31.416)^2$$
$$W = -25 \times 986.96 = \boxed{-24\,674\,\text{J} \approx -24.67\,\text{kJ}}$$

The negative sign confirms that friction **removes** rotational kinetic energy from the flywheel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-up Trap:** When given RPM, you **must** convert to rad/s before applying $\omega^2$ or $\alpha$ formulas. $1\,\text{rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60}\,\text{rad/s}$. Forgetting this is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign of Torque:** Always declare your sign convention (CCW positive / CW positive) and stick to it. A negative answer without a stated convention will not get full marks.
> 3. **Perpendicular Axis Theorem misuse:** It is *not* valid for 3D solids like spheres, cylinders, or cones. Use it **only for thin laminae**.
> 4. **Forgetting $I_G$:** When asked for MOI about an arbitrary axis, students often give the centroidal value and forget to add $Md^2$ via the parallel axis theorem.
> 5. **Incomplete derivation in Part B (b):** The examiner expects the *integration limits* to be clearly written and *both* the integrand substitution and final simplification to be shown. Skipping algebraic steps costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 📌 **High-Density Revision Summary — Module 4: Rotation**

**1. Kinematic Variables (all measured in radians & seconds):**
- $\theta$ — angular position (rad)
- $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ — angular velocity (rad/s)
- $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ — angular acceleration (rad/s²)
- Five SUVAT-like equations apply when $\alpha$ is constant.

**2. Linear–Angular Coupling (a particle at radius $r$):**
- Arc length: $s = r\theta$
- Velocity: $v = r\omega$ (tangential)
- Tangential acceleration: $a_t = r\alpha$
- Centripetal acceleration: $a_n = r\omega^2 = v^2/r$

**3. The Master Kinetic Equation:**
- $\tau_{\text{net}} = I\alpha$ (Newton's 2nd Law in rotation)

**4. Moment of Inertia Essentials:**
- Definition: $I = \int r^2 \, dm$
- Radius of gyration: $I = Mk^2$
- Parallel axis: $I = I_G + Md^2$
- Perpendicular axis: $I_z = I_x + I_y$ (laminae only)
- Standard MOIs to memorize: rod (center & end), disc/cylinder, ring, solid/hollow sphere, cone, rectangular plate.

**5. Energy, Power, and Momentum:**
- Rotational KE: $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$
- Angular momentum: $H = I\omega$
- Work by torque: $W = \tau\theta$
- Power: $P = \tau\omega$

**6. Sign Convention & Vector Nature:**
- Use right-hand rule for direction of $\vec\omega$, $\vec\alpha$, $\vec\tau$, $\vec H$.
- In 2D problems, work with signed scalars (CCW positive by default).

**7. Common Engineering Applications:**
- Flywheels (energy storage via $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$)
- Vehicle wheels, pulleys, gears, turbines, gyroscopes
- Disc brakes (deceleration via $\tau = I\alpha$)
- Rolling motion (combined translation + rotation)

**8. Common Conversion Factors:**
- $1\,\text{rev} = 2\pi\,\text{rad}$
- $1\,\text{rpm} = \dfrac{\pi}{30}\,\text{rad/s}$
- $1\,\text{rps} = 2\pi\,\text{rad/s}$

**9. Exam Strategy Tips:**
- Always draw a free-body diagram and label the axis of rotation.
- Identify the type of body (rod/disc/sphere/lamina) and recall the standard MOI.
- Use the parallel axis theorem if the rotation axis does not pass through the centroid.
- Double-check units — a wrong unit = a guaranteed mark deduction.

**10. The "Golden Triad" to Remember:**
> **$F = ma$ $\longrightarrow$ $\tau = I\alpha$**
> **$p = mv$ $\longrightarrow$ $H = I\omega$**
> **$KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ $\longrightarrow$ $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$**

Every rotational equation is a mirror of its linear counterpart with $m \to I$, $v \to \omega$, $a \to \alpha$, $F \to \tau$. Once you internalize this triad, the entire module reduces to a single pattern-recognition exercise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematics of Rotation: Equation of Motion for a Rigid Body Rotating About a Fixed Axis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kinematics of Rotation: Equation of Motion for a Rigid Body Rotating About a Fixed Axis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kinematics of Rotation** is the branch of rigid body mechanics that describes the motion of a body rotating about a fixed axis without considering the forces or moments causing that motion. When a rigid body rotates about a fixed axis, every particle of the body traces a circular path in a plane perpendicular to the axis of rotation, and all particles rotate through the same **angular displacement** in the same time interval.

The **Equation of Motion for a Rigid Body Rotating About a Fixed Axis** relates the net external moment (torque) acting on the body to its moment of inertia and the resulting angular acceleration, analogous to Newton's second law ($F = ma$) for translational motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme focuses on deriving and applying the rotational equation of motion $T = I\alpha$, combined with kinematic relations $v = r\omega$, $a_t = r\alpha$, and $a_n = r\omega^2$. Numerical problems on pulleys, wheels, and flywheels are high-weightage.

The motion of a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis is fully described by three rotational kinematic quantities:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Vector Nature |
|----------|--------|---------|---------------|
| Angular displacement | $\theta$ | **radian (rad)** | Vector (along axis) |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s** | Vector (along axis) |
| Angular acceleration | $\alpha$ | **rad/s²** | Vector (along axis) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spinning Ceiling Fan**
> Imagine a ceiling fan switched on. Every point on the blade — from the tip to the hub — sweeps the *same angle* in *one second*. However, the *linear speed* is dramatically different. The tip of the blade (far from the axis) cuts through the air at a much higher speed than the point near the hub (close to the axis). This is because while the **angular velocity ($\omega$)** is the same for all particles, the **linear velocity ($v$)** depends on the radial distance $r$ from the axis: $v = r\omega$.

### Translational vs. Rotational Motion — Side-by-Side Intuition

| Translational (Linear) | Rotational (Angular) |
|------------------------|----------------------|
| Displacement $s$ (m) | Angular displacement $\theta$ (rad) |
| Velocity $v$ (m/s) | Angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) |
| Acceleration $a$ (m/s²) | Angular acceleration $\alpha$ (rad/s²) |
| Mass $m$ (kg) | Moment of inertia $I$ (kg·m²) |
| Force $F$ (N) | Torque $T$ (N·m) |
| Newton's Law: $F = ma$ | Newton's Law: $T = I\alpha$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** Just replace every linear quantity in your mind with the corresponding rotational quantity. The mathematics becomes identical, only the units and physical meaning change.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **1 revolution** $= 2\pi$ **radians** $\approx$ **6.2832 rad**
- **1 radian** $\approx$ **57.296°**
- **Earth's rotation:** $\omega_{Earth} \approx 7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ **rad/s**
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** (used in equivalent torque calculations)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Kinematic Relations for a Fixed-Axis Rotation

When a rigid body rotates about a fixed axis (say, the $z$-axis passing through point $O$), any particle $P$ at perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis moves along a circle of radius $r$. The relations between the linear motion of $P$ and the angular motion of the body are:

$$v = r\omega$$

$$a_t = r\alpha \quad \text{(Tangential acceleration)}$$

$$a_n = r\omega^2 \quad \text{(Normal / Centripetal acceleration)}$$

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} \quad \text{(Total acceleration magnitude)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these relations exist:** Every particle at radius $r$ traces an arc of length $s = r\theta$. Differentiating once gives $v = r\omega$, and differentiating again gives $a_t = r\alpha$. The normal acceleration exists because every particle undergoes *circular* motion, requiring a centripetal (inward) acceleration even at constant $\omega$.

## 2.2 Angular Motion with Constant Angular Acceleration

When $\alpha$ is constant, the following equations govern the motion (perfectly analogous to the SUVAT equations of translational kinematics):

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t$$

$$\theta = \omega_1 t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$$

$$\omega_2^2 = \omega_1^2 + 2\alpha\theta$$

$$\theta = \frac{(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{2} \cdot t$$

## 2.3 Newton's Second Law for Rotation: The Equation of Motion

For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis with moment of inertia $I$ about that axis, the net external torque about the axis equals the moment of inertia times the angular acceleration:

$$T = I\alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE Equation of Motion for KTU Module 4.** Every numerical problem you solve under this topic is essentially an application of $T = I\alpha$, after correctly identifying the torque $T$ and the moment of inertia $I$.

## 2.4 Torque and Moment of Inertia — Refresher

- **Torque** about the axis: $T = F \cdot r_{\perp}$, where $r_{\perp}$ is the perpendicular distance from the axis to the line of action of the force $F$.
- **Moment of Inertia** of a body of mass $m$ about the fixed axis: $I = \int r^2 \, dm$, which represents the rotational equivalent of mass.

**Moment of Inertia of Standard Bodies (KTU Reference):**

| Body | Axis | Moment of Inertia $I$ |
|------|------|------------------------|
| Thin Rod (length $L$, mass $M$) | Through centre, $\perp$ to rod | $\dfrac{ML^2}{12}$ |
| Thin Rod (length $L$, mass $M$) | Through one end, $\perp$ to rod | $\dfrac{ML^2}{3}$ |
| Solid Cylinder/Disc (radius $R$, mass $M$) | Central longitudinal axis | $\dfrac{MR^2}{2}$ |
| Hollow Cylinder/Ring (radius $R$, mass $M$) | Central longitudinal axis | $MR^2$ |
| Solid Sphere (radius $R$, mass $M$) | Through centre (any diameter) | $\dfrac{2MR^2}{5}$ |
| Hollow Sphere (radius $R$, mass $M$) | Through centre (any diameter) | $\dfrac{2MR^2}{3}$ |
| Rectangular Plate (sides $a, b$, mass $M$) | $\perp$ through centre | $\dfrac{M(a^2 + b^2)}{12}$ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and stick it in your notebook.** These are the only formulas needed to solve any KTU Module 4 problem on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{s}{r}$ | Angular displacement from arc length | rad |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$ | Angular acceleration | rad/s² |
| 4 | $v = r\omega$ | Linear velocity of a particle | m/s |
| 5 | $a_t = r\alpha$ | Tangential (linear) acceleration | m/s² |
| 6 | $a_n = r\omega^2$ | Normal (centripetal) acceleration | m/s² |
| 7 | $a = r\sqrt{\alpha^2 + \omega^4}$ | Total linear acceleration magnitude | m/s² |
| 8 | $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t$ | Constant angular acceleration | rad/s |
| 9 | $\theta = \omega_1 t + \dfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$ | Angular displacement | rad |
| 10 | $\omega_2^2 = \omega_1^2 + 2\alpha\theta$ | Velocity–displacement relation | rad²/s² |
| 11 | $T = I\alpha$ | **Rotational Equation of Motion** | N·m |
| 12 | $T = F \cdot r_{\perp}$ | Torque from a force | N·m |
| 13 | $I = \int r^2 \, dm$ or $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$ | Moment of Inertia | kg·m² |
| 14 | $P = T\omega$ | Rotational Power | W |
| 15 | $\text{KE}_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | Rotational Kinetic Energy | J |
| 16 | $\text{KE}_{total} = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | Combined translational + rotational KE | J |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flywheels** in automobiles: Store rotational kinetic energy to smooth out engine torque pulsations. The equation $T = I\alpha$ governs their angular acceleration.
- **Turbine blades** in power plants: Designed using $a_t = r\alpha$ and $a_n = r\omega^2$ to predict stress distribution.
- **Pulley systems** in cranes and elevators: Use combined $T = I\alpha$ for the pulley and $F = ma$ for the hanging mass.
- **Hard disk drives (HDD):** Spin at 5400–15000 rpm; rotational dynamics determine seek times and reliability.
- **Gyroscopes** in aircraft and ships: Rotational moment of inertia and angular momentum stabilize navigation.
- **Ceiling fans, washing machines, helicopter rotors:** All designed using these kinematic and kinetic principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relation between Linear and Angular Quantities

Consider a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis $O$. A particle $P$ located at perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis moves along a circular arc.

**Step 1:** As the body rotates by an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$, particle $P$ moves through an infinitesimal arc length $ds$ on its circular path.

$$ds = r \, d\theta$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = r \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $\dfrac{ds}{dt}$ is the linear speed $v$ of the particle, and $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ is the angular speed $\omega$ of the body:

$$v = r\omega$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $v = r\omega$ with respect to $t$ (with $r$ constant for fixed-axis rotation):

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = r \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize $\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ as the tangential acceleration $a_t$ (the linear acceleration along the direction of motion) and $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ as the angular acceleration $\alpha$:

$$a_t = r\alpha$$

**Step 6:** Since particle $P$ is in circular motion, it also experiences a centripetal (normal) acceleration directed radially inward toward the axis:

$$a_n = \frac{v^2}{r} = \frac{(r\omega)^2}{r} = r\omega^2$$

**Step 7:** Total acceleration magnitude (tangential and normal are perpendicular):

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} = \sqrt{(r\alpha)^2 + (r\omega^2)^2}$$

$$\boxed{a = r\sqrt{\alpha^2 + \omega^4}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equation of Motion for a Rigid Body Rotating About a Fixed Axis ($T = I\alpha$)

This is the most important derivation in KTU Module 4. We will derive it from Newton's second law applied to each particle of the rigid body.

**Step 1:** Consider a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis $O$. The body is composed of $n$ particles of masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$ at perpendicular distances $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n$ from the axis.

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's second law in the tangential direction to particle $i$. The net tangential force $F_{t,i}$ on particle $i$ produces a tangential acceleration $a_{t,i}$:

$$F_{t,i} = m_i a_{t,i} = m_i r_i \alpha$$

**Step 3:** Take the moment (torque) of this tangential force about the axis $O$. The moment arm is $r_i$:

$$T_i = F_{t,i} \cdot r_i = m_i r_i^2 \, \alpha$$

**Step 4:** Sum the moments for all particles of the body. Internal forces cancel in pairs by Newton's third law, leaving only the net external torque $T$:

$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2 \, \alpha$$

**Step 5:** Since $\alpha$ is the same for all particles (rigid body, fixed axis), factor it out:

$$T = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2 \right) \alpha$$

**Step 6:** Define the sum as the moment of inertia $I$ of the body about the fixed axis:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $I$ into the equation:

$$\boxed{T = I\alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This is the rotational analogue of $F = ma$. Torque $T$ is the rotational "force," moment of inertia $I$ is the rotational "mass," and angular acceleration $\alpha$ is the rotational "acceleration." A body with a large $I$ resists angular acceleration just as a heavy body resists linear acceleration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rotational Power

**Step 1:** When a torque $T$ acts on a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, in a small time $dt$ the body rotates by $d\theta$.

**Step 2:** The work done by the torque is:

$$dW = T \, d\theta$$

**Step 3:** Power is the rate of doing work:

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt} = T \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Since $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$:

$$\boxed{P = T\omega}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Rotational Kinetic Energy

**Step 1:** Each particle $i$ of the rigid body has linear kinetic energy $\dfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $v_i = r_i \omega$:

$$KE_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i (r_i \omega)^2 = \frac{1}{2} m_i r_i^2 \omega^2$$

**Step 3:** Sum over all particles:

$$KE_{rot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2} m_i r_i^2 \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2 \right) \omega^2$$

**Step 4:** Recognizing the sum as $I$:

$$\boxed{KE_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Analytical Problem (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A solid cylindrical pulley of mass $M = 10$ kg and radius $R = 0.2$ m is free to rotate about its central horizontal axis. A light inextensible string is wound around it, with a mass $m = 2$ kg hanging from its free end. The system is released from rest. Find:
(a) The angular acceleration of the pulley.
(b) The linear acceleration of the hanging mass.
(c) The tension in the string.
(d) The velocity of the mass after it falls $1$ m. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the moment of inertia of the pulley.**

For a solid cylinder rotating about its central longitudinal axis:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} M R^2 = \frac{1}{2} (10)(0.2)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(10)(0.04) = 0.2 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2: Write the equation of motion for the hanging mass (translation).**

Forces on $m$: weight $mg$ (down) and tension $T$ (up). Taking downward as positive:

$$mg - T = ma \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 3: Write the equation of motion for the pulley (rotation).**

The tension $T$ in the string exerts a torque $T \cdot R$ on the pulley about its axis. This is the only torque (assume frictionless axle):

$$TR = I\alpha \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 4: Use the kinematic constraint.** The string does not slip, so the linear acceleration $a$ of the mass equals the tangential acceleration at the rim of the pulley:

$$a = R\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{a}{R} \quad \cdots (3)$$

**Step 5: Substitute (3) into (2).**

$$TR = I \cdot \frac{a}{R}$$

$$T = \frac{Ia}{R^2} \quad \cdots (4)$$

**Step 6: Substitute (4) into (1).**

$$mg - \frac{Ia}{R^2} = ma$$

$$mg = ma + \frac{Ia}{R^2} = a\left(m + \frac{I}{R^2}\right)$$

$$a = \frac{mg}{m + \dfrac{I}{R^2}}$$

**Step 7: Plug in numerical values.**

$$\frac{I}{R^2} = \frac{0.2}{(0.2)^2} = \frac{0.2}{0.04} = 5 \text{ kg}$$

$$a = \frac{2 \times 9.81}{2 + 5} = \frac{19.62}{7} = 2.803 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Part (a): Angular acceleration.**

$$\alpha = \frac{a}{R} = \frac{2.803}{0.2} = 14.014 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 14.0 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$

**Part (b): Linear acceleration.**

$$\boxed{a = 2.803 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Part (c): Tension in the string.**

From (4):

$$T = \frac{Ia}{R^2} = 5 \times 2.803 = 14.014 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 14.0 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification using (1):** $mg - T = 2(9.81) - 14.014 = 19.62 - 14.014 = 5.606$ N. And $ma = 2(2.803) = 5.606$ N. ✓

**Part (d): Velocity after falling 1 m.**

Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with $u = 0$, $a = 2.803$ m/s², $s = 1$ m:

$$v^2 = 0 + 2(2.803)(1) = 5.606$$

$$v = \sqrt{5.606} = 2.368 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 2.37 \text{ m/s}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Solution Procedure for Rotational Equation of Motion Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: KTU Rotational Problem] --> B[Identify the rigid body and fixed axis]
    B --> C{Is the body a standard shape?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use standard formula for I]
    C -- No --> E[Use I = sum m_i r_i^2 or integration]
    D --> F[Identify all external torques about axis]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute net torque T_eq = sum of T_i]
    G --> H[Apply kinematic constraints v = r omega and a = r alpha]
    H --> I[Write translational equation F = ma if masses are involved]
    I --> J[Combine translational and rotational equations]
    J --> K[Solve algebraically for unknowns]
    K --> L[Verify: check limiting cases and units]
    L --> M[End: Final Answer]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Free-Body Force and Torque Analysis for Pulley–Mass System

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    blockArrowId1<[" "]</br>DOWNWARD WEIGHT mg"] --> massBlock["Hanging Mass m"]
    massBlock --> stringBlock["Light String"]
    stringBlock --> pulleyBlock["Solid Pulley: I = MR^2/2"]
    pulleyBlock --> torqueLabel["Torque T*R about axis"]
    massBlock --> FnetLabel["Net Force: mg - T = ma"]
    pulleyBlock --> torqueLabel2["T = I*alpha"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Vector Relationship between Linear and Angular Quantities for a Rotating Particle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Particle P at radius r] -- r perpendicular to axis --> B[Axis of rotation O]
    A -- linear velocity v tangent to circle --> C[Direction of v: perpendicular to r]
    A -- tangential acceleration a_t --> D[Along tangent, alpha direction]
    A -- normal acceleration a_n --> E[Radially inward toward axis]
    F[Angular velocity omega: vector along axis] --> A
    G[Angular acceleration alpha: vector along axis] --> A
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Force–Torque Block Diagram for a Rigid Body

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 1
    block:rigid
        columns 3
        F1["Force F1 at r1"] 
        F2["Force F2 at r2"] 
        F3["Force F3 at r3"]
    rigid
    block:axis
        FixedAxis["Fixed Axis of Rotation O"]
    axis
    F1 -- T1 = F1*r1 --> FixedAxis
    F2 -- T2 = F2*r2 --> FixedAxis
    F3 -- T3 = F3*r3 --> FixedAxis
    FixedAxis -- "Net T = I*alpha" --> ResultBlock["Angular Acceleration alpha"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The torques $T_1, T_2, T_3$ are *vector quantities* directed along the axis. Use the **right-hand rule** to determine their sign. Torques producing rotation in the conventional positive sense (counter-clockwise when viewed along axis) are positive.

## 4.5 Kinematic Constraint Visualization (No-Slip Condition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulley Radius R rotates by angle theta] --> B[Arc length s = R*theta unwound]
    B --> C[Hanging mass descends by distance s]
    A --> D[Angular velocity omega]
    D --> E[Linear velocity of mass v = R*omega]
    A --> F[Angular acceleration alpha]
    F --> G[Linear acceleration of mass a = R*alpha]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are short, direct, and test definitions, formulas, or simple one-step applications. Answers should be concise (3–5 lines).

### Question A1: Definition (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the moment of inertia of a rigid body about a given axis. State its physical significance and SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**

The moment of inertia of a rigid body about a given axis is defined as the sum of the products of the mass of each particle and the square of its perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation. Mathematically,

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2 \quad \text{or} \quad I = \int r^2 \, dm$$

**Physical Significance:** Moment of inertia represents the rotational inertia of a body, i.e., the resistance offered by the body to a change in its rotational motion. It is the rotational analogue of mass in translational motion.

**SI Unit:** **kg·m²**

---

### Question A2: Conceptual (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A solid disc and a ring of the same mass and same radius are rotated about their central axes with the same angular velocity. Which has greater rotational kinetic energy and why?**

**Model Answer:**

The rotational kinetic energy is given by $KE_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$.

- For the **solid disc:** $I_{disc} = \dfrac{1}{2}MR^2$
- For the **ring:** $I_{ring} = MR^2$

Since both have the same $M$ and $R$, $I_{ring} = 2 I_{disc}$.

Therefore, with the same $\omega$, the **ring has greater rotational kinetic energy** (twice that of the disc), because it has a larger moment of inertia due to all its mass being concentrated at the maximum distance $R$ from the axis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Question: A solid cylinder pulley of mass 20 kg and radius 0.25 m has a light string wound around it. The string carries a 5 kg mass at its free end. Calculate:

**(a)** The angular acceleration of the pulley and the linear acceleration of the mass. (7 marks)

**(b)** The tension in the string and the kinetic energy of the system after the mass has fallen 2 m from rest. (7 marks)

### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Moment of inertia of solid cylinder pulley about its central axis:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2 = \frac{1}{2}(20)(0.25)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(20)(0.0625) = 0.625 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Equation of motion for the hanging mass (translation), taking downward as positive:

$$mg - T = ma \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 3:** Equation of motion for the pulley (rotation) about its axis. The string tension $T$ exerts torque $T \cdot R$:

$$TR = I\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(0.25) = 0.625 \alpha \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 4:** No-slip kinematic constraint:

$$a = R\alpha = 0.25\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{a}{0.25} = 4a \quad \cdots (3)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute (3) into (2):

$$T(0.25) = 0.625 (4a)$$

$$0.25 T = 2.5 a$$

$$T = 10a \quad \cdots (4)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute (4) into (1) with $m = 5$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$5(9.81) - 10a = 5a$$

$$49.05 = 5a + 10a = 15a$$

$$a = \frac{49.05}{15} = 3.27 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 7:** Angular acceleration:

$$\alpha = 4a = 4(3.27) = 13.08 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{a = 3.27 \text{ m/s}^2, \quad \alpha = 13.08 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Tension in the string from equation (4):

$$T = 10a = 10(3.27) = 32.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Find velocity of mass after falling 2 m using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with $u = 0$:

$$v^2 = 0 + 2(3.27)(2) = 13.08$$

$$v = \sqrt{13.08} = 3.617 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Angular velocity of pulley:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{3.617}{0.25} = 14.47 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4:** Total kinetic energy of the system (translational KE of mass + rotational KE of pulley):

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$$

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2}(5)(3.617)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(0.625)(14.47)^2$$

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2}(5)(13.083) + \frac{1}{2}(0.625)(209.38)$$

$$KE_{trans} = 32.71 \text{ J}$$

$$KE_{rot} = 65.43 \text{ J}$$

$$KE_{total} = 32.71 + 65.43 = 98.14 \text{ J}$$

**Step 5:** Verification using energy conservation: $mgh = 5(9.81)(2) = 98.1$ J. ✓

$$\boxed{T = 32.7 \text{ N}, \quad KE_{total} = 98.14 \text{ J}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the no-slip constraint:** Many students write $a$ and $\alpha$ as independent. Always link them via $a = R\alpha$.
> 2. **Wrong moment of inertia formula:** Students often confuse $I = MR^2$ (ring) with $I = \dfrac{1}{2}MR^2$ (solid cylinder). Read the problem carefully.
> 3. **Sign errors in torque:** When setting up $TR = I\alpha$, ensure you take the torque about the *fixed axis of rotation*, not about an arbitrary point.
> 4. **Forgetting rotational KE:** When asked for total kinetic energy, many students compute only $\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ and miss the $\dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ term. This costs 3–4 marks.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question: A flywheel of mass 50 kg and radius of gyration 0.4 m is rotating at 300 rpm. A braking torque of 25 N·m is applied uniformly until the flywheel stops.

**(a)** Determine the angular acceleration of the flywheel during braking and the time taken to come to rest. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the number of revolutions made by the flywheel before stopping and the heat energy dissipated by the brake. (7 marks)

### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Find the moment of inertia using the radius of gyration $k$:

$$I = Mk^2 = 50(0.4)^2 = 50(0.16) = 8 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Initial angular velocity (convert 300 rpm to rad/s):

$$\omega_1 = 300 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 300 \times 0.10472 = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Final angular velocity: $\omega_2 = 0$ (flywheel stops).

**Step 4:** Apply the equation of motion $T = I\alpha$ with $T = -25$ N·m (negative because it opposes motion):

$$\alpha = \frac{T}{I} = \frac{-25}{8} = -3.125 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

The negative sign indicates *deceleration* (angular acceleration opposite to motion).

$$\boxed{\alpha = -3.125 \text{ rad/s}^2 \text{ (magnitude 3.125 rad/s}^2\text{)}}$$

**Step 5:** Time to stop using $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t$:

$$0 = 31.416 + (-3.125)t$$

$$t = \frac{31.416}{3.125} = 10.053 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 10.05 \text{ s}}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Use $\omega_2^2 = \omega_1^2 + 2\alpha\theta$ to find total angular displacement:

$$0 = (31.416)^2 + 2(-3.125)\theta$$

$$\theta = \frac{(31.416)^2}{2(3.125)} = \frac{986.96}{6.25} = 157.91 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2:** Number of revolutions:

$$N = \frac{\theta}{2\pi} = \frac{157.91}{6.2832} = 25.13 \text{ revolutions}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 25.13 \text{ revolutions}}$$

**Step 3:** Heat energy dissipated by the brake. By the work-energy theorem, the work done by the braking torque equals the loss in rotational kinetic energy:

$$W_{brake} = \Delta KE_{rot} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}I\omega_1^2 = 0 - \frac{1}{2}(8)(31.416)^2$$

$$W_{brake} = -\frac{1}{2}(8)(986.96) = -3947.84 \text{ J}$$

The negative sign indicates that the brake *removes* energy from the system. The magnitude of heat dissipated is:

$$Q = \vert W_{brake} \vert = 3947.84 \text{ J} \approx 3.948 \text{ kJ}$$

**Alternative verification using $W = T\theta$:**

$$W = (-25)(157.91) = -3947.84 \text{ J} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 3947.84 \text{ J}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Failing to convert rpm to rad/s is the most common mistake. Remember: $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = N \text{ (rpm)} \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$.
> 2. **Sign of Angular Acceleration:** In braking/deceleration problems, $\alpha$ comes out negative. Don't drop the sign — it tells you the direction.
> 3. **Radius of Gyration Confusion:** Some students use the *radius* $R$ instead of the *radius of gyration* $k$ in $I = Mk^2$. These are different quantities!
> 4. **Heat Calculation:** Many students compute rotational KE and forget that all of it is dissipated as heat by the brake (assuming no other losses).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorize These Before the Exam)**

- ✅ **Core equation of motion:** $T = I\alpha$ (the rotational Newton's second law).
- ✅ **Three kinematic relations:** $v = r\omega$, $a_t = r\alpha$, $a_n = r\omega^2$.
- ✅ **No-slip condition** for pulley–string systems: $a = R\alpha$ and $v = R\omega$.
- ✅ **Constant angular acceleration formulas** mirror SUVAT: $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + \alpha t$, $\theta = \omega_1 t + \dfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$, $\omega_2^2 = \omega_1^2 + 2\alpha\theta$.
- ✅ **Moment of inertia** is the rotational analogue of mass; for a point mass at distance $r$: $I = mr^2$.
- ✅ **Standard $I$ values** for solid cylinder $\frac{MR^2}{2}$, ring $MR^2$, rod $\frac{ML^2}{12}$ (centre), $\frac{ML^2}{3}$ (end) — **memorize these**.
- ✅ **Radius of gyration:** $k = \sqrt{I/M}$, so $I = Mk^2$.
- ✅ **Rotational kinetic energy:** $KE_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$; **Total KE** (combined motion) = $\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$.
- ✅ **Power in rotation:** $P = T\omega$.
- ✅ **Work done by torque:** $W = T\theta$ (when $T$ is constant).
- ✅ **Right-hand rule** for angular velocity/torque direction: curl fingers in direction of rotation, thumb gives vector direction along axis.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** 1 rev $= 2\pi$ rad, $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ where $N$ is rpm.
- ✅ **For pulley–mass problems:** Always draw free-body diagrams of (i) the mass and (ii) the pulley separately, then write $F = ma$ for the mass and $T = I\alpha$ for the pulley, and link them with the no-slip condition.
- ✅ **Limiting case check:** If $I \to 0$ (massless pulley), $T = 0$ and the mass falls freely with $a = g$. If $I \to \infty$ (locked pulley), $a = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotation Under a Constant Moment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCEST103 - ENGINEERING MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotation Under a Constant Moment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotation Under a Constant Moment** is a state of rigid body motion in which a body rotates about a fixed axis under the action of an external moment (torque) that remains constant in magnitude and direction. This produces a **uniformly accelerated angular motion** characterised by a constant angular acceleration $\alpha$, analogous to linear motion under constant force.

When a **rigid body** of fixed mass moment of inertia $I$ about the axis of rotation is subjected to a **constant moment** $M$, the body's angular velocity changes uniformly with time, and the angular displacement follows a parabolic trajectory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Exams:**
> - **Moment (Torque):** The rotational equivalent of force, measured in **N·m**
> - **Mass Moment of Inertia ($I$):** The rotational equivalent of mass, measured in **kg·m²**
> - **Angular Acceleration ($\alpha$):** Rate of change of angular velocity, measured in **rad/s²**
> - **Angular Displacement ($\theta$):** Angle swept in **radians (rad)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Spinning Top":**
> Imagine a toy top on a table. When you give the spinning top's string a constant, steady pull, you are applying a constant torque. The top doesn't just spin at a fixed rate; it spins faster and faster, with the rotation speed increasing linearly with time. This is exactly "rotation under a constant moment" — just as a car under constant engine force (and no friction) accelerates uniformly, a body under constant torque rotates with uniform angular acceleration.

**Connecting Linear and Rotational Motion:**

| Linear Motion (Force) | Rotational Motion (Moment) |
|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Force $F$ (constant) | Moment $M$ (constant) |
| Mass $m$ | Moment of Inertia $I$ |
| Acceleration $a = F/m$ | Angular Acceleration $\alpha = M/I$ |
| Velocity $v$ | Angular Velocity $\omega$ |
| Displacement $s$ | Angular Displacement $\theta$ |

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **1 radian** = $180°/\pi \approx 57.296°$
- **SI unit of moment** = **N·m** (do NOT confuse with Joule, though dimensionally equal)
- **SI unit of mass moment of inertia** = **kg·m²**
- **g (gravitational acceleration)** = **9.81 m/s²** (standard value used in KTU numericals)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always express angles in **radians** when substituting into kinematic equations. Mixing degrees and radians is a guaranteed mark-loser!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Rotational Motion Under Constant Moment

The governing principle is **Newton's Second Law for Rotation:**

**Step 1 — Apply the Rotational Equation of Motion**

The resultant external moment about the fixed axis of rotation equals the product of mass moment of inertia and angular acceleration:

$$M = I \cdot \alpha$$

**Why this works:** Just as $F = m \cdot a$ in linear motion (where mass resists change in linear velocity), $M = I \cdot \alpha$ says that a moment causes angular acceleration, with $I$ being the body's resistance to change in angular velocity.

**Step 2 — Determine Angular Acceleration**

If $M$ is constant and $I$ is constant about the fixed axis:

$$\alpha = \frac{M}{I} = \text{constant}$$

This is the **defining condition** of the motion — $\alpha$ is uniform throughout the rotation.

**Step 3 — Apply the Rotational Kinematic Equations**

Since $\alpha$ is constant, the body follows kinematic equations strictly analogous to uniformly accelerated linear motion.

**Step 4 — Find Angular Velocity and Displacement**

These can be found as functions of time $t$ or as a function of each other (eliminating $t$).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for KTU Board Exams (2024 Scheme):**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $M = I \cdot \alpha$ | Newton's Law for Rotation | N·m |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{M}{I}$ | Angular Acceleration (constant) | rad/s² |
| 3 | $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$ | Angular velocity after time $t$ | rad/s |
| 4 | $\theta = \omega_0 t + \dfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$ | Angular displacement after time $t$ | rad |
| 5 | $\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta$ | Velocity–displacement relation | (rad/s)² |
| 6 | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}(\omega + \omega_0)t$ | Average angular velocity relation | rad |
| 7 | $\theta = \omega_0 t + \dfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$ | Displacement with initial velocity | rad |
| 8 | $n = \dfrac{60\omega}{2\pi}$ | RPM to rad/s conversion | rpm |
| 9 | $T_{eng} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Time for $N$ revolutions | s |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Notation:** $\omega_0$ = initial angular velocity; $\omega$ = final angular velocity; $t$ = elapsed time; $\theta$ = angular displacement. Do NOT confuse $\omega$ (rad/s) with $\Omega$ (ohms in electrical contexts) or $\omega$ (frequency in Hz — for rotational mechanics, $\omega$ is always in **rad/s**).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flywheel Design:** Energy storage in flywheels depends on $I\omega^2/2$; constant-moment rotation governs the spin-up phase.
- **Electric Motors & Turbines:** The rotor of an induction motor experiences a near-constant torque during start-up — the analysis is a textbook example of constant-moment rotation.
- **Pendulum Clocks & Governors:** Centrifugal governors in steam engines rely on rotational dynamics under constant driving moment.
- **Vehicle Drivetrains:** Wheels rotating under constant engine torque (during constant-throttle acceleration) follow these equations.
- **Industrial Mixers & Centrifuges:** Constant-moment drives enable predictable angular acceleration profiles essential for process control.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In any KTU numerical where the body starts from rest, immediately set $\omega_0 = 0$ — this simplifies all four kinematic equations significantly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Governing Equation

**Starting Point — Newton's Second Law for Rotation:**

The resultant external moment $M$ acting on a rigid body about a fixed axis of rotation is equal to the time rate of change of angular momentum.

For a rigid body (constant $I$ about the axis):

$$M = \frac{d}{dt}(I\omega) = I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Define Angular Acceleration**

By definition, angular acceleration is the time derivative of angular velocity:

$$\alpha = \frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Definition**

Replacing $d\omega/dt$ with $\alpha$ in the moment equation:

$$M = I \cdot \alpha$$

This is the **governing differential equation of motion** for rigid body rotation about a fixed axis.

**Step 3 — Apply the "Constant Moment" Condition**

Given that $M = \text{constant}$ and $I = \text{constant}$ (rigid body about fixed axis), we conclude:

$$\alpha = \frac{M}{I} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to Find Angular Velocity**

Starting from the definition $\alpha = d\omega/dt$, separate variables:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = \alpha = \frac{M}{I}$$

Integrate both sides with respect to $t$:

$$\int_{\omega_0}^{\omega} d\omega = \int_{0}^{t} \alpha \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Integration**

Since $\alpha$ is constant, it comes outside the integral:

$$\omega \Big\vert_{\omega_0}^{\omega} = \alpha \cdot t \Big\vert_{0}^{t}$$

This gives:

$$\omega - \omega_0 = \alpha t$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t}$$

This is the **first kinematic equation of rotation** under constant moment.

**Step 6 — Integrate to Find Angular Displacement**

Substitute $\omega = d\theta/dt$ into the result of Step 5:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega_0 + \alpha t$$

Separate variables and integrate:

$$\int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta = \int_{0}^{t} (\omega_0 + \alpha t) \, dt$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the Displacement Integral**

The right side splits into two integrals:

$$\theta = \omega_0 \int_{0}^{t} dt + \alpha \int_{0}^{t} t \, dt$$

$$\theta = \omega_0 \cdot t + \alpha \cdot \frac{t^2}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\theta = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2}$$

This is the **second kinematic equation**.

**Step 8 — Derive the Velocity–Displacement Relation**

Starting from $\alpha = d\omega/dt$ and using the chain rule $\alpha = (d\omega/d\theta)(d\theta/dt) = \omega \cdot d\omega/d\theta$:

$$\omega \, \frac{d\omega}{d\theta} = \alpha$$

Separate variables:

$$\omega \, d\omega = \alpha \, d\theta$$

Integrate from initial state $(\omega_0, 0)$ to final state $(\omega, \theta)$:

$$\int_{\omega_0}^{\omega} \omega \, d\omega = \alpha \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

$$\frac{\omega^2}{2} - \frac{\omega_0^2}{2} = \alpha \theta$$

Multiplying both sides by 2:

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta}$$

**Step 9 — Derive the Average Velocity Form**

The average angular velocity for uniform angular acceleration is:

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{\omega_0 + \omega}{2}$$

Multiplying by $t$:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{1}{2}(\omega_0 + \omega)t}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute $\alpha = M/I$ for Application**

In any KTU numerical where $M$ and $I$ are given, replace $\alpha$ with $M/I$ in any of the above equations:

$$\omega = \omega_0 + \frac{M}{I} t$$

$$\theta = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{M}{I}\right)t^2$$

$$\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\left(\frac{M}{I}\right)\theta$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (Foundation for KTU 2024 Style):**
> *A flywheel of mass moment of inertia 5 kg·m² is rotating at 100 rpm. A constant braking moment of 20 N·m is applied. Find (i) the time to come to rest, and (ii) the number of revolutions made before stopping.*

**Given Data:**

| Symbol | Value | Units |
|--------|-------|-------|
| $I$ | 5 | kg·m² |
| $\omega_0$ | 100 rpm | — |
| $M$ | $-20$ (braking) | N·m |
| $\omega$ (final) | 0 | rad/s |

**Step A — Convert Units**

Convert initial angular velocity from rpm to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \times 100}{60} = \frac{200\pi}{60} = \frac{10\pi}{3} \approx 10.472 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step B — Compute Angular Acceleration**

$$\alpha = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{-20}{5} = -4 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration (consistent with braking).

**Step C — Part (i): Time to Stop**

Using $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$ with $\omega = 0$:

$$0 = 10.472 + (-4) t$$

$$t = \frac{10.472}{4} = 2.618 \text{ s}$$

**Step D — Part (ii): Revolutions Before Stopping**

Using $\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta$ with $\omega = 0$:

$$0 = (10.472)^2 + 2(-4)\theta$$

$$\theta = \frac{(10.472)^2}{8} = \frac{109.66}{8} = 13.708 \text{ rad}$$

Convert to revolutions: $N_{rev} = \theta / (2\pi) = 13.708 / (2\pi) = 2.18$ revolutions

**Final Answers:**
- Time to stop: $t \approx 2.618$ s
- Revolutions: $\approx 2.18$ rev

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Check:** $\theta = \frac{1}{2}(\omega_0 + \omega)t = \frac{1}{2}(10.472 + 0)(2.618) = 13.71$ rad ✓ (matches Step D result)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constant-Moment Rotation Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Given Data] --> B[Convert All Angles to Radians]
    B --> C[Convert RPM to rad/s if needed]
    C --> D{Is Moment Constant?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute alpha = M / I]
    D -- No --> F[Use Variable alpha Analysis]
    E --> G[Identify Initial Conditions]
    G --> H[omega0 = Initial Angular Velocity]
    H --> I[theta0 = Initial Angular Displacement]
    I --> J[Select Appropriate Kinematic Equation]
    J --> K[Equation 1: omega = omega0 + alpha t]
    J --> L[Equation 2: theta = omega0 t + 0.5 alpha t squared]
    J --> M[Equation 3: omega squared = omega0 squared + 2 alpha theta]
    J --> N[Equation 4: theta = 0.5 times omega0 + omega times t]
    K --> O[Solve for Required Unknown]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Convert rad to RPM if Required]
    P --> Q[Convert rad to Revolutions if Required]
    Q --> R[Final Answer with Units]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Rotational Kinematics Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Constant Moment M] --> EQL[Newton Law for Rotation]
    I[Moment of Inertia I] --> EQL
    EQL --> EQM[M equals I times alpha]
    EQM --> A[Angular Acceleration alpha]
    A --> K1[Kinematic Equation 1]
    A --> K2[Kinematic Equation 2]
    A --> K3[Kinematic Equation 3]
    A --> K4[Kinematic Equation 4]
    K1 --> W1[omega equals omega0 plus alpha t]
    K2 --> W2[theta equals omega0 t plus 0.5 alpha t squared]
    K3 --> W3[omega squared equals omega0 squared plus 2 alpha theta]
    K4 --> W4[theta equals 0.5 times omega plus omega0 times t]
    W1 --> OUT[Final Rotational State]
    W2 --> OUT
    W3 --> OUT
    W4 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Description of Physical Setup (Forced Visualization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Diagram (Text-Based Schematic):**
>
> ```
>        ↑ y-axis
>        |
>        |     Tangential force F at radius r
>        |     ↓ (creates moment about z-axis)
>        |    ___________
>        |   /           \
>        |  |  RIGID      |   ← Rigid body rotating about z-axis
>        |  |  BODY       |      (passing through centre O)
>        |   \___________/
>        |         |
>        |         ●--------→ x-axis
>        |        / O
>        |       / z-axis (out of page)
>        |
> ```
>
> **Components shown:**
> - **O:** Centre of rotation (origin)
> - **r:** Radius at which tangential force acts
> - **F:** Tangential force → produces moment $M = F \times r$
> - **z-axis:** Axis of rotation (perpendicular to plane of page or through O)
> - **Direction of rotation:** Determined by right-hand rule (curling fingers along rotation, thumb points along $\omega$)

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic:** The angular acceleration vector $\vec{\alpha}$ points **along the axis of rotation** in the same direction as $\vec{\omega}$ if speeding up, and **opposite to $\vec{\omega}$** if slowing down.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. State the rotational analogue of Newton's Second Law of Motion and define each term involved. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The rotational analogue of Newton's Second Law states that the resultant external moment acting on a rigid body about a fixed axis of rotation is equal to the product of the mass moment of inertia about that axis and the resulting angular acceleration.

Mathematically:

$$M = I \cdot \alpha$$

Where:
- $M$ = resultant external moment about the axis of rotation (N·m)
- $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the body about the axis of rotation (kg·m²)
- $\alpha$ = angular acceleration of the body (rad/s²)

This is also expressed as: "The resultant moment is equal to the time rate of change of angular momentum."

---

**Q2. A wheel starts from rest and attains an angular velocity of 20 rad/s in 5 seconds under a constant moment. Find (a) the angular acceleration and (b) the angular displacement in this interval. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $\omega_0 = 0$, $\omega = 20$ rad/s, $t = 5$ s

**(a) Angular acceleration:**

Using $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$:

$$20 = 0 + \alpha \times 5$$

$$\alpha = \frac{20}{5} = 4 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**(b) Angular displacement:**

Using $\theta = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$:

$$\theta = 0 + \frac{1}{2}(4)(5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(4)(25) = 50 \text{ rad}$$

**Final Answers:** $\alpha = 4$ rad/s², $\theta = 50$ rad

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Section (14 Marks, Module Choice)

> **ESE Module Question Pattern: Answer ANY ONE full question from this module (a × 7 + b × 7 = 14 marks)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** A solid cylindrical rotor of mass 40 kg and radius 0.5 m is rotating about its central longitudinal axis at 300 rpm. A constant braking moment is applied and the rotor comes to rest in 50 revolutions. Determine:
   (i) The angular deceleration of the rotor.
   (ii) The magnitude of the braking moment.
   (iii) The time taken to come to rest. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the kinematic equation $\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta$ for rotation under constant angular acceleration. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Given Data:**

| Symbol | Value | Units |
|--------|-------|-------|
| $m$ | 40 | kg |
| $r$ | 0.5 | m |
| $N_0$ | 300 | rpm |
| $N$ (final) | 0 | rpm |
| Revolutions before stop | 50 | — |

**Step 1 — Calculate Mass Moment of Inertia**

For a solid cylinder about its central longitudinal axis:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}mr^2 = \frac{1}{2}(40)(0.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(40)(0.25) = 5 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert Initial Angular Velocity to rad/s**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi N_0}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Angular Displacement to Radians**

$$\theta = 50 \times 2\pi = 100\pi \approx 314.159 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — (i) Find Angular Deceleration**

Using $\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta$, with $\omega = 0$:

$$0 = (31.416)^2 + 2\alpha(314.159)$$

$$\alpha = -\frac{(31.416)^2}{2 \times 314.159} = -\frac{986.96}{628.32} \approx -1.571 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration.

**Step 5 — (ii) Find Braking Moment**

Using $M = I \alpha$:

$$M = 5 \times (-1.571) = -7.854 \text{ N·m}$$

The negative sign indicates the moment opposes the direction of rotation. The **magnitude** is:

$$|M| = 7.854 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Find Time to Stop**

Using $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$ with $\omega = 0$:

$$0 = 31.416 + (-1.571)t$$

$$t = \frac{31.416}{1.571} = 20 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answers (a):**
- (i) $\alpha = -1.571$ rad/s² (deceleration)
- (ii) $|M| = 7.854$ N·m
- (iii) $t = 20$ s

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Assumption:** The angular acceleration $\alpha$ is constant throughout the motion.

**Starting Equation:** By definition of angular acceleration:

$$\alpha = \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Chain Rule**

Since $\omega$ is a function of $t$ and $\theta$ is also a function of $t$, by the chain rule:

$$\alpha = \frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{d\omega}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{d\omega}{d\theta} \cdot \omega$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha = \omega \frac{d\omega}{d\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Separate Variables**

Multiplying both sides by $d\theta$:

$$\alpha \, d\theta = \omega \, d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Within Bounds**

Integrating from the initial state ($\omega = \omega_0$ at $\theta = 0$) to the final state ($\omega = \omega$ at $\theta = \theta$):

$$\int_{0}^{\theta} \alpha \, d\theta = \int_{\omega_0}^{\omega} \omega \, d\omega$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integrals**

Since $\alpha$ is constant, it can be taken outside the left integral:

$$\alpha \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta = \int_{\omega_0}^{\omega} \omega \, d\omega$$

$$\alpha \cdot \theta \Big\vert_{0}^{\theta} = \frac{\omega^2}{2} \Big\vert_{\omega_0}^{\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Limits**

$$\alpha \theta = \frac{\omega^2}{2} - \frac{\omega_0^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 2 on Both Sides**

$$2\alpha \theta = \omega^2 - \omega_0^2$$

**Step 7 — Final Rearrangement**

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha \theta}$$

This is the required kinematic equation, derived under the assumption of **constant angular acceleration**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]*

**(a)** A flywheel of mass moment of inertia 10 kg·m² about its axis of rotation is acted upon by a constant driving moment of 50 N·m. If the flywheel starts from rest, determine:
   (i) The angular velocity after 8 seconds.
   (ii) The number of revolutions completed in 8 seconds.
   (iii) The kinetic energy of rotation at the end of 8 seconds. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Establish the relationship between linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ for a point on a rotating body, and show that linear acceleration $a$ has two components: tangential and centripetal. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Given Data:**

| Symbol | Value | Units |
|--------|-------|-------|
| $I$ | 10 | kg·m² |
| $M$ | 50 | N·m |
| $\omega_0$ | 0 | rad/s |
| $t$ | 8 | s |

**Step 1 — Compute Angular Acceleration**

$$\alpha = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — (i) Find Angular Velocity After 8 s**

Using $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$:

$$\omega = 0 + 5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Find Number of Revolutions in 8 s**

Using $\theta = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$:

$$\theta = 0 + \frac{1}{2}(5)(8)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(5)(64) = 160 \text{ rad}$$

Convert to revolutions:

$$N = \frac{\theta}{2\pi} = \frac{160}{2\pi} = \frac{80}{\pi} \approx 25.46 \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Find Kinetic Energy of Rotation**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \frac{1}{2}(10)(40)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(10)(1600) = 8000 \text{ J}$$

**Final Answers (a):**
- (i) $\omega = 40$ rad/s
- (ii) $N \approx 25.46$ revolutions
- (iii) $KE = 8000$ J = 8 kJ

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Relationship Between Linear and Angular Velocity**

Consider a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis (z-axis) with angular velocity $\omega$. Consider a point P on the body at a perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis of rotation.

In a small time $dt$, the body rotates through an angle $d\theta$:

$$d\theta = \omega \, dt$$

The point P traces an arc of length $ds$ on a circle of radius $r$:

$$ds = r \, d\theta$$

Dividing both sides by $dt$:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = r \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

Since $ds/dt = v$ (linear velocity) and $d\theta/dt = \omega$ (angular velocity):

$$\boxed{v = r \omega}$$

The direction of $v$ is **tangent** to the circular path at P.

**Step 2 — Decompose Linear Acceleration**

Differentiating $v = r\omega$ with respect to $t$:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = r \frac{d\omega}{dt} = r\alpha$$

This is the **tangential component** of acceleration:

$$\boxed{a_t = r\alpha}$$

This component is **tangent** to the circular path and represents the change in **magnitude** of velocity.

**Step 3 — Centripetal (Radial) Component**

For circular motion, the direction of velocity continuously changes. This change is due to the centripetal (radial) acceleration:

$$\boxed{a_c = \frac{v^2}{r} = r\omega^2}$$

This component is directed **radially inward** (towards the centre of rotation) and represents the change in **direction** of velocity.

**Step 4 — Total Linear Acceleration**

The total linear acceleration of point P is the vector sum of tangential and centripetal components:

$$\boxed{a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_c^2} = \sqrt{(r\alpha)^2 + (r\omega^2)^2} = r\sqrt{\alpha^2 + \omega^4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of Components:**
> - **Tangential acceleration** $a_t = r\alpha$ — along the tangent, due to change in speed
> - **Centripetal acceleration** $a_c = r\omega^2$ — along the radius, due to change in direction

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing rpm with rad/s in the same equation. **Always convert to SI units (rad/s, kg·m², N·m) at the start.**
> 2. **Sign Confusion with Braking Moment:** When a braking/decelerating moment is applied, $\alpha$ is negative. Don't forget to include the sign or be consistent with magnitudes.
> 3. **Wrong Moment of Inertia Formula:** For a solid cylinder about its **central longitudinal axis**, $I = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$; about the **diameter**, $I = \frac{1}{4}mr^2 + \frac{1}{12}mL^2$. Read the geometry carefully.
> 4. **Degrees vs. Radians:** When asked for the number of revolutions, remember: 1 revolution = $2\pi$ rad. Never directly substitute degrees into $\theta = \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$.
> 5. **Forgetting to Convert Final Answer:** If a problem asks for angular velocity in **rpm** but you compute in **rad/s**, you lose 1 mark for "missing final unit conversion."
> 6. **Substitution Errors:** When $\omega_0 = 0$, students sometimes still write $\omega_0 t$ term — for clarity and to avoid confusion, explicitly state "since the body starts from rest, $\omega_0 = 0$."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⚡ **Core Governing Equation:** $M = I\alpha$ — Newton's Second Law for rotation.
- ⚡ **Constant Moment = Constant $\alpha$:** The defining condition of the problem type.
- ⚡ **Four Kinematic Equations to Memorize:**
  1. $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$
  2. $\theta = \omega_0 t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$
  3. $\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta$
  4. $\theta = \frac{1}{2}(\omega_0 + \omega)t$
- ⚡ **Unit Conversions:** 1 rpm = $\frac{2\pi}{60}$ rad/s; 1 revolution = $2\pi$ rad.
- ⚡ **Linear–Rotational Analogy:** $F \leftrightarrow M$, $m \leftrightarrow I$, $a \leftrightarrow \alpha$, $v \leftrightarrow \omega$, $s \leftrightarrow \theta$.
- ⚡ **Kinetic Energy of Rotation:** $KE = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ (analogous to $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$).
- ⚡ **Point on a Rotating Body:** Linear velocity $v = r\omega$; tangential acceleration $a_t = r\alpha$; centripetal acceleration $a_c = r\omega^2$.
- ⚡ **Power Transmitted by a Rotating Shaft:** $P = M \cdot \omega$ (in watts when $M$ in N·m and $\omega$ in rad/s).
- ⚡ **Deceleration Convention:** If a body is slowing down, $\alpha$ is **negative** in the chosen positive direction of rotation. The braking moment acts **opposite** to the angular velocity.
- ⚡ **Sign of Moment and Angular Acceleration:** $M$ and $\alpha$ have the **same sign** (from $M = I\alpha$ with $I > 0$ always).
- ⚡ **Starting from Rest:** Always set $\omega_0 = 0$ explicitly in such problems.
- ⚡ **Final-State Indicator:** If "comes to rest" or "stops" — the final $\omega = 0$.
- ⚡ **Real-World Examples:** Flywheels, motors, turbines, vehicle wheels, mixers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024 Exams:** *"Convert units first, identify $\omega_0$ and $\omega$, compute $\alpha = M/I$, then pick the kinematic equation that contains the unknown."* This 4-step mantra solves 90% of the standard numericals on this topic.
